mt_WcfaSfVT33.title: Counting Adventures!
explanation: Let’s learn about counting! When we count, we want to make sure each thing gets its own number. It's like giving a special sticker to every toy in your box – you wouldn’t give two stickers to the same teddy bear, would you? We need to point to or touch each item as we say its number so we know exactly how many there are.

Imagine you have five blocks! You can count them one by one. That means you touch each block with your finger as you say ‘one’, then ‘two’, then ‘three’, then ‘four’, and finally ‘five’. It’s important not to skip any blocks or count the same block twice, because that would make our number wrong!

Sometimes, someone might accidentally count a block more than once. If they said 'one, one, two, three,' you could gently show them that they counted the same block twice and say, ‘Let’s try again! We need to touch each block only once.’ This helps us be super careful counters!
keyIdeas: Each item gets its own number.
We count one thing at a time.
It's important not to skip or repeat numbers.

questions.prompt: I have three crayons. Let’s count them together! Point to each crayon as you say the number.
questions.explanation: Great job! You counted one, two, and three – that's right!

questions.prompt: Someone says ‘one, two, two, three.’ Is this correct?
questions.explanation: That’s not quite right! We need to touch each item only once.

questions.prompt: If you have four apples, how would you count them?
questions.explanation: Think about touching each apple as you say the number. Can you show me?

mt_mvXufozy2s.title: Asking for Help When You're Stuck!
explanation: Sometimes, we start a puzzle or building with our LEGOs and try really hard to make it look just right. We might put the blue blocks next to the red ones, or stack them up tall! But then, after trying for a little while, we still can’t finish it – it's tricky!

That’s okay! It’s important to know when you need help. Asking for help doesn’t mean you’re not good at things; it means you are smart enough to realize that sometimes, you need someone else’s ideas to solve a problem. Think about building a sandcastle on the playground – you can build and build, but if the waves keep washing it away, you might ask a grown-up for help with making a strong wall.

Asking for help is a super skill! It shows that you're brave enough to try something new and smart enough to know when you need a little support.  When we ask for help, someone can give us a fresh idea or show us a different way to do things – it’s like getting a secret superpower!
keyIdeas: Trying first is important.
It's okay to ask for help.
Asking helps you learn new ways.

questions.prompt: You are building with blocks and can’t finish. What should you do?
choices: A. Keep trying until you get it right.
B. Ask a grown-up for help.
C. Give up and throw the blocks away.
questions.explanation: Asking for help is a good way to solve problems!

questions.prompt: Building a sandcastle and the waves keep washing it away. What could you do?
choices: B. Ask a grown-up for help making a strong wall.
questions.explanation: Asking for help can give you a new idea to make your castle stronger.

questions.prompt: Why is it good to ask for help sometimes?
questions.explanation: It’s good because someone might have an idea you didn't think of!

mt_LhkP_KKIRS.title: Feeling Faces!
explanation: Let’s talk about how we feel! Sometimes we feel happy, like when you get a brand new toy car and zoom it around the playroom. Other times, we might feel sad, maybe if your favorite teddy bear gets lost or broken. It's important to know what feelings are – they tell us something about our bodies and what’s happening around us.

Our faces show how we feel too! When you’re happy, you smile really big with crinkly eyes. If you’re angry, your face might get red and you could frown.  When you're scared, you might squeeze your eyes shut tight because something unexpected startled you. Recognizing these different expressions helps us understand each other.

Everyone feels lots of different emotions throughout the day! It’s okay to feel sad sometimes, or even angry if someone takes your crayon. Learning about our feelings and talking about them with grown-ups and friends is a super important way to grow up healthy and happy.
keyIdeas: Happy
Sad
Angry

questions.prompt: You see your friend crying at the birthday party. What might they be feeling?
questions.explanation: They are crying, which usually means they feel sad.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows a happy face?
questions.explanation: Smiling is a common sign that someone is feeling happy.

questions.prompt: If you lose your favorite toy, what emotion might you feel?
questions.explanation: This question is open-ended to encourage the child to identify their own feelings based on the scenario.

mt_ggcamLzXAy.title: Feelings Change and Differ
explanation: Understand that everyone has feelings, that feelings change throughout the day, and that the same event can make different people feel different things
You know this idea when you can: Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day; Explain why two children might feel differently about the same event.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day
Explain why two children might feel differently about the same event
Recognise that feelings are temporary and can change

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day
questions.explanation: Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain why two children might feel differently about the same event
questions.explanation: Explain why two children might feel differently about the same event

questions.prompt: True or false: Recognise that feelings are temporary and can change
questions.explanation: Recognise that feelings are temporary and can change

mt_dmNvjroCPT.title: How Many in Total?
explanation: Let’s learn about counting! When we count things, like toys or snacks, we want to know how many there are. Remember, the last number you say is the most important – it tells us exactly how many things we counted. It doesn't matter if we put them in a different order; that final number is always correct!

Imagine you have your building blocks. You might start counting from 1, 2, 3… and then stop at 7. Even if you move the blocks around and start again at 1, 2, 3... 7, it *still* has seven blocks! That’s because the last number we said – ‘7’ – tells us the total.

Let's try another example with your favorite fruit, like apples. If you count out five red apples and then ask 'How many apples are there?', you should answer 'five'! It doesn’t matter if you put them in a circle or line; five is always the right number.
keyIdeas: The last number counted tells us the total.
Rearranging objects doesn't change the count.
Counting in different orders gives the same total.

questions.prompt: You count out 6 crayons and then ask, 'How many crayons are there?' What should you say?
questions.explanation: The last number you said was ‘6’, so that’s the total!

questions.prompt: You have a set of toys and rearrange them. You then ask, 'How many toys do we have?'
questions.explanation: Rearranging the toys doesn't change how many there are in total.

questions.prompt: You count out 4 cookies and then ask, 'How many cookies?'
questions.explanation: Can you tell me the answer to this question?

mt_WBfj79OqXz.title: Holding My Pencil Just Right!
explanation: Let's talk about how we hold our pencils! When you’re drawing or writing, it’s important to have a good grip so your hand doesn’t get tired. Imagine holding a crayon – you want to hold it in a way that feels comfy and lets you move it easily, just like this! We use a special way of holding the pencil called a ‘tripod grip,’ which means using your thumb, pointer finger, and middle finger to hold it.

Now, let’s look at how we start writing letters. Every letter has a special starting point – it's where you begin drawing that letter! When you start at the right place, the letter will grow bigger and bigger in the right direction. Think about writing the letter ‘a’ – you always start from the top right corner of the paper.

It’s also important to keep your letters the same size when you write on lined paper. If one letter is tiny and another is huge, it can be confusing! We want all our letters to be just right so everyone knows what we are writing. Practice makes perfect, so let's try holding our pencils and making some letters!
keyIdeas: Tripod Grip
Starting Points
Consistent Size

questions.prompt: When you hold your pencil, which fingers do you use?
choices: B. Thumb, pointer finger, and middle finger
questions.explanation: Using your thumb, pointer finger, and middle finger is the tripod grip – it's the best way to hold a pencil!

questions.prompt: Where should you start writing a letter?
choices: B. At the special starting point for that letter
C. In the middle of the page
questions.explanation: Every letter has a specific place where you begin, which helps it grow correctly.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to keep your letters the same size?
choices: A. It makes them look prettier
C. So everyone can read what you wrote clearly
questions.explanation: If all your letters are the same size, it's easier for people to understand what you're writing.

mt_zuKAX6lcYR.title: Subtraction is Taking Away!
explanation: Let's learn about subtraction! Subtraction means taking things away or separating them from a group. It’s like when you have some toys and you give one to your friend – that’s subtraction in action! We use it all the time, even with yummy snacks like cookies.

Imagine you have five biscuits on your plate (hold up 5 fingers). Now, let's pretend you eat two of them (take away 2 fingers).  We are taking those biscuits *away* from the group. Counting what’s left – one, two, three – shows us we have three biscuits remaining! This is subtraction!

So, when we subtract, we're figuring out how many things are *left over*. It’s like if you have nine building blocks and you take away three, you can count the blocks that are still standing to see how many you have left. That number is your answer!
keyIdeas: Subtraction means taking away.
It helps us find what's left.
We use subtraction every day.

questions.prompt: You have 7 crayons and you give 3 to your sister. How many crayons do you have left?
questions.explanation: Let's count! You started with 7, gave away 3, so 7 - 3 = 4.

questions.prompt: Lily has 8 apples. She eats 2 of them. How many apples are left?
questions.explanation: If you start with 8 and take away 2, you have 8 - 2 = 6 apples remaining.

questions.prompt: What does subtraction help us do?
questions.explanation: Subtraction helps us find out how many things are left after we take some away.

mt_OvyoRo47K-.title: Let's Add!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about adding! Adding means putting things together to find out how many there are in all. Imagine you have some building blocks – let’s say you have 2 red blocks and 1 blue block. We can put them together to see how many blocks we have total!

We can use our toys to help us understand adding. Let's pretend you have three toy cars, and your friend gives you two more! You would count all the cars together – one, two, three, four, five, six! That means you have six cars altogether because you put the groups of three and two together.

Adding is like making a big pile. When we add, we’re combining things to get a bigger number. If you have 4 cookies and your mom gives you 2 more, you can count all the cookies – one, two, three, four, five, six! Now you have six yummy cookies altogether because you added the groups together.
keyIdeas: Adding means putting things together.
We use counting to find the total.
Addition helps us know how many there are in all.

questions.prompt: You have 2 apples and your friend gives you 1 apple. How many apples do you have?
questions.explanation: That’s right! You put the two apples you had plus the one your friend gave you together.

questions.prompt: You see 1 teddy bear and then 3 more teddy bears arrive. How many teddy bears are there now?
questions.explanation: Excellent! You added the first teddy bear to the three that arrived.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when we add?
questions.explanation: Adding means putting things together to find out how many there are in all!

mt_LpSuPgL31x.title: Sharing is Caring: Dividing Things Up!
explanation: Let's talk about sharing! When we share something with our friends, we want to make sure everyone gets a fair amount. Division helps us do just that – it’s like making groups of things so everyone has the same number. Imagine you have 10 yummy cookies and you want to give them equally to you and your brother; division will show you how many cookies each of you gets!

We can also think about dividing things into sets. Let's say we have 12 building blocks, and we want to make groups of three. We can count out three blocks at a time until all the blocks are gone – that’s division! It’s like making little piles of the same number of toys; this helps us understand how many groups we can create.

Let's try an example with grapes! If you have 8 grapes and want to share them equally between 2 friends, you would give each friend 4 grapes. That means you divided the grapes into two equal groups.  Seeing things in groups is a really important part of understanding division – it’s all about fairness!
keyIdeas: Division helps us share fairly.
Dividing means making equal groups.
We can use objects to see how many groups we make.

questions.prompt: You have 6 crayons and want to give them equally to you and your friend. How many crayons does each of you get?
questions.explanation: Each of you gets 3 crayons because 6 divided by 2 equals 3.

questions.prompt: You have 9 toy cars and want to make groups of 3. How many groups do you make?
questions.explanation: You make 2 groups because 9 divided by 3 equals 3 groups.

questions.prompt: If you have 10 stickers and want to share them equally between 5 people, how many stickers does each person get?
questions.explanation: Each person gets 2 stickers. (10 / 5 = 2)

mt_mB7DVai-Uf.title: Listening and responding
explanation: Listen and respond appropriately to adults and peers; follow agreed-upon rules for discussion such as listening to others and taking turns speaking
You know this idea when you can: Demonstrate attentive listening by making eye contact and responding relevantly; Follow classroom discussion rules (e.g. raise hand, wait for turn).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate attentive listening by making eye contact and responding relevantly
Follow classroom discussion rules (e.g. raise hand, wait for turn)
Show understanding of what was said by paraphrasing or responding appropriately

questions.explanation: Show understanding of what was said by paraphrasing or responding appropriately

mt__h7hvT4tEb.title: Comparing groups: more or fewer
explanation: Compare two groups of objects to determine which has more, fewer, or whether they are equal, using matching and counting strategies
You know this idea when you can: Use one-to-one matching to compare two groups; State which group has more/fewer after counting both.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Use one-to-one matching to compare two groups
State which group has more/fewer after counting both
Use the language 'equal to', 'more than', 'less than', 'fewer', 'most', 'least'

mt_NtJYlJdUe9.title: Shapes All Around Us!
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes! Everything around us has different parts, and we can describe them. We can say things like ‘long,’ ‘short,’ ‘big,’ or ‘small.’ These words help us understand how things look and feel – they're called attributes!

Imagine you have a favorite toy car. You can see it’s long and maybe even a little heavy if you push it! Or perhaps your building blocks are tall and square. We use these words to describe what we see, like saying ‘this block is taller than that one.’

When we look at things like our juice box or a stack of books, we can also talk about how much space they take up – this is called capacity! A big bottle has more capacity than a small cup. Let’s use words like 'full' and ‘empty’ to describe it!
keyIdeas: We can describe objects using words.
‘Long,’ ‘short,’ ‘heavy,’ and ‘full’ are ways to talk about things.
Comparing objects helps us understand their attributes.

questions.prompt: Which word describes a long crayon?
questions.explanation: A long crayon is longer than it is short.

questions.prompt: Your friend has a big box of crayons and you have a small one. Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: The word ‘smaller’ means less than, so your crayon is smaller.

questions.prompt: Which of these things could you describe as 'full'?
questions.explanation: Something 'full' has a lot of something inside it, like water in a bottle.

mt_7XcCG43ZZW.title: Number Pairs!
explanation: Let’s learn about numbers up to ten! Sometimes, we can break a number into smaller parts that add up to it. Think of your favorite toy – maybe you have a teddy bear. You can see that the teddy bear is one whole thing, but we can also think about how many arms it has and how many legs it has too!

We can make pairs! Let’s take the number five. We can say 5 is made of 1 plus 4, or 2 plus 3, or even 0 plus 5 – that's still adding up to five! It’s like building with blocks; you can use different amounts to build one big tower.

When we find these pairs, we write them as equations. This means we are showing how the numbers add together. So, 5 = 1 + 4 is an equation that tells us how to make five using two different groups of things.
keyIdeas: Numbers can be broken into parts.
We can find more than one way to make a number.
Equations show how numbers add together.

questions.prompt: If you have 3 blocks, and your friend gives you 2 more, how many blocks do you have in total?
questions.explanation: We can make 3 + 2 = 5.  That’s one way to pair the numbers!

questions.prompt: Which of these shows how we can make the number 4?
questions.explanation: Two plus two makes four! This is a pair that works.

questions.prompt: Can you make the number 6 using different pairs of numbers?
questions.explanation: Think about it! Can you tell me how to make six by adding two numbers together?

explanation: Find the number that makes 10 when added to a given number from 1 to 9 (number bonds to 10)

You know this idea when you can: Distinguish between complete sentences and fragments; Compose a complete sentence with a subject and verb.

Compose a complete sentence with a subject and verb

mt_1KCwbGvm1F.title: Reading Adventures!
explanation: Let’s talk about how we read! When you look at a book, it's like a secret code. The letters and words are arranged in a special way to tell us stories and share information with us. We use our eyes to see these shapes – the letters – and our brains to understand what they mean.

When we read, we always start at the top of the page and move across the words, left to right, just like we write! Imagine you're building a road for your toy cars; you wouldn’t start in the middle, would you?  It’s important to follow the direction so we don’t miss any of the story.

Think about your favorite picture book – maybe one with a teddy bear or yummy cookies. When you look at the page, you know to begin reading at the top and move across the words until you reach the end before moving down to the next line. That's how we unlock all the amazing stories inside!
keyIdeas: Print has meaning.
We read left to right.
Books are organized by pages.

questions.prompt: Which way do we look when reading a book?
questions.explanation: We read from left to right, just like writing!

questions.prompt: What should you do first when you see a new page in a book?
choices: B. Start reading at the top of the page
questions.explanation: We always begin reading at the top of the page.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re looking at a picture book - where do you start to read?
questions.explanation: You should start at the top-left corner and follow the words left to right, then move down to the next line.

mt_Qcp2d_kuta.title: Wonderful 3-D Shapes!
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes! You know that some shapes are flat, like squares and circles? But today we're learning about *three-dimensional* shapes – they have length, width, and height! These shapes take up space around us, just like your favorite building blocks or a toy car.

There are lots of different 3-D shapes. A cube is like a box with all the same sides – think of a dice! A sphere is round, like a ball you might bounce at the playground.  A cylinder looks like a can of juice, tall and smooth on both ends.

And don’t forget about pyramids and cones! A pyramid has a flat base and pointy top, like some building blocks or even a party hat. A cone is similar but always has a point at its very top – like an ice cream cone you might eat for dessert!
keyIdeas: 3-D shapes have length, width, and height.
Different 3-D shapes look and feel different.
You can find 3-D shapes all around you.

questions.prompt: Which shape is round like a ball?
questions.explanation: A sphere is perfectly round, just like a bouncy ball!

questions.prompt: What shape is like a can of soup or juice?
questions.explanation: A cylinder has two flat ends and a curved side, just like a can.

questions.prompt: Can you name one 3-D shape you see at home?
questions.explanation: Think about things around your house – is there anything shaped like a cube or a sphere?

mt_SeNxOZTHCN.title: Words for Big Feelings
explanation: Know and use the key words for managing big feelings — calm, strategy, cope, settle, patience, overwhelmed, and breathe — and understand that having words for these ideas is the first step to using them

You know this idea when you can: Use words like 'frustrated', 'anxious', 'overwhelmed', and 'disappointed' accurately to describe how they feel in difficult moments; Explain what 'calm down' strategies are in their own words and name at least two they have tried (e.g. deep breaths, counting, walking away).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

mt_uhuxX8sg9f.title: Tall and Short!
explanation: Let’s talk about how things can be different sizes! Sometimes something is tall, like a grown-up or a big building. Other times something is short, like a little toy car or your teddy bear. We can use words like ‘tall’ and ‘short’ to describe how high something is – it's all about measuring how much space something takes up!

Imagine you have a block tower and a Lego house. The block tower might be shorter than the Lego house, right? That means the Lego house is taller! We can say that the Lego house is ‘taller’ than the block tower because it reaches higher up in the air. It's like comparing how high your favorite building blocks stack!

Let’s look at ribbons! You have a long ribbon and a shorter ribbon. The long ribbon is longer than the short ribbon, so we can say that the long ribbon is ‘longer’. And the short ribbon is shorter than the long ribbon – it's just the opposite! Now you know how to compare things by their length!
keyIdeas: Tall means high; Short means low.
We use words like 'taller', 'shorter', 'longer', and 'shorter' to describe sizes.
Comparing objects helps us understand differences.

questions.prompt: Which is taller, a chair or a book?
questions.explanation: The chair is usually much taller than a book.

questions.prompt: If you have a long crayon and a short crayon, which one is longer?
questions.explanation: ‘Longer’ means it's bigger in length.

questions.prompt: Can you point to something that is shorter than you?
questions.explanation: Think about things around you – a toy, a block, or even your own shoes!

explanation: Compare and describe capacity and volume using language such as full, empty, more than, less than, half full

mt_-P1kdZhHbL.title: Heavy or Light?
explanation: Today, we're going to learn about how things feel when they are heavy or light! Imagine you have a favorite toy car – maybe it’s red and speedy. Sometimes toys feel really heavy in your hands, like a big building block, and sometimes they feel super light, like a fluffy teddy bear.

When we talk about something being 'heavy' or 'light', we mean how much stuff is inside it. A rock is usually heavier than a balloon because rocks have lots of tiny pieces packed together. We can use our hands to feel the difference – does it make your arm tired when you hold it, or does it feel easy?

Let’s try an experiment! I'm going to give you two things and ask you to tell me which one is heavier or lighter. If one thing feels like it needs a lot of effort to lift, we call that ‘heavy’. If it feels light and doesn't make your arms tired, we say it’s ‘light’.
keyIdeas: Heavy means more stuff inside.
Light means less stuff inside.
We can use our hands to compare heavy and light.

questions.prompt: I have a big book and a small crayon. Which one is heavier?
choices: C) They are the same weight
questions.explanation: The big book has more paper inside, so it’s heavier.

questions.prompt: You hold a ball and a feather. Which is lighter?
choices: C) They are the same weight
questions.explanation: Feathers have very little stuff inside, so they feel light.

questions.prompt: If something feels like it takes a lot of effort to lift, is it heavy or light?
questions.explanation: Things that are heavy require more strength to lift.

mt_-vvVxpOHG2.title: Time Order Adventures!
explanation: Let’s talk about what happens every day! We do lots of things, and sometimes they happen in a special order, like building with blocks. First, you might stack the big blocks up high, then you add the smaller ones on top. It's important to think about *when* each thing happened – did it come before or after?

We can use words like ‘first’, ‘next’, and ‘last’ to tell a story about our day! Imagine your favorite toy car: first, you might take it out of the box, next you drive it around the room, and last you put it back in its box. Using these words helps us understand what comes before and after something exciting.

Sometimes we talk about things that happened yesterday, today, or tomorrow! Yesterday is when you played with your friends, today is when you’re listening to this story, and tomorrow might be a day for a special treat. Knowing the difference between these times helps us understand how time works!
keyIdeas: Order events using ‘first’, ‘next’, ‘last’
Use ‘yesterday’, ‘today’, ‘tomorrow’ correctly.
Describe events as happening in the ‘morning’ or ‘afternoon’

questions.prompt: First, you brush your teeth. Next, you… ?
questions.explanation: We need to think about what happens *after* brushing our teeth!

questions.prompt: Yesterday, you ______. ?
questions.explanation: Yesterday is before today – remember that!

questions.prompt: The sun comes up in the ______.
questions.explanation: The morning is when we see the first light of the day!

mt_frDIaXzWbx.title: Let's Learn Our Letters!
explanation: Hello everyone! Today we’re going on an adventure to learn all the letters of the alphabet. The alphabet is like a special list that tells us how to write words, and it has twenty-six letters – wow! We'll start with the big letters, called uppercase letters, like the ones you see at the beginning of your name.

Let’s look at some familiar things and find their letters. See this teddy bear? The word ‘teddy’ starts with the letter T! Or how about an apple? The word ‘apple’ begins with the letter A. We can find letters everywhere if we just take a good look, and that's really exciting!

Now let’s try to say them all together! We start with A, then B, C, D…all the way until Z! It’s like singing a song – ‘A is for apple, B is for bear, C is for cat!’ Practicing our letter names will help us read and write lots of words.
keyIdeas: There are 26 letters in the alphabet.
We can find letters in everyday things.
Knowing letter names helps us read!

questions.prompt: What letter comes after 'B'?
questions.explanation: Remember, the alphabet goes A, B, C...

questions.prompt: Which letter is at the beginning of the word ‘dog’?
questions.explanation: Let's look closely – dog starts with the letter D.

questions.prompt: Can you name one letter from A to Z?
questions.explanation: Try your best! We are learning!

mt_n6GhzDPllD.title: Shape Detectives!
explanation: Let's become shape detectives! Shapes are all around us, like the blocks we build with or the yummy cookies shaped like stars. We see squares in our windows and circles in a plate of pizza – shapes make up everything! It’s fun to look closely and try to find different shapes hiding in plain sight.

When we talk about shapes, we can use special words like ‘square,’ ‘circle,’ ‘triangle,’ and ‘rectangle.’ Let's say you see a ball; it might be round like a circle.  You could say, 'I think the ball is a circle because it’s round!' That’s exploring an idea with our words.

Talking about shapes helps us understand them better. If we build a tower with blocks and make it into a triangle, we're thinking about how shapes fit together.  It’s like playing a game where you use your words to figure out what something is – that’s called exploring ideas through talk!
keyIdeas: Shapes are everywhere.
We can describe shapes with words.
Talking helps us understand shapes better.

questions.prompt: What shape is a pizza?
questions.explanation: Pizza is usually round, just like a circle!

questions.prompt: I think it might be because…
choices: C. I’m trying to figure something out
questions.explanation: This is how we explore ideas – asking ‘why?’

questions.prompt: Which shape has three sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle always has three straight sides!

questions.explanation: Distinguish short vowel sounds in CVC words (e.g. /a/ in 'cat' vs /e/ in 'bed')

questions.explanation: Associate long and short vowel sounds with common single-letter spellings

mt_KJeEeTutJI.title: Shapes All Around Us!
explanation: Let’s learn about shapes! Shapes are all around us – they have sides and corners. We see them in lots of things, like building blocks or even the yummy pizza we eat for dinner. These shapes help us understand what we're looking at!

There are different kinds of 2-D shapes, which means they only have two dimensions: length and width. A circle is round just like a ball you might play with! A triangle has three sides that meet to make a point, like the roof of a house.

A rectangle has four straight sides, where opposite sides are equal in length - think about a book or a window. And a square is special because it’s a rectangle where all four sides are exactly the same! Now let's look for shapes everywhere!
keyIdeas: Circles are round.
Triangles have three sides.
Rectangles have four straight sides.

questions.prompt: Which shape has three sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle always has three sides! Can you draw a triangle?

questions.prompt: What shape is like a window or a book?
questions.explanation: A rectangle has four straight sides, just like a window or a book.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes is round?
questions.explanation: A circle never has corners; it’s always perfectly round!

mt_PvU3eoikev.title: Sound Detectives: Onsets & Rimes!
explanation: Let's become sound detectives! We’re going to learn about how words are made of smaller parts, like building blocks. These blocks are called ‘onsets’ and ‘rimes’. An onset is the beginning part of a word – it’s the first sounds you hear, like in the word 'sun'.  A rime is everything else after the onset; it's the vowel sound and any other letters that make a long sound, such as in the word 'play'.

Think about your favorite toy! Let’s say you have a red block. The ‘red’ part of the word is the rime – it tells us what color the block is. The ‘block’ part is the onset – it tells us what kind of thing it is. We can put these parts together to make the whole word, 'block'!  We do this all the time when we talk and read!

Now let's try changing a word! If you say '/b/ /a/ /t/' slowly, can you blend those sounds together to say ‘bat’? That’s because ‘bat’ is made of those three parts. We can even change one sound – if we take the /c/ from 'cat' and put it in 'bat', we make a brand new word! This helps us understand how words work.
keyIdeas: Onsets are the beginning sounds.
Rimes are the vowel sound and other letters.
Changing one sound makes a new word.

questions.prompt: What part of the word ‘sun’ is the onset?
choices: B. The first sounds you hear
questions.explanation: The onset is the beginning part of a word, like the first sounds we hear.

questions.prompt: Which sound is in the rime of the word ‘tree’?
questions.explanation: The rime includes the vowel sound and any letters that make a long sound.

questions.prompt: If you change the /m/ in 'map' to /s/, what new word do you make?
questions.explanation: Changing the ‘m’ to ‘s’ creates a different word – ‘sap’.

mt_4GiE83rJF_.title: Rhyme Time!
explanation: Let’s talk about words that sound alike! When we say ‘cat,’ ‘hat,’ and ‘mat,’ do you hear how they are similar? These words *rhyme* because the ending sounds are exactly the same – they make a fun, bouncy noise when we say them. Rhyming is like a secret code in our language, making stories and songs extra special.

Think about your favorite building blocks! If you have a red block, a blue block, and a green block, do they all sound the same? Probably not – but if you had a ‘block’ and a ‘clock,’ those words rhyme because their endings sound alike.  We can find rhymes in lots of things we see and hear every day, like toys or yummy snacks!

When we read stories with rhyming words, it's like listening to a song! We often hear the same words repeated over and over again, which helps us guess what comes next.  Joining in and saying the rhymes with the storyteller is super fun, and it’s how we learn to love reading even more.
keyIdeas: Rhyming words have similar ending sounds.
We can find rhyming words everywhere!
Repeating rhymes helps us predict what comes next.

questions.prompt: Which of these words rhymes with ‘dog’?
questions.explanation: ‘Log’ and ‘dog’ both end with the ‘-og’ sound.

questions.prompt: Can you think of a word that rhymes with ‘sun’?
questions.explanation: ‘Run’ and ‘sun’ share the same ending sounds.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when words rhyme?
choices: C. They are made of blocks
questions.explanation: Rhyming words have similar ending sounds, making them sound pleasing to the ear.

mt__KHQttMde3.title: Blending Sounds!
explanation: Let's learn how to read words by blending sounds together! When you see a new word, like 'cat', we don’t just look at the whole word all at once. Instead, we break it down into its smaller parts – the sounds each letter makes.

Each letter has a sound, and when we put those sounds together in the right order, we can read the whole word! Think about your building blocks; you stack them one by one to build something amazing. Blending sounds is just like stacking sounds to make words!
It’s like playing a game of ‘I Spy’! You listen carefully for each sound and then put them together. Let's try it with the word 'dog'. Can you say /d/-/o/-/g/? That's right – 'dog'!
keyIdeas: Sound out letters.
Blend sounds together.
Reading is like building!

questions.prompt: What do we do when we see a new word?
choices: A. Look at the whole word instantly.
B. Break it down into its sounds.
C. Ask someone else to read it.
questions.explanation: We break the word down into individual sounds that we can then blend together.

questions.prompt: If you hear /b/-/a/-/g/, what word are you reading?
questions.explanation: The sounds /b/, /a/, and /g/ when blended together make the word 'bag'.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to sound out words?
choices: B. It helps us read correctly.
questions.explanation: Sounding out the letters allows you to put them together in the correct order to form a word.

mt_wwdRhPyz6s.title: Group discussions
explanation: Participate actively in collaborative conversations staying on topic; continue a conversation through multiple exchanges; maintain attention in discussions
You know this idea when you can: Stay on topic during a conversation for multiple turns; Build on what another speaker has said (e.g. 'I agree because...').
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Stay on topic during a conversation for multiple turns
Build on what another speaker has said (e.g. 'I agree because...')
Maintain focus and contribute meaningfully in group discussions

questions.prompt: True or false: Stay on topic during a conversation for multiple turns
questions.explanation: Stay on topic during a conversation for multiple turns

questions.prompt: True or false: Build on what another speaker has said (e.g. 'I agree because...')
questions.explanation: Build on what another speaker has said (e.g. 'I agree because...')

questions.prompt: True or false: Maintain focus and contribute meaningfully in group discussions
questions.explanation: Maintain focus and contribute meaningfully in group discussions

mt_4A7FYmvVhA.title: Let's Tell Stories with Words!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn how to tell stories really well! When you want someone else to understand what you’re talking about, it’s important to give lots of details. Think about your favorite teddy bear – it's not just a teddy bear, is it? It has fuzzy brown fur, big button eyes, and maybe even a little bow!

Speaking clearly means using our voices so everyone can hear us! Imagine you are telling your friend about building a tower with blocks; you need to say each word loud enough for them to understand.  If you mumble or whisper, it’s hard for others to follow along and picture what you're describing.

Organizing our ideas helps too! Let’s say we’re talking about going to the park – first we might talk about getting ready (putting on shoes!), then we could describe the playground (the swings, the slide), and finally, we could tell about playing with our friends. This makes our stories easier to understand.
keyIdeas: Use lots of details when you speak.
Speak clearly so everyone can hear you.
Organize your ideas for a better story.

questions.prompt: My friend is building a tower. What should I do to help them understand what they are doing?
choices: A. Whisper so I don't bother them
B. Speak clearly and tell them about the blocks
C. Ignore them and play with my toys
questions.explanation: Speaking clearly helps your friend understand the steps involved.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are describing a yummy cookie! What details would you include?
choices: B. Describe its color, shape, and how it tastes
C.  Don’t say anything about cookies
questions.explanation: Giving lots of details makes your description more interesting.

questions.prompt: What is one way to organize a story you are telling?
choices: B. Tell it in order, like getting ready for school
C.  Just shout the most exciting parts
questions.explanation: Organizing your ideas helps make your story easier to follow.

mt_qeZYF6HZ4o.title: Where Are You?
explanation: Let’s talk about where things are! We can use special words to tell you exactly where something is. These words like ‘above,’ ‘below,’ ‘beside,’ ‘in front of,’ and ‘behind’ help us understand how things are arranged around us. It's like giving directions, but instead of saying 'turn left,' we say 'the toy is behind the book!'

Imagine you have your favorite building blocks – let’s say you have a big red cube and a small blue block. You can put the cube ‘on top of’ the table! That means it's higher than the table, like it’s sitting right on top. We use these words to describe how things are related to each other in space.

Now let's play a game! I want you to 'put the cube behind the box.' This means you need to move the cube so that it is at the back of the box, away from you.  Using these words helps us understand and follow instructions perfectly – just like when your mom or dad tells you where to put your toys!
keyIdeas: Above
Below
Beside

questions.prompt: Where is the teddy bear?
questions.explanation: The question asks for a location, and ‘beside’ means next to something.

questions.prompt: What should you do with the block?
choices: A. Put it behind the car
B. Put it on top of the shelf
C. Put it under the rug
questions.explanation: The question asks for a positional action, and ‘behind’ means at the back of something.

questions.prompt: If you see a book, where is the crayon?
questions.explanation: This question tests understanding of 'beside,' meaning next to.

explanation: Articulate and justify answers, arguments, and opinions; participate in discussions, presentations, performances, role play, and debates

keyIdeas: State an opinion and give a reason (e.g. 'I think ... because ...')
Participate in a class presentation or performance with confidence
Explain thinking when answering a question rather than just giving a one-word answer

mt_BtMbZibZUj.title: Vowel Digraph Adventures!
explanation: Let's explore some cool words where two vowels work together to make a new sound! These are called vowel digraphs, and they’re like secret codes in reading. When you see ‘ai’ or ‘ay’, it means the ‘a’ makes a different sound than if it was all by itself.  It’s like when you have a friend who speaks with a funny accent – their words sound different!

Think about your favorite toy, maybe a teddy bear! The word 'rain' has the digraph ‘ai’. When we say ‘rain’, we hear the ‘a’ make a short sound, unlike if we just said ‘a’.  Similarly, the word ‘boat’ uses the ‘oa’ digraph, where the ‘o’ makes a long sound.  These combinations help us read and understand so many words!

Now let's try spelling! If you see the digraph ‘ee’, it often means the ‘e’ makes a long sound too, like in the word 'tree'. We can use these patterns to write our own words – for example, we could spell ‘coin’ using the ‘oi’ digraph.  Learning about vowel digraphs is like unlocking a secret language of reading!
keyIdeas: Vowel Digraphs are two vowels together.
Digraphs make new sounds.
We can use them to read and spell.

questions.prompt: Which digraph makes the ‘a’ say its name?
questions.explanation: The ‘ai’ digraph is a common vowel digraph where the ‘a’ says its own sound.

questions.prompt: What does ‘oo’ usually make when it's in a word?
questions.explanation: The ‘oo’ digraph often makes a long ‘o’ sound as seen in words like ‘moon’.

questions.prompt: Can you spell the word ‘coin’ using a vowel digraph?
questions.explanation: Encourage child to answer with 'oi' or another correct response.

explanation: Represent addition and subtraction using objects, drawings, and mental images
You know this idea when you can: Use cubes or counters to show 3 + 2; Draw a picture to represent a subtraction situation.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Use cubes or counters to show 3 + 2
Draw a picture to represent a subtraction situation
Use fingers to model an addition problem

mt_yJmvUCCym7.title: Adding and Subtracting Fun!
explanation: Let's learn about adding and subtracting! Adding means putting things together, like when you have some blocks and your friend gives you more. Subtracting means taking things away, like if you have a cookie and you eat one – that’s subtraction! We can use our fingers or draw pictures to help us solve these problems.

Imagine you're building with LEGOs. You start with 5 red bricks and then your brother gives you 3 blue ones. To add them together, we count all the bricks: 5 + 3 = 8! That means you have 8 LEGO bricks in total. Now, let’s say you want to build a tower and use 7 blocks, but you accidentally drop 2 – that's subtracting!

Sometimes, word problems tell us to ‘put together’ or ‘take apart’. If we have 4 crayons and someone gives us 2 more, we put them all together to find out how many we have.  If we had 9 stickers and took away 5, we are taking them apart to see what is left. These problems help us understand adding and subtracting in a fun way!
keyIdeas: Addition means putting things together.
Subtraction means taking things away.
We can use pictures or our fingers to solve.

questions.prompt: Lily has 3 balloons, and her mom gives her 2 more. How many balloons does Lily have?
questions.explanation: We add 3 + 2 = 5

questions.prompt: Tom had 8 cookies, and he ate 4. How many cookies are left?
questions.explanation: We subtract 8 - 4 = 6

questions.prompt: Sarah has 2 toy cars and her friend gives her some more. If she now has 5 cars, how many did her friend give her?

mt_EqXlZfB4jp.title: Shape Detectives!
explanation: Let's become shape detectives! Shapes are all around us, like the blocks you build with or the wheels on your toy car. We see shapes in our homes – a square window, a round table, and maybe even a triangle in a building block set. Learning about shapes helps us understand what things look like!

Shapes have names: circle, square, triangle, rectangle, and many more! Each shape has special sides and corners. When we draw a square, we make sure all four sides are the same length and all four corners are right angles – that’s what makes it a square.  It's like building with LEGOs; you need to follow the rules of each shape.

Today, we’re going to look for shapes in our world! Let’s find a rectangle - maybe a book or a picture frame? Or perhaps a circle – like a ball or a plate. Recognizing these shapes is fun and helps us describe what we see around us every day.
keyIdeas: Shapes have sides and corners.
We can name different shapes.
Shapes are everywhere!

questions.prompt: What shape has four equal sides?
questions.explanation: A square is the only shape with four equal sides.

questions.prompt: When you put sounds together to read a word, what do we call that?
questions.explanation: Blending sounds together helps us read words faster.

questions.prompt: Can you point out a shape in the room?
questions.explanation: Great job!  Identifying shapes is an important skill.

mt_BhYJZUsErp.title: Sound Detectives!
explanation: Let’s become sound detectives! When we talk, our words are made up of tiny sounds called phonemes – like the ‘c’ in 'cat,' or the ‘a’ in 'apple.’ These sounds aren't always big and loud; they can be super small. We can hear these little sounds if we listen carefully, just like detectives searching for clues!

Today, we are going to learn how to break words apart into their sounds. Imagine you have a LEGO tower – you take it apart brick by brick! We’ll do the same with words; we'll listen and say each sound slowly. For example, let's say the word is 'dog'. You can hear ‘d’, ‘o’, and ‘g’ - that’s three sounds!

Now, let’s write those sounds down! We can use letters to represent each sound. If we hear ‘d’, we write ‘d’. Then ‘o’, we write ‘o’, and finally ‘g’, so we write ‘g’. When we put them together, we spell 'dog'! This is how we start learning to read and write.
keyIdeas: Words are made of sounds.
We can break words into individual sounds.
Letters represent sounds.

questions.prompt: What are the tiny sounds that make up words called?
questions.explanation: Phonemes are the smallest units of sound in a word.

questions.prompt: Let’s say you hear the word ‘sun’. How many sounds do you hear?
questions.explanation: The word 'sun' is broken down into the sounds /s/, /u/, and /n/.

questions.prompt: If you heard the word ‘hat’ what letters would you write?
questions.explanation: The word 'hat' is spelled with the letters h, a and t.

mt_TcG90kS8nu.title: Time Adventures!
explanation: Let’s talk about time! Time is how long things take. Sometimes things happen quickly, like when you race your toy car across the playroom floor – it zooms by so fast! We can use words to describe how quick or slow something is, like ‘quicker’ or ‘slower’.

Imagine you're building with blocks with a friend. One person might build their tower quicker than the other, right? That means they took less time to finish it! We can say that person was 'faster' at building, and the other one was 'slower'. It’s like comparing how long it takes to eat your favorite snack – some snacks are enjoyed quickly!

Now let’s talk about ‘earlier’ and ‘later’. If you wake up earlier than your brother, that means you woke up before him. And if you go to bed later, you stay up longer! These words help us understand when things happen in order, like telling a story.
keyIdeas: Quick & Slow
Earlier & Later
Comparing Times

questions.prompt: My brother finished his drawing quicker than me. Which word describes how he did it?
questions.explanation: ‘Quicker’ means something took less time.

questions.prompt: I ate my breakfast earlier than my dad. What does ‘earlier’ mean in this sentence?
questions.explanation: ‘Earlier’ means before a certain time.

questions.prompt: If you finish your homework before your friend, who finished first?
questions.explanation: You finished before your friend, so you finished first.

mt_8-POYyg7GJ.title: What Happens Next?
explanation: Let’s talk about stories and how we can guess what will happen! When you read a book, sometimes it's like watching a movie – you want to know what happens next, right? Before you turn the page, let’s try to make a prediction. We can think about what has already happened in the story and use that to guess.

Imagine you're reading about a toy car zooming down a ramp (like your favorite race car!). The story says the car is going fast and heading towards a big pile of blocks. You might predict that the car will crash into the blocks! That’s because we know cars go fast, and crashing into things is often what happens when they do.

But as you read on, maybe the car doesn't crash! Instead, it rolls right over the blocks and keeps going. This shows us that our first prediction wasn't correct – that’s okay! We can change our minds if we learn new information in the story. Predicting is about using what we know to make a good guess.
keyIdeas: Predictions are guesses about what will happen.
We use clues from the story to make predictions.
Sometimes our predictions change as we read more.

questions.prompt: The car is zooming down a ramp. What do you think will happen next?
choices: A) The car will crash into the blocks.
B) The car will stop and rest.
C) The car will drive around a corner.
questions.explanation: Based on what we read, the car is going fast and heading towards something.  That suggests it might crash!

questions.prompt: The title of the story is 'Race Day'. What does this title tell you about the story?
choices: A) It means there will be a race.
B) It means the story is about animals.
C) It means the story is very long.
questions.explanation: Titles often give us clues about what the story is *about*. 'Race Day' clearly suggests something to do with racing!

questions.prompt: You read that a character was sad. Later, you read they found a friend. What prediction might you make now?
questions.explanation: Think about how things change in the story and what new information you have learned.

mt_pAcaehday5.title: Counting with Friends!
explanation: Let's learn about numbers! Numbers are how we count things, like toys or snacks. We can use our hands to show how many there are – that’s called counting! It’s fun to see how many blocks you have or how many cookies your mom baked.

There are lots of ways to show a number. You could use building blocks like LEGOs, or even small toys like cars. If you have three toy cars, you can put them in a row and count them out loud: ‘One, two, three!’ That shows the number three.

We can also draw pictures to show numbers! We can make dots, or use tally marks – lines we make to keep track. Or, if you’re learning about bigger numbers, we can use a number line, which is like a road with numbers on it, and point to the number we want.
keyIdeas: Numbers represent quantities.
We can show numbers with objects.
There are different ways to represent numbers.

questions.prompt: I have 4 crayons. How many crayons do I have?
questions.explanation: Great job! Four is the correct answer.

questions.prompt: Let's draw 2 dots!  How many dots did you draw?
questions.explanation: Excellent! You correctly drew two dots.

questions.prompt: If we have a number line from 1 to 10, where would we find the number 7?
questions.explanation: You got it! The number seven is in the middle of the number line.

mt_sYpKWbq5ra.title: One More!
explanation: Let's talk about counting! When we count, we start with numbers like one, two, three... and so on. Each number tells us how many things we have – like if you have three blocks, that’s three! We keep going until we reach a bigger number, but the important thing is that each number shows us how much there is.

Imagine you're building with LEGOs and you have five colorful bricks. If someone gives you *one more* brick, now you have six! That means six is just one more than five – it’s like adding a little extra to our count. It’s really fun to see how many we can build when we add one at a time.

Let's try another example: You might have eight crayons in your box. If you take away one crayon, then someone gives you back one more, you now have eight again! Knowing that 8 is one more than 7 helps us understand how numbers grow as we keep adding.
keyIdeas: Adding one makes the next number bigger.
Each number shows a quantity (how many things).
Knowing 'one more' helps us count higher.

questions.prompt: You have 4 apples. Someone gives you one more apple. How many apples do you have now?
questions.explanation: That's right! Four plus one equals five.

questions.prompt: Which number is one less than nine?
questions.explanation: Nine minus one equals eight. Good job!

questions.prompt: If you have 2 cookies and your friend gives you one more, how many do you have?

mt_of2GggtxFl.title: Spaces Between Words
explanation: Separate words with spaces in writing; understand that spaces mark word boundaries in print
You know this idea when you can: Leave clear finger-spaces between words when writing; Count the number of words in a written sentence by identifying spaces.

Count the number of words in a written sentence by identifying spaces

mt_oAK9GXSfqV.title: Let's Write Numbers!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn how to write all the numbers from zero to nine! It’s like learning a secret code – each number has its own special way of being written. We want to make sure our numbers look neat and easy for everyone to read, just like when you draw a picture for your friends.

When we write a number, it's important to start at the right place. For example, if you’re writing the number 5, you need to begin at the top of the circle. Also, each number should be about the same size as the letters we use – like ‘A’ or ‘B’. This makes our numbers look balanced and pretty!

Writing numbers legibly means that people can easily understand them when they see them. Think about your favorite building blocks; you want to build a tower that's easy for everyone to see, right?  So, let’s practice writing each number carefully so others can read it too!
keyIdeas: Each digit has a special way of being written.
Start numbers at the correct point.
Write numbers clearly for easy reading.

questions.prompt: Which part do you start writing the number 3 at?
choices: A. The top of the circle
B. The bottom of the circle
C. The side of the circle
questions.explanation: You always begin writing the number 3 at the very top of its circle.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct size for a number like 5?
choices: B. About the same size as ‘A’
questions.explanation: Numbers should be roughly the same height as letters to look balanced.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to write numbers clearly?
choices: B. So others can easily read them
C. So you don't make a mess
questions.explanation: Clear writing helps people understand the number quickly.

mt_fR0UtsSREU.title: Numbers to Twenty!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn all about numbers! Numbers are how we count things, and they help us understand how many toys we have or how many snacks we can eat. We'll be learning to read them – saying the number out loud – and writing them down too. It’s like a secret code for counting!

Let’s look at some numbers! Imagine you have twenty building blocks, just like the ones you might use at school or when playing in your backyard. We can write that number as ‘20’, and we say it as ‘twenty’. Each number has its own special symbol, and we need to learn what each one looks like and how to say it correctly.

When we write numbers, the little digits – like 1, 2, and 3 – have different places. The number 17 means you have seven ones and one ten. It’s important to understand that each digit has a value, helping us count bigger and bigger amounts! This is how we know what number we are really talking about.
keyIdeas: Numbers help us count.
We can read numbers out loud.
Each digit in a number has a special place.

questions.prompt: What number do we say as ‘three’?
questions.explanation: Three is the number that makes the sound ‘three’.

questions.prompt: If you have fifteen crayons, how would you write that number?
questions.explanation: Fifteen is written as ‘15’ because it has one ten and five ones.

questions.prompt: Can you draw 8 objects (e.g., circles, squares) and then tell me the number that matches?
questions.explanation: This question requires a physical activity to assess understanding.

mt_M5PPDJStGm.title: Counting Adventures!
explanation: Let's learn how to count! Counting is like a special game where we say numbers in order, one after another. We start with the number zero and keep going up – one, two, three, four… it’s so much fun to see all the numbers come out!

Imagine you have a box of LEGO bricks. You want to build something really big! To know how many bricks you need, you'll count them carefully, saying each number as you put them in the box – one brick, two bricks, three bricks… and so on until you reach ten or even more!

Now let’s try counting backwards too. Let’s start at twenty and count down to zero: twenty, nineteen, eighteen... it's like a magical journey back! We can also start from any number we want – like 23 - and keep going until we reach 100!
keyIdeas: Counting is saying numbers in order.
We can count forwards or backwards.
We can start counting at any number.

questions.prompt: What comes after 5?
questions.explanation: Let’s see if you remember your counting! The number after five is six.

questions.prompt: If you count backwards from 20, what number comes before 15?
questions.explanation: Counting backwards means going down the numbers.  Fourteen is right before fifteen.

questions.prompt: You start counting at 37, can you count to 100?
questions.explanation: Can you say all the numbers from 37 up to 100?

mt_xmmgAzxe5j.title: Teen Numbers: Ten and More!
explanation: Let’s talk about teen numbers! These are special numbers that come after nine, like ten, eleven, twelve, thirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, and nineteen. They're called ‘teen’ numbers because they’re in between the regular numbers we use every day, and they’re a little tricky to understand at first. Think about your LEGOs – you can build big towers with them, but sometimes you need extra blocks to make something even bigger!

Teen numbers are made up of ten and some more! For example, if you have 14 building blocks, you can see it as one whole tower of ten blocks (that’s the ‘ten’) and then four extra blocks leftover (those are the ‘ones’). We write this as 10 + 4 = 14.  It's like having a big plate of cookies – you have a whole plate, but you also have some extra cookies to eat!

Let’s look at another teen number: 17. You can see it as ten blocks plus seven blocks. We write this as 10 + 7 = 17!  Or, 13 is ‘one ten and three ones’. It's important to remember that the 'ten' is always a whole group of ten – just like your fingers when you count!
keyIdeas: Teen numbers are between 10 and 20.
They’re made up of ‘ten’ and ‘ones’.
You can use objects to show how teen numbers are put together.

questions.prompt: I have 12 blocks. How many tens do I have?
questions.explanation: You have two full groups of ten (two tens).

questions.prompt: Which number is the same as 10 + 8?
questions.explanation: If you add 10 and 8 together, you get 18.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have 15 crayons. How many ones are there?
questions.explanation: There are five ones – that’s the number left over after making one full group of ten.

mt_nvdpxAJTBG.title: Counting in 2s
explanation: Count in multiples of 2, 5, and 10 (skip counting)

explanation: Read, write, and interpret the symbols +, −, and = in number sentences

Write a number sentence to match a concrete addition situation

questions.explanation: Read 3 + 2 = 5 aloud as 'three plus two equals five'

questions.explanation: Write a number sentence to match a concrete addition situation

questions.explanation: Interpret the = sign as 'is the same as' rather than just 'the answer is'

mt_-hTTat0mBR.title: What Is a Half?
explanation: Let's learn about something special called 'a half'! A half means that we’ve broken something into two equal pieces. Imagine you have a yummy chocolate bar – if you break it right down the middle, so both sides are exactly the same size, then each piece is one half of the whole chocolate bar! It’s like sharing your favorite toy with a friend and making sure you both get an equal amount.

Now, let's try something fun. Let's take a square piece of paper and fold it in half. Make sure you crease it really well so that one side lines up perfectly with the other.  You’ve now made two halves! Each part is exactly the same size as the other; we call each of these parts 'a half'. This shows us how something can be split into two equal pieces.

Let's say you have 8 building blocks and want to share them with your friend. You would put all eight blocks in a line and then divide them into two groups, making sure each group has four blocks.  Each of those groups is one half of the whole set of eight blocks! Seeing if shapes are split into equal parts helps us know when something is divided into halves.
keyIdeas: A 'half' means two equal parts.
We can make halves by folding things in half.
Halves are used to share equally.

questions.prompt: If you cut a cookie in half, how many pieces do you have?
questions.explanation: Cutting something in half always makes two equal parts.

questions.prompt: You have 6 crayons. You want to share them equally with your friend. How many crayons does each person get?
questions.explanation: Sharing equally means dividing into two equal groups.

questions.prompt: Which shape is divided into halves? (Imagine a simple square or circle)
questions.explanation: Halves are always about splitting something into two equal parts, like a square.

mt_g3W0mdADVu.title: Sharing Our Treats: Learning About Quarters!
explanation: Let's talk about sharing! Imagine you have a yummy chocolate bar – it’s divided into squares, right? When we share something equally with our friends, we want to make sure everyone gets the same amount. A quarter is one of those equal parts when we split something up into four pieces that are exactly the same size.

We can use shapes too! Let's take a square piece of paper and fold it in half, then fold it in half again. Now you have four smaller squares – each one is called a quarter! It’s like cutting a pizza into four slices; each slice is a quarter of the whole pizza.  Seeing these equal parts helps us understand what a quarter really means.

Now let's try with something else, like 12 cookies! If we want to share them equally between four people, we need to give each person three cookies – that’s one-quarter (or a quarter) of the whole box.  So, a quarter is just another way of saying ‘one out of four’ when things are split fairly.
keyIdeas: A quarter is one of four equal parts.
We can find quarters in shapes and groups.
Sharing equally means dividing into quarters.

questions.prompt: I have 8 toys. If I want to share them equally with 2 friends, how many toys does each person get?
questions.explanation: If you divide 8 toys between 2 people, each person gets 4 toys (8 divided by 2 = 4).

questions.prompt: Which shape has been cut into four equal parts?
questions.explanation: A square can be easily folded into four equal parts, making each part a quarter.

questions.prompt: What does ‘a quarter’ mean?
choices: B) One out of four pieces
questions.explanation: A quarter means one portion when something is divided into four equal parts.

mt_S4G6GLKr1-.title: Don’t Give Up! Learning is an Adventure!
explanation: Sometimes, learning something new can feel really tricky, like building a tall tower with blocks. Imagine you're trying to build your favorite LEGO castle – it might wobble and fall down lots of times before it stands strong! That’s okay because even grown-ups make mistakes when they are learning new things too.

When you find something hard, the most important thing is not to give up right away. If you keep trying, even if you make a mistake like dropping your blocks or coloring outside the lines on a picture, you're still learning!  It’s like when you learn to ride your bike – you might fall down a few times before you get it just right.

Instead of saying ‘I can’t do this,’ try something new. Maybe you build the tower with different blocks or draw a picture using crayons instead of pencils. Talking about how hard something is and that you're getting better helps too – like saying, 'This is tricky, but I'm learning!'
keyIdeas: Keep Trying!
Mistakes Help You Learn
Positive Thinking

questions.prompt: You are building with blocks and they keep falling down. What should you do?
choices: A) Ask a grown-up to build it for you
B) Keep trying different ways to build it
C) Give up and play something else
questions.explanation: It’s important to keep practicing even if you make mistakes!

questions.prompt: You are coloring, but your picture looks messy. What can you do?
choices: A) Say ‘I’m not good at coloring’
B) Try using a different crayon or color
C) Throw the paper away and start over with a new one”
questions.explanation: Trying a different approach is a great way to learn!

questions.prompt: Someone says, ‘This is hard!’ What could you say back?
choices: B) ‘This is hard, but I'm getting better!’
questions.explanation: Positive words help you keep trying and learning.

mt_KaF0SQvaiu.title: Measuring Shapes!
explanation: Let’s learn about measuring! Measuring means figuring out how long or tall something is. We can do this by using things around us to help us, like blocks or crayons. It's like being a detective and trying to find out exactly how big something is – it’s super fun!

Imagine you have a toy car and you want to know how long it is. You could use building blocks to measure! Lay the blocks end-to-end until they match the length of your car, then count how many blocks you used. That number tells you how long your car is – we call that its ‘length’!

Now, let's try using a ruler! A ruler has lines marked on it in centimetres, which are like little steps to help us measure things more precisely. We can use the ruler to see how tall our toy blocks are or how long our crayons are and write down what we find.
keyIdeas: Measuring is figuring out size.
We can use blocks and rulers to measure.
Measurements have units like centimetres.

questions.prompt: I am using building blocks to measure the length of my toy car. What am I doing?
choices: A: Counting how many blocks it takes.
B: Drawing a picture of the car.
questions.explanation: You are counting the number of blocks needed to match the car's length.

questions.prompt: A ruler shows us measurements in what?
questions.explanation: A ruler measures things in centimetres, which are little steps for measuring.

questions.prompt: You measured a crayon and found it was 5 centimetres long. How would you write that down?
questions.explanation: The answer should be ‘5 cm’ – the number followed by the unit (centimetres).

mt_bj1YCgNWUx.title: Time Travelers!
explanation: Let’s talk about time! Time is like a big, long story that keeps going and going. We use words to tell us when things happen – like ‘today,’ ‘yesterday,’ or ‘tomorrow.’ Imagine you're building with LEGOs; you put one brick on top of another, right? Well, time works the same way, adding more moments together!

There are lots of ways we talk about time. We have seven days in a week – Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday! Then there are twelve months in a year – January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November, and December. It's like learning the names of all your favorite toys!

A year is made up of all those months, and each month has about four weeks. So, we count the days to see how much time has passed! Knowing these words helps us plan our day, remember special events, and understand how long things take – like waiting for a birthday!
keyIdeas: We use words to talk about time.
There are seven days in a week.
A year is made up of twelve months.

questions.prompt: What day comes after Wednesday?
questions.explanation: Remember, the days of the week go in order: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

questions.prompt: How many months are there in a year?
questions.explanation: There are twelve months in a year – January through December!

questions.prompt: If today is Friday, what day was yesterday?
questions.explanation: Yesterday was one day before Friday, which is Thursday.

mt_zVLOm6U7bh.title: Amazing Animals!
explanation: Let’s talk about all the amazing animals around us! Animals are living things, and they come in lots of different shapes and sizes. We can group them into groups like fish, frogs, snakes, robins, and puppies – that's a lot of fun to learn about!

Imagine you have a toy box full of toys, and some are animals! Fish live in the water and swim around, like goldfish. Amphibians, like frogs, start their lives in the water but can also hop on land. Reptiles, such as snakes and lizards, often have scales and live on dry ground.

Birds fly through the sky with beautiful feathers, like robins or eagles. Mammals are warm-blooded animals that give birth to babies, just like puppies and kittens! Learning about these different groups helps us understand how all living things are special in their own way.
keyIdeas: Animals belong to groups.
Fish live in water, birds fly!
Mammals have babies.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals lives in the water?
questions.explanation: Goldfish are fish and live in water.

questions.prompt: What helps birds fly?
questions.explanation: Birds have feathers that help them lift off the ground and fly.

questions.prompt: Which animal group gives birth to babies?
questions.explanation: Mammals, like puppies and kittens, give birth to their young.

mt_PZ909yPrEC.title: Multiplication is Like Groups!
explanation: Let’s talk about how we can add numbers in a faster way! Sometimes, instead of adding the same number over and over again, we can think about it as having groups. Imagine you have three toy cars – that's like saying ‘3 groups of 1 car’. We can also say it’s the same as just adding one car to itself three times: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3.

Let’s try an example with cookies! If we want to make 2 plates and put 4 cookies on each plate, we have two groups of four. We can count all the cookies by adding them up: 4 + 4 = 8 cookies! This is what multiplication helps us do – it's a shortcut for repeated addition.

You might see something called an ‘array’ when you look at building blocks or even a checkerboard! An array shows things in rows and columns, like having 3 rows of 2 blocks each. That means we have 3 groups of 2 blocks – just like our cookies example; it's the same as adding 2 + 2 + 2.
keyIdeas: Multiplication is a shortcut for addition.
Groups of equal size can be represented by multiplication.
Arrays show us how things are grouped in rows and columns.

questions.prompt: If you have 2 groups of 3 apples, how many apples do you have?
questions.explanation: We can solve this by adding 3 + 3 = 6.

questions.prompt: Which picture shows 4 groups of 2 stars?
choices: A: A star with 2 dots inside.
B: Four stars, each with 2 dots inside.
C: Two stars, each with 4 dots inside.
questions.explanation: This represents 4 groups of 2, so we need to picture four stars and count the dots in each.

questions.prompt: Sarah has 5 friends. Each friend gets 1 sticker. How many stickers does Sarah give out?
questions.explanation: This is a repeated addition problem: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5. Or, we can say 5 groups of 1 sticker.

mt_yGv8doDAmp.title: Amazing 3-D Shapes!
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes! We already know about flat shapes like squares and circles, called 2-D shapes. These shapes are like drawings on a piece of paper – you can draw them easily! But today we're going to learn about 3-D shapes, which have height, width, and depth; they’re the kind of shapes you can build with blocks or maybe even find in your toy box!

A 3-D shape has sides and corners. When we count the sides, we call them edges. And when we count the corners where the edges meet, we call those vertices. Think about a block – it has six square faces, each with four corners or vertices.  We can use our fingers to feel how many sides and corners make up a shape.

Let's look at a cube! A cube is a special 3-D shape that has all six of its faces as squares. That means each face is like a smaller square, and every corner where the squares meet is a sharp point.  It’s like building with LEGOs – you can make lots of cubes by connecting them together!
keyIdeas: 3-D shapes have height, width, and depth.
Shapes have edges (sides) and vertices (corners).
A cube has six square faces.

questions.prompt: I am a shape with 3 sides and 3 corners. What am I?
questions.explanation: Triangles always have three sides and three corners.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has more corners than a square?
questions.explanation: A cube has 8 corners, while a square only has 4.

questions.prompt: What do we call the sides of a 3-D shape?
questions.explanation: The edges are the lines that make up the outside of the shape.

explanation: Identify and name common wild and garden plants, including deciduous and evergreen trees
