Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Combine two simple sentences using 'and' (e.g. 'I like cats and dogs')
Join two related clauses with 'and' (e.g. 'We went to the park and we played')
Use 'and' in lists of items within a sentence

questions.prompt: True or false: Combine two simple sentences using 'and' (e.g. 'I like cats and dogs')
questions.explanation: Combine two simple sentences using 'and' (e.g. 'I like cats and dogs')

questions.prompt: True or false: Join two related clauses with 'and' (e.g. 'We went to the park and we played')
questions.explanation: Join two related clauses with 'and' (e.g. 'We went to the park and we played')

questions.prompt: True or false: Use 'and' in lists of items within a sentence
questions.explanation: Use 'and' in lists of items within a sentence

mt_7OJjLOl0fz.title: Amazing Animals of the Arctic!
explanation: Let’s explore some amazing animals that live in the very, very cold places called the Arctic! We already know about polar bears and penguins, but there are so many other wonderful creatures who call these icy lands home. These animals have special tricks to help them stay warm when it's freezing outside – like a thick coat or lots of fat!

Imagine you’re playing with your toy trucks at the playground, and it suddenly gets super cold! Some animals, like seals, have thick layers of blubber under their skin. Blubber is like a fluffy blanket that keeps them warm when they swim in icy waters. Walruses also have blubber, and Arctic foxes grow a really thick, white fur coat to blend in with the snow – this helps them hide from hungry animals!

Let’s meet some more! Snowy owls are big and fluffy and turn completely white in the winter so they can sneak up on their food. Then there's narwhals, which have a long, spiral tusk that sticks out of their heads! They live in the cold Arctic waters and use their tusks to find food.
keyIdeas: Many animals live in the Arctic.
Animals have special ways to stay warm.
Narwhals have a long tusk!

questions.prompt: Which animal has thick blubber to keep it warm?
questions.explanation: Seals have a lot of blubber, which is like a warm blanket under their skin!

questions.prompt: What color do Arctic foxes turn in the winter?
questions.explanation: Arctic foxes change to white fur so they can hide in the snow.

questions.prompt: What is a narwhal’s tusk used for?
questions.explanation: Narwhals use their long tusks to find yummy things to eat in the Arctic waters.

mt_K0mZxY2AM8.title: Why Do We Have Seasons?
explanation: Imagine your favorite toy car – maybe a red racecar! During the summer, we play outside for so long because it’s light out almost all day. The sun is high in the sky and shines brightly, giving us lots of time to run around and have fun. This happens because the Earth is tilted on its side as it travels around the Sun.

The Earth goes around the Sun like a racecar going in circles. Because the Earth is tilted, sometimes we’re leaning towards the sun, so we get more sunlight and warmer weather – that's summer! Other times, we’re leaning away from the sun, and we get less sunlight and cooler weather – that’s winter. It’s like having a big blanket of sunshine or not having one at all!

Think about when you go to the playground. In the summertime, there are lots of kids playing outside because it's warm and sunny! But in the winter, sometimes it's too cold and snowy for everyone to play. The amount of daylight – how long the sun is up – helps us know if it’s summer or winter, and that changes what we can do!
keyIdeas: The Earth tilts.
Daylight hours change throughout the year.
Tilt affects our weather.

questions.prompt: Which time of year has the most daylight?
questions.explanation: During summer, the Earth is tilted towards the sun, so we have more sunlight!

questions.prompt: When the Earth leans away from the sun, what happens to the weather?
questions.explanation: When the Earth is tilted away from the sun, it receives less sunlight and the weather becomes cooler.

questions.prompt: What causes the seasons to change?
questions.explanation: The Earth’s tilt is what causes the seasons to change because it affects how much sunlight different parts of the world receive.

mt_XirhnAB6Ye.title: Super Scientists Ask Questions!
explanation: Have you ever wondered about something? Like why does your favorite toy car stop rolling when you push it far enough? Scientists are people who love to ask questions about the world around them, and they try to find answers! Asking ‘how’ or ‘why’ questions is the first step in being a super scientist – it helps us understand how things work.

Sometimes, just looking closely at something can help us answer our questions. For example, if you're curious about why leaves change color in the fall, you could carefully observe them! You might notice that some leaves are green, and as they get older, they start to turn yellow, orange, or red before falling off the tree. Observing is a great way to gather information.

But there are other ways too! Scientists can test things – like seeing if water helps plants grow faster. Or, they could ask someone who knows a lot about trees, like a park ranger, for help. Reading books and looking up information online can also give us answers to our questions.
keyIdeas: Scientists ask ‘how’ and ‘why’ questions.
Observation is one way to find answers.
Asking experts or reading about things helps too!

questions.prompt: Why do you think leaves change color?
choices: A. Because they are getting older.
B. Because the sun is shining brighter.
C. Because the weather gets colder.
questions.explanation: Leaves have a green pigment called chlorophyll, which helps them make food from sunlight. As it gets colder, the trees stop making this pigment, and we can see the other colors that were already there!

questions.prompt: If you want to know why your toy car stops rolling, what could you do?
choices: B. Observe how far it rolls before stopping.
C. Ask a friend to push it.
questions.explanation: Observing the car's movement will give you clues about why it’s stopping – maybe it’s running out of energy or hitting something.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a way scientists can find answers?
choices: B. Asking someone who knows a lot.
questions.explanation: Scientists need to pay attention and try to figure things out, not just ignore their questions!

mt_CK2orHetl3.title: Amazing Penguins!
explanation: Let’s talk about penguins! They live in a really cold place called Antarctica, which is at the very bottom of the world. Penguins are amazing animals because they can't fly like birds we see in our backyards, but they swim better than almost anything else. Imagine your favorite toy boat – penguins are like that, but they’re real and super good at moving through water!

Penguins are built for swimming! They have strong flippers to help them paddle and their feathers are very dense, which keeps them warm in the icy waters. Emperor penguins are the biggest kind of penguin, and when it gets really cold, they huddle together with lots and lots of other penguins to share their warmth. It’s like a giant, fluffy hug!

But it's even more special because penguin parents work as a team! The daddy or mommy penguin takes turns sitting on the egg to keep it warm with their feet in the freezing temperatures. They do this all day and all night until the baby penguin hatches – isn’t that wonderful?
keyIdeas: Penguins can't fly.
Emperor penguins huddle for warmth.
Parent penguins keep eggs warm.

questions.prompt: Which of these is true about penguins?
choices: A. Penguins can fly high in the sky.
C. Penguins live in hot deserts.
questions.explanation: Penguins spend their whole lives swimming and diving in cold water!

questions.prompt: Why do Emperor penguins huddle together?
choices: A. To play games with each other.
B. To keep warm when it’s very cold.
questions.explanation: Huddling helps them share their body heat and stay warm in the Antarctic winter.

questions.prompt: What do penguin parents do to help a baby penguin?
choices: B. They keep its egg warm on their feet.
C. They build a nest for it.
questions.explanation: The parent penguins carefully sit on the egg to keep it warm until it’s ready to hatch.

mt_O9dH94NFae.title: Polar Bears
explanation: Know key facts about polar bears — they live only in the Arctic (not the Antarctic), they have thick white fur and a layer of fat (blubber) to keep warm, they are excellent swimmers, and they hunt seals by waiting at holes in the sea ice
You know this idea when you can: State that polar bears live in the Arctic, not the Antarctic; Describe at least two ways polar bears stay warm: thick white fur and a layer of blubber (fat).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: State that polar bears live in the Arctic, not the Antarctic
Describe at least two ways polar bears stay warm: thick white fur and a layer of blubber (fat)
Explain how polar bears hunt seals by waiting at breathing holes in the sea ice

questions.prompt: True or false: State that polar bears live in the Arctic, not the Antarctic
questions.explanation: State that polar bears live in the Arctic, not the Antarctic

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe at least two ways polar bears stay warm: thick white fur and a layer of blubber (fat)
questions.explanation: Describe at least two ways polar bears stay warm: thick white fur and a layer of blubber (fat)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain how polar bears hunt seals by waiting at breathing holes in the sea ice
questions.explanation: Explain how polar bears hunt seals by waiting at breathing holes in the sea ice

mt_iGSfQg3g5c.title: Sunlight's Warm Hug
explanation: Let’s talk about the sun! The sun is a giant ball of light and heat, and it sends warmth all around our planet. When sunlight shines on things like your toys or the sidewalk outside, it gives them a warm hug. This warmth comes from something called energy – it's what makes things feel hot!

Think about playing in the park on a sunny day. The ground feels warmer under your feet than it does when you’re sitting in the shade under a tree. That’s because sunlight is hitting the ground directly, and that heat is making it feel warmer to us.  It’s like the sun is giving the earth a big, cozy hug!

Sunlight doesn't just warm things; it actually *gives* them warmth! This warmth is called heat energy, and it travels from the sun to everything it touches. When you stand in the sunlight, you can feel that heat energy making your skin feel warmer too.
keyIdeas: Sunlight provides warmth.
Warmth comes from energy.
Sunlight makes things feel hotter.

questions.prompt: Which feels warmer?
choices: A. A rock in the sun
B. A rock in the shade
questions.explanation: The rock in the sun is getting warmth from sunlight.

questions.prompt: What does sunlight give to Earth's surface?
questions.explanation: Sunlight provides heat energy, which makes things warm.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re sitting in the sun. What do you feel?
questions.explanation: You feel warmth from the sunlight – that's heat energy!

mt_szw1Ln490b.title: Exploring Ancient Egypt!
explanation: Let's travel back in time to ancient Egypt! This amazing civilization was very old, like a really, really long-ago story. The people who lived there built incredible things like giant pyramids and temples – they were the most powerful rulers called pharaohs. These pharaohs ruled over everyone and everything, making sure their kingdom ran smoothly.

The Egyptians believed in many gods and goddesses, and they carefully prepared for life after death. They wrapped their dead bodies in cloth to become mummies, placing them inside a beautiful stone box called a sarcophagus. Special jars, called canopic jars, held the mummy's organs while natron – a type of salt – was used to dry out the body completely.

Ancient Egyptians wrote using pictures called hieroglyphs on special paper made from plants called papyrus. Archaeologists study these ancient writings and find amazing objects like pottery or tools - we call them artefacts! They carefully uncover these items, learning more about how people lived thousands of years ago.
keyIdeas: Pharaohs were the rulers of Ancient Egypt.
Pyramids were built as tombs for pharaohs and important people.
Hieroglyphs are pictures used to write messages in ancient Egypt.

questions.prompt: What was a Pharaoh?
choices: B. The ruler of Ancient Egypt
questions.explanation: Pharaohs were the kings and queens who led ancient Egypt.

questions.prompt: Why did the Egyptians mummify their dead?
choices: A. To make them look scary
B. To keep their bodies preserved for the afterlife
C. Because they liked being wrapped in cloth
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed that preserving a body was important for life after death.

questions.prompt: What are hieroglyphs?
choices: B. Pictures used to write messages
questions.explanation: Hieroglyphs were the ancient Egyptian way of writing using pictures.

mt_x5ZrQMAZ5v.title: Rainforest Plants!
explanation: Imagine a really, really busy place – that’s kind of like the rainforest! Rainforests are super special because they have *tons* of plants growing all around. These aren't just any plants; some of them are gigantic and amazing, like kapok trees which can grow taller than your house! They also have lots of vines called lianas that wrap around other plants – think about climbing a jungle gym!

Rainforest plants need sunlight to grow, just like you need sunshine to play. Because there are so many plants trying to reach the sun, they grow very tall and strong. It’s like a race to see who can get the most light! This is why you'll find huge trees towering above everything else in the rainforest.

Some rainforest plants have really cool tricks too! Orchids are flowers that love to grow on branches of trees – it’s like they’re having a party right up there. And lianas, those climbing vines, can wrap themselves all around tree trunks as they grow upwards. Isn't nature amazing?
keyIdeas: Rainforests have many different kinds of plants.
Plants grow tall to get sunlight.
Some rainforest plants have unusual ways of growing.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a type of rainforest plant?
questions.explanation: Kapok trees are very tall and grow in the rainforest.

questions.prompt: Why do some rainforest plants grow so tall?
questions.explanation: Plants need sunlight to grow, and they compete for it in the rainforest.

questions.prompt: Where do you find orchids growing?
questions.explanation: Orchids are flowers that grow on branches of trees.

mt_EHuU1ZeUFA.title: Our Amazing Solar System!
explanation: Let’s explore our solar system! It's like a giant neighborhood in space, and at the very center is the Sun – it’s a huge star that gives us light and warmth. Around the Sun travel eight amazing planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. These planets are all different sizes and have their own special features, but they all go around the sun!

Think about your favorite toy car – it circles a track, right? Planets do something similar called ‘orbiting’. Orbit means that each planet travels in a path around the Sun because of its gravity. The closer a planet is to the Sun, the faster it orbits! Our moon also orbits around Earth.

Now, let’s learn what makes each thing special. A planet is big and round and goes around a star like Earth does around the sun. A star, like our Sun, gives off light and heat. And an asteroid is a small rocky object that travels through space – they're not planets!
keyIdeas: There are eight planets in our solar system.
Planets orbit the Sun due to gravity.
A planet is different from a star and an asteroid.

questions.prompt: What is the first planet from the sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is closest to the Sun.

questions.prompt: Which word describes how planets move around the sun?
questions.explanation: Orbit means a planet travels in a circular path around the sun.

questions.prompt: Is a star like our Sun a planet or a star?
questions.explanation: A star is a giant ball of hot gas that gives off light and heat.

mt_vJO5Bxk4z-.title: Shape Detectives!
explanation: Let's become shape detectives! We can use our eyes to look really, really closely at things around us. Sometimes, we need a little help to see the details – like a magnifying glass! A magnifying glass makes small things look bigger so we can notice all their amazing shapes and sizes.

Imagine you have a toy car or building block. You can use your hand lens to examine it carefully. Notice how many straight lines make up its shape, or if any parts are curved.  We can describe what we see using words like ‘square,’ ‘circle,’ ‘triangle,’ or even ‘rectangle’ – these are all shapes!

Now, let's look at something outside! If you find a leaf on the playground, use your magnifying glass to see its shape. Does it have pointy edges? Is it smooth or bumpy?  By carefully observing and describing what you see, you become a super-smart shape detective!
keyIdeas: Magnifying glasses help us see details.
We can describe shapes with words like ‘square’ and ‘circle’.
Comparing objects helps us understand their differences.

questions.prompt: I use a magnifying glass to make things look bigger. What am I doing?
questions.explanation: Using a magnifying glass is about observing details carefully.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has four equal sides and four right angles?
questions.explanation: A square is defined by having four equal sides and four right angles.

questions.prompt: You are looking at a rock with your magnifying glass. You notice it's bumpy and rough. What word could you use to describe its texture?

mt_E7avIa-tcE.title: Pharaohs and Tutankhamun
explanation: Long, long ago, in a place called Ancient Egypt, people lived with amazing rulers! These rulers were called pharaohs – think of them like kings or queens, but even more important because they controlled everything. Pharaohs lived in big, fancy palaces and made sure everyone followed the rules, just like your parents do at home.

One very famous pharaoh was Tutankhamun. He became a pharaoh when he was only a boy! Scientists found his amazing tomb filled with gold and treasures – it’s like finding a giant, sparkly toy chest buried under the sand.  He ruled Egypt for many years before he died, but people still remember him today because of his beautiful golden mask.

Pharaohs were super powerful! They made all sorts of important decisions about how everyone in Egypt should live and what they should do. They built amazing pyramids – giant stone buildings that are still standing today – and led armies to protect their land.  It’s like a really, really big job!
keyIdeas: Pharaohs were rulers of Ancient Egypt.
Tutankhamun was a famous pharaoh who ruled as a child.
Pharaohs made important decisions for everyone.

questions.prompt: What was a pharaoh?
choices: B. The ruler of Ancient Egypt
questions.explanation: Pharaohs were the kings and queens who controlled everything in ancient Egypt.

questions.prompt: Which pharaoh had his tomb discovered with lots of gold?
questions.explanation: Tutankhamun’s tomb was found full of golden treasures and a famous mask.

questions.prompt: Pharaohs made important decisions for everyone. Give an example of something they might have decided to do.
questions.explanation: They could have decided where people should live, what jobs they should have, or how to protect their land.

mt_H7DquwQi_F.title: Let's Make Big Letters!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn how to make big, beautiful letters! Just like when you build with blocks, making letters takes practice and knowing the right way to put them together. Each letter has a special shape that helps us read stories and write our names – it's like having secret instructions for drawing!

Let’s look at some examples. See how a ‘B’ looks like a little mountain with a valley? Or how an ‘O’ is just a perfect circle, like the yummy donut you might eat for snack time! We need to make sure our letters are big and clear so everyone can see them easily.

When we write, it's important that capital letters are taller than lowercase letters. Think about when you build with LEGOs – you want your towers to be tall!  So, let’s practice making all the big letters in the alphabet, paying attention to their shapes and sizes.
keyIdeas: Letters have specific shapes.
Capital letters are bigger than lowercase letters.
Practice makes our writing neat!

questions.prompt: Which letter looks like a triangle?
questions.explanation: Look closely at the shape of each letter – which one is shaped like a triangle?

questions.prompt: Capital letters are usually ____ than lowercase letters.
questions.explanation: Remember, capital letters need to stand out! They're designed to be taller.

questions.prompt: Can you draw a big ‘A’ and a small ‘a’?
questions.explanation: Try drawing the letter A – make it tall and pointy. Then, draw the letter a – make it short and round.  See how they are different sizes?

mt_-vsLvsxp0L.title: Animal Babies!
explanation: Let’s talk about how animals grow up! Just like you need a mommy and daddy to help you, animals also need grown-up animals to take care of them. Some animals make babies in very special ways, and it's really cool to learn about all the different methods they use. We can look at some examples like birds building nests with eggs or puppies being born from their mothers – these are just a few of the amazing ways baby animals come into the world.

Some animals, like chickens and turtles, lay tiny eggs! The mommy bird carefully puts her eggs in a cozy nest, and after a little while, they hatch – that means a baby chick pops out! Other animals, such as dogs and cats, have babies that are born alive. This is called giving birth; the mommy animal has the babies inside of her tummy until they're ready to come out into the world.

It’s amazing how different baby animals look! Take a frog for example – it starts as a tiny swimming creature called a tadpole with a tail. Then, slowly, it changes and grows legs and eventually becomes a hopping frog! Or think about caterpillars; they're chubby little worms that eat leaves, and then they build a special case around themselves to become beautiful butterflies.
keyIdeas: Animals have different ways of having babies.
Some animals lay eggs, others give birth to live young.
Baby animals can look like their parents or be very different!

questions.prompt: Which animal lays eggs?
questions.explanation: Chickens are birds and they lay eggs to make baby chickens.

questions.prompt: How do most mammals have babies?
choices: C. They hatch from a shell
questions.explanation: Most mammals, like dogs and cats, give birth to live young.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when an egg hatches?
choices: A. The baby animal is born
B. A tiny bird comes out of the egg
questions.explanation: When an egg hatches, a baby animal emerges from inside.

mt_PiWZA8Z0ZJ.title: The Sun, Moon, and Stars!
explanation: Let’s look up at the sky! You see lots of amazing things there – like the Sun, the Moon, and stars. These are all special objects that we can find in space. They’re different from each other, but they're all part of our beautiful night and day skies.

The Sun is a giant ball of fire, and it’s super important for us! It appears during the daytime, shining bright light and giving us warmth – like when you feel cozy in the sunshine.  Think about your favorite toy car; it needs sunlight to shine just like we need the sun's light!

At night, when it gets dark, we can see stars twinkling in the sky. They’re very far away and look like tiny points of light. Sometimes, you might even see the Moon during the day too – it changes shape as it goes around our planet!
keyIdeas: The Sun gives us light and heat.
Stars twinkle at night.
The Moon appears in both day and night.

questions.prompt: What object in the sky gives us warmth during the day?
questions.explanation: The sun is a giant ball of fire that provides light and heat.

questions.prompt: When do we usually see stars?
questions.explanation: Stars appear in the sky when it's dark outside.

questions.prompt: Which of these things can you sometimes see during the day?
questions.explanation: The moon orbits around Earth and appears in our sky at different times.

mt_0XxyaQLRhn.title: Time Explorers!
explanation: Let’s learn about telling time! A clock helps us know what time it is, like when we have lunch or playtime. Clocks show us hours – that's how many times the sun comes up and goes down in a day. We can see the big hand, called the hour hand, pointing to the number showing how many hours it is.

The little hand tells us minutes! It moves around the clock face, going from 0 to 60. When the little hand points to the 3, we say ‘half past three’ because that's thirty minutes.  It’s like when you eat half a cookie – it’s not the whole thing, just part of it!

Let’s look at some clocks! One clock shows 3 o’clock, meaning the hour hand is pointing to the number 3. Another clock shows ‘half past seven,’ which means the little hand is pointing to the number 6 because there are six numbers between the 3 and the 9. Now you can draw your own clock faces!
keyIdeas: Clocks show hours.
‘Half past’ means 30 minutes.
The hour hand tells us the hour, and the minute hand tells us the minutes.

questions.prompt: What does ‘half past’ mean?
choices: A. It means it's time for a nap.
B. It means 30 minutes have passed.
C. It means the clock is broken.
questions.explanation: ‘Half past’ means that thirty minutes have gone by since the hour.

questions.prompt: If the hour hand on a clock points to the number 9, what time is it?
questions.explanation: The hour hand pointing to the 9 shows that it is 9 o’clock.

questions.prompt: Draw a clock face and show ‘half past four’. What will the hands look like?
questions.explanation: Encourage children to draw a blank clock face and position the hour and minute hands correctly.

mt_SsS7GptD_o.title: What Money Is
explanation: What money is and why we use it; that money is exchanged for goods and services; brief history from barter to coins to digital payments
You know this idea when you can: Explain that people use money to pay for things they need or want; Give an example of how buying worked before money existed (swapping or bartering).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Explain that people use money to pay for things they need or want
Give an example of how buying worked before money existed (swapping or bartering)
Name at least two forms money can take (coins, notes, card, phone payment)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that people use money to pay for things they need or want
questions.explanation: Explain that people use money to pay for things they need or want

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of how buying worked before money existed (swapping or bartering)
questions.explanation: Give an example of how buying worked before money existed (swapping or bartering)

questions.prompt: True or false: Name at least two forms money can take (coins, notes, card, phone payment)
questions.explanation: Name at least two forms money can take (coins, notes, card, phone payment)

mt_cPZwlUk8Nd.title: Coin Detectives!
explanation: Let’s become coin detectives! Coins are little pieces of money that we use to buy things. There are lots of different coins, each with its own special value. We can find them in our piggy banks or when we go shopping – they're everywhere!

Each coin has a number on it that tells us how much it’s worth. For example, a 1p coin is worth one penny, and a 2p coin is worth two pennies. The bigger the number, the more money you have! We use these coins to count up all our toys or snacks.

We also have special paper money called notes – like £5 and £10. These notes are worth more than any of the coins because they represent a larger amount of money.  It’s important to know that a £2 coin is bigger and therefore has a higher value than a 50p coin.
keyIdeas: Coins have different values.
The number on the coin tells you how much it's worth.
Notes are paper money with larger values.

questions.prompt: Which coin is worth 5 pennies?
questions.explanation: The 5p coin has a ‘5’ on it, which means it's worth five pennies.

questions.prompt: Which is worth more: a 20p coin or a 10p coin?
questions.explanation: The 20p coin has a bigger number, so it’s worth more.

questions.prompt: What does the number on a £10 note tell you?
questions.explanation: It tells you that the note is worth ten pounds.  You can use it to buy lots of things!

mt_XlyF294bPR.title: Our Amazing Solar System!
explanation: Have you ever looked up at the night sky and seen twinkling stars? Those stars are so far away, and they’re part of something even bigger called our solar system! Our solar system is like a giant family – it includes our planet Earth, but also lots of other planets that travel around the Sun. It's super exciting to think about all these worlds out there!

The Sun is at the very center of our solar system and it’s a huge star that gives us light and warmth. All the planets, like Mars or Jupiter, go around the Sun in paths called orbits. Imagine spinning around and around – that's what the planets do! This whole group of planets traveling around the sun is called the solar system.

Now, space is everything beyond our Earth’s sky! It’s a really, really big place where all the stars and planets live. Space is so vast that we can only see a tiny part of it from here on Earth. Isn't it amazing to think about how much there is out there?
keyIdeas: Earth is a planet.
The solar system includes the Sun and planets.
Space is beyond our world.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Mars is one of the planets that travels around the Sun.

questions.prompt: What is the name for the group of planets orbiting the sun?
questions.explanation: The solar system includes our Sun and all the planets that go around it.

questions.prompt: Where does space begin?
questions.explanation: Space is everything beyond our planet's atmosphere.

mt_B3W5EfimJw.title: Pushes & Pulls!
explanation: Have you ever played with a toy car? When you push the car forward, it starts moving! A push is when you use your hand or foot to move something. It’s like giving an object a little bit of energy to get going – that's what a force is!

Now, imagine you’re pulling a wagon full of blocks. Pulling is the opposite of pushing; it’s when you use your hands or feet to pull something towards you. Just like a push makes things move away, a pull makes them come closer!  The strength of the push or pull can also change how fast something moves.

Sometimes, we need a gentle push, like when we nudge a friend playfully. Other times, we need a strong push, like when we launch a ball really far.  Understanding pushes and pulls helps us understand why things move – it’s all about the force that makes them change their speed or direction!
keyIdeas: Pushes make objects move.
Pulls make objects move towards you.
Stronger pushes/pulls make things move faster.

questions.prompt: What is a push?
choices: B. Using your hand to move an object
questions.explanation: A push uses your strength to make something move away from you.

questions.prompt: If you give a toy car a gentle push, what will happen?
choices: B. It will go very fast
questions.explanation: A gentle push gives the car less energy to move quickly.

questions.prompt: You are pulling a rope and your friend is pushing it in the opposite direction. What will happen?
choices: A. The rope will stay still
B. The rope will move across the ground
C. The rope will move sideways
questions.explanation: Both pushes and pulls create forces that can change the direction of movement.

mt_Wa44s-f8Ws.title: Let's Be Scientists!
explanation: Today, we’re going to become little scientists! Scientists are people who ask questions and try to find answers by doing tests. It’s like being a detective – you look for clues to solve a mystery. We can do this with things around us, like our toys or even the food we eat!

To be a good scientist, we need to change just one thing at a time when we test something. Let's say we want to know if a sponge absorbs more water than a towel. We would only change what we’re testing – whether it’s the sponge or the towel – and keep everything else the same, like the amount of water.

After our test, we need to carefully watch what happens and write down what we see! This is called observing. Then, we can use what we saw to guess the answer to our question. If the sponge soaked up more water, we’d know that sponges are good at soaking things up!
keyIdeas: Change one thing.
Observe carefully.
Use your observations to guess.

questions.prompt: I want to test if a red crayon draws better than a blue crayon. What should I do?
choices: A) Change only the color of the crayon.
C) Use both crayons at the same time.
questions.explanation: To make sure we know what's changing, we need to only change one thing – in this case, the color of the crayon.

questions.prompt: You are testing if a full glass of water spills more easily than an empty glass. What should you do?
choices: A) Pour both glasses at the same time.
B) Change the size of the glass.
C) Only change how much water is in each glass.
questions.explanation: To see what's happening, we need to only change one thing – the amount of water.

questions.prompt: You tested a toy car on a carpet and then on tile. The car went faster on the tile! What does this tell us?
questions.explanation: This tells us that the type of surface (carpet or tile) affects how quickly the car rolls.

mt_mMMXD4v9Sh.title: Exploring Ancient Greece & Rome!
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to learn about two amazing ancient civilizations: Ancient Greece and Ancient Rome! These places were very, very old – even older than our grandparents were born! They lived a long, long time ago, and they built incredible things like temples and roads. Imagine building your own castle; these people did something similar but on a much grander scale!

Both Ancient Greece and Ancient Rome were located around the Mediterranean Sea, which is a big, salty ocean that connects Europe, Africa, and Asia. Think about when you build sandcastles at the beach – the sea is important! These ancient civilizations relied on the sea for trade and travel, bringing them goods from all over the world. It’s like having a giant playground around the water!

Now, Ancient Greece came before Ancient Rome.  We can picture it like this: first, the Egyptians built amazing pyramids (that's in the past!), then Ancient Greece started building cities and ideas, and finally, Ancient Rome grew up after Greece. They were all connected by time and location, especially near that beautiful Mediterranean Sea.
keyIdeas: Greece & Rome were ancient civilizations.
They lived around the Mediterranean Sea.
Rome came after Greece in time.

questions.prompt: Which sea did Ancient Greece and Ancient Rome live near?
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean Sea was a very important part of their lives!

questions.prompt: Ancient Greece came before which other ancient civilization?
questions.explanation: Ancient Egypt built pyramids long before Greece and Rome.

questions.prompt: Where did you find Ancient Greece and Ancient Rome on a map?
questions.explanation: You found them near the Mediterranean Sea!

mt_ZFwPZaDJ0_.title: Let's Be Data Detectives!
explanation: Today we’re going to be like data detectives! That means we’ll learn how to collect information and keep track of it. When you want to find out something, like how many blocks your friend has, or how many swings you can go on at the playground, you need to gather clues. We do this by watching carefully and writing down what we see – that's collecting data!

There are lots of ways to record our findings! One way is with a table, like a little grid where we write numbers or words. Another way is to use a tally chart, which is like drawing lines for every time something happens. For example, if you’re counting how many times someone throws a ball, you could make a line for each throw – that's tallying! Drawings and pictures are also great ways to show what we observed.

Once we have our data, it helps us answer our questions! Let's say you wanted to know which color blocks your friend has the most. You would count how many red blocks, blue blocks, green blocks, etc., and write those numbers in a table or chart. Then you can see which color has the biggest number – that’s your data telling you the answer!
keyIdeas: Collect information
Record observations
Use tables and charts

questions.prompt: You want to see how many times a dog barks in an hour. What’s the best way to record this?
choices: A) Write down every word the dog says.
B) Make a chart and mark each time you hear a bark.
C) Ask someone else to count for you.
questions.explanation: Making a chart lets you keep track of how many times you hear the bark.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re counting how many cars pass your house in 10 minutes. What should you use?
choices: A) A big drawing of all the cars.
B) A table with columns for ‘Time’ and ‘Number of Cars’.
C) Ask a friend to count for you.
questions.explanation: A table helps you organize your counting so you can see how many cars passed at each time.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to record data?
choices: A) It’s just fun to write things down.
B) It helps us answer our questions and learn about the world.
C) It makes your homework easier.
questions.explanation: Recording data gives us clues that help us understand what's happening.

mt_mmudyxf7bT.title: Amazing Pyramids and the Great Sphinx!
explanation: Long, long ago, in a place called Ancient Egypt, people built amazing things called pyramids! These weren't just piles of rocks; they were giant tombs – like special houses – where the pharaohs, who were like kings and queens, would be buried after they died. The Egyptians believed that if they had a beautiful tomb with everything they needed, their spirit could live on forever in the afterlife.

The biggest pyramid is called the Great Pyramid at Giza. It’s incredibly big – much bigger than your house or even a school! It was built thousands of years ago and it's still standing today, which means it hasn't fallen down! People travel from all over the world to see this amazing wonder of the ancient world.

Near the Great Pyramid stands another incredible thing called the Sphinx. Imagine a giant lion with the head of a person – that’s the Sphinx! It was made of stone and built to protect the pyramids, watching over them for many, many years.
keyIdeas: Pyramids were tombs for pharaohs.
The Great Pyramid is very old and still standing.
The Sphinx protected the pyramids.

questions.prompt: Why did the ancient Egyptians build pyramids?
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed that the pyramids would protect the pharaoh's body and help them live forever.

questions.prompt: What is the Great Pyramid at Giza known for?
choices: A. Being a small, simple structure
B. Being one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
C. Being used as a playground
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid was considered one of the most impressive buildings ever created.

questions.prompt: What does the Sphinx look like?
choices: B. A lion with a human head
questions.explanation: The Sphinx has the body of a lion and the head of a person – it’s a very unique statue!

mt_t1JXeNgKcu.title: Contractions!
explanation: Let’s talk about words that are a little bit sneaky! Sometimes, when we say things, we leave out letters. It's like when you build with LEGOs – you take pieces away to make something new. Contractions happen when we join two small words together and use an apostrophe (') to show where the missing letter is.

Look at your favorite teddy bear! You might say ‘Teddy’s’ instead of ‘Teddy's’. The 's' is missing because it’s already part of the word ‘teddy.’  Contractions are super common in writing and talking, and they make our words shorter and easier to say. They help us write faster too!

The apostrophe always points to where a letter has been left out. So, when you see 'it's', it means 'it is'. The little mark tells you that the ‘i’ is missing!  Learning about contractions makes reading and writing much more fun – let’s practice!
keyIdeas: Contractions use apostrophes.
Apostrophes show missing letters.
Contractions make words shorter.

questions.prompt: What does the apostrophe in 'it's' show?
choices: B. Where a letter is missing
C. That it’s a big word
questions.explanation: The apostrophe shows that the ‘i’ has been left out of the word ‘it is’.

questions.prompt: 'I'm' means what?
questions.explanation: 'I'm' is a contraction for 'I am'.

questions.prompt: Can you draw an apostrophe and write a short contraction with it?
questions.explanation: Encourage the child to create their own example, such as ‘he’s’ or ‘they’re’.

mt_S9SKah-yi_.title: Volcanoes! Hot Rocks from the Earth!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs, and sometimes the bricks get really hot from playing outside. A volcano is a bit like that – it’s an opening in the ground where super hot stuff comes out! This ‘stuff’ isn’t made of LEGOs though; it’s melted rock called lava, which is like really, really hot water mixed with rocks.

Deep down inside our planet, Earth is very warm. Sometimes, this heat melts rocks into a gooey liquid – that's lava! It pushes upwards and finds a weak spot in the ground, creating a volcano. Think about your favorite toy car; sometimes it rolls downhill because of gravity - lava does something similar!

Let’s look at a picture of a volcano! See how it has a big opening? That’s where the lava comes out when the volcano erupts – that means it's exploding with hot, melted rock. It’s like a giant, messy surprise from inside the Earth!
keyIdeas: Volcanoes have openings in the ground.
Lava is melted, super-hot rock.
Volcanoes erupt when pressure builds up.

questions.prompt: What happens at a volcano?
choices: A. Rain falls from the sky
questions.explanation: Volcanoes are known for releasing hot, melted rock called lava!

questions.prompt: What is lava made of?
questions.explanation: Lava is a mixture of molten rock – it's like really hot water mixed with stones.

questions.prompt: If you saw a volcano erupting, what would you say?
questions.explanation: You would explain that it’s an opening in the ground where very hot melted rock comes out!

mt_VLu59hpQ4T.title: Shades of Meaning
explanation: Distinguish shades of meaning among verbs describing similar actions and among adjectives differing in intensity; make real-life connections between words and their use
You know this idea when you can: Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance'); Order adjectives by intensity (e.g. big, huge, gigantic).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance')
Order adjectives by intensity (e.g. big, huge, gigantic)
Connect vocabulary to personal experiences (e.g. note things at school that are 'colourful')

questions.prompt: True or false: Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance')
questions.explanation: Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance')

questions.prompt: True or false: Order adjectives by intensity (e.g. big, huge, gigantic)
questions.explanation: Order adjectives by intensity (e.g. big, huge, gigantic)

questions.prompt: True or false: Connect vocabulary to personal experiences (e.g. note things at school that are 'colourful')
questions.explanation: Connect vocabulary to personal experiences (e.g. note things at school that are 'colourful')

mt_bvxkT1nepy.title: Egyptian Gods and the Afterlife
explanation: Long ago, in a place called Ancient Egypt, people believed in lots of amazing gods and goddesses! These weren’t just stories; they were like superheroes who lived among them. They had names like Ra, the sun god, and Isis, who was a powerful goddess known for helping people. The Egyptians thought these gods helped them with everything from growing crops to keeping safe.

The Egyptians believed that when someone died, their life didn’t just end. Instead, they thought it continued in a special place called the afterlife – kind of like a really amazing dream! They imagined this afterlife was filled with beautiful things and happy times, where they could be with loved ones forever. It was important to them to make sure everything was ready for when their time on Earth was over.

Because they believed in the afterlife, the Egyptians built incredible tombs for the people who died. These tombs were full of treasures like gold, food, and games – because they thought the person would need these things in the afterlife! They also put in everyday objects, so the dead person could still have familiar comforts when they woke up in their new world.
keyIdeas: Ancient Egyptians worshipped many gods.
They believed life continued after death.
Tombs were filled with treasures for the afterlife.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a god in Ancient Egypt?
questions.explanation: Ra was one of the many gods worshipped by the ancient Egyptians.

questions.prompt: Why did the Egyptians fill tombs with food and games?
choices: A. To eat them in the afterlife
B. Because they liked to play games
C. They thought the dead person would need them in the afterlife
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed these items were needed for the person's life after death.

questions.prompt: What did the ancient Egyptians believe happened when someone died?
choices: B. Their life continued in the afterlife
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed their spirit would live on in a special place.

mt_FIkqA0qhnj.title: Money Detectives!
explanation: Let’s become money detectives! We use special pieces of paper and metal to buy things we want, like a yummy ice cream cone or a brand new toy car. These are called coins and notes, and they each have a different value – just like numbers on a ruler that measure different lengths.  We'll learn about the most common ones so you can start understanding how money works.

You’ll see lots of different coins, like pennies (worth one cent), nickels (worth five cents), dimes (worth ten cents), and quarters (worth twenty-five cents). There are also notes – paper money – that come in different amounts like $1, $5, $10, and $20.  Each coin and note has a picture on it to help us know how much it's worth, and they all add up to make bigger amounts of money!

Imagine you have 3 quarters! That’s 75 cents – enough for some small candies. But you could also use 1 dime and 2 nickels to get the same amount too!  It's fun to see how many different ways we can put coins and notes together to buy something, and that’s what being a money detective is all about.
keyIdeas: Coins have values (penny, nickel, dime, quarter).
Notes are paper money with different amounts.
Different combinations of coins and notes can equal the same amount.

questions.prompt: Which coin is worth 5 cents?
questions.explanation: A nickel has five points, and each point represents 5 cents.

questions.prompt: What does a $1 note look like?
choices: A. A picture of a dog
B. A picture of the President
C. A picture of a star
questions.explanation: The $1 note has a portrait of the current president on it.

questions.prompt: You have 2 dimes and 1 nickel. How much money do you have?
questions.explanation: Two dimes are worth 20 cents, and one nickel is worth 5 cents, so 20 + 5 = 25 cents.

mt_18qkgxr_-T.title: Shapes All Around!
explanation: Let's talk about shapes! Shapes are everywhere we look – they’re like building blocks for the world around us. We see them in our toys, like a square block or a round ball; even yummy snacks can be shaped like squares or circles, like graham crackers or cookies.  Shapes help us understand what things look like and how they fit together.

When we find shapes, we need to name them! If you see a rectangle, you might say ‘a rectangle’. That means you’re describing the shape – it has four sides and four corners. We can also use words like 'square' for something with all sides equal, or 'circle' for something round like a wheel on your bike.

Plural nouns are just about saying more than one thing! If we have one toy car, we say ‘car’. But if we have two toy cars, we say ‘cars’ – that’s because the word ‘car’ becomes ‘cars’ when there's more than one.  It's like magic - adding 's' or 'es' to make it plural!
keyIdeas: Plural nouns mean more than one.
We add '-s' or '-es' to make nouns plural.
Shapes help us describe things around us.

questions.prompt: What does ‘cat’ become when there are two cats?
questions.explanation: Adding '-s' to 'cat' makes it plural, meaning more than one.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a shape?
questions.explanation: A circle is a basic geometric shape with no corners or sides.

questions.prompt: If you have one apple and your friend has one apple, how many apples do you have together?
questions.explanation: Having two of the same thing is plural – so you have two apples.

mt_P0HBNfp46z.title: Life in Ancient Egypt
explanation: Long ago, a very long time before we were born, there was a place called ancient Egypt! People who lived there, called Egyptians, had a really different life than we do today. They built amazing pyramids and temples, but they also did lots of things every day to keep their families happy and healthy. Imagine living by the Nile River – it was a very important part of their lives!

Most ordinary Egyptian families were farmers. They grew crops like wheat and barley, which are types of grains used for bread and other foods. They planted these seeds near the river because the water from the Nile helped them grow big and strong. Families also ate fish, fruit like dates, and vegetables like onions – it was a simple but tasty way to live.

Children in ancient Egypt played games just like kids do today! They might have played with wooden toys or even had pets like cats, which were very important animals back then. While life was different, families still loved spending time together, just like we do now – it's a reminder that people everywhere share some similar feelings.
keyIdeas: Farmers grew crops by the Nile.
Families ate simple foods like bread and fish.
Ancient Egyptians played games with children today.

questions.prompt: What did most Egyptian families do for food?
choices: A. They ate pizza and hamburgers.
B. They grew wheat and barley.
C. They ate ice cream and cookies.
questions.explanation: The Egyptians were farmers, so they grew their own food like wheat and barley.

questions.prompt: What animal was important to the ancient Egyptians?
questions.explanation: Cats were very special animals in ancient Egypt and were often kept as pets.

questions.prompt: How is life in ancient Egypt similar to life today?
choices: B. Families still spend time together.
questions.explanation: Just like today, families in ancient Egypt loved spending time with their loved ones.

mt_uTKgmWqSoI.title: Making Good Choices!
explanation: Let's learn about making choices! Sometimes we have to decide between different things, like choosing what toy to play with or whether to eat an apple or a cookie. When we make a choice, it’s important to think about what might happen next – that’s called a consequence. A consequence is the result of something you do; it can be good or bad!

Imagine you build a really cool tower out of blocks, but accidentally knock it over! That's a negative consequence – it wasn’t very safe and your tower is gone. But if you share your toys with a friend, that’s a positive consequence – they are happy, and you have someone to play with too!  It’s important to think about the possible consequences before we make any choice.

Being ‘responsible’ means making good choices and taking care of ourselves and others. It also means being ‘safe,’ which means avoiding things that could hurt us or anyone around us. Always talk to a trusted adult – like your mom, dad, teacher, or grandma – when you need help figuring out what's the right thing to do.
keyIdeas: Consequence: The result of a choice.
Responsible: Making good choices and taking care of others.
Safe: Avoiding things that could hurt us.

questions.prompt: If you break a toy, what might be a consequence?
choices: A) You get to play with it again.
B) Your mom might make you fix it.
questions.explanation: The consequence is the result of breaking the toy – your mom will likely ask you to help repair it.

questions.prompt: Which choice is most responsible?
choices: A) Eating all your candy at once.
B) Telling a trusted adult if you feel sad.
questions.explanation: Telling an adult when you're feeling sad shows you’re taking care of your feelings and seeking help.

questions.prompt: Why do we have rules at school?
choices: A) Rules are fun to break.
B) Rules help keep everyone safe and fair.
questions.explanation: Rules exist to create a safe and orderly environment for all students.

mt_wRlf0g2MbB.title: Understanding Others!
explanation: Let’s talk about how we understand what other people are feeling and thinking! Sometimes, it's hard to know exactly what someone else is going through. When you see a friend sad because they lost their favorite toy car – maybe they feel disappointed or upset. That's where ‘empathy’ comes in; empathy means trying to imagine yourself in their shoes and understand how *they* are feeling.

Imagine you're building with LEGOs, and your brother is using the same blocks! You might both want to build the same spaceship, but you have different ideas about what it should look like. That’s because everyone has a ‘perspective,’ or a ‘point of view.’ It means seeing things in your own way – just like how you see the LEGOs differently than your brother does.

When we live together in a ‘community’, like our school or neighborhood, it's important to be ‘kind’ and ‘fair’ to everyone. We can also think about what makes us ‘similar’ and what makes us ‘different.’  It’s wonderful when everyone feels they belong and is included – that's called ‘inclusion,’ and it means we care about each other!
keyIdeas: Empathy: Feeling what others feel.
Perspective: Seeing things differently.
Community: Caring for those around us.

questions.prompt: If your friend is crying because they fell and scraped their knee, you could say you have empathy by…
choices: A. Telling them to stop crying.
B. Trying to imagine how much it hurts.
questions.explanation: Empathy means understanding how someone else feels.

questions.prompt: Two people are watching the same soccer game, but one is cheering for Team Red and the other is cheering for Team Blue. They have different…
questions.explanation: Perspective means seeing things from your own point of view.

questions.prompt: Which of these words best describes how you should treat someone who is new to our class?
questions.explanation: Kindness and inclusion are important for a happy community.

mt_muxjw0fxxN.title: Wild, Farm & Pet Animals!
explanation: Let's talk about all the amazing animals in the world! Some animals live out in nature, like a fox running through the garden. These are called wild animals – they find their own food to eat, like berries and small creatures, and build their own homes, like dens in trees or burrows in the ground. They don’t need us to take care of them; they're perfectly happy living on their own!

Then we have farm animals! Think about a fluffy sheep grazing in a field – farmers keep these animals for things like wool to make sweaters and blankets, or even milk to drink. Farm animals live with people and rely on us for food and shelter. It’s important to remember that they are helping us too!

Finally, we have pets! Our furry friends, like our cats and dogs, are kept by us for companionship – meaning we love spending time playing and cuddling with them. Pets need us to feed them, take care of them, and give them lots of love and attention. It’s important to always remember that wild animals should be admired from a distance.
keyIdeas: Wild animals live independently.
Farm animals are raised by people.
Pets are kept for companionship.

questions.prompt: Which animal is a farm animal?
questions.explanation: Cows live on farms and give us milk!

questions.prompt: Why should we watch wild animals from far away?
choices: B. Because they need their space
C. Because they want to play with us
questions.explanation: Wild animals are used to living on their own and don't like being bothered.

questions.prompt: What kind of animal is a dog?
questions.explanation: Dogs are kept by people as pets for love and fun!

mt_UHQfb-n-w3.title: Actions and Consequences
explanation: Let’s talk about how our actions, or things we do, can make things happen. When you build a tall tower out of blocks, it's your action! If you carefully put each block on top of the other, it will stand up really high – that’s a good consequence. But if you bump into the table while building, the whole tower might fall down – that’s a bad consequence because all your hard work is gone!

Everything we do has an effect, like when you decide to share your crayons with a friend. Sharing is a kind action and makes your friend happy! However, if you don't ask before taking someone else’s toy from the playroom, it can make them sad and angry – that’s a consequence of not asking first. It’s important to think about how our choices affect others.

Sometimes, actions have good results, like helping your mom set the table. When you help, she doesn't get as tired and has more time to read you a story! Other times, actions can be tricky, like shouting during playtime. Shouting might make other children upset and stop playing with you – that’s a consequence we want to avoid.
keyIdeas: Actions affect us.
Choices impact others.
Good actions have good results.

questions.prompt: If you throw a ball really hard, what might happen?
choices: A. It will land softly on the grass.
B. It could hit someone nearby.
C. It will float in the air.
questions.explanation: Throwing something hard can cause it to go too far and potentially hurt someone.

questions.prompt: Imagine you want to draw a picture, but you need your friend’s favorite pencil. What should you do?
choices: A. Take the pencil without asking.
B. Ask your friend if you can borrow it.
questions.explanation: Asking is a good action that shows respect for others and their belongings.

questions.prompt: True or False: When you help someone clean up, it’s always a good thing.

mt_RNRymbz5SO.title: Shopping Smart!
explanation: Imagine you’re at the toy store and you see a really cool race car! When you want to buy something, like that race car, you first look at how much it costs. The shop will show you a sign with a price – let's say it says ‘£3’. This tells you exactly how many pennies or coins you need to give the person helping you.

Next, you choose your item and then you pay! You might use a coin like a pound (£1) or a note (a piece of paper money). The shop worker will count your money carefully to make sure it’s enough. If you don't have enough, they won't let you buy the race car – that’s why it’s important to know how much things cost before you go shopping!

Finally, if you pay with more money than the item costs, you get change back! Change is extra money given back to you. For example, if the race car cost £3 and you gave the shop worker a £5 note, they would give you £2 in change. It’s like getting a little bonus for being a good shopper!
keyIdeas: Prices tell us how much things cost.
You need to pay before you can take something home.
Change is extra money given back after you buy something.

questions.prompt: If a toy costs £2, and you give the shop worker £5, how much change will you get?
questions.explanation: You subtract the price (£2) from the money you gave (£5) to find the change.

questions.prompt: What does a price label show you?
choices: A) How many friends are in the shop.
B) How much the item costs.
C) Where to sit and rest
questions.explanation: Price labels tell us the cost of the items we want to buy.

questions.prompt: You want to buy a sticker that costs 50p. You give the shop worker a £1 coin. How much change will you get?

mt_V_wIdRZLsG.title: Nocturnal Animals – Sleepy Stars!
explanation: Imagine you have a favorite teddy bear, right? Teddy loves to play with you all day long! But what happens when the sun goes down and it’s dark outside? Many animals are sleepy and want to rest, but some special animals are awake at night – we call them nocturnal. Nocturnal means they're active when most people are sleeping!

There are lots of amazing nocturnal animals like owls, bats, and foxes! Owls have big eyes that help them see in the dark because they hunt for mice when it’s nighttime. Bats use echolocation to find their way around – they make sounds and listen for how they bounce back! Even a little hedgehog likes to come out at night to search for bugs.

Nocturnal animals often have special features that help them survive in the dark. For example, bats use big ears to hear tiny insects buzzing around, while foxes rely on long whiskers to feel their way through tall grass. These amazing adaptations allow them to thrive when we’re asleep!
keyIdeas: Nocturnal animals are active at night.
They have special features for seeing and finding food in the dark.
Many nocturnal animals sleep during the day.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals is nocturnal?
questions.explanation: Owls are known to be active at night.

questions.prompt: What helps bats find their way around in the dark?
questions.explanation: Bats use echolocation – bouncing sounds off objects – to navigate.

questions.prompt: Why do nocturnal animals sleep during the day?
questions.explanation: To avoid being seen by predators (animals that hunt other animals) and to conserve energy when it’s bright outside.

mt_wzUzVEBqJb.title: Other People's Feelings and Thoughts
explanation: Understand that other people have their own feelings and thoughts, and that these might be different from your own — a foundational awareness that not everyone sees or feels things the same way
You know this idea when you can: State that other people can feel differently about the same thing; Give an example of a time someone felt differently from them about the same situation.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: State that other people can feel differently about the same thing
Give an example of a time someone felt differently from them about the same situation
Accept another child's feeling as valid even when it differs from their own

questions.prompt: True or false: State that other people can feel differently about the same thing
questions.explanation: State that other people can feel differently about the same thing

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of a time someone felt differently from them about the same situation
questions.explanation: Give an example of a time someone felt differently from them about the same situation

questions.prompt: True or false: Accept another child's feeling as valid even when it differs from their own
questions.explanation: Accept another child's feeling as valid even when it differs from their own

mt_pwo81ls_J-.title: Amazing Animal Hide-and-Seek!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how some animals look just like something else? That's because they use a clever trick called camouflage! Camouflage means blending in with their surroundings, so they can hide from danger or sneak up on yummy snacks. It’s like a super-secret hiding game that nature plays!

Let’s look at some amazing animals who are masters of disguise. Think about a leaf insect – it looks exactly like a green leaf! Or how about an Arctic hare? In the winter, its fur turns completely white to blend in with the snow and ice. Even some fish have camouflage; they can change their colors to match the sea floor!

Camouflage is super important for animals because it helps them stay safe. If a lion is trying to catch a zebra, the zebra uses its stripes to confuse the lion and make it harder to see. Similarly, if a sneaky fox wants to surprise a little mouse, it can use camouflage to hide in the bushes and get closer.
keyIdeas: Camouflage means blending in.
Animals use camouflage to hide or sneak.
Camouflage helps animals survive.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals uses camouflage to blend into the snow?
questions.explanation: Arctic hares have white fur in winter to match the snowy environment.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when an animal is camouflaged?
choices: B. It blends in with its surroundings
C. It can fly really fast
questions.explanation: Camouflage means an animal looks like something else around it.

questions.prompt: Why do animals use camouflage?
choices: B. To hide from predators or sneak up on food
questions.explanation: Animals use camouflage to stay safe and catch their meals.

mt_83gRQ9OPkc.title: My Amazing Body!
explanation: Let’s talk about our bodies! We are all made up of different parts, and each part helps us do amazing things. Our body has special tools called senses that help us learn about the world around us – like seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and touching. Think about your favorite toy car; you can see it with your eyes!

Each part of our body is connected to a sense. Your eyes help you *see* things, like bright colors or silly faces! Your ears help you *hear* sounds, like music or someone talking.  Your nose helps you *smell* yummy food, and your tongue helps you *taste* sweet treats – just like when you eat an apple!

And don’t forget your skin! It's all over your body and helps you *touch* things, like feeling a soft blanket or a bumpy rock.  So many parts work together to make us who we are, and each sense gives us important information about the world!
keyIdeas: We have five main senses.
Each sense is linked to a body part.
Our senses help us learn about our surroundings.

questions.prompt: Which part of your body helps you see?
questions.explanation: Your eyes are special because they help you see things around you!

questions.prompt: What do you use your ears for?
questions.explanation: Your ears help you listen to sounds like music or conversations.

questions.prompt: If you smell something sweet, what is helping you?
questions.explanation: Your nose is detecting the scent of something sweet!  Can you think of another time you used your nose?

mt__7hXSTbu9s.title: Minibeast Movers!
explanation: Let’s talk about how amazing minibeasts move! Minibeasts, like ants and beetles, are super busy exploring the world around us. They don't walk like we do; they have special ways to get from place to place. Some crawl really slowly, others zoom through the air, and some even wiggle underground!

Imagine an ant carrying crumbs – it’s crawling along the ground! Or think about a beautiful butterfly fluttering its wings as it flies from flower to flower. We can also see worms slithering in the garden or grasshoppers jumping high into the air – each one has a different way of moving around.

Let's look closely at how many legs they have too! Ants and beetles have six little legs that help them crawl, while spiders have eight long legs to move across surfaces. Counting their legs can help us understand how these minibeasts are built and how they move.
keyIdeas: Minibeasts move in different ways.
Some move by crawling, flying, or slithering.
The number of legs helps us learn about minibeasts.

questions.prompt: Which way do ants usually move?
questions.explanation: Ants are known for crawling slowly along the ground.

questions.prompt: What does a butterfly do to move around?
questions.explanation: Butterflies use their wings to fly through the air.

questions.prompt: How many legs does an ant have?
questions.explanation: Ants are famous for having six legs that help them move!

mt_GLY3R3YSlf.title: Superheroes Compared!
explanation: Today, we’re going to be detectives! We'll look at two stories and see if they have anything in common. Let’s imagine we’re reading about superheroes – maybe one is Spider-Man who swings around New York City, and the other is Batman who fights crime in Gotham. Just like when you compare your toys, we can find things that both heroes share!

When we read, it's important to notice what happens to each character. Maybe both Spider-Man and Batman have a special suit that helps them do their jobs. Or perhaps they both need to be brave and help people in danger. Finding these similarities is like building a puzzle – the more pieces you find, the clearer the picture becomes!

Even though they’re different, it's also fun to see what makes each superhero unique. Maybe Spider-Man uses his webs to catch bad guys, while Batman relies on gadgets and detective skills. Recognizing these differences helps us understand how each story is special and exciting in its own way.
keyIdeas: Characters can share similar goals.
Characters can have different powers or tools.
Comparing stories helps us understand them better.

questions.prompt: Spider-Man and Batman both want to…
questions.explanation: Both superheroes are dedicated to protecting their cities and assisting those in need.

questions.prompt: Which of these is something Batman uses?
questions.explanation: Batman’s iconic bat-suit is a key part of his identity and fighting style.

questions.prompt: If you read two stories about brave knights, what could you compare?
choices: A. The color of their armor
C. The type of food they ate
questions.explanation: Comparing how the knights acted bravely is a good way to understand both stories.

mt_BnabTHkNIp.title: Meet Some Amazing Dinosaurs!
explanation: Dinosaurs lived a *long*, long time ago – much longer than we have! They were giant animals that walked around the Earth before there were people. Scientists called paleontologists study dinosaurs by digging up their bones and looking at fossils, which are like dinosaur skeletons that have turned to stone. We can learn so much about these incredible creatures just by looking at pictures or even building with blocks!

Let's meet some of the most famous dinosaurs! The Tyrannosaurus Rex was a super strong hunter with tiny arms – imagine a giant lizard with huge teeth and short little arms for holding onto its prey. Triceratops had three big horns on its head, which it used to protect itself from other dinosaurs. Stegosaurus is known for having plates along its back and spikes sticking out of its tail!

The Brachiosaurus was an enormous dinosaur that could eat leaves high up in the trees because it had a very long neck – like a giraffe! Diplodocus was another giant, also with a really long neck. It’s amazing to think about how different these dinosaurs looked and how they lived millions of years ago. Now you know some names!
keyIdeas: Tyrannosaurus Rex had tiny arms.
Triceratops used horns for protection.
Dinosaurs were very, very big!

questions.prompt: Which dinosaur is known for having three horns?
questions.explanation: The Triceratops used its three horns to defend itself!

questions.prompt: What did the Brachiosaurus use its long neck for?
choices: B. To eat leaves from tall trees
questions.explanation: The Brachiosaurus had a very long neck to reach high up into the trees!

questions.prompt: Which dinosaur has plates on its back?
questions.explanation: Stegosaurus is famous for the plates that ran down its back.

mt_VY3rBq8RyP.title: Prepositions
explanation: Understand and use the most frequently occurring prepositions of location and direction (e.g. to, from, in, out, on, off, for, by, with)
You know this idea when you can: Use prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g. 'on the table', 'under the bed'); Follow instructions involving prepositions (e.g. 'Put the book on the shelf').
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Use prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g. 'on the table', 'under the bed')
Follow instructions involving prepositions (e.g. 'Put the book on the shelf')
Describe positions using prepositional phrases

questions.prompt: True or false: Use prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g. 'on the table', 'under the bed')
questions.explanation: Use prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g. 'on the table', 'under the bed')

questions.prompt: True or false: Follow instructions involving prepositions (e.g. 'Put the book on the shelf')
questions.explanation: Follow instructions involving prepositions (e.g. 'Put the book on the shelf')

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe positions using prepositional phrases
questions.explanation: Describe positions using prepositional phrases

mt_GBY8enpzO0.title: Forces All Around Us!
explanation: Let’s talk about forces! A force is like a push or a pull – anything that can make something move or change direction. Think about your favorite toy car; when you push it, you're using a force to get it moving! Forces are all around us and they help things happen every day.

There are different *types* of forces too. Some forces need you to touch something to work, like friction. Friction is what stops your shoes from slipping on the floor when you walk – it’s a push against the ground! Gravity is another force that pulls everything down towards the Earth; that's why if you drop an apple, it falls to the ground.

Other forces don't need you to touch them. Magnetism is a non-contact force that can pull metal objects together without touching. Air resistance is also a non-contact force – it’s like air pushing against things as they move through it, slowing them down, just like friction. Understanding these different forces helps us understand how the world works!
keyIdeas: Force: A push or pull.
Contact Forces: Need to touch something.
Non-Contact Forces: Act from a distance.

questions.prompt: What is a force?
questions.explanation: A force is exactly what it sounds like – something that makes things move!

questions.prompt: Which of these is a contact force?
questions.explanation: Friction needs to be in contact with an object to work, unlike gravity or air resistance.

questions.prompt: What happens when you drop something?
questions.explanation: The force of gravity pulls the object down towards the Earth! Can you name this force?

mt__ab4knIaSL.title: Needs vs. Wants!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about something really important: the difference between things we *need* and things we *want*. Needs are things that help us stay safe, healthy, and comfortable – like food when we’re hungry or a warm coat when it’s cold outside.  These are things everyone needs to live happy and healthy lives! Let's think about our favorite toys; they're fun, but not something we absolutely have to have.

Wants are things that make us feel good or happy, but we don’t *have* to have them. For example, a shiny new toy car is a want because it's super cool and fun to play with, but you could still go outside and play tag!  Sometimes, we get wants – like a yummy ice cream cone – but we know that having too much of anything isn’t always good. It’s important to understand the difference so we can make smart choices.

Families often have to make choices about spending money because money doesn't grow on trees!  They usually buy things they *need* first, like food and a place to live – that’s called shelter. Then, if there’s some money left over, they might choose to buy something they *want*, like a special treat or maybe even a new toy. It’s all about making the best use of our resources!
keyIdeas: Needs are things we must have.
Wants are things that make us happy but aren't essential.
Families often prioritize needs before wants.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re hungry. Which of these do you *need*?
questions.explanation: A sandwich provides food, which is something we need to stay healthy.

questions.prompt: Everyone needs a place to live. What is an example of a need?
questions.explanation: A house gives us shelter and a safe place to sleep – that’s a basic need.

questions.prompt: Your family buys groceries (food) first. Why might they do this?
questions.explanation: They are prioritizing needs before wants, ensuring their essential requirements are met.

mt_TfOiog-ALs.title: Grammar words: letter, word, sentence
explanation: Use basic grammatical terminology when discussing reading and writing: letter, capital letter, word, singular, plural, sentence, punctuation, full stop, question mark, exclamation mark
You know this idea when you can: Name punctuation marks correctly when pointed out in text; Use terms like 'capital letter' and 'full stop' when editing writing.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name punctuation marks correctly when pointed out in text
Use terms like 'capital letter' and 'full stop' when editing writing
Explain what a 'sentence' is using the correct term

questions.prompt: True or false: Name punctuation marks correctly when pointed out in text
questions.explanation: Name punctuation marks correctly when pointed out in text

questions.prompt: True or false: Use terms like 'capital letter' and 'full stop' when editing writing
questions.explanation: Use terms like 'capital letter' and 'full stop' when editing writing

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain what a 'sentence' is using the correct term
questions.explanation: Explain what a 'sentence' is using the correct term

mt_QdMMLRYWhn.title: Super Spelling Adventures!
explanation: Let's talk about how we make words even more interesting! Sometimes, when we want to describe something or say what someone is doing, we add little endings to the end of our words. These endings change the word a tiny bit and help us understand it better. For example, if you have one toy car, you can make many toy cars by adding ‘s’ at the end – that's how we make things plural!

There are some special endings we use a lot! One is '-ing', like when you 'jumping' with your friends on the playground. Another ending is '-ed,' which tells us something happened in the past, like when you 'helped' build a tower. These endings don’t change the root word; they just give it more information about what’s happening or how many things there are.

Another cool thing we can do is add '-er' and '-est'! If you want to describe something that is faster, you could say 'quicker'.  If you want to describe something that is the fastest, you would say ‘quickest’. These endings help us compare things – like who’s running faster or who’s wearing the coolest shoes!
keyIdeas: Plurals use -s or -es.
'ing' and 'ed' show actions.
-er and -est compare things.

questions.prompt: I have one cat. How do I make it plural?
questions.explanation: Adding -s to the end of ‘cat’ makes ‘cats’, which is more than one cat!
