questions.prompt: You are ____ jumping.
questions.explanation: The '-ing' ending shows that you are doing the action of 'jumping'.

questions.prompt: Which word is spelled with -est?
questions.explanation: ‘Quickest’ uses the -est ending to show the *most* fastest thing.

mt_tiPyEkm4cU.title: Breathing Adventures!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs, and you need to take a big breath! We breathe in air just like we use our muscles to build something amazing. The air goes into our bodies through two ways: our nose or our mouth – it’s like opening a door for the air to come inside. This air is super important because it helps us do all sorts of things, like running and playing!

Inside our body, there are special parts called lungs. Think of them as balloons that fill up with air! The lungs take in oxygen from the air – oxygen is a gas that our bodies need to stay strong and healthy, just like plants need sunshine. Without oxygen, we wouldn’t be able to run or jump or even think clearly!

When we breathe out, we push the old air (called carbon dioxide) back out of our lungs. Our body doesn't need this carbon dioxide; it’s like a little bit of waste that we get rid of. So, we breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide – it’s a fantastic exchange happening all the time!
keyIdeas: We breathe air into our lungs.
Lungs take in oxygen for energy.
We breathe out carbon dioxide.

questions.prompt: Where does the air go when we breathe in?
choices: B. Into our nose and mouth
questions.explanation: The air enters our body through our nose or mouth, just like a doorway!

questions.prompt: What does the oxygen do for our bodies?
choices: B. Gives us energy to play
questions.explanation: Oxygen helps our muscles work and gives us the power to move around.

questions.prompt: What gas do we breathe out?
questions.explanation: We breathe out carbon dioxide, which is a waste product for our bodies.

mt_hi8cVycbwn.title: Modelling with Sketches
explanation: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem
You know this idea when you can: Create a sketch, drawing, or physical model of a design solution; Explain how the shape or structure of the design helps solve the problem.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Create a sketch, drawing, or physical model of a design solution
Explain how the shape or structure of the design helps solve the problem
Relate the model to the real-world problem it addresses

questions.prompt: True or false: Create a sketch, drawing, or physical model of a design solution
questions.explanation: Create a sketch, drawing, or physical model of a design solution

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain how the shape or structure of the design helps solve the problem
questions.explanation: Explain how the shape or structure of the design helps solve the problem

questions.prompt: True or false: Relate the model to the real-world problem it addresses
questions.explanation: Relate the model to the real-world problem it addresses

mt_nNYo5A-7Bl.title: Paper Plane Power! Comparing Designs
explanation: Let's talk about paper airplanes! You’ve probably made one before, and you know they can fly – sometimes really far! But did you ever wonder why some paper airplanes fly better than others? It all comes down to the design, or how it's built. We can test different designs to see which one is the best at flying.

To make this a science experiment, we need to try two different paper airplane designs and collect information about them. Let’s say you build one with long wings and another with short wings. You could then test how far each plane flies, or maybe even how quickly it lands – that's collecting data! This is like being a detective, looking for clues to figure out what works best.

Once we've tested both planes, we need to compare the results. If one plane flew much further than the other, we can say that design was stronger! We use the information we collected – the distance it flew, how fast it landed - as evidence to help us decide which design is better and what could make it even better. It’s all about using facts to make a good choice.
keyIdeas: Design matters for performance.
Collecting data helps us compare.
Evidence shows us what works best.

questions.prompt: Which of these is important when testing paper airplanes?
choices: A. Making the plane really colorful
B. Collecting information about how far it flies
C. Only building one type of airplane
questions.explanation: Collecting data, like measuring distances, helps us compare the designs.

questions.prompt: If one paper airplane flew twice as far as another, what does this show?
choices: A. The first airplane is made of glitter
B. The first airplane's design is stronger
C. Both airplanes are equally good
questions.explanation: The longer distance shows that the design was more effective.

questions.prompt: What do we call information we collect during an experiment?
questions.explanation: Evidence is facts and data collected to help us understand something.

mt_VjxyJLtIbT.title: Roman Builders – Strong and Long-Lasting!
explanation: Long ago, there were amazing people called the Romans! They were super strong soldiers who marched all over a huge empire, which is like a really big country with lots of land. These Roman soldiers weren’t just fighting; they were building things too – roads and walls to protect their empire. Imagine them marching in a line, ready for adventure!

The Romans were fantastic builders! They built straight roads that stretched for miles and miles, and they made strong stone walls to keep people safe. You can still see some of these Roman roads and walls today if you visit special places like Italy or England! It’s amazing how long their buildings have lasted.

Why did the Romans build so well? Because they were really good at it! They used strong materials like stone and bricks, and they planned everything carefully. This meant that their roads and buildings could last for thousands of years – a very, very long time! That’s why we can still see them today.
keyIdeas: Roman soldiers built an empire.
Romans built straight roads and strong walls.
Roman buildings lasted a very long time because they were well-built.

questions.prompt: What did Roman soldiers do?
choices: B. They marched and built things.
questions.explanation: The Romans were famous for their army, which included marching and building roads and walls.

questions.prompt: Which of these can you still see today?
questions.explanation: Some Roman roads and walls are still standing after thousands of years!

questions.prompt: Why did the Romans build so well?
questions.explanation: The Romans were skilled builders who used strong materials and planned carefully.

mt_zh_RyesCgZ.title: Romulus & Remus: The Legend of Rome
explanation: Long, long ago, in Ancient Greece and then later in Ancient Rome, people told a very special story about how their city started. This story is called the legend of Romulus and Remus! It’s like a really cool fairytale that helps us understand where things came from.

The story says that Romulus and Remus were twin brothers who were born to be kings. But, some not-nice people didn't want them to be king so they put the babies in a basket and set it afloat on a river. Luckily, a kind wolf found the basket and protected the boys until they were discovered.

When people grew up, Romulus and Remus became brave heroes! They built a great city called Rome, which is where many of us live today. But remember, this story isn’t exactly true; it's a legend that Romans told to explain how their amazing city began.
keyIdeas: Twins abandoned as babies
Raised by a wolf
Legendary founding of Rome

questions.prompt: What animal helped raise Romulus and Remus?
questions.explanation: The story says a she-wolf protected the boys when they were babies.

questions.prompt: Who founded the city of Rome?
questions.explanation: Romulus is credited with building and naming the city of Rome.

questions.prompt: Is the story of Romulus and Remus a real historical event?
choices: A. Yes, it happened exactly as told in the story
B. No, it’s a legend or story people told
questions.explanation: Legends are stories that aren't always facts; they tell us about important beliefs and traditions.

mt_lp1eTsQen7.title: The Amazing Heart!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – you need to push them together to make a strong tower, right? Our heart is like a super strong muscle that pushes something really important: blood! Blood carries oxygen and food all around our bodies so we can run, play, and even think. It’s always working hard, just like your favorite toy car needs batteries to move!

The heart lives inside of us and it's amazing because it never stops! It beats – thump-thump, thump-thump – all day and all night. This beating pushes blood through special tubes called blood vessels. These blood vessels are like little roads that carry the blood to every part of our body, like our arms, legs, and brain.

Let’s try to find your heartbeat! Put your hand on your chest or gently touch your wrist with your fingers. You can feel a thump-thump – that's your heart pumping blood! It’s constantly working to make sure every part of you gets the things it needs to stay healthy and strong.
keyIdeas: The heart is a muscle.
Blood travels through tubes called blood vessels.
The heart beats continuously to pump blood.

questions.prompt: What does the heart do?
choices: B. It pumps blood around our body
questions.explanation: The heart pushes blood, which carries oxygen and food to all parts of your body.

questions.prompt: What are the tubes that carry blood called?
questions.explanation: Blood vessels are like roads for the blood to travel on.

questions.prompt: Can you feel your heart beating?
choices: A. Only when you run fast
B. Always, by putting your hand on your chest or wrist
questions.explanation: Yes! You can feel the thump-thump of your heart pumping blood.

mt_H1pAi4F_Oh.title: Meet the Greek Gods!
explanation: Long ago, people in Ancient Greece believed in amazing gods and goddesses! These weren't like superheroes we see today; they were powerful beings who controlled things like weather and stories. They had special jobs and lived together on a giant mountain called Mount Olympus – imagine it as their super cool home!

There were lots of different Greek gods, each with their own important role. For example, Zeus was the king of all the gods and he could control thunder and lightning! Athena was the goddess of wisdom and she helped people make smart choices, while Poseidon ruled over the oceans with his powerful trident. These gods were like the leaders of everything!

The Greeks would often pray to these gods for help or good luck. If they needed rain for their crops, they might pray to Zeus. If they wanted to be brave, they could ask Athena for strength. It’s like asking a friend to help you – the ancient Greeks believed the gods listened and answered their prayers!
keyIdeas: Greek gods lived on Mount Olympus.
Each god had special powers.
Ancient Greeks prayed to the gods.

questions.prompt: Which god rules over the sea?
questions.explanation: Poseidon was the god of the oceans and used a trident to control the waves.

questions.prompt: Where did the Greek gods live?
questions.explanation: The ancient Greeks believed all the gods lived together on Mount Olympus.

questions.prompt: What might you pray to Zeus for?
questions.explanation: Zeus was the god of thunder and lightning, so people prayed to him for weather-related things.

mt_oN7fI4d_kU.title: What Is a Castle?
explanation: Imagine you have a really, really big toy castle! A castle is like a super strong house that people built long ago during a time called the Middle Ages. These castles were made to protect people and keep them safe from enemies. They were amazing buildings with lots of cool parts, just waiting for knights and princesses to live inside!

Castles weren't just homes; they were also big defenses! They had strong walls that were super thick, so invaders couldn’t break through. Many castles even had a moat – a deep pool of water surrounding the castle – to make it even harder for anyone to get close. Plus, there were towers where guards could watch out for danger and drawbridges to raise and lower to let people in and out.

People built castles because life was very different back then. Kings and queens needed places to live safely and protect their land from other kings and armies. A castle gave them a place to sleep, eat, and keep all their important things – like weapons and treasure! It’s like having the best superhero headquarters ever!
keyIdeas: Castles were strong homes.
They had defenses like walls and moats.
Kings and queens built castles to protect themselves.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a part of a castle?
questions.explanation: Castles had tall towers for guards to watch over the land.

questions.prompt: Why were castles built?
choices: B. To protect people from enemies
questions.explanation: Castles provided a safe place for kings and queens to live and defend their lands.

questions.prompt: What was a moat used for in a castle?
choices: C. To make castles smell nice
questions.explanation: The moat was a deep pool of water surrounding the castle to stop enemies from attacking.

mt_NP101Zl-4g.title: Using New Vocabulary
explanation: Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, being read to, and responding to texts in own speech and writing

mt_e29VrLfmYt.title: What Is an Earthquake?
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – sometimes the blocks wobble and fall down, right? An earthquake is kind of like that, but it’s happening way under our feet! It’s when the ground suddenly shakes really fast. This shaking happens because big pieces inside the Earth are moving around, pushing and pulling.

Sometimes earthquakes are very small and you might not even notice them. Other times, they can be super strong – like when you drop a toy from a tall height! Scientists measure how strong an earthquake is using something called a ‘scale,’ and it goes from weak to really, really powerful. It’s important to remember that the Earth is always moving, but earthquakes are special shaking events.

During an earthquake, things might feel wobbly or strange. You might see things fall over, or you could feel like you're bouncing up and down. It’s a good idea to stay calm and follow what grown-ups tell you to do – just like when you’re playing on the playground and someone tells you to stop running!
keyIdeas: Earthquakes cause shaking.
Earthquakes have different strengths.
It's important to be safe during an earthquake.

questions.prompt: What happens during an earthquake?
choices: B. Rain falls from the sky
questions.explanation: An earthquake is when the ground moves quickly and shakes! This is what causes all the damage.

questions.prompt: Which of these earthquakes would be strongest?
choices: A. A tiny shake you don’t feel
B. A big shake that makes things fall over
questions.explanation: Stronger earthquakes cause more shaking and can damage buildings and other things.

questions.prompt: What might it *feel* like during an earthquake?
questions.explanation: During an earthquake, you might feel like you're bouncing or that things are moving around unexpectedly.

mt_19qy2uuaKp.title: Ancient Kids vs. Today's Kids!
explanation: Imagine you’re a child living in ancient Greece or Rome, long, long ago! These civilizations were around thousands of years before our grandparents and great-grandparents were even born. Children back then had very different lives than we do today – it's like stepping into a time machine!

Let’s look at what they did differently. School was very different; instead of using pencils and paper, children wrote on wax tablets with special tools! They also trained to be strong and brave, especially in places like Sparta where boys learned how to fight. It's amazing to think about how much things have changed over time.

Even what they ate and wore was unique. Ancient kids often ate olives, bread, and grapes – yummy but different from pizza or ice cream! They wore tunics (long shirts) and sandals instead of jeans and sneakers. These differences show us just how far back in history these ancient civilizations lived.
keyIdeas: Ancient children had different schools.
Ancient children ate different foods.
Ancient civilizations were very old.

questions.prompt: A child today uses what to write?
questions.explanation: Today, children use pencils and paper to write their homework.

questions.prompt: What did ancient Roman children often eat?
questions.explanation: Ancient Romans commonly ate bread, grapes, and olives as part of their diet.

questions.prompt: How long ago did Ancient Greece and Rome exist?
questions.explanation: Ancient civilizations existed thousands of years before our grandparents were born.

mt_XbGfVhfiUz.title: Computers Everywhere!
explanation: Have you ever played a game on your tablet or watched a video on your phone? Those are both using computers! A computer isn't just a big, shiny machine like the ones in schools. It’s actually something that follows instructions and does things for us – it’s like a really good helper.  Lots of things around us have tiny computers inside them, making our lives easier.

All computers do the same thing: they take information, think about it, and then do something with it. Imagine you're building with LEGOs; you follow instructions to put them together in a certain way. A computer does this too, but with numbers and codes! Even things like your washing machine uses a tiny computer to decide how long to wash your clothes.

Let’s look around our house and see if we can find some computers!  Things like traffic lights, smart speakers that play music, and even some toys have computers inside. They all work together to make things happen – it's pretty amazing!
keyIdeas: Computers follow instructions.
Computers process information.
Many everyday objects contain computers.

questions.prompt: Which of these has a computer inside?
questions.explanation: Traffic lights use computers to change colors and make sure cars can drive safely.

questions.prompt: What do all computers do?
choices: B) They follow instructions and process information
C) They fly in the sky
questions.explanation: Computers take information, think about it, and then do something with it – just like you following directions to build a tower.

questions.prompt: Your mom’s washing machine has a computer. What does this computer do?
questions.explanation: The computer tells the washing machine how long to spin and rinse your clothes.

mt_H4YZ1rSKP3.title: All About Me!
explanation: Let’s talk about ourselves! It’s super important to know what we feel and think, and having the right words helps us do that. When you're feeling happy or sad, or even a little bit angry, it can be hard to explain exactly how you are. That’s why we have special words like ‘self-awareness,’ which means paying attention to your own feelings and thoughts – like noticing when you smile because you’re excited!

Sometimes, things happen that make us feel certain ways. We call these ‘triggers.’ A trigger is something that starts a feeling, like if someone says something unkind or if you don't get the toy you wanted. When we notice a trigger, we can 'reflect,' which means thinking about what happened and how it made us feel. It’s like looking back at a picture to remember what happened!

It’s also important to understand that everyone sees things differently! We have our own ‘perspective,’ meaning the way *we* look at something. For example, if you accidentally bumped into someone on the playground, from your perspective it might seem like they were moving too fast, but from their perspective, maybe you were running towards them. Using these words helps us understand ourselves and others better.
keyIdeas: Self-awareness means knowing your feelings.
Triggers are things that start our feelings.
Perspective is how we see things differently.

questions.prompt: What does ‘self-awareness’ mean?
choices: B. Paying attention to your feelings
questions.explanation: Self-awareness means understanding what you are feeling.

questions.prompt: If someone says something that makes you feel upset, it’s a…
questions.explanation: Something that makes you sad is a trigger.

questions.prompt: Imagine your friend and you are arguing about a game. You think it's unfair, but your friend doesn’t. What does ‘perspective’ mean in this situation?
choices: A. That you always have to be right
B. That you both see the situation differently
C. That you should just give up
questions.explanation: Perspective means understanding how each person sees things.

mt_69hFD2NgGe.title: My Feelings!
explanation: Sometimes, we feel lots of different things – like when you’re building a tall tower with your blocks! You might feel happy and excited when it gets higher and higher, or maybe a little frustrated if it falls down. It's okay to have these feelings; everyone does! Learning about our feelings is like learning new words for how we feel inside.

When you’re feeling something, it helps to name it. If you’re sad because your favorite toy broke, you can say ‘I am sad.’ Or if you are super excited when you get a present, you can shout ‘I'm so happy!’  Naming our feelings is the first step to understanding why we feel that way and what we might do about it.

Just like learning new words for shapes – circle, square, triangle – we need new words for how we feel. When you tell me you’re feeling angry, or maybe scared, or even calm, you're using your ‘feeling words.’  Using these words helps us understand ourselves and talk about our feelings with others.
keyIdeas: Recognize Feelings
Name Your Emotions
Words for Feelings

questions.prompt: I feel happy when…
choices: A. I get a new toy
questions.explanation: Happiness is a feeling of joy and excitement!

questions.prompt: If you are scared, what could you say?
questions.explanation: Saying 'I am scared' helps someone know how you feel.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a feeling?
questions.explanation: ‘Grumpy’ describes how you might be feeling – not something you see!

mt_uUa6cgv8zV.title: Earth Is Made of Rock!
explanation: Imagine building with blocks – you use lots of different shapes and sizes, right? Well, the Earth is kind of like a giant block puzzle made up of many things! It’s not just dirt; it's mostly rock. These rocks are all around us, deep inside the Earth and even on the surface.

Let’s look at your toys or maybe some food you eat – like a chocolate bar! Chocolate bars are made from different ingredients, right? The Earth is similar, but instead of yummy treats, it's made of solid materials like rock and stone. These rocks come in all sorts of colors and shapes, just like the blocks we use to build!

The ground beneath our feet – when you play on the playground or even walk around your house – is covered with rock! Everything that makes up Earth, from mountains to oceans, is made of these natural materials. It’s a really big puzzle, and scientists are still learning more about it every day.
keyIdeas: Earth is mostly made of rock.
Rocks come in many shapes and sizes.
The ground we walk on is made of rock.

questions.prompt: What is the Earth mostly made of?
questions.explanation: That’s right! The Earth is mainly built from solid rock.

questions.prompt: Which of these could be a piece of the Earth?
questions.explanation: Stones are made of rock, which is part of what makes up our planet.

questions.prompt: If you found a big, gray rock in your yard, what could you say about the Earth?
questions.explanation: You could say that the whole Earth is made of rocks like this one!

mt_adpdEaKEBC.title: Fast & Slow Earth Changes
explanation: Our amazing planet Earth is always changing! Sometimes these changes happen really, really fast – like when a volcano erupts. Imagine building with LEGOs and suddenly the whole tower crashes down; that’s kind of like a volcanic eruption where hot, melted rock explodes out onto the ground. These quick changes can make big mountains or new land appear in just a short time.

Other times, Earth's surface changes slowly over many years. Think about a river flowing and wearing away at a cliff – that’s called erosion. The water is like a tiny sculptor, slowly shaping the rocks into different shapes. This happens because wind and rain are always working to change things, just very, very slowly.

So, you see, some changes on Earth happen quickly, like a volcano or earthquake, and they make big changes in a short time. Other changes take a long, long time, like the river carving out a canyon. Both types of changes are important for making our planet look how it does today!
keyIdeas: Earth changes constantly
Some changes are fast (volcanoes, earthquakes)
Some changes are slow (erosion)

questions.prompt: A volcano erupts and covers the ground with hot rocks. This is an example of a…
questions.explanation: This is a quick change because it happens suddenly and dramatically.

questions.prompt: Wind and rain slowly wear away a mountain over many years. This is an example of…
questions.explanation: Erosion is the process of slow changes caused by wind or water.

questions.prompt: Which happens faster: a small earthquake shaking the ground or a river slowly carving a path through stone?
questions.explanation: Think about it – an earthquake shakes quickly, while a river takes a long time to shape the rock.

explanation: Explore rock pools (tide pools) as small ocean habitats where crabs, anemones, starfish, and small fish can be found, and understand that these creatures are adapted to survive crashing waves and changing water levels
You know this idea when you can: Name at least three creatures found in rock pools; Describe a rock pool as a small ocean habitat on the shore.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Describe a rock pool as a small ocean habitat on the shore

questions.explanation: Explain one way rock pool animals cope with waves or changing water levels

mt_PThM5P7Umd.title: A Day in Our Medieval Village
explanation: Imagine a time long ago, before cars or computers! Many people lived in small villages like this one, and they had very different lives than we do today. These villages were filled with cozy homes called cottages, which often had roofs made of straw – that’s what ‘thatched’ means! The villagers worked hard to make a living, growing their own food and helping each other.

Let's think about what a typical day might have looked like for someone in the village. They would wake up early to help with farming, planting seeds or tending to animals like cows and sheep.  They’d spend hours working in the fields, bringing in crops like wheat and barley which they used to bake bread – yummy! It was important work that helped everyone in the village.

Life was very different then compared to today. For example, children didn't go to school as we do; instead, they learned by helping their parents with chores. Also, people didn’t have electricity or running water like we have now – they had to fetch water from a well and build fires for warmth and cooking!  It was a simpler time, but also a very busy one.
keyIdeas: Villages were small communities.
Medieval villagers worked hard on farms.
Life in the village was different than today.

questions.prompt: What kind of roof did many houses in a medieval village have?
questions.explanation: Thatched roofs were made of straw, which was common in medieval villages.

questions.prompt: Which of these was a job a villager might have done?
questions.explanation: Villagers spent much of their time baking and preparing food for the community.

questions.prompt: What is one thing that was different about life in a medieval village compared to today?
questions.explanation: There were many differences, such as not having electricity or running water.  Think about how things are different now!

mt_UGf6jICEhs.title: Learning from Mistakes
explanation: Understand that making mistakes is a normal part of learning and that everyone — including adults — makes mistakes, and begin to see mistakes as opportunities to learn rather than reasons to give up
You know this idea when you can: Describe a time they made a mistake and what they learned from it; Respond to a mistake by trying again rather than giving up.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Respond to a mistake by trying again rather than giving up

questions.explanation: Respond to a mistake by trying again rather than giving up

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain why mistakes help people learn
questions.explanation: Explain why mistakes help people learn

mt_DJh2JPwTf6.title: Plant-Eaters vs. Meat-Eaters!
explanation: Let’s talk about dinosaurs! Dinosaurs lived a long, long time ago and they came in all sorts of shapes and sizes. Some dinosaurs loved to eat plants – we call them herbivores! Herbivores like to munch on leaves, ferns, and flowers just like a cow or a deer eats today.

Other dinosaurs were meat-eaters, also known as carnivores. These dinosaurs had sharp teeth for tearing into their food, which was usually other animals. Think about a lion – it uses its strong jaws and pointy teeth to catch and eat its prey; that’s how carnivores worked!

We can tell the difference between plant-eaters and meat-eaters by looking at their teeth! Herbivore dinosaurs had flat teeth, perfect for grinding up plants into mush. Carnivore dinosaurs had sharp, pointed teeth, designed for ripping and tearing meat – it's like a really strong pair of scissors!
keyIdeas: Herbivores eat plants.
Carnivores eat meat.
Teeth help us tell the difference.

questions.prompt: Which dinosaur would have flat teeth?
questions.explanation: Triceratops was a plant-eater, and plant-eaters need flat teeth to grind their food.

questions.prompt: Which of these dinosaurs is a meat-eater?
questions.explanation: Velociraptors had sharp teeth for tearing meat, making them carnivores.

questions.prompt: What do herbivores eat?
questions.explanation: Herbivores eat plants like leaves and flowers.

mt_WRRv1ABECC.title: Smart vs. Not Smart Devices
explanation: Let’s talk about things around us that can do different jobs! Some things are just there to sit and do the same thing over and over again, like a toy car or a building block. These aren't 'smart' because they don’t notice anything or change what they do based on what happens around them. Think of your LEGOs – you build with them exactly how you want, every single time!

Now, some things are ‘smart’. A smart speaker, like the one we use to listen to music, is smart because it *listens* for our voice! When you say ‘play my favorite song’, it understands and starts playing. It’s different from a regular radio because the radio just plays whatever station you choose; it doesn't understand what you are asking for.

There are even other smart things like automatic doors at stores or lights that turn on when it gets dark! These devices ‘sense’ something – like someone walking through a doorway or how much light is in a room – and then they *respond* by doing something different. That's what makes them special and smart!
keyIdeas: Smart devices respond to us.
Not-smart devices do the same thing every time.
Some machines sense their surroundings.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a smart device?
questions.explanation: A smart speaker can listen to your voice and respond.

questions.prompt: A regular light switch does what every time you flip it?
questions.explanation: A regular light switch always turns the light on or off, it doesn’t notice anything.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a smart device?
questions.explanation: An automatic door senses when someone approaches and opens itself.

mt_u6SYiVx7FX.title: Talking to Robots! Voice Assistants
explanation: Have you ever asked Alexa or Siri a question, like ‘What color is the sky?’ They’re called voice assistants, and they're really clever computers that listen to your voice! When you talk to them, first, they hear what you say – just like you listening carefully. Then, they try to figure out exactly what words you used, kind of like a detective searching for clues.

Let’s imagine you ask Siri, ‘Can I have an apple?’ and she says, ‘Yes, please eat your vegetables!’ That's because Siri is trying to understand *what* you asked, but sometimes it misunderstands. Voice assistants aren’t perfect; they are learning all the time! They need lots of information to give us the best answers.

These voice assistants are actually computer programs – like a set of instructions that tells the device what to do. They don't really *know* things or feel emotions like we do, so they can sometimes make mistakes. Think of it like following a recipe; if you miss a step, the cake might not turn out perfectly!
keyIdeas: Voice assistants listen to your voice.
They try to understand what you mean.
They are computer programs, not people.

questions.prompt: When you talk to Alexa, the first thing it does is:
choices: B. Listens to what you say.
C. Asks you a follow-up question.
questions.explanation: The voice assistant needs to hear you before it can understand!

questions.prompt: Sometimes Siri gives the wrong answer because:
choices: B. She doesn’t know how to speak clearly.
C. She is trying to understand your words but sometimes makes a mistake.
questions.explanation: Voice assistants are learning and can misunderstand what you mean.

questions.prompt: Are voice assistants people? Why or why not?
questions.explanation: Voice assistants are computer programs, so they don’t have feelings or understand things like we do.

mt_Wc6cOTQ1bA.title: Right Choices, Happy Hearts!
explanation: Let’s talk about making good choices! Sometimes we have to decide if something is ‘right’ or ‘wrong.’ A right choice means doing what's kind and helpful for others and ourselves. Think about your favorite building blocks – you wouldn’t want someone else to smash them, would you? That’s a wrong choice because it hurts and breaks something that belongs to someone else.

When we make choices, we need to think about how our actions affect other people.  If you see a friend crying, a right choice is to ask them what's wrong and offer a hug or help. A wrong choice might be to ignore their feelings or say something mean, which would make them feel even sadder. It’s important to remember that everyone deserves to be treated with kindness and respect.

There are rules in our families and schools to keep us safe and happy. These rules often tell us not to hurt others or take things that aren't ours – like when you share your toys with a friend, that’s a right choice!  Following these rules helps everyone feel comfortable and secure, and it shows we care about each other.
keyIdeas: Kindness matters
Honesty is important
Respect others

questions.prompt: If your friend accidentally breaks your favorite toy, what should you do?
choices: A. Hide it and blame someone else.
B. Tell the truth and offer to help fix it.
questions.explanation: Choosing B shows honesty and responsibility.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a right choice?
choices: A. Taking a crayon that doesn’t belong to you.
B. Sharing your snack with a friend who hasn't eaten much.
C. Saying mean things about someone else’s drawing.
questions.explanation: Sharing shows kindness and generosity.

questions.prompt: Why is it important not to hurt others?
choices: B. It’s okay as long as you don't mean it.
C. Hurting others can make them sad and scared.
questions.explanation: Hurting others causes pain and distress.

explanation: Know that the body needs food for energy, water to stay hydrated, sleep to rest and grow, and exercise to keep muscles and the heart strong — and that these are basic needs every human body has

mt_X5fdB4haHf.title: The Mighty Vikings!
explanation: Let’s talk about a really cool group of people called the Vikings! They lived a long, long time ago in a place we now call Scandinavia – that's like Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. These brave Vikings were amazing sailors and explorers who loved to travel across the ocean in their special ships.

The Vikings used something called longships. These ships were super important because they were very fast! They could also sail in shallow water, meaning they didn’t need deep rivers or oceans to travel. Many of these ships even had pointy decorations at the front called ‘dragon prows,’ which looked like fierce dragons!

Now, you might hear that Vikings raided villages and fought with swords, and that's true sometimes. But it wasn’t all fighting! They were also clever traders who bought and sold things from other countries, and they became farmers, growing food to feed their families.
keyIdeas: Vikings came from Scandinavia.
Longships helped them travel far across the sea.
Vikings did more than just raid – they were traders and farmers too!

questions.prompt: Where did the Vikings come from?
questions.explanation: The Vikings started in Norway, Sweden, and Denmark – that’s Scandinavia!

questions.prompt: What made longships special?
choices: B. They could sail in shallow water
C. They only sailed in the ocean
questions.explanation: Longships were built to travel quickly and safely, even in small rivers and bays.

questions.prompt: Were Vikings just fighters?
choices: A. Yes, they always fought wars
B. No, they also traded and farmed
C. Only men were allowed to fight
questions.explanation: Vikings were traders and farmers as well as warriors.

You know this idea when you can: Use 'cooperate' and 'compromise' correctly — e.g. 'We cooperated by taking turns choosing the game'; Explain what 'active listening' means and demonstrate it: facing the speaker, not interrupting, responding to what was said.

mt_vpMDMbx4pc.title: Who Is a Customer?
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs, and your friend wants to buy some of your creations! A customer is someone who buys something – it could be toys, food, or even an idea. When a business sells things like cookies at a bake sale or books at a store, the people buying those items are customers.

Let’s say you're selling lemonade at school. If you want to attract customers, they might look for things like how yummy it looks, if it tastes good, and how much it costs.  Good quality is important – does it taste fresh? And price matters too; is it a fair price compared to other drinks?

It’s really important to think about what *customers* want! You might love making super-sour lemonade, but maybe other kids would prefer something sweeter. Businesses try to make things that people will actually *want* to buy – this means thinking about their needs and desires, not just what the business owner wants to create.
keyIdeas: A customer buys something.
Customers look for quality and price.
Businesses think about what customers want.

questions.prompt: If you're selling drawings, what might a customer look for?
choices: A. How many colors are used
B. If the drawing looks nice
questions.explanation: Customers often care about how something looks.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to think about what customers want?
choices: A. So you can make exactly what you like
B. So people will actually buy your thing
C. So you can build a really tall tower
questions.explanation: Businesses need to create things that people are interested in.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're selling cookies. What’s one important thing to consider?

mt_YbX3LD0Eca.title: Listening Superpowers!
explanation: Imagine you're building a really cool LEGO tower with your friend, Liam. If you start talking about how *you* want to build it before he’s even finished telling you what he wants, he might feel like you aren’t listening! Listening is like having superpowers – it helps us understand our friends and family better and make them feel happy.

When someone is talking, it's important to look at them. That shows them you are paying attention and that you care about what they have to say. It also means waiting patiently until they’ve finished their whole thought before you start speaking – just like waiting for your turn in a game!

Let’s try this with our toys! If your sister is showing you her new teddy bear, don't interrupt and tell her about *your* favorite toy. Instead, look at the teddy bear, wait until she’s done talking, and then ask her something like, ‘What’s his name?’ That shows you were listening!’
keyIdeas: Look at the speaker.
Wait for them to finish.
Respond to what they said.

questions.prompt: When someone is talking, what should you do first?
questions.explanation: Looking at the speaker shows you're paying attention.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to wait until someone finishes before responding?
choices: A. It’s faster to talk first
B. They might get upset if you interrupt
C. You don’t need to listen
questions.explanation: Interrupting shows them you weren't listening and they may feel sad.

questions.prompt: If your friend is telling you about their favorite game, what should you do?
choices: A. Tell them about your favorite game right away
B. Ask them questions about the game
C. Ignore what they are saying
questions.explanation: Asking questions shows you were listening and interested in what they said.

mt_HZvTriQWTh.title: Let's Watch the Weather!
explanation: Today we’re going to be weather detectives! We can learn all about what happens outside and notice how it changes over time. Just like you might look at your LEGO blocks and see different shapes, we can look at the weather and see patterns too. It's really exciting to watch the clouds move across the sky!

Let’s think about rainy days – do you remember when you played in the puddles after it rained? Sometimes it rains for a little while, then stops. Other times, it might rain all day long! We can write down what we see each day like if it was sunny, cloudy, or raining to help us notice these changes.

When we look at our weather diary, we’re collecting evidence. This means we're noticing and writing down things about the weather so that we can understand how it works. If it rains a lot on Tuesdays, maybe we can guess it will rain again on Tuesday next week!
keyIdeas: Weather changes every day.
We can record what we see in our weather diary.
Looking for patterns helps us predict the weather.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to ‘record’ something?
choices: A. To draw a picture of it
B. To write down what you see
questions.explanation: Recording means writing down or noting something so we can remember it later.

questions.prompt: If it rains every Monday, what might you predict on the next Monday?
questions.explanation: Predicting means guessing what might happen based on what we’ve seen before.

questions.prompt: What is one thing you can write down in your weather diary?
questions.explanation: You could write down the temperature, if it was sunny or cloudy, or if it rained.

mt_SdIWVjzopp.title: Bones & Muscles – Our Amazing Bodies!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with blocks! Your body is like a giant block tower, and the bones are the strong, sturdy blocks that give it its shape. They help us stand up straight and keep our bodies from falling apart. Inside our bodies, we have a special framework called a skeleton made of many different bones – they're what make us who we are!

Let’s look at some of the important bones! You can feel your ribs sticking out on either side of you; that’s part of your ribcage which protects your heart and lungs. Then there’s your skull, a big bone that keeps your brain safe inside. And don't forget your legs – they have long bones like your femur (the big one in your thigh) that help you run and jump!

Now, how do we move? Muscles are attached to our bones, and when muscles squeeze and pull, they make our bones wiggle! Think about when you lift a toy car - your arms and hands use muscles to pull it up. Without muscles, we couldn’t walk, talk, or even smile – pretty amazing, right?
keyIdeas: Bones give us shape.
Muscles help us move.
Our skeleton protects important organs.

questions.prompt: Which part of your body helps you stand up straight?
questions.explanation: Bones are strong and give our bodies their shape!

questions.prompt: What does the ribcage protect?
questions.explanation: The ribcage is a shield that keeps your heart and lungs safe inside.

questions.prompt: If muscles pull on bones, what happens?
questions.explanation: Muscles pulling on bones allows us to move around!

mt_iFkd0CTwlA.title: Hieroglyphs and Papyrus
explanation: Know that the ancient Egyptians used a special writing system called hieroglyphs — pictures and symbols that stood for sounds and words — and that they wrote on a paper-like material called papyrus, which was made from a plant that grew along the Nile
You know this idea when you can: Describe hieroglyphs as a writing system that uses pictures and symbols; Explain that papyrus was a material like paper made from a river plant.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe hieroglyphs as a writing system that uses pictures and symbols
Explain that papyrus was a material like paper made from a river plant
Understand that only specially trained people called scribes could read and write hieroglyphs

questions.explanation: Describe hieroglyphs as a writing system that uses pictures and symbols

questions.explanation: Explain that papyrus was a material like paper made from a river plant

questions.prompt: True or false: Understand that only specially trained people called scribes could read and write hieroglyphs
questions.explanation: Understand that only specially trained people called scribes could read and write hieroglyphs

mt_FNSeo9_T2Z.title: Keeping Our Money Safe!
explanation: Let’s talk about money! Money is really special because it helps us get things we want and need, like yummy snacks or toys from the store. When you have money, it's important to take care of it so that it doesn't disappear. Just like we keep our favorite crayons in a box, we can keep our money safe too!

If you lose your money, it’s sad because it’s gone! Imagine losing your favorite building blocks – you wouldn’t be able to build the amazing castle you were planning. Money is the same; if we lose it, we can't use it to buy things anymore, and that makes us feel disappointed. So, keeping track of our money is super important.

We need to treat coins and notes with care! Let’s look at these shiny pennies and dollar bills – they are valuable. We don’t want to drop them on the floor or leave them lying around where someone might accidentally take them.  Keeping them in a safe place shows we respect their value.
keyIdeas: Keep money in a safe place.
Lost money can't be used.
Treat money with care.

questions.prompt: Where is a good place to keep your coins?
choices: B. In a purse or wallet
questions.explanation: Putting money in a purse or wallet helps keep it safe and easy to find.

questions.prompt: What happens if you lose your money?
choices: C. Someone gives it back to you
questions.explanation: If you lose money, you can't use it anymore because it's disappeared.

questions.prompt: What should you do with a dollar bill?
choices: A. Crumple it up and throw it away
B. Put it in your pocket to keep safe
C. Leave it on the table for someone else to find
questions.explanation: Putting a dollar bill in your wallet or money box shows you understand its value.

mt_9IzhGUZ30z.title: Number Words to Twenty!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn about counting and how to say numbers! We use words like ‘one,’ ‘two,’ ‘three,’ all the way up to ‘twenty.’ It's like learning a secret code for numbers – instead of just seeing the little dots or lines, we can say them out loud. Let's practice saying our numbers together, starting with one!

Imagine you have twelve building blocks! You could count them by saying 'one, two, three… twelve!' That’s how we know that ‘twelve’ means 12. We can write it down too – 12 – and see the number and its word form together. This helps us understand what each number represents.

Let's play a game! I will show you some cards with numbers written on them, and you tell me the name of the number. Then we can match the number words to the numbers too! It’s like building a bridge between counting and saying the names of those numbers.
keyIdeas: We use number words to represent amounts.
Each number word tells us how many things there are.
We can read and write number words from one to twenty.

questions.prompt: What does ‘five’ mean?
questions.explanation: Five means there are five things! Can you think of something that has five pieces?

questions.prompt: Which word describes the number 18?
questions.explanation: Let’s check – eighteen means there are eighteen things.  It's a big number!

questions.prompt: Write down the word for the number 14.

mt_zrCyqhngYm.title: Saving Money
explanation: Why people save money; piggy banks and saving jars; setting a savings goal; the idea that not spending now means having more later
You know this idea when you can: Explain why someone might save money instead of spending it straight away; Describe a savings goal they could set and how they would work towards it.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Explain why someone might save money instead of spending it straight away
Describe a savings goal they could set and how they would work towards it

questions.explanation: Explain why someone might save money instead of spending it straight away

mt_4A4RpX-Go9.title: Shape Detectives!
explanation: Let's become shape detectives! Shapes are everywhere around us, and we can learn to recognize them. They have sides and corners, and sometimes they’re even curved like a smile or a ball. We use shapes to build things, draw pictures, and even describe the world!

Think about your favorite building blocks – do you see squares, circles, or triangles? Maybe you have a toy car that's shaped like a rectangle, or a plate at dinner that’s round.  Shapes are part of our everyday lives, helping us understand what we see and how things fit together.

When we look closely at something, we can find shapes hidden inside! For example, if you were building a castle out of blocks, you'd use lots of squares to make the walls. Learning about shapes is like unlocking a secret code for seeing the world in a new way.
keyIdeas: Shapes have sides and corners.
Shapes are found everywhere around us.
We can identify shapes by looking closely.

questions.prompt: Which shape has three sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle is a shape that always has three sides.

questions.prompt: Your building blocks are often shaped like what?
questions.explanation: Blocks are frequently made of squares.

questions.prompt: What do we call it when we look closely at something to find shapes?

mt_2agkUcdah9.title: Shade Detectives!
explanation: Let’s talk about the sun! The sun gives us light and warmth, but sometimes it gets *too* warm. When the sun shines on things like our toys outside or a yummy ice cream cone, it makes them feel hot. We want to find ways to make shady spots so we can play comfortably and keep our treats cool!

Today, we're going to be shade detectives! That means we’ll build something – maybe an umbrella or a little roof – that will block some of the sun’s rays. Think about how an umbrella protects you from the rain; it does the same thing with sunlight! We can use things like cardboard boxes and blankets to make our shade.

Now, let's test if our shade works! We’ll put a thermometer in both a sunny spot and a shady spot. If the thermometer shows that the shaded area is cooler, we know our shade is working because it blocked some of the sun’s heat.
keyIdeas: Sunlight makes things warm.
Shade blocks sunlight.
Testing helps us see if our shade works.

questions.prompt: What does the sun give us?
questions.explanation: The sun gives us light and warmth, which can make things hot.

questions.prompt: Why do we want to build a shade?
choices: B. To block the sun’s rays
questions.explanation: Shade blocks the sun's rays, which can make things too warm.

questions.prompt: What do we need to do to see if our shade works?
choices: A. Build a really tall tower
B. Measure the temperature with a thermometer
C. Sing a song about the sun
questions.explanation: Measuring the temperature helps us see if the shaded area is cooler than the sunny area.

mt_IR8kIjZn_V.title: Tutankhamun's Amazing Tomb!
explanation: Imagine you were a detective, trying to solve a mystery! Archaeologists are like detectives too – they dig in the ground to find old things from long ago. They look for broken tools, pieces of pottery, or even toys that people used hundreds and thousands of years before we were born. These objects give us clues about how people lived when they were alive!

A very famous archaeologist named Howard Carter was searching in Egypt when he made a super exciting discovery in 1922. He found the tomb of a boy king named Tutankhamun! This tomb was hidden deep underground, and it was filled with amazing treasures like golden statues, jewelry, and even furniture – everything was covered in gold!

Because Howard Carter and other archaeologists carefully dug up and studied Tutankhamun’s tomb, we now know a lot about ancient Egypt. We can see what the Egyptians wore, what they ate, and how they believed in gods and goddesses. It's like reading a giant storybook written by the sand itself!
keyIdeas: Archaeologists dig to find old things.
Howard Carter discovered Tutankhamun’s tomb.
Discoveries teach us about ancient civilizations.

questions.prompt: Who found the amazing tomb of Tutankhamun?
questions.explanation: Howard Carter was the archaeologist who made the incredible discovery!

questions.prompt: What was mostly inside Tutankhamun’s tomb?
questions.explanation: The tomb was filled with beautiful and valuable golden objects.

questions.prompt: Why do archaeologists dig up old things?
choices: B. To learn about the past
questions.explanation: Archaeologists dig to find clues and learn about people who lived long ago.

mt_lAvS72LOUO.title: Being Kind and Caring
explanation: Let's talk about how we can make the world a better place, just like you build amazing towers with your LEGO bricks! Kindness means thinking about how other people feel and wanting to help them when they need it. It’s like giving someone a hug when they are sad or offering a toy to share – these small things show that you care about others.

Imagine your friend, Leo, is building a sandcastle on the playground and accidentally knocks it over. He looks really upset and starts to cry! Being kind means noticing how he feels and wanting to help him fix his castle or even build a new one with him. This shows we are caring for someone’s feelings.

Kindness isn't just about big gestures; it can be in the little things too, like saying ‘You did great!’ when someone finishes drawing a picture. Or perhaps you could help your mom put away toys after playtime – that’s also being kind and helpful! Remember, showing kindness makes everyone feel good.
keyIdeas: Kindness is about caring for others.
Helping others feels good!
Small acts of kindness make a big difference.

questions.prompt: If your friend falls down and starts to cry, what should you do?
choices: A. Ignore them and keep playing.
B. Go over to help them up and ask if they’re okay.
questions.explanation: Helping someone who is hurt or sad shows that you care about their feelings.

questions.prompt: Your brother dropped all his crayons on the floor. What could you do?
choices: B. Help him pick them up and put them away.
questions.explanation: Helping with a task without being asked is a kind thing to do.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows you are being kind?
choices: B. Giving someone a hug when they are sad.
C. Not sharing your toys with others.
questions.explanation: Giving a hug shows that you care about how someone feels.

mt_BLQ2_OXPod.title: Volcano Power!
explanation: Imagine a really, really big surprise – that's what a volcano eruption is like! It’s an incredibly powerful event where hot, melted rock called lava shoots out from the side of a mountain. These eruptions are super exciting and can make lots of noise and dust, showing just how much power is hidden inside our Earth.

Volcanoes don't just make a mess; they actually change the land around them! The lava flows down like a river, covering everything in its path – trees, rocks, even buildings. Over time, all that cooled lava builds up and makes new hills and mountains, changing the shape of the whole area around the volcano.

Think about Legos! Before an eruption, you have a neat tower, but after it’s over, the tower might be broken into lots of pieces spread out across the floor. That's similar to what happens with volcanoes – they change the land dramatically, creating new shapes and features where there used to be flat ground.
keyIdeas: Volcano eruptions are powerful.
Lava changes the land around a volcano.
Eruptions create new mountains and hills.

questions.prompt: A volcano eruption is like…
questions.explanation: Volcanoes shoot out hot lava and make a lot of noise!

questions.prompt: What happens to the land around a volcano after an eruption?
choices: A. It stays exactly the same
B. Lava covers it and makes new hills
questions.explanation: The lava flows down and cools, changing the shape of the area.

questions.prompt: Imagine a volcano before an eruption – what does it look like?
questions.explanation: It's a mountain with a hole at the top where the hot rock is hidden!

mt_fLOkq-HfPB.title: Shapes Around Us!
explanation: Let’s learn about shapes! Shapes are everywhere around us, like the blocks you build with or the yummy cookies you eat. They have sides and corners, and they come in all different sizes and forms – circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles. We see them on our toys, in our houses, and even when we’re playing outside!

To help us understand shapes better, let's use things we can see! Imagine you have a toy car – it’s usually shaped like a rectangle or square. Or think about an apple; it’s round, which is a circle. We can draw these shapes too, and that helps us to learn what they look like.

Now, let's use our shapes to solve problems! If you have three squares and add one more square, how many squares do you have in total?  We can count them or draw them to see the answer. Using shapes is a fun way to think about math!
keyIdeas: Shapes are all around us.
We can use objects to help us understand shapes.
Shapes can be used to solve problems.

questions.prompt: I have a circle, a square and a triangle. How many shapes do I have?
questions.explanation: You have three different shapes! Counting them helps us understand the problem.

questions.prompt: Which shape has four sides and four corners?
questions.explanation: A square is a shape with four equal sides and four corners. This makes it easy to recognize!

questions.prompt: Draw a picture of a rectangle and count the sides. How many sides does your rectangle have?
questions.explanation: Count the sides of your drawing to see if you got it right!  This shows us how shapes can be used in real life.

mt_OJVkWvIaM_.title: All Kinds of People!
explanation: Let's talk about all the amazing people around us! Just like you have your favorite toys and foods, people are different in lots of ways. Some people might have brown hair, while others have blonde or black hair – that’s just a difference in appearance. We can see differences in our families too; some families have one mom and one dad, while others have two moms or two dads.

Imagine you're at school and you notice everyone speaks a little differently. Some kids might speak English really well, but another kid might be learning to speak English and uses different words sometimes! That’s okay because people come from all over the world and speak many languages – it makes our community so interesting and full of new things to learn. It's like having a big box of crayons with lots of colors!

It’s important to remember that even though we are all different, we can still be friends! When someone is different from you, try to be curious and ask them about their life – you might discover something new and exciting. Celebrating our differences makes the world a much more wonderful place for everyone.
keyIdeas: People look and act differently.
Differences make communities interesting.
It's good to be curious about people who are different from you.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a way people can be different?
choices: A. They all like the same ice cream
C. They all wear blue shirts
questions.explanation: People come from many countries and speak different languages.

questions.prompt: Someone who speaks a language you don’t understand is…
choices: B. Someone you can learn from
questions.explanation: Learning about other cultures and languages is exciting!

questions.prompt: What should you do if someone seems different from you?
questions.explanation: Being curious and asking questions helps us understand others.

mt_zBUcAPDRPM.title: The Prefix un-
explanation: Spell words using the prefix un- to change meaning; understand that un- creates opposites or reverses actions
You know this idea when you can: Spell words with un- prefix correctly (e.g. 'unhappy', 'unfair', 'undo'); Explain how un- changes the meaning of a word.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Spell words with un- prefix correctly (e.g. 'unhappy', 'unfair', 'undo')
Explain how un- changes the meaning of a word
Create new words by adding un- to known words

questions.explanation: Spell words with un- prefix correctly (e.g. 'unhappy', 'unfair', 'undo')

mt_Jq0MjURrRC.title: Tricky Words!
explanation: Let’s talk about some words that can be a little tricky to spell! These are called ‘tricky words,’ and they don't always follow the rules we learn when reading. Think of your LEGO bricks – you build them together in a certain way, but sometimes you need special pieces that don’t fit into the usual pattern. These tricky words are like those special LEGOs; they have letters that aren’t spelled the way they sound.

Many words we use every day are tricky! For example, when you play with your building blocks at home, you might say ‘said’ to tell someone what you did. Or maybe you ask ‘were’ you able to build a tall tower? These words have letters that aren't spelled the way they sound, so we need to learn them by heart. Learning these words helps us write stories and communicate better!

Let’s practice spelling the days of the week! They are Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Writing them down correctly is a super important skill – you can use them when talking about your weekend plans or learning about time. Remember to focus on each letter carefully as you write!
keyIdeas: Tricky words don't follow typical spelling rules.
We need to learn tricky words by memory.
Practice writing the days of the week.

questions.prompt: Which word is spelled correctly?
questions.explanation: The ‘ie’ makes the ‘a’ say /ee/.

questions.prompt: What day comes after Wednesday?
questions.explanation: Let's count the days of the week!

questions.prompt: Can you write down all seven days of the week?
questions.explanation: Write them out carefully - Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

mt_5n-O41lUgn.title: Connecting reading to experience
explanation: Link what is read or heard to own experiences; draw on background knowledge and vocabulary to support understanding of texts
You know this idea when you can: Make text-to-self connections (e.g. 'This reminds me of when I...'); Use personal experience to understand a character's feelings.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Make text-to-self connections (e.g. 'This reminds me of when I...')
Use personal experience to understand a character's feelings
Relate events in a story to own life to deepen comprehension

questions.explanation: Make text-to-self connections (e.g. 'This reminds me of when I...')

questions.prompt: True or false: Use personal experience to understand a character's feelings
questions.explanation: Use personal experience to understand a character's feelings

questions.explanation: Relate events in a story to own life to deepen comprehension

mt_u0TeRwRII_.title: Dinosaur Sizes
explanation: Compare dinosaur sizes to familiar things — some dinosaurs were as tall as a house, others were as small as a chicken — and understand that dinosaurs came in a huge range of sizes
You know this idea when you can: Compare the size of a large dinosaur to a familiar object like a bus or house; Name a very large dinosaur (e.g. Brachiosaurus) and a small one (e.g. Compsognathus).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Compare the size of a large dinosaur to a familiar object like a bus or house
Name a very large dinosaur (e.g. Brachiosaurus) and a small one (e.g. Compsognathus)
Use words like 'taller than', 'longer than', 'heavier than' when comparing

mt_thsY1ZesaU.title: Shape Detectives!
explanation: Today we’re going to be shape detectives! Shapes are everywhere around us, like the blocks you build with or the wheels on your toy car. We can learn to see different shapes – circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles – in all sorts of things. Let's look closely at our world and try to find them!

When we talk about shapes, it’s important to be specific. A circle is round like a ball or a plate, while a square has four equal sides and four corners just like your building blocks! Triangles have three sides and corners, and rectangles are long with two sets of equal sides, like a book or a door.  Understanding these differences helps us describe what we see.

Now let’s practice! Imagine you're looking at a pizza – it’s round, so that’s a circle! Or think about the window in your house; it's often rectangular. By noticing shapes and naming them, we become shape detectives!
keyIdeas: Shapes are all around us.
Different shapes have different sides and corners.
Naming shapes helps us understand our world.

questions.prompt: What shape is a basketball?
questions.explanation: A basketball is round, so the correct answer is a circle.

questions.prompt: Which of these has four sides and four corners?
questions.explanation: A square always has four equal sides and four corners, making it the right choice.

questions.prompt: Can you name a shape you see in your classroom?
questions.explanation: Think about things like desks, windows or books – what shapes do they have?

mt_HBcvu0UxYe.title: Being a Super Friend!
explanation: Let’s talk about what makes a super friend! A really good friend is someone who is kind – that means they are nice and gentle to others. They also try to make you feel happy and smile, because everyone deserves to have a friend who cares about them. Think about your favorite teddy bear; it's always there to be friendly and comforting.

Being a good friend also means being honest and reliable. Honest friends tell the truth, even when it’s hard, and they keep their promises – like saying you’ll play with someone after school! Reliable friends are people you can count on, just like your mom or dad always helps you find something.

Finally, good friends include others! They don't leave anyone out of games or activities.  They share their toys and ideas, making sure everyone feels part of the fun. Imagine building a tower with blocks – it’s much more fun when you invite your friend to help!
keyIdeas: Kindness
Honesty
Inclusion

questions.prompt: Which of these is something a good friend does?
choices: A. Says mean things to others
B. Shares their toys with you
questions.explanation: Sharing shows kindness and caring!

questions.prompt: If your friend says they’ll play with you, but then doesn't show up, what is that?
choices: C. They are telling the truth
questions.explanation: Reliable friends always keep their promises.

questions.prompt: What can you do to be a good friend?
questions.explanation: Think about ways you can be kind, honest, and include others in your playtime!

mt_JednrdYqpt.title: Real Dinosaurs vs. Make-Believe!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs and you have a dinosaur! Dinosaurs are super cool animals that lived on Earth a *really* long time ago – even longer than your grandparents! Scientists who study dinosaurs, called paleontologists, dig up their bones to learn about them. But sometimes, we see pictures of dinosaurs in movies or cartoons, and it can be tricky to tell what's real and what isn’t.

Let’s talk about some animals that look like dinosaurs but aren’t! Pterosaurs were flying reptiles – they had wings just like a bat, so they could soar through the sky. They weren't actually dinosaurs because they didn’t have the same kind of bones as true dinosaurs.  Think of it like this: your teddy bear looks like a bear, but it isn't a real one!

Another animal that lived long ago was a plesiosaur! Plesiosaurs were giant swimming reptiles that lived in the ocean, just like a whale. They had long necks and flippers to help them swim around.  Even though they might look like dinosaurs from far away, they weren’t actually part of the dinosaur family – they belonged to a different group of prehistoric creatures.
keyIdeas: Pterosaurs flew, but weren't dinosaurs.
Plesiosaurs swam, but weren't dinosaurs.
Movie dinosaurs often look different from real ones.

questions.prompt: Which animal could fly?
questions.explanation: Pterosaurs had wings and were able to fly through the air.

questions.prompt: Where did plesiosaurs live?
questions.explanation: Plesiosaurs were marine reptiles, meaning they lived in the sea.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a movie dinosaur and NOT a real dinosaur?
choices: C) A dinosaur from Jurassic Park
questions.explanation: The dinosaurs in movies are often made up or changed to look more exciting!

mt_nYU6x2E2T8.title: Sharing Our Stories!
explanation: Let's talk about sharing our amazing stories! When you write something, like drawing a picture or writing a sentence about your favorite toy car – let’s say it’s a red racecar – you’ve created something special. It’s important to share that story with others so they can hear what you’ve thought about and enjoy your words just as much as you do.

Sharing our stories involves reading them aloud clearly. Imagine you're telling a friend about your red racecar, but you mumble!  To make sure everyone understands, we need to use our voices to speak each word carefully and with feeling – like when you’re excited to tell someone something cool. This shows that you are proud of what you wrote.

Now, sometimes we can even share our stories in a special way using computers or tablets! Typing our words helps us make them look neat and tidy for everyone to read, and some programs let us ‘publish’ our writing so others can see it online.  It's like sending your story out into the world!
keyIdeas: Read clearly to share your ideas.
Sharing makes our writing special.
Computers help us publish our stories.

questions.prompt: When you share your story, what are you doing?
choices: C. Drawing a picture of it
questions.explanation: Sharing means letting others hear and understand what you wrote.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to read your writing with feeling?
choices: A. It makes the words sound silly
B. It helps people understand how you feel about it
questions.explanation: Using expression shows you are proud of what you wrote.

questions.prompt: What can you use to type your story and share it with others?
questions.explanation: Typing allows you to create a digital copy of your writing.

Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Include correct spelling of taught exception words in dictation
Apply sentence punctuation in dictated writing

questions.explanation: Write a dictated sentence using phonically plausible spellings

questions.explanation: Include correct spelling of taught exception words in dictation

questions.explanation: Apply sentence punctuation in dictated writing

mt_uG2mjHFOlO.title: Spotting mathematical patterns
explanation: Notice simple patterns and structures: spot that changing order doesn't change the total, and recognise how numbers relate to each other
You know this idea when you can: Notice that 3 + 2 gives the same answer as 2 + 3 (early commutativity); Recognise that teen numbers are 'ten and some more' (e.g. 14 is 10 and 4).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Notice that 3 + 2 gives the same answer as 2 + 3 (early commutativity)
Recognise that teen numbers are 'ten and some more' (e.g. 14 is 10 and 4)
Spot a pattern in a sequence of objects or numbers and predict what comes next

questions.explanation: Notice that 3 + 2 gives the same answer as 2 + 3 (early commutativity)

questions.explanation: Recognise that teen numbers are 'ten and some more' (e.g. 14 is 10 and 4)

mt_URezjbU-6f.title: Let's Talk About Weather!
explanation: Have you ever looked outside and wondered if it will rain, snow, or be sunny tomorrow? Weather forecasting is like a special guess about what the weather will be like! Grown-ups use tools and information to try and predict the weather, so we can plan our days and know how to stay safe. It’s important because sometimes the weather changes quickly!

When there's a big storm coming, like a thunderstorm with lots of lightning, people prepare by staying inside and away from windows. We might also gather supplies like water and snacks just in case!  A flood is when too much rain falls and makes the rivers swell; we need to stay safe and listen to grown-ups about warnings. Preparing for weather helps us feel more secure.

Sometimes, it gets really hot outside – that's called a heat wave! We can prepare by drinking lots of water, wearing light clothes, and staying in cool places like air-conditioned rooms.  It’s important to listen to the weather forecast so we know when to take extra care and stay safe from dangerous weather.
keyIdeas: Weather forecasts help us plan.
We prepare for severe weather to be safe.
Listening to the weather is important.

questions.prompt: Why do we look at a weather forecast?
choices: A. To see if it’s going to rain.
C. To guess what color the sky will be.
questions.explanation: The weather forecast tells us about what the weather *will* be, so we can plan our day!

questions.prompt: What is one thing you can do to get ready for a really hot day?
questions.explanation: Drinking water helps keep us cool when it’s very hot!

questions.prompt: If you hear a warning about a big storm, what should you do?
choices: A. Run outside to see the lightning.
B. Stay inside with your family and listen for more information.
questions.explanation: It’s important to stay safe during a storm by listening to grown-ups.

explanation: Use mathematical words carefully when counting, comparing, and describing shapes and positions

You know this idea when you can: Use 'more than', 'fewer than', and 'the same as' correctly when comparing groups; Name shapes correctly and describe their features using words like 'sides' and 'corners'.

Name shapes correctly and describe their features using words like 'sides' and 'corners'
Use positional words (above, below, next to) precisely to describe where objects are
