mt_IM1G_7QzTa.title: Shapes All Around Us!
explanation: Let’s look around and see if we can find shapes! Shapes are everywhere – they're the outlines of things. We see circles like a yummy pizza, squares like building blocks, and triangles like the roof of our house. Recognizing shapes helps us understand the world better!

Imagine you have a toy car that’s shaped like a rectangle. That means it has two long sides and two short sides – just like a box! We can draw a picture of the car to show all its straight lines and corners, which are important parts of making up a shape.  Shapes help us describe what we see!

Now let’s try something fun! If you have some building blocks, you could make a tower with lots of different shapes stacked on top of each other. You can count how many blocks of each shape you used to build your tower – that's using maths and shapes together!
keyIdeas: Shapes are everywhere.
Shapes have straight sides and corners.
We can draw shapes to help us understand them.

questions.prompt: What shape is a ball?
questions.explanation: A ball is round, and a circle is the shape with no straight sides.

questions.prompt: You have 3 blocks and your friend gives you 2 more. How many blocks do you have now?
questions.explanation: This is an addition problem! 3 + 2 = 5.

questions.prompt: Which shape has three sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle always has three straight sides.

mt_0u3QNroZ34.title: Rainforest Adventures!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – you need a strong base to build tall! Rainforests are like that strong base for many plants and animals. They’re found in special places all around the world, mostly near the middle of our planet, which we call the Equator.

The Equator is an imaginary line that circles the Earth, and rainforests grow where it's super warm and wet almost all year long. Think about a big bowl of ice cream – it’s melting because it’s hot! That warmth and rain help plants like giant trees and colorful flowers to grow really tall.

You can find rainforests in South America, like the Amazon, where you might see monkeys and parrots! They also exist in Africa (the Congo) and Asia (Southeast Asia). Even though Antarctica is super cold, there are still small rainforests there too – it’s amazing!
keyIdeas: Rainforests are near the Equator.
They have lots of warm and wet weather.
You can find them in South America, Africa, and Asia.

questions.prompt: Which part of the world has big rainforests like the Amazon?
questions.explanation: Rainforests are mostly found in South America, especially in a place called the Amazon!

questions.prompt: Why do rainforests grow near the Equator?
choices: B. It’s hot and wet all year
questions.explanation: The warm, wet weather near the Equator helps plants grow big and strong!

questions.prompt: Name one continent where you can find a rainforest.
questions.explanation: Think about places like Africa or South America – which continent did you choose?

mt_aS-Gdh-MHx.title: Life Changes Are Normal!
explanation: Sometimes, things in our lives change! Just like when you get a new toy – maybe a sparkly race car or a cuddly stuffed animal – it’s exciting and different. These changes can make us feel lots of things: happy, excited, surprised, or even a little bit worried. It's okay to have these feelings because everyone feels them when something new happens.

Let’s think about some other times things change. Maybe you started kindergarten – that was a big change! Or perhaps your family got a new baby brother or sister. Even moving to a new house can feel like a big change, with new rooms and maybe even new neighbors. Remember, changes happen all the time, and they’re usually okay.

When something changes, it's important to know you aren't alone. Talking about how you feel is a super helpful thing to do. You could talk to your mom, dad, grandma, or anyone you trust. Keeping things like routines – like brushing your teeth every morning – can also help you feel more comfortable when things are different.
keyIdeas: Change happens to everyone.
It’s okay to have different feelings about change.
Talking about your feelings helps.

questions.prompt: You are starting a new school. How might you feel?
questions.explanation: It's okay to feel sad or worried when you start somewhere new! It’s normal to miss your old friends.

questions.prompt: If a change makes you feel upset, who could you talk to?
choices: B) Your favorite teacher or parent
questions.explanation: Talking to someone you trust can help you understand and cope with your feelings.

questions.prompt: What is one thing that helps you feel calm when things are changing?
questions.explanation: Think about something familiar, like a favorite toy or activity.  Doing this can bring comfort.

mt_RFeVlw0QvX.title: Counting Patterns!
explanation: Let's talk about how we count! Sometimes, when we count, we can find a special pattern that helps us do it faster. Imagine you have a box of LEGO bricks – let’s say you have 10 red ones, 10 blue ones, and 10 yellow ones. You could count them one by one, but there's a quicker way!

One way to count quickly is to skip counting!  If we count by twos, we say ‘2, 4, 6, 8, 10…’. See how it keeps going? This is because every other number is even. We can use this pattern to count things like the windows in our house or the stars in the sky – it’s much faster than counting each one individually!

Another super cool thing we can notice is that when we add 1 to any number, we always get the next number! If you have 5 blocks and you add 1 more, you now have 6.  We can use this pattern on a hundred chart by adding 10 repeatedly – it always takes us down one row, helping us predict where the next number will be.
keyIdeas: Skip counting helps count faster.
Adding 1 makes numbers go in order.
Patterns repeat to help us predict.

questions.prompt: If you count by twos, what number comes after 8?
questions.explanation: Counting by twos means adding two each time. 8 + 2 = 10.

questions.prompt: What happens when you add 1 to the number 15?
questions.explanation: Adding 1 to 15 makes it 16. This is because each number comes after the last.

questions.prompt: Imagine a hundred chart. If you add +10, what will happen?
choices: C: You’ll move down one row
questions.explanation: Adding 10 to any number on the hundred chart always moves us down one row because we are adding 10 consecutive numbers.

mt_oiqsIP97V3.title: Astronauts: Space Explorers!
explanation: Have you ever looked up at the stars and wished you could visit them? Astronauts are special people who get to do just that! They travel in big rockets, like giant space cars, to go far, far away into outer space. These brave explorers wear special suits to protect them from the cold and the lack of air.

A really amazing thing is that humans have actually walked on the Moon! This happened during something called the Apollo missions – long ago when scientists and astronauts were very excited about exploring our planet's neighbor. They left footprints in the moon dust, which is a super cool adventure for anyone to imagine!

Today, there’s even a place where astronauts live and work: it’s called the International Space Station! It’s like a giant spaceship that circles around Earth, and astronauts spend their time doing experiments and learning about space. They are constantly working to help us understand more about our universe.
keyIdeas: Astronauts travel in rockets.
People have walked on the Moon.
The International Space Station is where astronauts live.

questions.prompt: What does an astronaut use to get into space?
questions.explanation: Astronauts travel in rockets to go to outer space!

questions.prompt: Which famous mission let people walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: The Apollo missions were when humans first walked on the moon.

questions.prompt: Where do astronauts live and work in space?
questions.explanation: The International Space Station is a place where astronauts live while exploring space.

mt_PhIZNl2230.title: Theseus and the Minotaur
explanation: Long ago, in a land called Crete, there was a giant maze called a labyrinth. This labyrinth was super tricky – it twisted and turned with walls so high you couldn’t see the end! Inside lived a scary monster named the Minotaur. The Minotaur looked like a man but had the head of a bull, and he loved to gobble up people who wandered inside.

The ancient Greeks told stories like this one about Theseus, a brave young hero. Every nine years, Athens had to send young men and women into the labyrinth to be eaten by the Minotaur. The Greek king, King Aegeus, was very worried because these people were disappearing, and he wanted to stop it!

Theseus sailed to Crete with his father and a magical sword. He bravely entered the labyrinth, used his cleverness to find the Minotaur, and fought him until he defeated the monster! The Greeks told this story to show how brave heroes could overcome scary monsters and teach us about courage and problem-solving.
keyIdeas: Heroes were brave.
Myths explained the world.
Stories taught important lessons.

questions.prompt: Who fought the Minotaur?
questions.explanation: Theseus was the hero who entered the labyrinth and defeated the monster.

questions.prompt: What kind of creature lived in the labyrinth?
questions.explanation: The Minotaur was a monster with the head of a bull.

questions.prompt: Why did the Greeks tell stories like this one?
choices: B. To explain things and teach lessons
C. Because they wanted to make people scared
questions.explanation: The Greek myths helped them understand the world around them.

mt_SrmHaJXKrX.title: Ice & Snow!
explanation: Have you ever built a snowman or made a snow angel? That’s because water can change into something very different – ice and snow! When water gets really, really cold, it turns solid. Just like when your juice freezes in the freezer, turning from liquid to a hard block of ice.

Icebergs are amazing giant blocks of ice floating in the ocean. They're much bigger than you might think! Most of an iceberg is hidden underwater, like a secret treasure waiting to be discovered beneath the waves. This happens because ice is very heavy and pushes water away.
You know how your toys float on water? That’s because ice floats too! Frozen water is lighter than liquid water, so it doesn't sink. It's like a magical trick – something solid can still float!
keyIdeas: Water changes states (solid, liquid, gas).
Icebergs are huge and mostly hidden underwater.
Ice floats because it’s less dense than liquid water.

questions.prompt: What happens to water when it gets really cold?
choices: A. It turns into a gas
questions.explanation: Ice and snow are frozen water, meaning it has turned from liquid to solid.

questions.prompt: What is mostly hidden under an iceberg?
questions.explanation: Most of the iceberg's size is hidden beneath the ocean’s surface.

questions.prompt: Why does ice float on water?
choices: A. Ice is heavier than water
B. Ice is lighter than water
questions.explanation: Frozen water is less dense, which means it's lighter and therefore floats.

mt_asRwlPZXC3.title: Jobs People Do!
explanation: Let’s talk about all the amazing jobs people do! Some people build houses with hammers and nails, that's called a builder. Others drive big trucks to deliver food to our grocery stores – those are truck drivers! And there are teachers who help us learn new things at school, or firefighters who bravely protect our homes from danger.

When someone does a job, they get paid money for it. It’s like when you help your mom with chores around the house; she might give you an allowance as a thank you! People work hard to earn money so they can buy things they need and want, like toys or yummy snacks.  It's important that people are rewarded fairly for their efforts.

Think about what *you* enjoy doing – maybe you love animals! If you loved animals, you could become a veterinarian, which is a doctor for pets. Or perhaps you really like to draw pictures; you could be an artist and sell your artwork.  Lots of jobs come from things we’re interested in!
keyIdeas: People do many different kinds of work.
We get paid money for the work we do.
Our interests can help us think about what kind of job we might like.

questions.prompt: What does a firefighter do?
choices: B. Protects our homes from fire
questions.explanation: Firefighters use their skills and bravery to keep us safe!

questions.prompt: Why do people get paid for their jobs?
questions.explanation: People work hard, and companies or individuals pay them to show appreciation for their efforts.

questions.prompt: If you love animals, what job could you have?
questions.explanation: Veterinarians care for sick and injured animals!

mt_x8TshvbbQT.title: Reading and writing numbers to 100
explanation: Read and write numerals from 0 to 100
You know this idea when you can: Read two-digit numerals up to 100 correctly; Write any number 0–100 as a numeral from dictation.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Read two-digit numerals up to 100 correctly
Write any number 0–100 as a numeral from dictation

questions.prompt: True or false: Read two-digit numerals up to 100 correctly
questions.explanation: Read two-digit numerals up to 100 correctly

questions.prompt: True or false: Write any number 0–100 as a numeral from dictation
questions.explanation: Write any number 0–100 as a numeral from dictation

questions.prompt: Can you read and write any number up to 100 — both as a numeral and by saying what it means?
questions.answer: Answers will vary — check against the evidence criteria above.
questions.explanation: Use the mastery evidence to judge the response.

mt_rkrG2w7WXI.title: Why Do We Have Rules?
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs at home! If everyone just grabbed blocks and started stacking them wherever they wanted, it would be a big mess, right? That's why we have rules – like 'take turns' or 'don't build on top of someone else's creation.' Rules help us do things together in a way that’s kind and doesn’t cause problems. They are like instructions for how to play nicely and share.

Rules aren’t just about stopping bad behavior; they’re also about making sure everyone feels safe and happy. When we have rules at school, like 'raise your hand before speaking,' it helps us listen to each other and learn without shouting or interrupting. These agreements help groups of people work together smoothly and fairly, so everyone has a chance to participate.

Think about the playground! If there were no rules about sharing the swings or taking turns on the slide, lots of kids would be sad because they wouldn’t get a turn. Rules make sure that everyone gets a fair shot at playing and having fun – it's all about being responsible and thinking about how our actions affect others.
keyIdeas: Rules help us share.
Rules keep us safe.
Rules make things fair.

questions.prompt: Why do we have rules when playing with toys?
choices: B. To play nicely and take turns.
C. To build the tallest tower.
questions.explanation: Rules help us share and play together without causing problems.

questions.prompt: If there were no rules at school, what might happen?
choices: A. Everyone would learn a lot.
B. There would be lots of shouting and people wouldn't listen.
C. Teachers would have an easy time.
questions.explanation: Rules help us listen to each other and learn without interruptions.

questions.prompt: What is one reason why rules are important?
questions.explanation: Rules make sure everyone feels safe, happy, and has a chance to participate.

mt_FwI7q7DSIx.title: Taking Turns and Sharing!
explanation: Imagine you're building a really cool tower with your blocks – it’s so exciting to add each new block, right? But what happens if only one person is building the whole time? That wouldn’t be very fair, would it? Taking turns means that everyone gets a chance to play and build, like when we take turns going down the slide at the playground.

Sharing is also super important! Let's say you have a yummy cookie – sharing means letting someone else have a bite too. When we share, we make our friends happy and it’s more fun for everyone because we can all enjoy things together. It’s like when you build with LEGOs; if you only had one set, you couldn’t build as many amazing things!

Taking turns and sharing makes group activities so much better! When everyone gets a chance to play or do something, it means no one feels left out and everyone has fun. It's important to remember that waiting for your turn shows kindness and helps us all be good friends – just like when we wait our turn in line at school.
keyIdeas: Take turns fairly.
Sharing makes others happy.
Cooperation is fun!

questions.prompt: You are playing with a friend and it’s your turn to roll the dice. What should you do?
choices: A. Grab the dice before your friend rolls it.
B. Let your friend roll the dice first.
C. Shout out what number you want to roll.
questions.explanation: Taking turns means letting others go first.

questions.prompt: Your sister wants to play with your favorite toy car. What is a good way to handle this situation?
choices: A. Keep the car and tell her she can't play.
B. Let her play with it for a little while, then give it back.
C. Throw the car away because you don’t want to share.
questions.explanation: Sharing means letting others use your things.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to take turns in a game?
choices: A. It makes the game longer.
B. It helps everyone have fun and feel included.
C. It’s okay to always go first.
questions.explanation: Taking turns ensures everyone gets a chance to play and enjoy themselves.

mt_PdYlsA33jB.title: Asking for Help
explanation: Ask for help when they need it — from a friend, teacher, or family member — and understand that asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness
You know this idea when you can: Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly; Identify who to go to for different kinds of help (academic, emotional, practical).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly
Identify who to go to for different kinds of help (academic, emotional, practical)
Explain why asking for help is sensible rather than something to be embarrassed about

questions.prompt: True or false: Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly
questions.explanation: Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly

questions.prompt: True or false: Identify who to go to for different kinds of help (academic, emotional, practical)
questions.explanation: Identify who to go to for different kinds of help (academic, emotional, practical)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain why asking for help is sensible rather than something to be embarrassed about
questions.explanation: Explain why asking for help is sensible rather than something to be embarrassed about

mt_70Ys4i1AB1.title: Super Word Builders: Compound Words!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how some words are made up of two smaller words joined together? These special words are called compound words, and they’re like building blocks for our language! When we make a compound word, we take two separate words and put them side by side to create one brand new word with a whole new meaning. Let's look at an example – think about your favorite teddy bear; it might be sitting on a ‘pillow’!

To find the pieces of a compound word, you need to spot the two smaller words that make it up. For instance, if we see the word ‘football’, we can break it down into ‘foot’ and ‘ball’. Each of these smaller words is called a base word, and when we put them together, they create something bigger and more interesting! This helps us understand how words are built.

Now you try! Let's say we see the word ‘rainbow’. Can you tell me what two words it’s made from? It’s ‘rain’ and ‘bow’!  Practicing with compound words is like learning a secret code – once you know how to decode them, you can read and understand lots of new words. You can even make your own compound words by combining different word pairs!
keyIdeas: Compound words are made from two smaller words.
You can find the two parts of a compound word.
Putting words together makes new words.

questions.prompt: What two words make up the word ‘flower’?
questions.explanation: The word 'flower' is made from the base words 'flow' and 'er'.

questions.prompt: If you see the word ‘bookcase’, what are the two smaller words?
questions.explanation: ‘Bookcase’ is made from the base words 'book' and 'case'.

questions.prompt: Can you make a compound word using the words ‘sun’ and ‘flower’?
questions.explanation: The answer would be ‘sunflower’.

mt_20WfHhnL39.title: Let's Solve Problems with Tools!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn how to solve math problems in a super fun way! Sometimes, numbers can be tricky to figure out all by ourselves. But guess what? We can use special tools to help us understand them better – like blocks, counters, or even our fingers!

Imagine you have 3 toy cars and your friend gives you 2 more. You want to know how many cars you have in total! Instead of just counting on your fingers, we could use cubes. We can put one cube for each car, and then add the other cubes together – that’s a great way to see the answer!

Choosing the right tool is important because it makes solving problems easier. If we were using our fingers, it might be hard to keep track of all the numbers. But with cubes, we can easily move them around and make it clear how many we have – that’s why we chose them!
keyIdeas: Tools help us understand math.
Choosing a tool makes solving easier.
We can use blocks, counters, or fingers.

questions.prompt: You have 4 apples and your mom gives you 3 more. Which tool could you use to help you find out how many apples you have?
choices: B) Use a block to represent each apple
C) Draw a picture of the apples
questions.explanation: Using blocks is a great way to see and add the numbers together!

questions.prompt: You want to compare 5 counters with 2 counters. Which tool would help you see which group has more?
questions.explanation: A ten frame helps us easily see how many objects we have.

questions.prompt: Why might someone choose to use their fingers instead of cubes when solving a problem?
questions.explanation: Because it's easier to move and rearrange the fingers to help them count!

mt__CMXZiPfTV.title: Reading Words with Special Endings!
explanation: Let’s talk about how we read words! Sometimes, words change a little bit when we add something to the end. It's like adding sprinkles to your ice cream – it changes the flavor just a tiny bit! These special endings are called suffixes and prefixes, and they help us understand what the word means.

Think about your favorite toy car. When you say 'car', that’s one thing. But if you say ‘cars’, we know you're talking about more than one! The ‘s’ at the end is a special ending called a suffix, and it tells us there are multiple cars.  Adding endings like -ing or -ed changes the word too, just like adding different toppings to your pizza!

We can learn to read these words by looking for the root word first – that's the main part of the word before the ending. For example, if we see ‘jumping’, we know ‘jump’ is the root and ‘-ing’ tells us it’s what someone or something is doing.  It’s like finding the base ingredient in a recipe and then adding the special flavors!
keyIdeas: Suffixes change words.
Prefixes also change words.
Finding the root word helps us read.

questions.prompt: Which ending changes ‘jump’ to ‘jumping’?
questions.explanation: The '-ing' suffix tells us what someone or something is doing.

questions.prompt: What is the root word in ‘unhappy’?
questions.explanation: ‘Unhappy’ comes from the root word ‘happy’.

questions.prompt: If you see the word ‘faster’, what does the ‘-er’ ending tell you?
choices: B. It describes how something moves.
C. It is a new color.
questions.explanation: The '-er' suffix means 'more'.

mt_bKV2JYNwf7.title: The Very First Olympics!
explanation: Long, long ago, before we had TVs or computers, there were amazing games called the Olympic Games! They started in a special place in Greece called Olympia. People built a huge temple to Zeus, who was like the king of the gods, and they held these games as a way to honor him and show off their strength and skill.

Imagine you’re playing with your building blocks – that's kind of like what happened at the Olympics! Athletes would compete in events like running, wrestling, or even chariot racing. It was important to be brave and strong to win, just like when you build a tall tower and want it to stand up!

Today, we watch the Olympic Games on TV, and they’re still exciting! But those games started thousands of years ago in ancient Greece. The modern Olympics got their idea from these very first games – so they're connected by history!
keyIdeas: The first Olympics were held in ancient Greece.
People competed in sports like running and wrestling.
Today’s Olympics are inspired by the ancient Greek Games.

questions.prompt: Where did the very first Olympic Games take place?
questions.explanation: The ancient Olympics were held in a city called Olympia, which was located in Greece.

questions.prompt: Which of these was an event that happened at the ancient Olympic Games?
questions.explanation: Athletes competed in running races as part of the ancient Olympics.

questions.prompt: How are today’s Olympic Games similar to the first ones?
choices: A. They were held in Rome
B. They got their ideas from the ancient Greek games
C. They only had one sport
questions.explanation: The modern Olympics took inspiration and started with the ancient Greek games.

mt_nwSWPTENmv.title: Let's Explore Our Senses!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn about our amazing five senses! These are how we experience the world around us – it’s like having secret superpowers! There are five main ways we can sense things: sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell. Each of these uses a special part of our body to help us understand what's happening.

Let’s talk about each one. Sight is how we see with our eyes – like when you look at your favorite toy car or the bright colors in a rainbow! Hearing lets us listen with our ears, just like when you hear music or someone talking. Touch uses our skin to feel things like smooth blocks or warm water, and it helps us know if something is hot or cold.

Taste uses our tongue to notice yummy flavors like sweet cookies or sour lemons, and smell uses our nose to pick up amazing scents – maybe the smell of freshly baked bread or a flower in your garden. Each sense gives us important information about everything we see, hear, feel, taste, and smell!
keyIdeas: We have five senses: sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell.
Each sense uses a different part of our body to work.
Our senses help us understand the world around us.

questions.prompt: Which part of your body helps you see?
questions.explanation: Your eyes are special because they can detect light, which is how we see!

questions.prompt: What does your tongue help you do?
questions.explanation: Your tongue helps you taste different flavors like sweet or sour.

questions.prompt: If you touch a cold ice cube, what does your skin tell you?
questions.explanation: Your skin can feel the cold temperature of the ice cube!

mt_ByXgbTld6R.title: The Moon's Amazing Shapes!
explanation: Have you ever looked up at the night sky and seen the moon? The moon isn’t always a big, bright circle like it is during a full moon! It actually changes its shape over about a month – we call these shapes ‘phases.’ Think of your favorite building blocks; sometimes you build a tall tower, other times you make a flat rectangle. The moon does something similar, changing its appearance as it circles around our planet Earth.

The moon doesn't *really* change shape, but it looks like it does because the sunlight shines on different parts of it as it goes around us. When we see the full moon, that means we’re seeing all the sunlit part of the moon at once! Sometimes you might even see a tiny sliver of light – that's called a crescent moon. It’s like when you cut a cookie into a small piece!

The moon goes through a special cycle and always returns to the same shapes again and again. This means it starts as a new moon (when we can't see it at all), then grows bigger, becomes half-moon, and finally gets full again before shrinking back down. It’s like a beautiful, repeating story in the sky!
keyIdeas: The Moon changes shape.
It goes through a cycle of phases.
These shapes repeat over time.

questions.prompt: What does the moon look like when we can’t see it at all?
questions.explanation: The new moon is dark because the sunlight isn't shining on it.

questions.prompt: Which shape of the moon looks like a big, bright circle?
questions.explanation: When the whole face of the moon is lit by the sun, we see a full moon.

questions.prompt: The moon’s shapes change and then they happen again. What word describes this?
questions.explanation: The moon's phases repeat in a pattern.

mt_jvJ35MmCvK.title: Real-World Robots
explanation: Have you ever seen a robot in a movie, like one that can talk or fight? Real robots aren’t quite like those! A robot is really just a machine – it's something made by people – that does things for us. These machines work because someone tells them exactly what to do, step-by-step.

There are lots of different kinds of robots working all around us. For example, there are robots in factories that help build cars! Robot vacuum cleaners like Roomba go around and clean your floors automatically, following a path they’ve been programmed to take. And even doctors use robot arms for very precise surgery – it's amazing!

It’s important to know that robots aren’t alive like you or me. They don’t have feelings or brains of their own. Instead, they do what people tell them through instructions written in computer code. This means they just follow directions and can’t think for themselves – it's all about the programming!
keyIdeas: Robots are machines.
Robots follow instructions.
Robots aren't alive.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a real robot that cleans floors?
questions.explanation: Roomba is a special robot designed to clean floors.

questions.prompt: What does a robot do in a factory?
questions.explanation: Robots in factories help build things like cars by doing repetitive tasks.

questions.prompt: Why don’t robots have feelings?
choices: A. They are made of metal
questions.explanation: Robots only do what people tell them to do; they don't have their own thoughts or emotions.

mt_BVpB5wyijZ.title: Spotting Constellations
explanation: Recognise a few star patterns (constellations) in the night sky, starting with the Big Dipper (the Plough), and understand that the North Star (Polaris) can be found using the Big Dipper
You know this idea when you can: Identify or draw the Big Dipper (the Plough) as a pattern of seven stars; Explain that the two end stars of the Big Dipper's bowl point towards the North Star.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Identify or draw the Big Dipper (the Plough) as a pattern of seven stars
Explain that the two end stars of the Big Dipper's bowl point towards the North Star
State that constellations are patterns of stars that people have named

questions.prompt: True or false: Identify or draw the Big Dipper (the Plough) as a pattern of seven stars

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that the two end stars of the Big Dipper's bowl point towards the North Star
questions.explanation: Explain that the two end stars of the Big Dipper's bowl point towards the North Star

questions.prompt: True or false: State that constellations are patterns of stars that people have named
questions.explanation: State that constellations are patterns of stars that people have named

mt_Wr6DDgr_kH.title: Caterpillar to butterfly
explanation: Caterpillar to butterfly: the life cycle of a butterfly as an observable transformation. Egg → caterpillar → chrysalis → butterfly. The idea that one creature can change its whole form. Classroom butterfly kits, The Very Hungry Caterpillar.

You know this idea when you can: Describe the four stages of a butterfly's life in order: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly; Explain that the caterpillar and the butterfly are the same creature at different stages of life.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe the four stages of a butterfly's life in order: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly
Explain that the caterpillar and the butterfly are the same creature at different stages of life
Retell or draw the transformation from caterpillar to butterfly after reading a story or watching it happen

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the four stages of a butterfly's life in order: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly
questions.explanation: Describe the four stages of a butterfly's life in order: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that the caterpillar and the butterfly are the same creature at different stages of life
questions.explanation: Explain that the caterpillar and the butterfly are the same creature at different stages of life

questions.prompt: True or false: Retell or draw the transformation from caterpillar to butterfly after reading a story or watching it happen
questions.explanation: Retell or draw the transformation from caterpillar to butterfly after reading a story or watching it happen

mt_gZIo5oiBMt.title: Reading Detectives!
explanation: Today, we’re going to be reading detectives! We get to look at different kinds of books and figure out what kind of story they are telling. Just like a detective looks for clues, we'll find clues in the book to see if it's a story, a poem, or something that teaches us facts. Let’s start with a really fun one – think about your favorite toy! Do you have a toy box full of stories?

There are lots of different ways books can tell us things. A *storybook* is like a pretend adventure filled with characters and exciting events, like the story of Cinderella or Harry Potter. A *poem* uses words to create pictures in our minds and often has a special rhythm and rhyme. Then there’s *non-fiction*, which means it's about real things – like a book about dinosaurs or how to build a sandcastle.

Every book needs helpers! The *author* is the person who writes all the words in the story, just like you write your name. The *illustrator* is the artist who draws all the pictures that help us understand and enjoy the story even more. They work together to make a wonderful book for us to read!
keyIdeas: Stories have characters and events.
Poems use words to create images.
Authors write the words, illustrators draw the pictures.

questions.prompt: Which of these is most like a pretend adventure?
questions.explanation: A storybook tells us made-up stories with characters.

questions.prompt: Who writes the words in a book?
questions.explanation: The author is the person who chooses and writes the words.

questions.prompt: If you see a picture of a rainbow in a book, who probably drew it?
questions.explanation: Illustrators draw pictures to help us understand the story.

mt_e6PP4ip39V.title: Plants and Animals at Home
explanation: Let’s talk about homes! Just like you have a special place you love to live, plants and animals also need places called habitats. A habitat is where an animal or plant lives and finds everything it needs to survive – things like food, water, and shelter. Think about your favorite teddy bear; it has a cozy bed to sleep in, just like a habitat gives a home to a bunny or a tree!

Every living thing has special needs that depend on its habitat. For example, a fish lives in the water because it needs water to swim and breathe! A cactus lives in the desert because it needs dry places to store water and protect itself from the hot sun. These habitats are built with things like plants, rocks, and soil – all of which help them survive.

Now, what if we put a fish in the desert? It wouldn’t be happy or able to live there! That's because deserts don't have water, and fish need water to survive. This shows us that habitats are super important; they give animals and plants everything they need to thrive.
keyIdeas: Habitats provide what living things need.
Animals and plants have different needs depending on their habitat.
A mismatch between an organism and its habitat can cause problems.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a habitat?
questions.explanation: A forest provides food, water, and shelter for animals like deer and squirrels.

questions.prompt: Why do fish live in the water?
choices: B: They need water to breathe and swim
questions.explanation: Fish have gills that take oxygen from the water, so they must be near it.

questions.prompt: What might happen if a bird was put in a desert?
choices: A: It would build a nest
questions.explanation: Deserts are very hot and dry, and birds need water and shade to stay cool.

mt_deexfCHU9m.title: Helping Our Planet!
explanation: Imagine our playground – it’s a wonderful place to play with your friends and build sandcastles! But sometimes, people leave things behind like wrappers from their snacks or plastic toys. This is called litter, and it makes the playground look messy and can hurt animals who might accidentally eat it. We want to keep our planet healthy too!

Humans do lots of things that can make a difference to nature, not always in a good way. For example, when factories make things, they sometimes release smoke into the air, which is pollution. Pollution makes it hard for plants and animals to breathe and live happily. Similarly, if we cut down too many trees, we take away homes for birds and other creatures.

But don’t worry! We can all do things to help our planet. Recycling means turning old things like paper and plastic into new ones – that way, we don't need to use as much stuff from the Earth. Planting trees helps clean the air and gives animals a place to live, just like our playground!
keyIdeas: Reduce waste
Recycle materials
Protect habitats

questions.prompt: What happens when we leave trash on the ground?
choices: C. It makes the sky blue
questions.explanation: Littering can be dangerous for animals and make our environment messy.

questions.prompt: What does recycling mean?
choices: B. Turning old things into new ones
questions.explanation: Recycling helps us use fewer resources from the Earth.

questions.prompt: How do trees help our planet?
choices: B. They clean the air and give animals homes
C. They grow very tall quickly
questions.explanation: Trees absorb pollution and provide shelter for many living things.

mt_22XbXTRq50.title: Brave Polar Explorers
explanation: Imagine you're building a really tall tower with your blocks! That takes lots of effort and you have to keep trying even if it falls down. Brave polar explorers, like Robert Falcon Scott and Roald Amundsen, did something very similar when they went to the South Pole – it’s the coldest, furthest place on Earth! They were super brave because it was a long, difficult journey with freezing temperatures and tricky ice.

Scott and Amundsen raced each other to be the first to reach the South Pole. Roald Amundsen was smarter; he used sled dogs to pull his equipment across the snow and ice. Robert Falcon Scott and his team also made it there, but they were a little slower because they brought more heavy tools with them for science experiments. It’s amazing how these explorers pushed themselves so hard!

Ernest Shackleton had an even bigger adventure! His ship, called Endurance, got stuck in giant blocks of ice. The ice trapped the ship for many months and was very dangerous. But Ernest Shackleton was incredibly brave and led his whole crew back to safety, showing everyone how important it is to never give up!
keyIdeas: Polar explorers are brave.
Explorers use different tools and strategies.
It’s important to be resilient (not give up) even when things get hard.

questions.prompt: Who raced to the South Pole with Roald Amundsen?
questions.explanation: Robert Falcon Scott and Roald Amundsen were two explorers who competed to be the first to reach the South Pole!

questions.prompt: What happened to Ernest Shackleton’s ship?
choices: A. It sailed around the world quickly
B. It got trapped in ice
C. It flew through the sky
questions.explanation: The ship Endurance became stuck in giant blocks of ice, making it very difficult for the crew.

questions.prompt: Which word describes someone who doesn’t give up?
questions.explanation: Being brave means you keep trying even when things are hard – like a polar explorer!

mt_4pLvMN8uzL.title: Storm Safety!
explanation: Let’s talk about storms! Storms are really exciting, like when you see big rain clouds and maybe even hear thunder. But sometimes, storms can be a little scary because lightning flashes across the sky – that's electricity from the cloud! It’s important to know how to stay safe during a storm so we don't get hurt.

When you hear thunder, it means there is lightning nearby and it’s very dangerous. The safest thing to do is go inside a building or into a car – this keeps you away from the electricity.  Don’t stand under trees or near water because lightning can strike them, and that's super risky! Think of your favorite toy box; if there was a storm, you'd want to be safe inside it!

Storms are natural events, meaning they happen all by themselves in nature. Scientists study storms to help us understand them better and predict when they might come.  Even though storms can seem frightening, we can stay safe by following simple rules like going indoors and staying away from trees and water – that’s how we keep ourselves protected.
keyIdeas: Lightning is dangerous.
Go inside during a storm.
Stay away from trees and water.

questions.prompt: What should you do when you hear thunder?
choices: A) Stand outside to see the lightning.
B) Go inside or into a car.
questions.explanation: Lightning is dangerous, so it’s important to find shelter.

questions.prompt: Why isn't it safe to stand near trees during a storm?
choices: A) Trees are very colorful and attract lightning.
C) Trees make the rain fall harder.
questions.explanation: Trees provide a path for electricity from the cloud to reach the ground.

questions.prompt: True or False: Storms are always scary, but there are ways we can stay safe.
questions.explanation: Even though storms seem frightening, knowing safety rules helps us stay protected.

mt_2VR963szuk.title: Shapes All Around Us!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about shapes! Shapes are all around us, like the blocks you build with or the yummy cookies you eat – they come in different sizes and forms. We can make shapes ourselves by putting things together, or even drawing them on paper. Let's start by building a triangle using sticks and clay balls; it’s a shape with three sides!

Imagine your favorite toy car - many cars are shaped like rectangles! A rectangle has four straight sides, two long ones and two short ones. We can draw a rectangle too – just make sure you have two longer lines and two shorter lines to make the perfect box.  It’s important to see that all these shapes are different but still part of the same group!

Now let's build something even cooler: a cube! A cube is like a square that has been folded into three dimensions, making it have six sides. We can use straws and connectors to build this shape – it’s a bit trickier than the triangle or rectangle but super fun to make.  Building shapes helps us understand how things are made!
keyIdeas: Shapes have different sides.
We can build shapes with blocks and other materials.
Drawing shapes is a great way to learn about them.

questions.prompt: I am building a shape with three sticks and three clay balls. What shape am I making?
questions.explanation: Great job! A triangle has three sides, just like the ones you used.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has four sides?
questions.explanation: That’s right! A rectangle always has four straight sides.

questions.prompt: Can you draw a shape that looks like a box?
questions.explanation: You can try drawing a square and then connecting the corners to make it look 3D!

mt_LTb0ZReMR2.title: Minibeast Friends!
explanation: Let’s talk about amazing little creatures called minibeasts! Minibeasts are tiny animals like beetles, worms, woodlice and snails – they live all around us in our gardens and parks. When we find a minibeast, it's important to be gentle with them because they are very sensitive and don’t like being scared or hurt. We can look at them carefully without trying to pick them up.

Imagine you’re playing outside and you spot a woodlouse crawling on the grass – that’s a minibeast! Instead of grabbing it, we let it walk onto our hand gently, just like a friendly visitor. Woodlice help break down dead leaves in the garden, which is super important for making sure plants have space to grow strong and healthy. They are part of a big team helping nature!

After we’ve looked at our minibeast, we always need to put it back where we found it. This is because their home is their special place, and we don't want to disturb their life or the homes of other creatures. It’s like returning a toy to its box – we take care of our friends and let them be happy in their own space.
keyIdeas: Be gentle with minibeasts.
Minibeasts help gardens grow.
Return minibeasts to where you found them.

questions.prompt: What should we do when we find a woodlouse?
choices: A. Grab it and take it home
B. Let it walk onto our hand gently
C. Shout at it to make it go away
questions.explanation: Minibeasts are delicate, so we need to be gentle!

questions.prompt: Why are minibeasts important for gardens?
choices: A. They eat all the flowers
B. They help break down dead leaves
C. They make the grass grow taller
questions.explanation: Minibeasts like woodlice and worms help to recycle old plants and keep the soil healthy.

questions.prompt: What should you do after looking at a minibeast?
choices: A. Keep it in your pocket
B. Put it back where you found it
questions.explanation: Minibeasts need to go home to their own special place!

mt_XjwUlmxdCT.title: Shape Adventures: Building Bigger Shapes!
explanation: Let's talk about shapes! You know squares, circles, triangles, and rectangles – they’re everywhere around us. We can even use them to build amazing things by putting smaller shapes together. Think about your building blocks; you might start with a small cube and then stack more on top to make a taller tower!

Today we're going to learn how to combine simple shapes to make bigger ones. For example, if you take two triangles and put them side-by-side, they form a rectangle! It’s like magic – just by joining shapes together, we can create something new. This is called combining shapes, and it’s a really important part of math.

You can do this with anything! If you have a box of crayons, you could use two circles to make a bigger circle or even a square.  Or, if you're building with LEGOs, you can combine smaller bricks to build a larger castle – it’s all about putting shapes together in clever ways!
keyIdeas: Shapes can be combined.
Joining shapes makes new shapes.
You can make bigger things by combining smaller shapes.

questions.prompt: If you put one circle and one circle together, what shape do you make?
questions.explanation: That's right! Two circles joined together make a bigger circle.

questions.prompt: You have two triangles. What could you build with them?
questions.explanation: Two triangles put together make a rectangle – it’s a classic shape combination!

questions.prompt: Can you draw a picture using at least three shapes?
questions.explanation: Great! Use your crayons or blocks to build something fun and show us how you combined shapes.

mt_fMd7v87IiI.title: Diphthongs & Vowel Adventures!
explanation: Let's talk about sounds in words! Sometimes, vowels make different sounds depending on where they are. We’re going to learn about special vowel groups called diphthongs – these are like secret codes for making new sounds when we read. Think of it like a magic trick where the vowel changes its voice!

Diphthongs are two vowel sounds mixed together in one word. For example, take the word ‘cow’ and ‘snow’. The ‘ow’ makes a different sound in each word!  You can even see this with words you know – like ‘cloud’, ‘blue’, or ‘pie’. These words have those special vowel combinations that make them fun to read.

Now let's look at words ending in -y. Sometimes, the 'y' makes a long sound like 'ee', as in the word 'happy'. Other times it makes an 'igh' sound, like in the word ‘my’.  It’s important to listen carefully to how the word is spoken to figure out which sound the 'y' is making – that’s part of becoming a super reader!
keyIdeas: Diphthongs are two vowel sounds together.
The ‘y’ at the end of words can make different sounds.
Listening carefully helps us read correctly.

questions.prompt: Which word has the ‘ow’ sound?
questions.explanation: The ‘ow’ makes a different sound in ‘cow’ than it does in ‘cat’.

questions.prompt: What vowel sound is in the word ‘blue’?
questions.explanation: The ‘ou’ makes the ‘oo’ sound as in ‘blue’.

questions.prompt: Which of these words has a ‘y’ making an ‘igh’ sound?
questions.explanation: In ‘my’, the ‘y’ makes an ‘igh’ sound like in ‘night’.

mt_q3vRl4dddK.title: Staying Safe Around Us!
explanation: Let’s talk about staying safe! It's super important to know some simple rules so we can keep ourselves and our friends happy and healthy. One rule is always look both ways before crossing the street – even if you think cars aren't coming. This helps us see if a car is speeding towards us, because sometimes drivers don’t see us!

Another important rule is to never talk to strangers! Strangers are people we don’t know well, and it’s best not to share our secrets or go anywhere with them. If someone you don't know asks you for help, tell a grown-up right away – like your mom, dad, teacher, or grandma. They can help you figure out what to do.

Finally, always be careful around water! Water can be fun to play in, but it can also be dangerous if we’re not being careful. That's why we should always have a grown-up with us when we're near pools or lakes, and never run around the edges of the water.
keyIdeas: Look both ways before crossing streets.
Never talk to strangers.
Be careful around water with an adult.

questions.prompt: You are walking across the street and see a car coming. What should you do?
choices: A) Run after the car to get to the other side.
B) Look both ways before crossing.
C) Ignore the car and keep walking.
questions.explanation: Looking both ways is the safest way to cross the street because it helps you see if any cars are coming!

questions.prompt: A stranger approaches you at the playground and asks for your help. What should you do?
choices: A) Tell them where you live.
B) Run away and tell a grown-up.
C) Play with them for a little while.
questions.explanation: It's important to tell a trusted adult if someone you don’t know asks for help because they can keep you safe.

questions.prompt: You are at the pool and want to jump in. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Always swim with an adult or someone who knows how to swim safely!

mt_qixeaiswFP.title: How Organisms Shape Habitats
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs – you want to make a cool castle, right? Animals and plants are like builders too, but they build their homes and food sources in the world around them! They change things to fit what *they* need. For example, think about your favorite teddy bear; it needs a cozy place to sleep, so it might have a little blanket or pillow.

Lots of animals and plants make changes to their surroundings. Beavers build dams in rivers – these dams create ponds where fish can swim and live! Birds build nests high up in trees, giving them a safe place to lay their eggs and raise their babies. And strong tree roots dig into the ground, slowly breaking apart rocks over many years.

These changes happen because animals and plants need things like shelter, food, and water. The beaver’s dam gives it a home and a place to find tasty wood. The bird's nest protects its young, and the plant roots help them get water from the soil. So, you see, living things are always working to make their lives easier!
keyIdeas: Organisms build homes and food sources.
Changes in habitats meet an organism’s needs.
Animals and plants shape the world around them.

questions.prompt: A beaver builds a dam. What is the beaver trying to do?
questions.explanation: The dam creates a pond, which is a place where the beaver can live and find food.

questions.prompt: Why do birds build nests in trees?
choices: B: To have a safe place to raise their babies
C: Because they like the color of the tree
questions.explanation: Nests protect baby birds and give them a comfortable spot to grow up.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of how plants change their environment?
choices: A: A dog wagging its tail
C: A cat sleeping in the sun
questions.explanation: Tree roots dig into the ground, slowly changing the shape of the land.

mt_2zJ1NrGgYm.title: Rainforest Homes and Helpers
explanation: Long, long ago, before cars and houses like ours, people called the Yanomami lived in the rainforests of South America! They’ve been there for thousands of years – that's a super, super long time! These amazing people knew all about the jungle because they were part of it.

The Yanomami built their homes using big leaves and branches from trees. Imagine building a house out of a giant leaf - pretty cool, right? They also used fruits and nuts for food, like bananas and Brazil nuts, and fished in the rivers with special hooks and lines. It’s like they were helping the rainforest grow!

These people knew which plants could help them when they were sick – some are still used today as medicine! The Yanomami have a deep understanding of the forest and its animals; they know where to find food, how to stay safe, and how everything is connected. It's like they’re best friends with the rainforest!
keyIdeas: Yanomami people live in rainforests.
They build homes from natural materials.
Indigenous peoples understand the forest deeply.

questions.prompt: Which of these groups lived in rainforests for thousands of years?
choices: B) People who live in cities
questions.explanation: The Yanomami people have been living in the rainforests for a very, very long time!

questions.prompt: What did the Yanomami use to build their homes?
choices: B) Leaves and branches from trees
questions.explanation: The Yanomami used leaves and branches to make strong, safe houses in the rainforest.

questions.prompt: Why do you think it's important for indigenous peoples to understand the forest?
questions.explanation: It’s important because they know how to take care of nature and use its resources wisely. They have a special connection with the plants and animals!

explanation: Describe the relationship between illustrations and the text in which they appear; use pictures to support and extend comprehension
You know this idea when you can: Use pictures to predict or confirm story events; Describe what is happening in an illustration and how it relates to the text.

mt_XL2gqdKJfu.title: Rainforest Insects!
explanation: Imagine you're exploring a rainforest – it’s super warm and wet, full of tall trees and amazing plants! Rainforests are like giant homes for millions of animals, but guess what? Insects are the *most* common ones there. They come in all shapes and sizes, from tiny little bugs to really big ones, making the rainforest incredibly busy.

Let’s look at some special insects you might find. Leaf-cutter ants are amazing farmers! They carry small pieces of leaves back to their nests where they grow a yummy fungus to eat. Giant beetles can be as big as your hand and jewel-coloured butterflies flutter around, painting the rainforest with bright colours. These insects play an important role in keeping the rainforest healthy.

Think about it – if you were counting all the animals in the rainforest, you’d find way more insects than anything else! That's because they are everywhere, building nests, eating plants and helping to spread seeds. Rainforest insects make this amazing place so special and full of life.
keyIdeas: Rainforests have lots of insects.
Insects are the most numerous animals in rainforests.
Leaf-cutter ants are busy farmers!

questions.prompt: Which of these is a type of insect found in the rainforest?
questions.explanation: Leaf-cutter ants are insects that live in the rainforest.

questions.prompt: What is one thing leaf-cutter ants do?
choices: B. Carry leaves to grow fungus
questions.explanation: Leaf-cutter ants carry pieces of leaves back to their nests to grow food.

questions.prompt: Which animal group is the MOST common in the rainforest?
questions.explanation: Insects are the most numerous animals found in the rainforest.

mt_3duNkf6Qmr.title: Midnight Sun & Polar Night
explanation: Imagine you have a favorite toy, like a teddy bear! When it’s daytime, you play with your teddy bear in the sunshine. But what if there was *never* an end to the day? That's what happens at the North and South Poles during summer – it's called ‘midnight sun’! The Sun stays up in the sky all day long, and doesn’t go down at night.

Now, let’s think about winter. When you build a snowman, you need the sunlight to see what you’re doing! At the North and South Poles during winter, something amazing happens: the sun stays *below* the horizon for weeks or even months! This is called ‘polar night’, and it means there's no sunshine at all.

It’s a really big difference from where we live. Here, every day has some sunlight and some darkness. But at the poles, they have either lots of daylight (midnight sun) or lots of darkness (polar night). It’s like a super-long daytime party or a super-long nighttime sleep!
keyIdeas: The Sun doesn't set in summer at the North and South Poles.
The Sun doesn't rise in winter at the North and South Poles.
Polar regions have extreme daylight and darkness compared to our homes.

questions.prompt: During summer at the North Pole, what happens to the sun?
choices: B. It stays up all day
C. It moves across the sky
questions.explanation: The Sun doesn’t set during summer at the North Pole – it's called midnight sun!

questions.prompt: What is ‘polar night’?
choices: A. When it snows a lot
B. When the sun stays below the horizon for months
C. When you play outside in the dark
questions.explanation: Polar night means the Sun doesn't rise during winter at the poles.

questions.prompt: Is daylight and darkness the same everywhere?
choices: A. Yes, every day is the same
B. No, it’s different at the North and South Poles
C. Only when you play outside
questions.explanation: The poles have extreme amounts of daylight or darkness – much more than we experience!

mt_nNDX_jZ-cb.title: Waiting Patiently!
explanation: Sometimes, we really want something right now – like a yummy cookie or a turn to play with your favorite toy! But sometimes, we have to wait. Waiting can be tricky because it means we don't get what we want immediately, and that might make us feel a little bit sad or frustrated. Learning to wait patiently is like building a super-power; it helps you handle those feelings and do something good.

Let’s think about when you’re at the playground. Maybe you really want to go down the slide first, but your friend is waiting their turn. If you can wait patiently for your turn, that's being patient! You could even try counting to ten while you wait – that helps take your mind off wanting to go on the slide right away.

When we have to wait, it’s important to remember why we’re doing it and that good things often come after waiting. Sometimes grown-ups need time to finish a task, or maybe we need to wait for a bus to arrive. Practicing patience is like learning a new skill – the more you do it, the easier it gets!
keyIdeas: Waiting is okay sometimes.
Thinking about something else helps us wait.
Practicing patience makes us strong.

questions.prompt: You want to play with your toy car, but your brother is using it. What should you do?
choices: A. Yell at him to give it back immediately.
B. Wait patiently for your turn.
questions.explanation: Waiting patiently shows respect and helps you get a turn later.

questions.prompt: When you have to wait, what can you do to help yourself feel better?
choices: B. Think about something fun or draw a picture.
questions.explanation: Thinking about something else helps distract you from wanting it right away.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to learn how to wait?
choices: A. It’s always easy to get what we want immediately.
B. Sometimes good things happen after waiting.
questions.explanation: Waiting allows us to be patient and often leads to positive outcomes.

mt_a6m7PqTuJN.title: R-Controlled Vowel Adventures!
explanation: Today we’re going to explore some special sounds in words! These sounds happen when the letter ‘r’ is next to a vowel – that means it changes how the vowel says its name. It's like the ‘r’ is whispering a secret to the vowel, making a new sound. Let’s look at some examples like 'car', 'bird', and 'fork'.

The letter ‘r’ doesn’t always make a loud ‘r’ sound like in ‘run’. Instead, it changes the way we say the vowel! Think about the word ‘cat’ – the ‘a’ says ‘ah’. But if we add an ‘r’ to make ‘cart’, the ‘a’ says ‘ahrt’ – that's because of the 'r'! It’s a little trick, but you can learn it!

Now let’s practice spelling words with these r-controlled vowels. We can write down words like ‘her’, ‘bird’, ‘farm’, and ‘urn’. When we spell them, we need to remember that the ‘r’ is changing how the vowel sounds. This helps us read and write better!
keyIdeas: R-controlled vowels change a vowel's sound.
The letter 'r' doesn't always make a loud 'r' sound.
Practice spelling words with r-controlled patterns.

questions.prompt: Which word has the ‘a’ saying ‘ahrt’?
questions.explanation: The ‘r’ in ‘cart’ changes the sound of the ‘a’.

questions.prompt: What happens when you add an ‘r’ to the word ‘bird’?
choices: C. It makes a louder ‘r’ sound
questions.explanation: Adding 'r' changes the vowel sound.

questions.prompt: Which of these words has an r-controlled vowel?
questions.explanation: ‘Fork’ contains the ‘or’ sound, which is an r-controlled vowel.

mt_0FYFiLTqx4.title: Robin Hood & King Arthur: Amazing Stories!
explanation: Let’s talk about two really cool stories from a long, long time ago – Robin Hood and King Arthur! These aren't real stories that happened exactly as they are told, but they tell us lots about how people thought back then. Imagine Robin Hood living in Sherwood Forest with his friends called the Merry Men; they were brave and kind, and they helped those who needed it most.

King Arthur is another fantastic story! He led a group of knights around a table called the Round Table. They wore shiny armor and used a magical sword named Excalibur to protect people and fight for what was right. These stories are full of adventure and bravery – like when you build a really tall tower with blocks!

It’s important to remember that Robin Hood and King Arthur are legends, which means they're amazing stories that have been told and retold over many years. They aren’t things that actually happened in the way we read about them. But these stories show us what people valued – like fairness and helping others – even if they weren’t real.
keyIdeas: Legends are exciting stories.
Robin Hood helped those who needed it.
King Arthur led brave knights.

questions.prompt: Who lived in Sherwood Forest and helped people?
questions.explanation: Robin Hood was a famous hero who lived in Sherwood Forest and protected those who were treated unfairly.

questions.prompt: What was the name of King Arthur’s magical sword?
questions.explanation: Excalibur was a special sword that gave King Arthur power to protect his kingdom.

questions.prompt: Are Robin Hood and King Arthur real people or just stories?
choices: A. They were definitely real people who lived in the past
B. These are legends – amazing stories, not facts
C. They were robots from another planet
questions.explanation: Legends are stories that have been passed down through generations; they’re meant to be fun and teach us things!

mt_uM6q_KBWKy.title: Big Letters for Important Words!
explanation: Let’s learn about big letters! Sometimes, we use special letters called 'capital letters'. They're like saying ‘pay attention!’ when we write a name or a day of the week. We don’t use them for everything – just for things that are important and have a specific name.

Think about your favorite toy car. You might say, "I love my red race car!" See how 'I' is written with a big letter? That’s because ‘I’ is a special word that tells us who is doing something. It’s like saying ‘me!’

We also use big letters for days of the week, like Monday or Friday. When we talk about school, we write 'Monday' and 'Friday'. Using big letters makes our writing look neat and helps everyone understand exactly what we mean – it’s important to be clear!
keyIdeas: Capital letters show importance.
'I' is always written with a capital letter.
Days of the week start with a capital letter.

questions.prompt: What should you do when writing your friend’s name?
choices: Use big letters only for numbers
questions.explanation: Capital letters are used to show the names of people and places.

questions.prompt: Which word always starts with a capital letter?
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'I' is always written with a big letter.

questions.prompt: What should you write when talking about the days of the week?
questions.explanation: Days of the week, like Monday and Tuesday, start with a capital letter.

mt_Dj2xr8CoI0.title: Dress for the Weather!
explanation: Let’s talk about weather! The weather is what’s happening outside – like if it's sunny, rainy, or cold. Just like we wear different clothes depending on how we feel, we need to wear different things when the weather changes. For example, when you build a sandcastle at the playground, you might want a sun hat and sunscreen to protect your skin from the hot sunshine!

Sometimes, the weather wants to make us wet! When it rains, we need a raincoat or boots to keep our clothes dry and to not slip on the wet ground.  A big umbrella helps too – it keeps the rain off of us while we’re walking home from school.  It's important to wear waterproof things when the weather is rainy because water can make us cold and uncomfortable.

When it’s really cold, we need warm clothes like sweaters and jackets to keep our bodies warm. If we don’t wear enough layers, we might get chilly and even sick! Weather affects what we do every day – if it's snowing, we might build a snowman instead of playing tag.
keyIdeas: Weather changes daily.
We need different clothes for different weather.
Weather impacts our activities.

questions.prompt: If it’s raining, what should you wear?
questions.explanation: Raincoats are made to keep us dry when it rains.

questions.prompt: Why do we need a sun hat on a hot day?
choices: B. To protect our faces from the sun
C. To help us see better
questions.explanation: Sun hats shield your face and head from harmful sunlight.

questions.prompt: What happens if you don’t wear a coat when it's cold?
choices: C. You will play outside longer
questions.explanation: Cold weather can make you feel cold, and wearing a coat helps keep you warm.

mt_s08-QxASd2.title: Everyday Foods from Rainforests
explanation: Know that many everyday foods come from rainforests — chocolate is made from cacao beans, bananas grow in tropical forests, coffee berries ripen in forest shade, and Brazil nuts fall from giant trees — connecting our daily lives to faraway forests

You know this idea when you can: Name at least three foods that come from rainforests, such as chocolate (cacao), bananas, and Brazil nuts; Explain that cacao beans grow on trees in the rainforest and are made into chocolate.

mt_R1xLS1c2Pg.title: Medieval Clothes!
explanation: Long ago, people lived in a time called the Middle Ages, or Medieval Times! People didn’t have stores like we do today, so they made most of their clothes themselves. They used strong materials like wool and linen – that's like the fabric your cozy sweaters are made from! Peasant families wore simple clothes to help them work hard all day long.

Now, if you looked at a king or queen, they would wear very fancy clothes! Noble people, like kings and queens, had special fabrics like silk and fur. Silk is super smooth and shiny, and fur kept them warm. They also used bright colors like red, gold, and purple because those were expensive to make – just like a really cool toy!

The clothes you wore told everyone what kind of job you had or how important you were. A farmer might wear brown wool clothing, while a knight would have shiny metal armor. It’s like wearing a superhero costume; your clothes showed the world who you are!
keyIdeas: Medieval people made their own clothes.
Peasants wore simple clothes made of wool and linen.
Clothes showed what job or status someone had.

questions.prompt: What kind of fabric did most peasants wear?
questions.explanation: Peasants wore wool because it was strong and cheap.

questions.prompt: Which group of people wore the fanciest clothes?
questions.explanation: Nobles had silk, fur, and bright colors – these were expensive materials.

questions.prompt: If you saw someone wearing a shiny helmet, what job might they have?
questions.explanation: That person could be a knight or soldier!

mt_2NKzPeLzIm.title: Medieval Food & Feasts
explanation: What people ate in medieval times: bread and pottage for ordinary people, grand feasts for the rich; no forks, eating with hands; the great hall as the centre of castle life
You know this idea when you can: Describe what ordinary people ate most days (bread, pottage, vegetables, ale); Explain how a feast in a great hall was different from everyday meals.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Explain how a feast in a great hall was different from everyday meals

mt_RlILL2sccX.title: Ramps and Rockets! Testing Push & Pull
explanation: Let's learn about pushing and pulling, and how we can make things move! When you push something, you give it a force – like when you push your toy car across the floor. And when you pull something, you’re using a force too, just like when you pull a wagon with your friends. These pushes and pulls are what make things start moving or stop moving!

Today we're going to build a ramp! A ramp is a sloped surface that helps us push a ball to reach a goal – maybe a cup on the floor. We’ll test how high our ramp is and see if it makes the ball roll far enough.  We can use this ramp just like you might use a slide at the playground, but we're trying to make the ball go into a target!

After we push the ball down the ramp, let's look closely at what happened. Did the ball go into the cup? If not, that means our design didn’t work perfectly! We can try changing things – like making the ramp steeper or smoother – to see if it helps the ball roll better and reach its goal.
keyIdeas: Pushing and pulling create forces.
Ramps help objects move downhill.
We can change designs to make them work better.

questions.prompt: What does pushing something do?
questions.explanation: Pushing gives an object a push, which is a force that makes it move.

questions.prompt: If your ramp is too flat, what might happen to the ball?
choices: A. It will roll really fast
B. It won't roll at all
C. It will roll into the cup
questions.explanation: A flat ramp doesn’t give the ball enough of a push to move far.

questions.prompt: What can you do if your design isn’t working?
choices: A. Give up and throw it away
B. Make changes to improve it
C. Ignore it and keep pushing
questions.explanation: If something doesn't work, we can try changing parts of the design to see if that helps.

mt_goZW_hQUa4.title: Amazing Animal Records!
explanation: Let’s talk about some super cool animals! There are lots of amazing records that animals can hold, like being the biggest or the fastest. Imagine a race – some animals are really good at winning because they're built for speed, while others win because they’re incredibly large. We’re going to learn about some of these incredible record-holders and how they compare to things we see every day.

First, let’s meet the blue whale! The blue whale is the biggest animal in the whole world – it can grow up to 100 feet long! That's longer than ten school buses! Then there’s the cheetah, which is the fastest land animal; it can run as fast as a rocket zooming across the playground.  And did you know that the bee hummingbird is the smallest bird? It’s so tiny, it fits in your hand!

Finally, let's talk about the giraffe – they are the tallest animals! A giraffe can grow up to 19 feet tall, which is taller than a two-story house.  It's amazing how different animals can be and how they’ve grown to be so special in their own ways. These record holders show us just how incredible nature is!
keyIdeas: Animals have records for size, speed, and height.
The blue whale is the largest animal.
The cheetah is the fastest land animal.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals is the biggest?
questions.explanation: The blue whale is the largest animal on Earth!

questions.prompt: What makes the cheetah so special?
choices: B. It’s the fastest land animal
questions.explanation: The cheetah runs incredibly fast – faster than any other land animal!

questions.prompt: Which animal is taller than a two-story house?
questions.explanation: Giraffes are very tall, reaching up to 19 feet!

mt__pMF-Xb0TE.title: Earthquake Safety - Be a Super Hero!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with your LEGOs, and suddenly the ground starts to shake! That's what an earthquake is – it’s like the Earth is having a little wiggle. When earthquakes happen, we need to be safe, and there are three simple things we can do: ‘Drop, Cover, and Hold On.’ These words will help us remember how to protect ourselves.

‘Drop, Cover, and Hold On’ means that when you feel the ground shaking, first you drop down to the floor. Then, you cover your head and neck with your arms – like a superhero shield! Finally, hold on to something sturdy, like a table or desk, so you don't get knocked over. It's important to do this quickly because earthquakes can happen very fast.

Moving away from windows and shelves is also super important during an earthquake. Windows and shelves can fall down and hurt people, so we need to stay as far away from them as possible. If you can, go under a strong table or desk with your family – that’s the safest place to be!
keyIdeas: Drop, Cover, Hold On
Stay Away From Windows
Tell a Trusted Adult

questions.prompt: What is the first thing you should do when you feel an earthquake?
questions.explanation: Remember ‘Drop, Cover, and Hold On’ – dropping to the floor is the first step.

questions.prompt: Why should you move away from windows during an earthquake?
choices: A. Windows are fun to look out of
B. Windows can fall down and hurt people
C. Windows always shake less than other parts of the building
questions.explanation: Falling objects during shaking could cause serious injuries.

questions.prompt: Who should you tell if you feel an earthquake?
questions.explanation: A trusted adult can help keep you safe and call for help.

mt_ntxlccnYzB.title: Exploring the Rainforest!
explanation: Imagine you’re walking into a rainforest – it's like stepping into a warm hug, but a really sticky one! The air feels heavy and damp because of all the rain and water dripping from the leaves. It’s much warmer than your house or even the playground, and that’s why it feels so different; this is called humidity.

Now look down – it's dark, isn't it? That's because the rainforest has giant trees that grow super tall! These trees block most of the sunlight from reaching the ground, so it stays shadowy and cool beneath them. Think about a big umbrella blocking the sun; the trees are doing the same thing!

And listen carefully! The rainforest is full of amazing sounds. You might hear colorful birds singing their songs, or busy insects buzzing around looking for food. Sometimes you’ll even hear the sound of rain falling – it's a very noisy and exciting place!
keyIdeas: Rainforest air is hot and humid.
Tall trees block sunlight, making the forest floor dark.
Rainforests are full of interesting sounds.

questions.prompt: The rainforest air feels ____ because of all the rain.
questions.explanation: The rainforest has lots of water, making the air feel hot and damp.

questions.prompt: Why is it dark on the forest floor?
choices: B. Tall trees block the sunlight
C. The ground is covered in mud
questions.explanation: The tall trees stop most of the sunlight from reaching the ground.

questions.prompt: Which of these sounds might you hear in a rainforest?
choices: B. Bird calls and insect buzzing
questions.explanation: Rainforests are full of animals making different noises!

Explain why certain letters are practised together
Form letters within the same family consistently using the shared movement pattern
