mt_sXRHr7tfS5.title: Listening Like a Superhero!
explanation: Imagine you’re telling your friends about your favorite LEGO set – the one with all the cool spaceships! To keep them listening, you wouldn't just talk really fast, right? You might look at each of them while you speak, and maybe even change how loud or quiet you are to make it more exciting. This is called engaging listeners; it means making sure people are interested in what you’re saying.

When someone shares their idea – like Priya saying she loves building castles – you can show you're listening too! You could say, 'I agree with Priya because castles are super fun to build, but I also think adding a moat makes them even cooler!' That shows you understood what she said and added your own thought.  It’s important to listen carefully and then respond in a way that shows you heard them.

Sometimes we use different words depending on who we're talking to. If you were giving a presentation about dinosaurs for the whole class, you might use big words like ‘paleontology’ because everyone understands what you’re talking about. But if you were chatting with your best friend about dinosaurs, you could say, 'That dinosaur was awesome!' – that’s more relaxed and easy to understand.
keyIdeas: Engage listeners by making eye contact.
Respond to others' ideas with agreement or addition.
Use different language for different audiences.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to engage your listeners?
choices: B. Look at everyone while you speak
questions.explanation: Engaging listeners means making sure people are interested in what you’re saying.

questions.prompt: Priya says she loves building castles. What could you say to respond?
choices: B. ‘I agree with Priya because castles are super fun to build, but I also think adding a moat makes them even cooler!’
questions.explanation: This shows you listened and added your own idea.

questions.prompt: Which type of language would you use when giving a presentation to the whole class?
questions.explanation: Formal language is used for presentations where everyone needs to understand.

mt_9EoS35vaYB.title: Ocean Animal Superpowers!
explanation: The ocean is a really amazing place, but it’s also a tough one for animals to live in! Just like you need special clothes for the cold or a raincoat when it rains, ocean animals have built-in ‘superpowers’ called adaptations. These are special features that help them survive and thrive in their watery homes. Think about a dolphin – its sleek, streamlined body is perfect for swimming really fast through the water.

Let's look at some examples: Many fish have camouflage, which means they can change color to blend in with rocks or sand! This helps them hide from hungry sharks or other predators who might want to eat them. Some animals, like seals and whales, have thick layers of blubber under their skin – this is like a warm coat that keeps them cozy when the ocean water gets very cold.

Another cool adaptation is having tentacles or suckers! Octopuses use their strong arms with suckers to grab slippery fish and crabs. These adaptations aren’t just random; they help animals solve problems like staying warm, avoiding danger from predators, and catching food efficiently. It's all about being perfectly suited for the ocean!
keyIdeas: Streamlined bodies help animals swim fast.
Camouflage helps animals hide.
Blubber keeps animals warm.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an adaptation that helps a fish hide from predators?
questions.explanation: Camouflage allows animals to blend in with their surroundings.

questions.prompt: Why do seals have blubber?
choices: A) To help them swim faster
B) To keep them warm in cold water
questions.explanation: Blubber is a thick layer of fat that acts like insulation, keeping the seal warm.

questions.prompt: An octopus uses its tentacles to _____.
questions.explanation: Octopus suckers help them grab and hold onto their prey.

mt_k7GOtslF-x.title: Force Diagrams: Seeing How Forces Work!
explanation: Let's talk about forces! A force is simply a push or a pull. Everything around us experiences forces – like when you kick a ball, or when your mom pushes you to clean up your room. These forces are what make things move, stop moving, or change direction.

Now, imagine drawing pictures of these pushes and pulls. We can use diagrams called force diagrams! On the diagram, we draw arrows showing the direction that the force is acting in. The length of the arrow shows how strong the force is – a longer arrow means a bigger push or pull. It's like using a superhero’s strength; a stronger hero has a longer arrow!

When we have more than one force acting on something, like a toy car rolling down a hill, we can draw all of them on the same diagram. If the forces are balanced, meaning they cancel each other out, the object won't move. But if there’s an unbalanced force, it will cause the object to speed up or slow down!
keyIdeas: Forces are pushes and pulls.
Force diagrams use arrows to show direction and strength.
Balanced forces mean no movement; unbalanced forces cause motion.

questions.prompt: Imagine a toy car sitting on a table. What force is pulling the car down towards the ground?
choices: A: A push to make it move
questions.explanation: Gravity is the force that pulls everything with mass towards each other – including you and the car!

questions.prompt: You are pushing a box across the floor. You also feel friction slowing it down. Which of these best describes what's happening?
choices: A: The forces are balanced, so the box will move at a constant speed.
B: There is an unbalanced force (friction) causing the box to slow down.
C: The forces are equal and opposite, so the box will stop moving.
questions.explanation: Friction is a force that opposes motion, so it’s an unbalanced force slowing the box down.

questions.prompt: If two arrows on a force diagram point in exactly opposite directions and have the same length, what does this mean?
choices: A: The object will move quickly.
C: The object is at rest.
questions.explanation: Balanced forces cancel each other out, resulting in no net movement.

mt_D4lyx0iYyB.title: Amazing Polar Animals!
explanation: Let’s talk about some super cool animals that live in the very, very cold places – the Arctic and the Antarctic! The Arctic is at the top of our world, near the North Pole, and it's home to amazing creatures like polar bears, arctic foxes, and walruses.  The Antarctic is even colder, located at the bottom of our world, near the South Pole, and you’ll find penguins, seals, and albatrosses there. These animals have special ways to survive in such freezing temperatures – let's discover how!

Polar animals need to stay warm because it gets incredibly cold! One way they do this is with thick fur or blubber. Blubber is like a layer of fat that acts as insulation, trapping heat close to their bodies and keeping them cozy.  Another important thing they do is huddle together – imagine a group of penguins snuggled up for warmth; it’s a fantastic strategy! They also change their coats seasonally, growing thicker fur in the winter.

Think about polar bears – they live only in the Arctic and have thick white fur that helps them blend in with the snow.  Penguins are amazing too because they live only in the Antarctic and use their blubber to stay warm while swimming in icy waters. These animals’ adaptations help them thrive in challenging environments, showing us how clever nature can be!
keyIdeas: Polar animals have special adaptations for cold weather.
Blubber and thick fur are important ways polar animals stay warm.
Arctic and Antarctic animals live in different parts of the world.

questions.prompt: Which animal lives only in the Arctic?
questions.explanation: Polar bears are uniquely adapted to survive in the cold, icy environment of the Arctic.

questions.prompt: What helps penguins stay warm while swimming?
questions.explanation: Penguins have a layer of blubber under their skin which acts like insulation to keep them warm in the water.

questions.prompt: Where do you find seals?
choices: C) Both the Arctic and Antarctic
questions.explanation: Seals primarily live in the Antarctic, where they can easily access food sources.

mt_FAXjFkgG6X.title: Shape Patterns!
explanation: Today, we're going to explore how shapes have patterns! Just like you might build with LEGOs and create repeating designs, shapes can also have patterns in their sides and corners. Think about your building blocks – you could make a tower that repeats the same color or size over and over again; shapes do something similar!

Shapes have special rules about how they're made. For example, squares always have four equal sides and four right angles (that means corners that are perfectly square!). Triangles always have three sides, and circles are round like a ball you might play with at the playground. These patterns help us understand and sort shapes.

When we look closely at shapes, we can notice how they're built. Some shapes have many sides, like a hexagon (a shape with six sides), while others have just a few, like a triangle.  We can even group shapes based on these patterns – all the squares are together because they share the same pattern of four equal sides!
keyIdeas: Shapes have repeating patterns.
Shapes have specific properties (like sides and angles).
We can sort shapes based on their patterns.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has 3 sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle always has three sides. That's its special pattern!

questions.prompt: If you have a square, and you cut it in half, how many pieces do you have?
questions.explanation: Cutting a square in half creates two identical rectangles. That's a pattern!

questions.prompt: Which shape has four sides and four right angles?
questions.explanation: Think about the shapes you know!  Can you name one that fits this description?

mt_u3Y3Tb-G_n.title: The Amazing Sun!
explanation: Have you ever looked up at the sky on a sunny day? You see this big, bright ball – that’s the Sun! But did you know that the Sun isn't just *any* ball of light? It's actually a star, just like all those twinkling stars we see at night. Stars are giant balls of hot gas and they make their own light and heat.

Now, sometimes the Sun looks bigger and brighter than other stars, right? That’s because it’s much closer to us than any other star! Imagine holding a flashlight really close to your eye – it would seem super bright, wouldn't it? The Sun is like that flashlight, but incredibly far away and still very powerful.

Our whole solar system goes around the Sun. It’s like a giant family where the Sun is at the center, and all eight planets – Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune – travel around it in circles. This path they take is called an orbit, and it takes them a really long time to complete one trip!
keyIdeas: The Sun is a star.
The Sun appears large because it's close.
Planets orbit the Sun.

questions.prompt: What is the Sun?
questions.explanation: The Sun is a giant ball of hot gas that makes its own light and heat – it’s a star!

questions.prompt: Why does the Sun look bigger than other stars?
choices: A. It's made of different materials
B. It's much closer to us
questions.explanation: The Sun is closer to Earth, so it appears larger and brighter than distant stars.

questions.prompt: What does the word ‘orbit’ mean?
choices: B. To fly in the sky
questions.explanation: An orbit is the path a planet takes as it travels around the Sun.

mt_kCSy3Lsgme.title: Tides, Waves & Currents!
explanation: Have you ever been to the beach and noticed the water going up and down? That’s called a tide! Tides happen because of something really big – the Moon. The Moon pulls on Earth, just like if you pull a wagon with a rope; it makes the water move towards it.

Waves are created when wind blows across the surface of the ocean. Just like blowing over the top of a bottle creates ripples, the wind pushes and pulls at the water, making waves grow bigger and bigger. The stronger the wind, the bigger the waves will be!

Now, imagine a giant river flowing through the ocean – that’s an ocean current! These currents are really powerful flows of water that travel all around the world, carrying warm or cold water and important things like food for sea creatures.
keyIdeas: Tides are caused by the Moon.
Wind creates waves.
Currents move water around the globe.

questions.prompt: What causes tides?
questions.explanation: The Moon pulls on Earth, causing the water to rise and fall.

questions.prompt: How are waves made?
choices: B. By wind blowing across the water
questions.explanation: Wind pushes and pulls at the water surface, creating waves.

questions.prompt: What do ocean currents carry?
questions.explanation: Currents transport heat and important substances throughout the oceans.

mt_gR5_n99Ntt.title: Poetry Adventures!
explanation: Today, we're going to explore the amazing world of poetry! Poetry isn’t just about rhyming words; it can tell stories, describe feelings, or even paint a picture with words. There are lots of different ways to write poems, and each way has special rules that make them unique. Let’s start by learning about three common types: narrative poetry, free verse, and haiku.

Narrative poetry is like telling a story in poem form! It usually has characters, a setting, and a plot – just like a regular book or movie. Think about your favorite fairy tale; it's probably a narrative poem! Free verse poems don’t follow any strict rules about rhyme or rhythm. They can be silly or serious, and the poet chooses how to put the words on the page.

Haiku poems are super short and special. They come from Japan and have exactly 17 syllables arranged in three lines – five, seven, and five. Haikus often describe nature and try to capture a moment in time. When we read poetry aloud, it’s important to use our voices to show how the poem makes us feel!
keyIdeas: Poetry comes in many forms.
Narrative poems tell stories.
We can change our voice when we perform a poem.

questions.prompt: Which type of poetry tells a story?
choices:
A. Haiku
B. Narrative Poetry
C. Free Verse
questions.explanation: Narrative poems are all about telling stories with characters and events.

questions.prompt: What makes a haiku special?
choices:
A. It has lots of rhymes.
B. It has exactly 17 syllables arranged in three lines.
C. It always tells about animals
questions.explanation: Haiku poems have a specific number of syllables that create their unique form.

questions.prompt: When you read a poem aloud, how can you show your understanding?
choices:
A. Read it very quickly.
B. Change your voice to match the mood.
C. Only look at the words on the page
questions.explanation: Changing your tone and volume helps us understand how the poet felt when writing the poem.

mt_nUnowllzaN.title: Measuring Like a Scientist!
explanation: Let’s learn how to be super careful when we measure things! Measuring accurately is really important in science because it helps us understand the world around us. When we want to know something, like how tall a toy building block is or how warm the bath water feels, we need to use tools that help us get precise answers. Scientists always try to be as correct as possible when they take measurements – it’s like being a detective trying to solve a puzzle!

There are lots of different tools we can use for measuring. A ruler helps us measure things in centimeters, which is like little steps! A thermometer tells us how hot or cold something is and measures temperature in degrees Celsius (°C). We also have measuring jugs that help us pour liquids like water into specific amounts – it’s important to read the markings carefully so we don't add too much or too little.

To make sure our measurements are good, we need to be systematic. This means following a plan and doing things in the same way each time. For example, when taking someone’s temperature with a thermometer, always point the end of the thermometer into their mouth and hold it there for a few minutes before reading the number – that's a systematic approach! Accurate measurements are the foundation of good science.
keyIdeas: Use standard units (cm, °C, g).
Follow a planned method consistently.
Accurate measurement is key to understanding.

questions.prompt: Which unit would you use to measure the length of your pencil?
questions.explanation: Centimeters are used for measuring length, like the size of a pencil.

questions.prompt: You’re using a thermometer to measure the temperature of your hot chocolate. What unit will you read?
questions.explanation: Thermometers measure temperature in degrees Celsius.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to follow a plan when measuring something?
questions.answer: To ensure you get the same results each time and your measurements are accurate.

mt_7IFpDVNsmt.title: Sorting Out Living Things!
explanation: Let’s talk about living things! Everything around us – like your favorite teddy bear or a juicy apple – is either alive or not. Scientists, people who study the world, need to sort these things into groups based on what makes them special. This process of sorting and grouping is called ‘classification,’ and it helps us understand how different plants and animals are related.

Imagine you have a box full of toys: cars, dolls, blocks, and stuffed animals. You could group them by color – all the red ones together, or all the blue ones together. Scientists do something similar with living things! They look for common characteristics like what they eat, where they live, and how they grow to decide which groups are best.

To help us sort, we can make a chart! We could write down everything we observe about an animal, such as its fur color, size, or if it flies. Then, we can use this information to create categories like ‘big animals’ or ‘animals that swim.’ This helps us organize our thinking and see patterns in the world around us.
keyIdeas: Living things have characteristics that help scientists group them.
Scientists use observations to classify living things.
Organizing information into charts is a helpful way to study living things.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a good reason for scientists to sort living things?
choices: A. To make toys more fun
B. To understand how different plants and animals are related
questions.explanation: Scientists want to learn about the differences between living things.

questions.prompt: You’re looking at a plant and notice it has large, bright red flowers. You might classify it as…
questions.explanation: The flower color is a key characteristic used to sort plants.

questions.prompt: What’s one thing you could do to organize information about animals?
questions.explanation: You could create a chart with different categories, like ‘animals that swim’ or ‘animals that fly.’

mt_bK84sPehyP.title: Amazing Animal Journeys: Migration!
explanation: Have you ever wondered how some animals seem to know exactly where to go? It’s because they do something called migration! Migration is when animals travel long distances, often over and over again, to find food, a good place to have babies, or because of the changing seasons. Think about your favorite toy car – it might drive around your room, but an animal's migration is like a super-long adventure!

Let’s look at some amazing animals that migrate. Arctic terns are incredible birds that fly from one end of the world to the other, traveling all the way from the North Pole to the South Pole and back every year! Monarch butterflies also make a long journey across North America, flying thousands of miles to warmer places for the winter. The wildebeest in Africa travel together in huge herds, following rain to find grass to eat – it’s like they're on a giant picnic trail!

These journeys aren't just random; they are driven by important things like food and weather. Animals migrate because they need to find places with plenty of food, or where it’s warm enough for them to have babies. The seasons also play a big role – some animals move south in the winter to escape the cold.
keyIdeas: Migration is a long journey animals take.
Animals migrate to find food and better places to live.
Different animals migrate for different reasons, like food or weather.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes migration?
choices: A. A short trip around the house
B. A long journey an animal takes to find food or a good place to live
C. Staying in one place all year
questions.explanation: Migration means traveling a very long distance, usually for important reasons like finding food or a safe place.

questions.prompt: Monarch butterflies migrate because…
choices: A. They want to play with other butterflies
B. They need warmer places to spend the winter
C. They are chasing after bees
questions.explanation: Butterflies migrate to find warmer climates where they can survive during colder months.

questions.prompt: Why do Arctic terns fly so far?
questions.explanation: Arctic terns fly long distances to follow food sources and breeding grounds, often traveling from pole to pole.

mt_fKwgN61ttR.title: Fossils Reveal Ancient Environments
explanation: Understand that fossils tell us not only about ancient animals but also about ancient environments — for example, marine fossils found on a mountaintop show that area was once underwater

You know this idea when you can: Give an example of a fossil that reveals a past environment different from today's; Explain that finding marine fossils inland means that area was once covered by sea.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Give an example of a fossil that reveals a past environment different from today's
Explain that finding marine fossils inland means that area was once covered by sea
State that plant fossils can show whether an area was once a forest, desert, or swamp

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of a fossil that reveals a past environment different from today's
questions.explanation: Give an example of a fossil that reveals a past environment different from today's

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that finding marine fossils inland means that area was once covered by sea
questions.explanation: Explain that finding marine fossils inland means that area was once covered by sea

questions.prompt: True or false: State that plant fossils can show whether an area was once a forest, desert, or swamp
questions.explanation: State that plant fossils can show whether an area was once a forest, desert, or swamp

mt_mayItsxMUu.title: Measuring & Plotting Lengths
explanation: Today, we're going to learn how to measure things and then draw pictures called line plots! Measuring means figuring out exactly how long something is. We’ll use a ruler or measuring tape to see if something is longer than a centimeter or shorter than a centimeter – it’s like being a detective for size!

Let's say we want to measure the length of our building blocks. First, we carefully line up the block with the edge of our table and look at how many centimeters it takes to reach the end. We write down that number – that's our measurement! Then, we do this for several different blocks to get a good idea of how long they are.

Once we have all our measurements, we can make a line plot. A line plot is like a chart where we put each measurement on a dot along a line. The line shows us the range of lengths we measured, and it helps us see which blocks were longer or shorter than others.
keyIdeas: Measure with a ruler.
Record your measurements carefully.
Use a line plot to show how long things are.

questions.prompt: Imagine you measure the length of five pencils. You record them as: 7cm, 8cm, 9cm, 7cm, and 6cm. What would a line plot look like?
choices: A. A single dot in the middle.
B. Dots along a number line showing each pencil's length.
C. Only dots for the longest pencils.
questions.explanation: A line plot shows each measurement as a dot on a number line, allowing us to see all the different lengths.

questions.prompt: You measure ten crayons and record them: 5cm, 6cm, 7cm, 8cm, 9cm, 10cm, 5cm, 6cm, 7cm, 8cm. Which crayon was the longest?
questions.explanation: Looking at the line plot, you can see that 8cm has the highest dot, indicating it's the longest crayon.

questions.prompt: What is a line plot used for?
choices: B. To show how many of something there are.
C. To measure the length of objects and see their range.
questions.explanation: Line plots help us understand the different lengths we measured and see the range of measurements.

mt_Bztatrv-_v.title: Amazing Animal Hunters!
explanation: Have you ever watched a nature show and seen a wolf hunting with its pack? That's an amazing strategy! Wolves hunt together because they can surround their prey, like deer or rabbits, making it much easier to catch them. This is called cooperative hunting – working as a team to get dinner. It’s like when you play tag with your friends; everyone helps to chase and catch someone!

Not all predators are big and strong. Some, like chameleons, use clever tricks too! Chameleons have long, sticky tongues that shoot out super fast to grab insects. They can even camouflage themselves perfectly to sneak up on their meals. Similarly, spiders build webs to trap bugs – a really smart way to catch dinner without having to chase!

But animals aren’t just trying to avoid being eaten; they also have ways of protecting themselves. Porcupines have prickly spines that stick out all over their bodies, making it very uncomfortable for predators like foxes or bears to try and eat them. Some animals, like skunks, spray a smelly liquid when they feel threatened, and poison dart frogs use their bright colors to warn predators they are dangerous!
keyIdeas: Predators use teamwork to hunt.
Prey animals have clever defenses.
Animals constantly adapt to survive.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a hunting strategy used by wolves?
questions.explanation: Wolves hunt together to surround their prey and make it easier to catch.

questions.prompt: What helps a porcupine defend itself from predators?
questions.explanation: The sharp spines on a porcupine are very uncomfortable for predators to touch.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists call this ‘arms race’ between predators and prey?
questions.explanation: Because both predators and prey are constantly developing new ways to survive – like better hunting strategies or stronger defenses. It's a never-ending competition!

mt_9NQEiYLQA3.title: Seeing Someone Else's Point of View
explanation: Practise perspective-taking by imagining how someone else might feel in a given situation — using prompts like 'How would you feel if that happened to you?' and applying this when reading stories or during real interactions
You know this idea when you can: Describe how a character in a story feels and give reasons based on the plot; Predict how another child would feel in a described scenario.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe how a character in a story feels and give reasons based on the plot
Predict how another child would feel in a described scenario
Use perspective-taking during a real disagreement to understand the other person's view

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe how a character in a story feels and give reasons based on the plot
questions.explanation: Describe how a character in a story feels and give reasons based on the plot

questions.prompt: True or false: Predict how another child would feel in a described scenario
questions.explanation: Predict how another child would feel in a described scenario

questions.prompt: True or false: Use perspective-taking during a real disagreement to understand the other person's view
questions.explanation: Use perspective-taking during a real disagreement to understand the other person's view

mt_YynJoQcm_M.title: Savanna Adventures!
explanation: Let's travel to the savanna, a grassy place found mostly in Africa! Imagine lions, zebras, elephants, and cheetahs roaming across wide open plains. These animals live in grasslands too – like the American prairies where bison graze. Savannas are special because they have lots of sunshine and warm weather, perfect for these amazing creatures.

You might be wondering why you see so many zebras running together in a big group! It’s called herding, and it's a super smart way to stay safe. When a lion tries to catch one zebra, all the other zebras run away, making it much harder for the predator to get its meal. This is like when you play tag – lots of people make it really hard to find just one person!

Lions are powerful hunters who love to chase down speedy zebras and wildebeest. But these animals aren’t always easy targets because they live in open spaces with few trees for cover. The savanna has a special balance: predators like lions need food, and prey like zebras need protection – it's a never-ending game of survival!
keyIdeas: Savannas are grassy habitats.
Herding helps animals stay safe from predators.
Predators hunt prey in open spaces.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals lives on the savanna?
questions.explanation: Lions are famous for living in warm, grassy places like the savanna!

questions.prompt: Why do zebras often travel in large groups?
choices: A) They like to play together
B) It helps them avoid being hunted
questions.explanation: Herding provides protection by making it harder for predators to catch one animal.

questions.prompt: What kind of habitat is a savanna?
choices: A) A forest with lots of trees
B) A grassland with few trees
questions.explanation: Savannas are open grasslands with very few trees.

mt_qgb76wHN2X.title: Fair Tests: Making Sure We Know!
explanation: Have you ever built a tower with blocks, and it fell over? Sometimes things happen by accident, but scientists want to know *why* something happens! A fair test is like building that tower carefully so we can figure out what makes it strong or weak. It’s all about being careful and making sure only one thing changes when we try something new.

To do a fair test, we need to think about what we're changing – this is called the ‘variable’. Let’s say you want to see if different amounts of water make plants grow taller. The variable you change would be the amount of water you give each plant. We also need to measure something – like how tall the plant grows, and keep everything else the same, like the type of plant and the sunlight it gets.

A fair test means we only change *one* thing at a time! If you want to see if different colored lights make plants grow faster, you would give each plant the same amount of water and put them in the same place. That way, you know the color of the light is what’s making the difference, not how much water they're getting.
keyIdeas: Change one thing at a time.
Measure carefully.
Keep everything else the same.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to have a fair test?
choices: A. Changing lots of things at once
B. Only changing one thing at a time
questions.explanation: A fair test is important because it helps us understand what’s really causing something to happen.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're testing how far a toy car goes. What would be the ‘variable’ you change?
choices: A. The color of the car
B. The amount of time you push the car
C. The surface the car rolls on
questions.explanation: The variable is what you are changing – in this case, the surface the car rolls on.

questions.prompt: Why do we need to keep everything else the same during a fair test?
questions.answer: To make sure that only one thing is causing any changes we see.

mt_7VrR1GzhrN.title: Shape Sleuths: Drawing Conclusions!
explanation: Today we’re going to be shape sleuths, which means we'll be detectives looking for clues! When you do something like build a tower with blocks or even just play with your LEGOs, you’re doing an investigation. Scientists and detectives both need to look closely at what they see – that’s called ‘evidence’ – to figure out what’s happening.

Evidence is anything you notice when you're exploring something. For example, if you were building a sandcastle on the playground, the evidence might be that it kept crumbling in the sun or that it was easier to build with wet sand.  It’s important to write down or describe this evidence carefully so we can use it later!  Think of it like collecting puzzle pieces – each piece helps us understand the bigger picture.

Once you have your evidence, you can draw a conclusion. A conclusion is what you think happened based on all the clues you found. For instance, if your sandcastle crumbled easily in the sun, you could conclude that the heat made it unstable.  Then, you might predict that building your castle in the shade would work better and suggest using more water to make the sand stick together – that’s a prediction and an improvement!
keyIdeas: Evidence is important for investigations.
Conclusions are based on evidence.
Predictions can help us improve our experiments.

questions.prompt: You built a tower of blocks, and it kept falling down. What’s one piece of evidence you might have noticed?
choices: C: You used a lot of blocks.
questions.explanation: The fact that the tower fell is important evidence about its stability.

questions.prompt: You tested how fast different toys roll across the floor. You found that the red car rolled the fastest. What conclusion can you draw based on this evidence?
choices: A: All cars roll at the same speed.
B: The red car is faster than other cars.
C: Toys don’t like to roll.
questions.explanation: The evidence (the red car rolling fastest) supports the conclusion that it's faster.

questions.prompt: You are building a boat out of cardboard and you want to make sure it floats. What prediction could you make based on your observations?
choices: A: The boat will always sink.
B: You should add more water to the boat.
C: The boat will float if it's made of cardboard.
questions.explanation: Based on evidence, a prediction is that adding material (cardboard) to make it buoyant would cause it to float.

mt_CVywzrjT_c.title: Shape Detectives: Finding Clues!
explanation: Today we're going to be shape detectives! When we ask questions about the world, like ‘Why do some toys have round edges and others have square ones?’ it’s important to look for clues. These clues are called evidence – they’re facts or observations that help us find an answer. Think of it like a puzzle; each piece of evidence helps us put everything together.

Let's say you’re building with LEGO bricks and notice that all the blocks used to build your castle have flat, rectangular sides. That’s a pattern! This pattern shows us that many buildings, including castles, are made with shapes that have straight lines and flat surfaces.  This is evidence because it tells us something about how builders create strong structures.

Now, when we answer questions, we need to use this evidence clearly. Instead of just saying ‘castles are strong,’ we can say ‘Castles are strong because many of the building blocks have rectangular shapes, which help hold them together.’  See how using the pattern (the shape of the bricks) gives us a better explanation?’
keyIdeas: Patterns show relationships.
Evidence supports answers.
Specific data helps explain things.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re looking at a pile of rocks and notice that all the smooth, rounded ones are near a river. What is this an example of?
choices: A) A random collection of rocks.
B) Evidence showing rocks get smoothed by water.
C) Rocks being used to build a wall.
questions.explanation: The pattern – smooth, rounded rocks – suggests a connection with water, which is evidence that water shapes the rocks.

questions.prompt: You’re measuring how tall your plants grow each week. You notice they always grow taller on sunny days. What does this show?
choices: A) Plants don't need sunlight to grow.
B) Sunlight helps plants grow taller.
questions.explanation: The pattern of growth on sunny days provides evidence that sunlight is important for plant growth.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes how evidence works when answering questions?
choices: A) It’s just a guess based on what you think.
B) It's information that helps support your answer with facts.
C) It’s always completely correct and doesn’t need explaining.
questions.explanation: Evidence provides the factual basis for an answer, making it more reliable.

mt_86DyHo9zO3.title: Formal or Fun?
explanation: Today we're going to learn about how we use words differently depending on who we’re talking to! Sometimes, we talk very seriously and politely, like when you ask your teacher for help. This is called using ‘formal’ language – it sounds more serious and respectful. Think of it like dressing up in a nice suit; formal language helps us show respect and be careful with our words.

Formal language often uses longer sentences and avoids slang or everyday expressions. For example, instead of saying 'Like, can I have some juice?', you might say ‘May I please have some juice?’ See how the second sentence sounds more polite and proper? It’s important to use formal language when talking about important things like schoolwork or writing a letter to someone in charge, such as your headteacher.

Now, let's talk about informal language! Informal language is relaxed and friendly – it’s what we usually say to our friends and family. You might say ‘Hey mate, wanna play?’ but you wouldn’t say that to your teacher! The choice between formal and informal depends on the situation and who you are talking to; it's all about being appropriate.
keyIdeas: Formal language is polite and respectful.
Informal language is relaxed and friendly.
The context (who you’re talking to) determines whether to use formal or informal language.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses formal language? a) ‘Hey, can I borrow your pencil?’ b) ‘May I please borrow your pencil?’ c) ‘Do you want to use my pencil?’
questions.explanation: ‘May I please’ is a polite way of asking for something. It uses formal language.

questions.prompt: You are writing a note to your friend saying you're going to the park. Which sentence would be more informal? a) ‘I am going to visit the playground.’ b) ‘I’m heading to the park!’ c) ‘I will be at the park.’
questions.explanation: ‘I’m heading’ is a casual way of saying you are going somewhere. It uses informal language.

questions.prompt: Why do we use formal language when writing to your school principal? a) Because it's fun to use big words. b) Because it shows respect for the person in charge. c) Because everyone likes talking formally all the time.
questions.explanation: Formal language is used to show respect and demonstrate seriousness when addressing someone important.

mt_bjlY5TE1y-.title: Medieval Pyramid of Power
explanation: How medieval society was organised: king at the top, then lords, then knights, then peasants/serfs; who owed what to whom; the pyramid of power and mutual obligations
You know this idea when you can: Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants; Explain what each level owed to the level above (loyalty, military service, labour).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants
Explain what each level owed to the level above (loyalty, military service, labour)
Describe what a serf's life was like and why they couldn't easily leave

questions.prompt: True or false: Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants
questions.explanation: Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain what each level owed to the level above (loyalty, military service, labour)
questions.explanation: Explain what each level owed to the level above (loyalty, military service, labour)

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe what a serf's life was like and why they couldn't easily leave
questions.explanation: Describe what a serf's life was like and why they couldn't easily leave

mt_k2WE0-22-4.title: Fraction Fun with Groups!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn about fractions! A fraction is a part of a whole. Imagine you have a box of twelve crayons – that’s our whole! We can split this whole into equal groups to see what parts we have.

Let’s say we want to find 1/4 (one-fourth) of those twelve crayons. That means we need to divide the crayons into four equal groups, like sharing them with three friends – each friend gets three crayons! We can then count how many crayons are in one group; there are three.

Now let's try another example: what if we wanted 3/4 (three-fourths) of the crayons? Again, we divide the twelve crayons into four groups. This time, we take three crayons from each group – so we have three crayons in each of the four groups.
keyIdeas: Fractions represent parts of a whole.
Dividing objects into equal groups helps us understand fractions.
Unit fractions have a numerator (top number) of 1.

questions.prompt: If you have 8 cookies and want to share 1/2 of them with your friend, how many cookies does your friend get?
questions.explanation: One-half means dividing the whole into two equal parts.  So, 8 divided by 2 is 4, and then we take half of that (4/2) which equals 2.

questions.prompt: You have a pizza cut into 6 slices. You eat 2 slices. What fraction of the pizza did you eat?
questions.explanation: Two out of six slices is two-sixths, or 2/6.  This represents half of the pizza.

questions.prompt: Which fraction shows one part shaded in a shape that is divided into 5 equal parts?
questions.explanation: The question requires the child to visually identify the fraction represented by the shaded portion of the shape.  This assesses their ability to recognize unit fractions.

mt_2jbUekyTu4.title: Patterns at the Table!
explanation: Let's talk about patterns! Patterns are things that repeat over and over again. Think about your dinner table – when you have a family gathering, you often set out lots of plates. Each plate is the same size, and if you count how many plates there are, you’ll see a pattern: one, two, three, four… it keeps going!

Sometimes, we can use what we already know to figure out new things about patterns. For example, let's say you know that 6 times 3 is 18. You could then use this information to work out 7 times 3! We can think of it like adding a little bit more each time – 7 is just one more than 6.

Patterns aren’t always about numbers; they can be about anything that repeats. Imagine building with blocks - you might start by stacking four red blocks, then four blue blocks, and then four yellow blocks. You've created a pattern! We can use these patterns to predict what comes next.
keyIdeas: Patterns repeat.
Use known facts to find new ones.
Describe rules for repeating sequences.

questions.prompt: If you know 5 x 4 = 20, what is 6 x 4?
questions.explanation: We can add 4 to 20 to find the answer.

questions.prompt: You know that 3 x 7 = 21.  What is 4 x 7?
questions.explanation: We can add 7 to 21 to find the answer.

questions.prompt: Imagine a pattern where you add 5 each time. If the first number is 2, what is the fifth number?
questions.explanation: The pattern is 2, 7, 12, 17, 22.

mt_-p_xp4hMvh.title: Friction Fun!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how your toys move differently depending on where you play? Sometimes a toy car zooms across the floor, but other times it slows down when you try to push it over carpet. This is because of something called friction – it’s like an invisible force that pushes back against things as they move. Friction happens whenever two surfaces rub together, and it's what makes it harder to slide things around.

Different surfaces create different amounts of friction. A smooth surface, like a wooden floor or a table, has less friction than a rough surface like carpet or sandpaper. When you push something on a smooth surface, the rubbing is gentle, so it can move easily. But when you push something on a rough surface, the rubbing is stronger, and that’s why it slows down faster – that's the friction working!

Think about playing on a playground! A swing set has metal chains and a wooden seat; those are relatively smooth. Now imagine trying to slide down a plastic slide - that's much rougher and creates more friction, making you slow down quicker as you go. Friction is always there, helping us understand why things move the way they do.
keyIdeas: Friction opposes movement.
Rough surfaces create more friction than smooth ones.
Friction slows down moving objects.

questions.prompt: Which surface would cause the most friction when pushing a toy car?
questions.explanation: Rugs and carpets have many fibers rubbing together, creating more friction.

questions.prompt: What is friction?
choices: A) A force that pulls things down.
B) An invisible push or pull that resists movement.
C) Something that makes objects float.
questions.explanation: Friction prevents surfaces from sliding past each other easily.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're pushing a toy truck across the floor. If it slows down quickly, what is most likely happening?
questions.explanation: The surface is creating a lot of friction, resisting the truck’s movement.

mt_v33BwiyRnd.title: Finding the Lesson in Stories!
explanation: Stories aren't just about what *happens* – they’re about what those events *mean*. Think about your favorite building blocks; you can build lots of different things with them, right? A story is like a set of building blocks too! It has characters and events, but it also has a hidden lesson or message that the author wants us to learn.

When we read a story, we need to look for the ‘big idea.’ This is called the central message. To find it, we can ask ourselves questions like, 'What did the character learn?' or 'What does this story teach us about being kind/brave/helpful?'. For example, in 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf,' the wolf keeps yelling for help, and eventually, no one believes him.

It’s important to remember that a summary just tells you what happened. It lists the events in order. The central message is deeper – it's about the *why* behind those events.  The Boy Who Cried Wolf teaches us that if we lie, people won't trust us anymore.
keyIdeas: Stories have a hidden lesson.
Look for what characters learn in a story.
A central message is about the meaning of events, not just what happened.

questions.prompt: In 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf', what did the boy do that made people stop believing him?
choices: A. He helped his neighbor with chores.
B. He repeatedly lied about seeing a wolf.
C. He built a beautiful castle.
questions.explanation: The boy’s constant lies caused everyone to ignore his cries for help when the real wolf appeared.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of 'The Tortoise and the Hare'?
choices: A. Hares are faster than tortoises.
B. Slow and steady wins the race.
questions.explanation: The story teaches us that perseverance and consistent effort are more important than speed.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes a summary of a story?
choices: A. The main lesson or moral of the story.
B. A list of all the events in order.
C. What the characters feel about the story.
questions.explanation: A summary simply recounts what happened, while the central message explains *why* it happened and what it means.

mt_yHQacItlhf.title: Uncovering Stories' Secrets: Themes and Messages
explanation: Stories aren’t just fun adventures; they often have big ideas hidden inside them! These big ideas are called themes, and they’re like the main message a story is trying to tell us. Think about your favorite LEGO set – it might be about building something amazing, but that's a theme too! Stories can explore things like being brave, making friends, or learning from mistakes.

Many stories use special ways of telling them, called conventions. These are like rules that help make the story feel magical and predictable. You’ll often hear phrases like ‘Once upon a time,’ which tells you it's a fairy tale! Another common convention is a hero who goes on a journey to solve a problem or defeat an enemy – this is known as a 'hero's journey'.

You might notice that the same theme can show up in different kinds of stories. For example, ‘friendship’ could be about two kids playing together in a game (like tag at school), or it could be about brave knights helping each other in a legend.  It shows us that these important ideas are always interesting and relevant, no matter where you find them.
keyIdeas: Themes are the main messages of a story.
Literary conventions help make stories feel familiar and magical.
The same theme can appear in different types of stories.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a theme?
choices: A) A princess wearing a sparkly dress
B) Learning to share with others
questions.explanation: A theme is the big idea or message a story tries to give us. Sharing is a common and important theme.

questions.prompt: What does ‘Once upon a time’ usually mean?
choices: A) It's a real story that happened yesterday
B) It’s the beginning of a fairy tale
C) It’s a warning about danger
questions.explanation: ‘Once upon a time’ is a common convention used in fairy tales to signal that it’s a magical story.

questions.prompt: Imagine you read a story about a knight who saves a princess. What kind of journey might the knight be on?
questions.explanation: The knight could be on a quest, which is a common convention where a hero goes on an adventure to achieve something important.

mt_oNWXXAn3cn.title: The insect body plan
explanation: The insect body plan: all insects share three body parts (head, thorax, abdomen), six legs attached to the thorax, and antennae on the head. Most have wings. They have an exoskeleton — a hard outer shell — instead of bones inside.

You know this idea when you can: Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen; State that all insects have exactly six legs and that the legs attach to the thorax.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen
State that all insects have exactly six legs and that the legs attach to the thorax
Explain that insects have an exoskeleton, a hard outer covering, instead of bones inside their body

questions.prompt: True or false: Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen
questions.explanation: Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen

questions.prompt: True or false: State that all insects have exactly six legs and that the legs attach to the thorax
questions.explanation: State that all insects have exactly six legs and that the legs attach to the thorax

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that insects have an exoskeleton, a hard outer covering, instead of bones inside their body
questions.explanation: Explain that insects have an exoskeleton, a hard outer covering, instead of bones inside their body

mt_H8dEMH_wik.title: My Reaction Radar!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how you sometimes feel a certain way, and then it happens again? That’s because you might have a pattern in your reactions! Just like when you build with LEGOs, you put blocks together to make something cool, we can look for patterns in *our* feelings. When something makes us happy or sad, we start to notice what usually causes those feelings.

Scientists say that as kids get older, around seven years old, they become better at understanding why they feel the way they do. This is called ‘self-understanding.’ It’s like learning a secret code about your own emotions – you begin to see connections between what happens and how you react. Think about when you're building with blocks; sometimes you get frustrated if you can't build it right, or super excited when you finally finish!

Let’s try to find some patterns in *your* reactions! Maybe you always feel a little grumpy after lunch, or maybe you get really shy when someone asks you a question in class. Recognizing these patterns helps us understand ourselves better and even help us manage our feelings when they pop up.
keyIdeas: Patterns are repeated events.
Self-understanding is learning about your emotions.
Recognizing patterns can help you manage feelings.

questions.prompt: You always feel a little sad when your favorite toy breaks. What does this show?
choices: B) There's a pattern in how you react to broken things.
questions.explanation: This shows that you have a reaction – sadness – when something you value is damaged. Recognizing this pattern can help you understand your feelings.

questions.prompt: Imagine you get nervous before a school play. What’s one thing you could do to notice if this feeling is a pattern?
choices: B) Pay attention to when you feel nervous and what's happening around you.
questions.explanation: By paying attention, you can see if there are specific things that make you nervous – like performing in front of people.

questions.prompt: If you notice you always get angry when someone takes your crayon, what could you do?

mt_LuwHnQItF_.title: The Mighty Medieval Church
explanation: Long, long ago, during a time called the Middle Ages or Medieval Times, the church was incredibly powerful. It wasn't just a place to pray; it controlled many things in people’s lives! The Church owned huge amounts of land – farms, villages, and even castles – which meant they had lots of money and resources. They also ran schools, so they taught everyone how to read and write, giving them control over knowledge.

Monks and nuns were the people who lived in monasteries, special homes for religious people. These men and women dedicated their lives to God, studying books, praying, and helping others. Imagine a monk like Brother Thomas – he might spend his day copying important texts by hand, carefully writing them out with beautiful letters. Nuns did similar things, often caring for the sick or teaching children.

People also traveled long distances on something called a pilgrimage to holy places. Think of it like a really special trip! Pilgrims believed visiting these sites would help them get closer to God and maybe even be forgiven for their sins. A famous pilgrimage was to Jerusalem, where Jesus lived, and people from all over Europe made the journey.
keyIdeas: The Church owned land and resources.
Monks and nuns dedicated their lives to prayer and learning.
Pilgrimages were religious journeys to holy places.

questions.prompt: Why was the Medieval Church so powerful?
choices: A. They had lots of land and money.
C. Only kings listened to them.
questions.explanation: The church controlled a lot of land, which gave them wealth and power.

questions.prompt: What did monks and nuns mostly do?
choices: C. They played games all day.
questions.explanation: Monks and Nuns spent their time studying religious texts and praying.

questions.prompt: Why did people go on pilgrimages?
choices: B. To get closer to God.
questions.explanation: Pilgrims believed visiting holy places would help them connect with God.

mt_lSFwVU7V9g.title: Exploring Ancient Egypt: Upper and Lower
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs – sometimes you have a long, skinny brick, and other times you have a wide, flat one. Ancient Egypt was like that too! It wasn't just one big country; it was divided into two parts called Upper Egypt and Lower Egypt. Upper Egypt was in the south of Egypt, along a narrow valley where the Nile River flowed steeply, almost like a long, skinny riverbed. Lower Egypt was in the north, forming a wide delta – that’s like where a river spreads out into many branches before reaching the sea.

The Nile River was super important to both Upper and Lower Egypt. The land south of the river, Upper Egypt, had steep hills and cliffs, while the land north of the river, Lower Egypt, was flat and sandy because it was built up by the river’s flow. Because of this difference in landscape, people living in each area developed different ways of farming and building! They used these differences to their advantage, creating amazing things like pyramids and temples.

The pharaohs, who were like kings, realized that working together made Egypt stronger. They united Upper and Lower Egypt under one rule, which was a really big achievement! To help farmers grow food all year round, they built canals and used tools called shadufs – these were like levers with buckets to lift water from the river into their fields. This clever system allowed them to grow crops even when it wasn’t raining.
keyIdeas: Egypt was divided into Upper and Lower based on landscape.
The Nile River was crucial for farming and transportation.
Pharaohs united Egypt for a stronger kingdom.

questions.prompt: Which part of Ancient Egypt had steep hills and cliffs?
questions.explanation: Upper Egypt was located in a narrow valley with steep slopes.

questions.prompt: What were shadufs used for?
choices: B. Lifting water from the river
questions.explanation: Shadufs were tools that helped farmers lift water to their fields.

questions.prompt: Why was uniting Upper and Lower Egypt important?
choices: C. To create a stronger kingdom
questions.explanation: Uniting the lands allowed for better organization and leadership.

mt_a6AYrbb7x4.title: Understanding Our Feelings About Others
explanation: Let’s talk about how we understand what other people are feeling and thinking. Social awareness is like being a good detective for people – it means noticing things about others and trying to see the world from their point of view. Sometimes, we make quick judgments about people based on small bits of information, which can be tricky and sometimes unfair. It’s important to remember that everyone is different and has their own unique experiences.

One way we do this is through something called a ‘stereotype.’ A stereotype is like a simple idea we have about a group of people – it might be that all boys are good at sports or that all girls love pink. These ideas aren’t always true, and they can cause problems because they don't allow us to see individuals for who they really are. 'Prejudice' is when we feel unfairly positive or negative feelings about someone *because* of a stereotype; it’s like having an opinion before you even know them.

Imagine your friend group has a favorite toy – maybe everyone loves LEGOs! That could be a ‘bias,’ meaning you all have a preference. However, if someone doesn't like LEGOs and feels left out because of this bias, that’s not fair. 'Equality' means giving everyone the same things, but sometimes that isn’t enough; 'equity' means giving people what they *need* to succeed – for example, some kids might need extra help with reading, so equity would mean providing that extra support.
keyIdeas: Stereotypes are simplified ideas about groups.
Prejudice is unfair feeling based on stereotypes.
Equity focuses on meeting individual needs.

questions.prompt: What does a stereotype mean?
choices: A. A fair way to treat everyone.
B. A simple idea about a group of people.
C. Feeling happy and excited about something.
questions.explanation: A stereotype is a basic belief we have about a group, not necessarily true.

questions.prompt: Which best describes ‘equity’?
choices: A. Giving everyone the same thing.
B. Giving people what they need to succeed.
questions.explanation: Equity means providing support based on individual needs, not just equal distribution.

questions.prompt: Why is discrimination unfair?
questions.explanation: Discrimination happens when someone is treated differently and unfairly because of their group identity (like race or gender). It’s a violation of fairness and respect.

mt_lcf8lx-LkZ.title: Contact & Non-Contact Forces
explanation: Notice that some forces need contact between two objects (contact forces) while magnetic forces can act at a distance (non-contact forces)
You know this idea when you can: Define contact forces as those needing objects to touch (e.g. push, pull, friction); Define non-contact forces as those acting at a distance (e.g. magnetism).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Define contact forces as those needing objects to touch (e.g. push, pull, friction)
Define non-contact forces as those acting at a distance (e.g. magnetism)
Sort examples of forces into contact and non-contact categories

questions.prompt: True or false: Define contact forces as those needing objects to touch (e.g. push, pull, friction)
questions.explanation: Define contact forces as those needing objects to touch (e.g. push, pull, friction)

questions.prompt: True or false: Define non-contact forces as those acting at a distance (e.g. magnetism)
questions.explanation: Define non-contact forces as those acting at a distance (e.g. magnetism)

questions.prompt: True or false: Sort examples of forces into contact and non-contact categories
questions.explanation: Sort examples of forces into contact and non-contact categories

mt_0zqOTjjW2k.title: Desert Animals: Amazing Survivors!
explanation: The desert is a really special place – it’s hot and dry, unlike most places we live! Many animals have learned to survive in these harsh conditions. Let's meet some amazing creatures like camels, fennec foxes, scorpions, and rattlesnakes. These animals are perfectly adapted to living where there isn’t much water or shade.

Desert animals use clever tricks to stay cool and find food. For example, camels have big humps that store fat – this fat can be turned into energy when they need it! Some animals, like meerkats, dig burrows underground during the hottest part of the day to keep themselves safe from the sun's heat.  They also often become active at night when it’s cooler.

Deserts aren’t always hot; some can get very cold at night! But one thing is always true: deserts are dry – they don’t have much rain or snow. Animals like fennec foxes have huge ears that help them to lose heat, just like a radiator! These adaptations allow them to thrive in this challenging environment.
keyIdeas: Deserts are hot and dry.
Animals adapt to survive in the desert.
Adaptations include being nocturnal and burrowing.

questions.prompt: Which animal uses its big humps to store fat for energy?
questions.explanation: Camels store fat in their humps, which they can use as a food source when water is scarce.

questions.prompt: Why do meerkats dig burrows underground?
choices: B) To stay cool during the day
questions.explanation: Burrows provide shade and protection from the intense desert heat.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes a desert?
choices: A) It’s always cold and snowy.
C) It has lots of rain and trees.
questions.explanation: Deserts are known for their high temperatures and lack of rainfall.

mt_19j_5AuuQI.title: Number Line Adventures!
explanation: Let's learn about number lines! A number line is like a road that shows all the whole numbers – that means zero, one, two, and so on. We use it to help us understand how big or small numbers are and how they change when we add or take away. Think of your favorite building blocks; you can arrange them in a row to show their sizes, just like we do with a number line!

When we add, we jump forward on the number line! For example, if we want to show 38 plus 27, we start at 38 and then make 27 jumps forward. Each jump represents one number, so we land on a bigger number. It's like when you’re playing tag and someone is ‘adding’ themselves to the game by jumping towards you!

Subtraction works just like adding, but this time we jump backward! If we want to show 52 minus 18, we start at 52 and make 18 jumps back. Each jump brings us closer to zero, so we end up with a smaller number.  It’s similar to taking away some of your toys from a pile – you're reducing the total!
keyIdeas: Number lines show whole numbers.
Addition is shown by jumping forward on a number line.
Subtraction is shown by jumping backward on a number line.

questions.prompt: If we want to show 25 + 13 on a number line, we would start at 25 and jump ____ spaces forward.
questions.explanation: We add 13 to 25, so we need to move 13 spaces forward.

questions.prompt: If we want to show 60 - 20 on a number line, we would start at 60 and jump ____ spaces backward.
questions.explanation: We subtract 20 from 60, so we need to move 20 spaces back.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows a number line correctly representing 45 + 17? (Imagine the numbers are marked on the line)
questions.explanation: This question requires visual understanding – encourage children to draw or imagine a number line.

mt_h-z88yf9Pn.title: Vocabulary: ethics and citizenship
explanation: Know and use the vocabulary of ethics and citizenship — bullying, cyberbullying, bystander, upstander, peer pressure, digital citizenship, rights, responsibility, and ethical — and understand the distinctions between these closely related terms

You know this idea when you can: Explain what 'ethics' means in their own words and give an example of an ethical dilemma; Use 'citizen', 'rights', and 'responsibilities' correctly — e.g. 'A citizen has the right to vote and the responsibility to follow laws'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Use 'citizen', 'rights', and 'responsibilities' correctly — e.g. 'A citizen has the right to vote and the responsibility to follow laws'
Define 'consent' and 'integrity' and explain why they matter in relationships and online behaviour

questions.explanation: Use 'citizen', 'rights', and 'responsibilities' correctly — e.g. 'A citizen has the right to vote and the responsibility to follow laws'

mt_uorNrPTh6U.title: Time, Place, and Cause Adventures!
explanation: Today we're going to learn how to tell stories in a really clear way! We can do this by using special words that connect ideas together. These words help us show when things happen, where they happen, and why they happen – like building blocks for our sentences. Let’s look at an example: Imagine you're playing with your LEGOs (that’s a place!), and you build a tower (that’s what happened!).

There are lots of ways to connect these ideas using words like ‘because’, ‘when’, ‘before’, or ‘after’. For instance, we could say ‘When you finish building the tower, you can add some LEGO people!’ See how ‘when’ tells us that one thing happens after another? Or maybe you 'Before you start playing, you need to build a strong base' – that shows cause and effect!

These words are like secret codes for telling stories! Using them helps us understand exactly what is happening, where it’s happening, and why it’s happening. When we write or talk, using these words makes our ideas much clearer and easier to follow. Let’s practice building our own sentences with these powerful words!
keyIdeas: Conjunctions connect ideas.
Adverbs show time and cause.
Prepositions tell us location and reason.

questions.prompt: I went to the park ______ it started raining. What word best connects these two ideas?
choices:
A) before
B) after
C) because
questions.explanation: The word ‘after’ shows that one thing happened following another.

questions.prompt: ______ you finish your homework, you can play a game. What word is used to show the order of events?
choices:
A) before
B) during
C) therefore
questions.explanation: ‘Before’ indicates that something happens earlier than another action.

questions.prompt: The dog barked ______ it saw a squirrel. What preposition shows the reason for the barking?
choices:
A) in
B) because of
C) during
questions.explanation: ‘Because of’ explains why the dog was barking – it's reacting to something.

mt_9yFAtUkoYr.title: Pronouns: Talking About People and Things!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how sometimes we repeat the same name over and over in a story or when talking to someone? It can get a little boring, right? Pronouns are words that help us avoid saying the same thing again and again. They’re like shortcuts – instead of saying ‘Sarah ran,’ we can say ‘she ran.’

Let's look at an example: Imagine you have a toy car, and your friend Michael is playing with it. You could say 'Michael drove his car,' but that's a lot to write! We can use the pronoun 'he' instead – 'He drove his car.' Pronouns make our writing and talking much clearer and smoother.

There are different kinds of pronouns, like ‘he,’ ‘she,’ ‘it,’ and ‘they.’ Reflexive pronouns, like ‘myself,’ ‘yourself,’ ‘himself,’ ‘herself,’ ‘itself,’ and ‘ourselves,’ are used when someone is doing something to themselves. For example, 'I made the cake myself' means you did it all on your own!
keyIdeas: Pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition.
Reflexive pronouns refer back to the same person or thing.
Using pronouns clearly helps make our writing and speaking easier to understand.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a pronoun correctly?
choices: A) The dog wagged its tail.
B) The dog wagged his tail.
C) The dog wagged her tail.
questions.explanation: The pronoun ‘its’ is correct because ‘it’ refers to the dog.

questions.prompt: What does the pronoun 'myself' mean?
choices: A) To give something to yourself.
B) To do something by yourself.
questions.explanation: ‘Myself’ is a reflexive pronoun, meaning you are doing something to yourself.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: ‘John told Mary he was tired.’ Who was tired?
questions.explanation: This sentence is ambiguous because 'he' could refer to either John or Mary. It needs more information to be clear.

mt_bhwf_rDXQL.title: The Ring of Fire!
explanation: Have you ever played with building blocks? Sometimes, when you stack them up high, they fall over! That's kind of like what happens with volcanoes and earthquakes. They mostly happen in a special area around the Pacific Ocean – we call it the Ring of Fire. This isn’t just a random collection of events; there’s a pattern to where these powerful forces appear.

The Ring of Fire is made up of many tectonic plates, which are like giant puzzle pieces that make up Earth's crust. These plates are always moving, slowly pushing against each other. When they bump and grind, it creates stress, and sometimes that stress builds up until the ground shakes – that’s an earthquake! Similarly, when molten rock (magma) from deep inside the Earth rises to the surface, it erupts as a volcano.

You can find the Ring of Fire if you look at a world map. It's a line circling almost the entire Pacific Ocean, and it includes places like Japan, California, Chile, and Indonesia. Scientists know that earthquakes and volcanoes aren’t scattered all over the Earth; they tend to group together in this specific zone because of how those tectonic plates are moving.
keyIdeas: Volcanoes and earthquakes often happen together.
The Ring of Fire is a line around the Pacific Ocean.
Plate tectonics cause movement that leads to these events.

questions.prompt: Where do most volcanoes and earthquakes occur on Earth?
choices: B. Around the Pacific Ocean’s Ring of Fire
questions.explanation: The Ring of Fire is a zone where tectonic plates meet, causing frequent volcanic activity and earthquakes.

questions.prompt: What causes earthquakes and volcanoes to happen?
choices: B. The movement of giant puzzle pieces (tectonic plates)
questions.explanation: The movement of tectonic plates creates stress that builds up until the ground shakes or magma rises.

questions.prompt: True or False: Earthquakes and volcanoes are spread evenly across the entire planet.
questions.explanation: No, they're mostly concentrated in a specific area called the Ring of Fire.

mt_vJUa62bxeR.title: Shape Explorers!
explanation: Today, we're going to become shape explorers! Shapes are everywhere around us – think about your LEGO bricks; they come in all sorts of shapes like squares and rectangles. We’ll learn how to draw these shapes carefully on paper, making sure the sides are straight and the corners are sharp.  Drawing accurately is important because it helps us understand how shapes work and how they can be built.

Now let's build with shapes! We can use things like straws and connectors to make 3-D shapes, just like a cube or a cuboid. A cube has six square faces, and a cuboid has six rectangular faces – it’s all about the sides meeting at corners.  Building these shapes helps us see how they fit together and understand their different sizes.

Sometimes we see 3-D shapes from different angles! Imagine looking at a pyramid – it might seem like one shape, but if you turn it around, you'll see that it’s made of triangles. This is called rotation, and it shows us that even though the same shape can look different depending on how we view it.
keyIdeas: 2-D shapes have straight sides.
3-D shapes are built from faces.
Shapes can be seen from different angles.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has four equal sides and four right angles?
questions.explanation: A square is defined by having four equal sides and four right (90-degree) angles.

questions.prompt: You are building a cube with straws. Which shape makes up one face of the cube?
questions.explanation: A cube has six square faces, and each square is a face of the cube.

questions.prompt: If you rotate a pyramid, what do you notice about its shape?
choices: A. It stays the same size.
B. It changes into a different shape.
questions.explanation: Rotating a 3D shape can change how we see it and reveal different faces or angles.

mt_iycQEai3dK.title: How Fossils Form
explanation: Explain in simple terms how fossils form: an organism dies and is quickly buried in sediment; over millions of years minerals replace the remains and the sediment turns to rock, preserving the shape

You know this idea when you can: Describe the basic sequence: organism dies, buried in sediment, minerals replace remains over time; Explain why fossilisation is rare — most organisms decompose before being buried.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Explain why fossilisation is rare — most organisms decompose before being buried

questions.explanation: Explain why fossilisation is rare — most organisms decompose before being buried

explanation: Distinguish between what a text explicitly says and what you have inferred, assumed, or read in — knowing which is which is fundamental to honest comprehension

mt_oDU_8zMZjp.title: Adding and Subtracting with Your Head!
explanation: Let's learn how to add and subtract numbers in our heads! It’s like a secret trick – you don’t always need paper and pencil. When we do mental math, we use the numbers we already know to figure out the answer quickly. This is really helpful when you’re buying something at a store or figuring out how many cookies you have left after sharing with a friend.

Imagine you have 345 building blocks and your friend gives you 7 more. To add them together mentally, we can focus on just the ones first. We know that 5 + 7 = 12, so we write down ‘12’ in the ones place of our answer. Then, we keep the hundreds and tens the same – we still have 345 blocks, just with an extra 12!

Now let's try subtracting! Suppose you had 462 crayons and gave 5 away to your little brother. Again, we start with the ones: 2 - 5 doesn’t work, so we ‘borrow’ one from the tens place – that makes our 6 become a 5, and our 2 becomes 12.  Then, 12 - 5 = 7, and finally, we have 462 - 5 = 457 crayons left.
keyIdeas: Focus on the ones digit first when adding or subtracting.
You can 'borrow' from a higher place value if needed to make your calculations work.
Mental math is a useful skill for everyday situations.

questions.prompt: What is 345 + 7?
questions.explanation: To add, we start with the ones: 5+7=12. We write down '2' in the ones place and carry over the '1' to the tens place. Then, 4 + 1 = 5, so the answer is 352.

questions.prompt: What is 462 - 5?
questions.explanation: To subtract, we start with the ones: 2-5 doesn't work. We 'borrow' one from the tens place (making 6 become 5) and add it to the two, so we have 12 - 5 = 7.  Then, 462 - 5 = 457.

questions.prompt: If you have 510 marbles and your friend gives you 28 more, how many do you have?
questions.explanation: First add 10 + 28 = 38. Then keep the 500 and add the 38 to get 538.  But we made a mistake! We need to add 10 + 20 + 8 = 38, then 500 + 38 = 538.

mt_vauULTecMH.title: Narrative Writing
explanation: Write narratives with developed settings, characters and plot, using dialogue and description to develop experiences and show character responses to situations

You know this idea when you can: Write a narrative that includes a described setting, at least one developed character, and a clear plot with a problem and resolution; Use dialogue to show what characters say and reveal their personality or feelings.

Use dialogue to show what characters say and reveal their personality or feelings
