mt_1LFVPjdGg-.title: Rainforest Food Webs!
explanation: Imagine you're walking through a rainforest – it’s amazing, right? The rainforest is full of life, and everything in the rainforest depends on something else for food. Plants are super important because they make their own food using sunlight, this process is called photosynthesis. These plants are called ‘producers,’ like the giant trees that give shade to all the animals.

Animals eat other things to get energy! Herbivores, like monkeys and sloths, eat only plants – so they’re ‘primary consumers.’ Then there are predators, such as jaguars or snakes, who hunt and eat herbivores. These are ‘secondary consumers,’ meaning they eat something that already ate a plant. It's all connected in a big circle of eating!

But what happens when an animal dies? That’s where decomposers come in! Fungi and insects break down dead plants and animals, turning them back into nutrients. These nutrients go back into the soil, which helps new plants grow – it’s like a giant recycling system that keeps the rainforest healthy and full of life.
keyIdeas: Producers make their own food.
Consumers eat other living things.
Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead organisms.

questions.prompt: Which animal is a producer in the rainforest?
questions.explanation: Trees use sunlight to make their own food, unlike animals which need to eat.

questions.prompt: What do herbivores eat?
questions.explanation: Herbivores are plant-eaters. Think of a sloth munching on leaves!

questions.prompt: Why are decomposers important in the rainforest?
choices: B) They break down dead things
questions.explanation: Decomposers return nutrients to the soil, helping plants grow. Without them, nothing would recycle!

mt_MlD0gwLSw9.title: Roman Army and Conquest of Britain
explanation: Describe how the Roman army was organised into legions of highly trained soldiers, how Julius Caesar first raided Britain in 55 BC and Emperor Claudius later conquered it in AD 43, and explain why the Romans wanted to expand their empire — for land, resources, taxes, and glory
You know this idea when you can: Describe a Roman legion as a large unit of highly trained, disciplined soldiers; Explain that Julius Caesar raided Britain first and Claudius conquered it later.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe a Roman legion as a large unit of highly trained, disciplined soldiers
Explain that Julius Caesar raided Britain first and Claudius conquered it later
Give at least one reason why the Romans invaded Britain

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe a Roman legion as a large unit of highly trained, disciplined soldiers
questions.explanation: Describe a Roman legion as a large unit of highly trained, disciplined soldiers

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that Julius Caesar raided Britain first and Claudius conquered it later
questions.explanation: Explain that Julius Caesar raided Britain first and Claudius conquered it later

questions.prompt: True or false: Give at least one reason why the Romans invaded Britain
questions.explanation: Give at least one reason why the Romans invaded Britain

mt_aVZJhPbc_1.title: Reading Detectives: Unlocking Text Secrets
explanation: Let's become reading detectives! When we read, it’s not just about the words on the page; authors use special tools to help us understand and enjoy their stories or information. These tools are called text features – things like headings, pictures, bold words, and how the pages are arranged. Just like a detective looks for clues, we can look for these features to discover what the author is trying to show us.

Authors carefully choose their words to make you feel certain ways! For example, if an author writes 'The little boy crept through the dark forest,' using the word ‘crept’ instead of ‘walked’ makes you imagine a sneaky and exciting adventure. Similarly, if a book has lots of big, bold words, that might mean those words are really important for understanding the story or explaining something tricky.  These choices help create a specific feeling or effect.

Text features aren't just decorations; they actually help us! Headings tell you what each section is about, so you can quickly find information. Diagrams show you how things work – like how a plant grows or how a machine operates. And bold words often highlight key vocabulary, making it easier to learn new words and understand the text better.
keyIdeas: Authors use word choices to create feelings.
Text structure (like headings) helps you find information quickly.
Presentational features (pictures, bold print) add meaning to a text.

questions.prompt: The author used the word 'crept' instead of 'walked'. What feeling does this make you think about?
choices: A. A happy walk in the park
B. A sneaky and tense adventure
questions.explanation: The word ‘crept’ suggests something secretive and potentially dangerous.

questions.prompt: Why do you think a book might have headings?
choices: A. To make the pages look pretty.
B. To help you quickly find information about each section.
C. To confuse you with lots of words
questions.explanation: Headings act as signposts, guiding you through the text.

questions.prompt: You see a diagram showing how a bicycle works. What is the main purpose of the diagram?
questions.explanation: Diagrams are visual representations that explain processes or concepts.

mt_Zy-CKUkq34.title: The Crusades: Adventures in the Holy Land
explanation: Long ago, people believed that a very special place called Jerusalem – where Jesus was born and died – was being controlled by Muslims. Christians wanted to take it back and make it their holy city again. So, around 1066, groups of European knights, which are like brave warriors, started traveling across the sea to fight for control of Jerusalem in a series of wars called the Crusades.

These knights weren't just fighting for land; they were also hoping to learn new things! They traveled through Islamic lands – that’s where people who followed Islam, a religion different from Christianity, lived. Europeans saw amazing cities like Baghdad and Damascus, full of clever inventions like windmills and advanced mathematics that the Muslims had developed.

However, it's important to remember that the Crusades weren't always peaceful. There was a lot of fighting between Christians and Muslims, and many people suffered during these wars. Both sides did terrible things, and sadly, many lives were lost. It’s a complicated part of history we need to understand with respect for everyone involved.
keyIdeas: The Crusades were religious wars.
Europeans traveled to the Holy Land seeking Jerusalem.
Cultural exchange occurred between Christian Europe and the Islamic world.

questions.prompt: Why did European knights travel to the Holy Land?
choices: A. To find gold and jewels
B. To fight for control of Jerusalem
questions.explanation: The knights wanted to take back Jerusalem, a holy city for Christians.

questions.prompt: What did Europeans learn from the Islamic world during the Crusades?
questions.explanation: Muslims had developed clever inventions like windmills and advanced math that Europeans saw.

questions.prompt: The Crusades involved violence. True or False?
questions.explanation: Yes, there was a lot of fighting between Christians and Muslims during the Crusades.

mt_R7LEuZjTmx.title: The Amazing World of Minibeasts!
explanation: Have you ever looked closely at a garden or a park and noticed all the tiny creatures crawling around? These are minibeasts – like insects, spiders, worms, snails, and centipedes! They’re actually the most numerous and diverse group of animals on Earth; there are more types of beetles than any other animal group. Scientists believe there are millions of different kinds of minibeasts living all over the world, making them incredibly important to our planet.

Minibeasts do some really amazing jobs! Some, like bees and butterflies, are pollinators – that means they help flowers grow by carrying pollen from one flower to another. Others, such as woodlice and worms, are decomposers; they break down dead plants and animals, returning important nutrients back into the soil. And many minibeasts, like beetles and grasshoppers, are food for bigger animals, creating a chain of life.

Let’s look at some differences! Most insects have six legs – that's how you can tell them apart from spiders, who have eight legs. Spiders are still minibeasts, but they hunt their own food instead of eating plants like many insects do. It’s fantastic to think about all the different roles these little creatures play in keeping our world healthy and balanced!
keyIdeas: Minibeasts are the most numerous animals on Earth.
They have important jobs as pollinators, decomposers, and food sources.
Insects have six legs while spiders have eight.

questions.prompt: Which group of animals is the MOST numerous on Earth?
questions.explanation: Minibeasts, including insects, are the most diverse and abundant animal group.

questions.prompt: What does a bee do that helps plants grow?
questions.explanation: Bees carry pollen from flower to flower, which is how they help plants make seeds.

questions.prompt: How many legs does a typical insect have?
questions.explanation: Most insects have six legs – this is a key way to identify them!

mt_i1kk9HDctI.title: Building Stronger You: Resilience and Self-Control
explanation: Let’s talk about being strong, not just physically, but inside too! When something difficult happens – like when you're building with LEGOs and it keeps falling apart, or when your favorite team loses a game – it’s important to know how to bounce back. This is what we call ‘resilience,’ which means having the ability to get back up after you fall down and keep trying. Think of a bouncy ball; it bounces again and again, even if it hits the ground hard!

Sometimes, our thoughts can make things harder or easier. A 'growth mindset' is believing that your abilities can be developed through dedication and hard work. For example, instead of saying ‘I’m not good at drawing,’ you could say, ‘I can’t draw it perfectly *yet*, but I can practice and get better with time.’ This way of thinking helps us face challenges with a positive attitude and a willingness to learn.

‘Self-regulation’ is all about managing your feelings and actions. It's like having an inside helper that tells you how to react when you feel frustrated or upset. One strategy could be taking deep breaths, counting to ten, or asking for help – these are ways we can control our reactions and not let a setback stop us from trying again.
keyIdeas: Resilience: Bouncing back after challenges.
Growth Mindset: Believing you can improve with effort.
Self-Regulation: Managing your feelings and actions.

questions.prompt: If you’re learning to ride a bike and keep falling, what does ‘resilience’ mean?
choices: B. Trying again even when it's hard
questions.explanation: Resilience means having the strength to keep going despite difficulties.

questions.prompt: Your friend says, ‘I’m not good at math.’ What kind of mindset does that show?
questions.explanation: A fixed mindset believes abilities are set and cannot be changed.

questions.prompt: You’re really angry because you lost a game. What is one way to use self-regulation?
choices: B. Taking deep breaths and asking for help
C. Ignoring everyone and shutting down
questions.explanation: Self-regulation involves managing your emotions in a healthy way.

mt_pAuo9Op89t.title: Growth Mindset
explanation: Understand the concept of a growth mindset — that abilities and intelligence can grow with effort, practice, and good strategies — as opposed to a fixed mindset where you believe you're either good at something or you're not
You know this idea when you can: Explain the difference between growth mindset and fixed mindset in their own words; Use 'yet' language when describing something they find difficult.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Explain the difference between growth mindset and fixed mindset in their own words
Use 'yet' language when describing something they find difficult
Give an example of something they got better at through practice and effort

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain the difference between growth mindset and fixed mindset in their own words
questions.explanation: Explain the difference between growth mindset and fixed mindset in their own words

questions.prompt: True or false: Use 'yet' language when describing something they find difficult
questions.explanation: Use 'yet' language when describing something they find difficult

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of something they got better at through practice and effort
questions.explanation: Give an example of something they got better at through practice and effort

mt_miGrca8zaS.title: Super Steps to Big Tasks!
explanation: Have you ever looked at a really big LEGO set and felt totally overwhelmed? It can seem like there are thousands of pieces, and it’s hard to know where to even start building! That's because big tasks can feel scary when we think about doing everything all at once. Instead of getting stressed, we can break the task into smaller, easier steps – just like building with LEGOs!

Let’s say you need to clean your room. It might seem like a huge job, but if you take it one step at a time, it's much less daunting. You could start by picking up all your toys and putting them in the toy box (that’s Step 1!). Then, you could make your bed – that’s Step 2! And finally, you can tidy up your desk - Step 3!

When we break a task into steps, it's like giving ourselves little wins. Each time you finish one step, you feel proud and motivated to do the next one. Celebrating those small successes makes even the biggest tasks seem much more manageable and fun! Remember, progress is better than perfection.
keyIdeas: Break down large tasks.
Focus on one step at a time.
Celebrate your accomplishments.

questions.prompt: You're writing a long story, and it feels too hard. What’s the first thing you should do?
questions.explanation: Breaking down a big task into smaller steps is key! Starting with just one paragraph makes the story seem less overwhelming.

questions.prompt: Your room is messy. What's a good first step to cleaning it?
choices: A) Immediately start scrubbing the floor.
B) Pick up all your toys and put them away.
C) Organize your clothes in the closet.
questions.explanation: Starting with one manageable task, like gathering your toys, is much easier than tackling everything at once.

questions.prompt: You’ve finished writing the first part of a school project. What should you do next?
choices: A) Start working on the conclusion immediately.
B) Take a break and celebrate your progress.
C) Ask for help from a friend right away.
questions.explanation: Acknowledging your progress after completing each step helps you stay motivated!

mt_Qzbh-_v0Gq.title: My Money Plan!
explanation: Imagine you have £10 – that’s like the money you might get from your parents for being good! A budget is simply a plan for how you spend that money. It's like making a map to help you decide what you really want to buy, instead of just spending it all at once and then saying ‘Oh no, I don’t have anything left!’ Planning helps us use our money wisely.

When you make a budget, you think about how much different things cost. For example, a small toy might cost £3, a candy bar could be £1.50, and maybe some stickers would be around £0.75.  A good budget shows you exactly how much money you have and what you can buy with it – it’s like making sure your spending doesn't go over the amount you started with.

Sometimes we want to buy lots of things, but we don’t have enough money! That’s where ‘trade-offs’ come in. A trade-off means choosing one thing and giving up something else.  Like, if you spend £8 on a cool game, you won't be able to buy that yummy ice cream – it’s about making choices!
keyIdeas: A budget is a plan.
Trade-offs help us make decisions.
Planning spending saves money.

questions.prompt: What does a budget help you do?
choices: A. Spend all your money at once
B. Plan how to spend your money
questions.explanation: A budget helps you plan how much you can spend so you don’t run out of money.

questions.prompt: You have £10 and want to buy a toy that costs £7 and some stickers that cost £2. How much money will you have left?
questions.explanation: £7 (toy) + £2 (stickers) = £9, so you’ll have £10 - £9 = £1 left.

questions.prompt: You really want a new book and a game. You only have £10. What is an example of a trade-off you might make?
questions.explanation: Choosing to buy the book instead of the game – you give up buying the game to get the book.

mt_doVAdMqfJg.title: Castle Design Through the Ages
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to learn about castles! Long ago, people needed strong places to live and protect themselves. Early castles started as simple wooden structures called motte-and-bailey castles. These were built on a raised hill – we call this the ‘motte’ – and had a courtyard below, which is like an open area for soldiers and animals; they weren't very fancy but they were good at stopping attackers.

As time went on, people realized wooden castles could be easily burned down. So, clever builders started using stone to make stronger castles! Later stone castles had thick walls, towers, and a ‘keep’ – this was the strongest part of the castle where the lord or lady lived and kept important things like weapons and food. These castles were much more difficult for enemies to break through.

Over many years, castle designs changed again because people found new ways to attack. For example, they built concentric castles with walls inside walls, making it even harder for attackers to get close! They also added arrow slits – small windows that allowed archers to shoot at enemies without exposing themselves. It’s amazing how castles evolved to stay safe!
keyIdeas: Castles started as simple wooden structures.
Stone castles were stronger and more resistant to attack.
Castle designs changed over time to defend against new weapons.

questions.prompt: Which of these was the first type of castle built?
questions.explanation: Motte-and-bailey castles were the earliest form, built on a hill with a wooden courtyard.

questions.prompt: What was the purpose of the ‘keep’ in a medieval castle?
choices: A. To store food and supplies
B. To be where the lord or lady lived
C. To defend against attacks from above
questions.explanation: The keep was the strongest part of the castle, serving as the lord's residence.

questions.prompt: Why did castles start being built with stone instead of wood?
choices: A. Stone was easier to find
B. Stone castles were much stronger and harder to burn down
C. People just preferred building with stone
questions.explanation: Stone provided a far more durable defense against attacks, especially fire.

mt_OtShuvs3x8.title: Rainforest Explorers: Sorting Amazing Animals!
explanation: Imagine you're a rainforest explorer! Rainforests are super exciting places filled with tons of different animals and plants. Scientists need to sort all these creatures into groups so they can learn more about them. Just like when you sort your LEGO bricks by color or shape, scientists use groups to make sense of the amazing variety of life in the rainforest.

Let’s look at some examples: Jaguars are mammals because they have fur and give birth to babies. Toucans are birds because they have beaks for eating fruit and can fly! Snakes are reptiles because they don't have feathers or fur, and they live on land. These groups help us understand what each animal needs – like food, shelter, and how they move around.

When scientists classify things, it means they’re finding out what makes something special and unique. This helps them study the rainforest better because they know where to look for certain animals or plants. It's like having a giant puzzle with all the pieces – once you sort the pieces into groups, you can see the whole picture!
keyIdeas: Animals are grouped by shared features.
Classification helps scientists understand diversity.
Sorting organisms makes studying nature easier.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals is a mammal?
questions.explanation: Mammals are warm-blooded animals that have fur or hair and feed their babies milk.

questions.prompt: What helps birds fly?
questions.explanation: Birds use feathers to flap their wings and lift themselves into the air.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists classify plants?
choices: A) To make them look pretty
B) To understand how they grow and what they need
C) To build houses for them
questions.explanation: Classifying plants helps scientists learn about their needs, like sunlight and water.

mt_6nqVnVdexe.title: Daily Life in a Roman Town
explanation: Describe daily life in a Roman town — the forum (marketplace and meeting place), public baths, amphitheatre, and villas — and explain that the Romans were brilliant engineers who built straight roads, aqueducts to carry water, underfloor heating (hypocaust), and Hadrian's Wall to mark the empire's northern frontier in Britain
You know this idea when you can: Describe at least three features of a Roman town such as forum, baths, or amphitheatre; Explain what an aqueduct was and why the Romans built them.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe at least three features of a Roman town such as forum, baths, or amphitheatre
Explain what an aqueduct was and why the Romans built them
Describe Hadrian's Wall and say why it was built

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe at least three features of a Roman town such as forum, baths, or amphitheatre
questions.explanation: Describe at least three features of a Roman town such as forum, baths, or amphitheatre

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain what an aqueduct was and why the Romans built them
questions.explanation: Explain what an aqueduct was and why the Romans built them

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe Hadrian's Wall and say why it was built
questions.explanation: Describe Hadrian's Wall and say why it was built

mt_35-DhMh_Yr.title: Super You: Talking to Yourself!
explanation: Sometimes, when we're having a tough time, our brains can send us some not-so-nice messages. Imagine you’re building with LEGOs and it keeps falling apart – you might think, 'I’m so bad at this! I can’t do anything right!' That thought makes you feel frustrated and maybe even want to give up on building altogether. Recognizing these thoughts is the first step in becoming a super builder (or a super anything!).

Our words have power – they affect how we feel inside. If you keep saying negative things like 'I’m not good enough,' it can make you feel sad and worried, which makes it even harder to try new things. But what if you changed those thoughts? You could say something like, ‘This is tricky, but I'm learning,’ or ‘I’m getting better with each piece!’

When we use positive self-talk, it’s like giving ourselves a little boost of encouragement! It helps us believe in ourselves and keeps us motivated when things get difficult. Think about your favorite toy – you probably talk to it kindly, right?  Treating yourself the same way can make all the difference.
keyIdeas: Recognize negative thoughts.
Replace them with positive statements.
Positive self-talk boosts confidence and motivation.

questions.prompt: You're struggling to solve a puzzle. You think, 'I’m so stupid! I can never figure this out.' What could you say instead?
choices: B) ‘This is hard, but I can keep trying.’
questions.explanation: Saying ‘This is hard, but I can keep trying’ shows that you're willing to work through the challenge and believe in yourself.

questions.prompt: Your friend tells you your drawing isn’t very good. You feel sad. What positive thing could you tell yourself?
choices: B) ‘I can always improve my drawings.’
questions.explanation: Saying 'I can always improve my drawings' focuses on the opportunity to learn and grow, rather than dwelling on a negative comment.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to use positive self-talk?
choices: A) It makes you feel worse about yourself.
B) It helps you believe in your abilities.
C) It doesn't matter what you say.
questions.explanation: Positive self-talk builds confidence and encourages you to keep trying, even when things are difficult.

mt_m6UaSmrQVG.title: Stopping Erosion!
explanation: Have you ever built a sandcastle at the beach and watched the waves slowly wash it away? That’s erosion! Erosion is when wind or water slowly changes the shape of land. It happens because these forces are constantly moving and wearing down rocks, soil, and even mountains over a very long time – like millions of years!

Think about how rain washes dirt from your garden after a big storm, or how strong winds can blow sand across a desert. Erosion isn’t always bad; it helps create new landforms! But sometimes, erosion is too powerful and we want to slow it down before it changes things too much, like when we build a playground.

There are lots of ways we can stop erosion. One way is to build walls – called retaining walls – to block the water from flowing quickly. Another great idea is planting grass or trees; their roots hold the soil together and slow down both wind and water. Let’s explore which of these methods works best!
keyIdeas: Erosion means land changes shape because of wind or water.
Retaining walls and plants can help stop erosion.
We can choose the best solution based on how well it protects the land.

questions.prompt: What is erosion?
choices: B. When wind or water changes the shape of land
questions.explanation: Erosion means that things like wind and water are slowly changing the shape of the ground.

questions.prompt: Which of these would help stop erosion from rain?
questions.explanation: Planting grass helps hold the soil together and slows down the water.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're building a small hill with dirt. Which would be the BEST way to stop it from washing away?
questions.explanation: Consider both retaining walls and planting vegetation – which one do you think would be most effective?

mt_DCelLx_H1A.title: Rainforest Adventures!
explanation: Imagine a place bursting with color, sounds, and amazing creatures – that’s the rainforest! Rainforests are found in warm places close to the equator, like near Brazil or Africa. They're super important because they have more different kinds of plants and animals than almost any other place on Earth; it’s like a giant treasure chest for wildlife!

Rainforests aren't just one big jungle; they have layers! The highest layer is called the canopy, which is full of tall trees where monkeys, toucans, and parrots love to live. Below that is the understory, with smaller plants and animals hiding in the shade. Finally, there’s the forest floor – it’s dark and damp, home to jaguars, frogs, snakes, and insects.

These rainforests are hot and wet because they're close to the equator, where the sun shines brightly all year round. This warmth and rain help create a perfect environment for so many different animals and plants to thrive! Think about your favorite toy – it needs a certain place to be happy, just like these amazing rainforest creatures.
keyIdeas: Rainforests have layers: canopy, understory, forest floor.
They’re found near the equator and are hot and wet.
Rainforests are incredibly diverse – they have more animal species than almost any other habitat.

questions.prompt: Which layer of the rainforest is home to toucans and monkeys?
questions.explanation: The canopy is the highest level, filled with tall trees where these animals live.

questions.prompt: Why are rainforests found near the equator?
choices: A. Because they’re cold and snowy
B. Because it's hot and wet there
C. Because they’re close to the ocean
questions.explanation: The heat and rain of the equator create the perfect conditions for rainforests to grow.

questions.prompt: Which animal lives on the forest floor?
questions.explanation: Jaguars are powerful predators that hunt on the dark, damp forest floor.

mt_VFsuftfvYM.title: Numbers Up To 1000!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn how to read and write big numbers – all the way up to 1000! Numbers are everywhere around us; think about your LEGOs. You might have a box with lots of bricks, and each brick has a number on it, right? We use these numbers to count things and understand how many there are.

When we write a number like 342, we can say it in words as ‘three hundred and forty-two.’ The first part, 'three hundred,' tells us the biggest group of one hundred. Then, 'forty' is a group of ten, and finally, ‘two’ is just two single ones.  Writing numbers in words helps us understand what they mean!

We can also write numbers in a special way called expanded form. This means breaking down the number into its parts – like 642 becomes 600 + 40 + 2. Each plus sign (+) shows that we’re adding those groups of one hundred, ten, and one together to get the whole number.
keyIdeas: Numbers can be read aloud.
We can write numbers in words.
Expanded form helps us understand how big a number is.

questions.prompt: What does ‘three hundred and forty-two’ mean?
choices: A. Three tens and four ones
B. Three hundreds, forty tens, and two ones
C. Three thousands, forty tens, and two ones
questions.explanation: This number is broken down into groups of one hundred (three hundreds), ten (forty tens), and one (two ones).

questions.prompt: Which of these shows the number 500 + 300 + 100 in expanded form?
questions.explanation: This question tests understanding of how to represent a number using its place values.

questions.prompt: Can you read the number 876 aloud?
questions.explanation: Encourage child to state: ‘eight hundred and seventy-six’

mt_I9iSzpGRn5.title: What is Bullying?
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs, and another child keeps taking your bricks without asking or even letting you play. That wouldn’t be fair, would it? Bullying is like that – it’s when someone does something repeatedly to hurt another person, either physically, by saying mean things, or by leaving someone out on purpose. It's important to understand that a one-time argument with a friend isn't bullying; bullying happens over and over again with the same intention to cause harm.

There are different ways people try to bully others. Sometimes it’s physical, like pushing or hitting. Other times, they say mean things, calling someone names or telling them they're stupid. Social bullying can be tricky – it might involve excluding a friend from a game or spreading rumors about them. And now, there’s cyberbullying, which happens online through texts, social media, and games where people are being treated badly.

If you see someone being bullied, or if you're being bullied yourself, it’s really important to tell a grown-up you trust – like your parents, a teacher, or another family member. You can also try to help the person who is being bullied by standing up for them, but only if you feel safe doing so. Remember, bullying is never okay, and there are people who care about you and want to help!
keyIdeas: Bullying is repeated harmful behavior.
There are many forms of bullying – physical, verbal, social, and cyberbullying.
Tell a trusted adult if you experience or witness bullying.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a form of bullying?
choices: C. Having a disagreement with a friend.
questions.explanation: A disagreement is just one-time conflict, not repeated harmful behavior.

questions.prompt: You see your classmate being left out of a game at recess. What should you do?
choices: A. Ignore it; it’s their problem.
B. Tell a teacher or adult about what you saw.
C. Join the game and try to include them.
questions.explanation: It's important to tell someone so they can help the person being left out.

questions.prompt: Someone is sending mean messages to another student on a phone app. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Cyberbullying uses technology to cause harm.

mt_yCmYV9ruQu.title: Be an Upstander!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs and your friend keeps taking your bricks without asking. That’s a situation where someone might just watch, or they could be an upstander! A bystander is someone who sees something happening but doesn’t do anything about it – like quietly watching that friend take your LEGOs. An upstander, on the other hand, steps in to help or stop the unkind behavior; they make a choice to act.

It's important to know there are different ways to be an upstander. Sometimes, you can gently tell your friend that taking someone’s toys without asking isn’t nice. Other times, it might mean walking away from the situation and getting help from a grown-up – like telling a teacher or parent what happened.  Being an upstander is about using your voice and courage to make things better.

Let's say you see kids at the playground pushing each other on the swings. A bystander would just watch, but an upstander might tell the children that pushing isn’t fair or ask a grown-up for help.  It takes bravery to stand up for what’s right, and even small actions can make a big difference in someone's day!
keyIdeas: Bystanders watch but don't act.
Upstanders take action to help.
Everyone has the power to be an upstander.

questions.prompt: What does a bystander do when they see someone being treated unfairly?
choices: A. Steps in and helps them
B. Watches but doesn't get involved
C. Runs away to avoid trouble
questions.explanation: A bystander is someone who sees something happening without taking action.

questions.prompt: Which of these actions would make you an upstander?
choices: A. Ignoring the situation and hoping it goes away
B. Telling a trusted adult what happened
C. Joining in on the unkind behavior
questions.explanation: An upstander takes action to help someone who is being treated unfairly.

questions.prompt: Imagine you see someone left out of a game at school. What could you do?
questions.explanation: Think about ways you can include others or get an adult's help.

mt_TiQbi027PE.title: Adding Up Adventures!
explanation: Let’s talk about adding numbers! Adding means putting things together to find out how many there are in all. When we add, we're like building something – we start with a small piece and then add more pieces until we have a whole collection. Think about your LEGO bricks; you can add different colors and shapes together to build amazing creations!

Adding numbers can seem tricky if you just start writing them down one after another. But there’s a super smart way to do it! We can break the problem into smaller parts by looking at the tens and ones separately. For example, let's say we want to add 14 + 23 + 32 + 19 – we can first look at just the tens: 10 + 20 + 30 + 10.

By grouping the numbers like this, it becomes much easier. We know that 10 + 10 = 20, and 20 + 20 = 40. Then we can add the ones: 4 + 3 + 2 + 9 = 18. Finally, we combine the tens and ones to get our answer! This strategy helps us understand where the numbers are coming from.
keyIdeas: Break down addition problems into smaller parts.
Focus on adding tens first, then ones.
Grouping numbers makes addition easier.

questions.prompt: Let’s add 25 + 34 + 18. First, let's look at the tens. What is 20 + 30?
questions.explanation: Adding 20 and 30 gives you 50, which is the first step in solving this problem.

questions.prompt: You are adding 16 + 28 + 35. What do you do with the ones?
choices: A) Add all the ones together
questions.explanation: When adding numbers, we always start by adding the ones column.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have 12 stickers, your friend gives you 17 more, and then you get 8. How many stickers do you have in total?
questions.explanation: This question tests your ability to apply the addition strategy to a real-world scenario.

mt_CSGqz245rV.title: Athens vs. Sparta: Two Different Greek Cities
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to Ancient Greece! Long, long ago, there were many different city-states – think of them like tiny countries all within the same area. Two of the most famous were Athens and Sparta. These cities were very different from each other, just like how some kids love building with LEGOs while others prefer drawing pictures.

Athens was a place where people loved to learn! They built amazing temples and studied things like math, writing, and music. The people in Athens also believed everyone should have a say in how the city was run – this is called democracy. Meanwhile, Sparta was all about being strong soldiers; they trained every day to be brave and ready for battle.

Imagine you're playing with building blocks at school. Some kids might carefully build tall towers, while others focus on making strong walls. Athens valued cleverness and art, while Sparta prized strength and discipline. These differences shaped everything about how people lived in each city-state.
keyIdeas: Athens valued learning and democracy.
Sparta focused on military training and obedience.
City-states were independent nations within Ancient Greece.

questions.prompt: Which city was known for its love of learning and art?
questions.explanation: Athens was famous for its philosophers, artists, and thinkers.

questions.prompt: What did the people in Sparta spend most of their time doing?
questions.explanation: Sparta's citizens were trained as warriors from a young age.

questions.prompt: What does ‘democracy’ mean?
choices: A. Everyone has a say in how things are run.
B. Only the strongest person makes decisions.
questions.explanation: Democracy means that citizens vote to make choices for their city.

mt_jwElFY7Syd.title: Earth's Amazing Layers!
explanation: Imagine our planet Earth is like a giant layered cake! It’s not just one solid piece, but it has different parts stacked on top of each other. Scientists have discovered that Earth actually has three main layers: the crust, the mantle, and the core. These layers are made up of very different materials and they're all shaped differently – some are thick like a giant blanket and others are thin like a shell.

The crust is the outermost layer, which is where we live! It’s like the skin of our cake; it’s relatively thin compared to the other layers. Think about your favorite building blocks or LEGO set - that's similar to the crust – a thin covering protecting everything underneath. The mantle is much thicker and hotter than the crust, making up most of Earth’s volume.

Deep down inside Earth, there’s a core! This core is incredibly hot – so hot that it can melt rock. It’s made mostly of iron and nickel, and scientists believe this heat is what causes things like volcanoes and earthquakes to happen. Just like a really hot oven, the core keeps everything moving!
keyIdeas: Earth has three main layers: crust, mantle, and core.
The crust is thin compared to the mantle.
Earth’s interior is extremely hot.

questions.prompt: Which layer of Earth is closest to our feet?
questions.explanation: The crust is the outermost layer and where we live!

questions.prompt: What is the mantle mostly made of?
questions.explanation: The mantle is a thick, hot layer mainly composed of rock and molten material.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists think Earth’s core causes earthquakes?
choices: B. The heat from the core creates vibrations
questions.explanation: The extreme heat within the core generates powerful vibrations that travel through the Earth, causing earthquakes.

mt_W_CNRTBgYR.title: Meet the Gods of Ancient Greece!
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to ancient Greece, a place filled with amazing stories and powerful gods! The Greeks believed that these gods and goddesses controlled everything around them – the weather, the sea, even people's feelings. They had very important jobs and were often seen as having human-like qualities, both good and bad, just like us.  It’s like when you play with your LEGOs and imagine a king or queen ruling over all the blocks; the Greek gods were similar – they ruled over different parts of the world!

There were many important gods to know! Zeus was the king of the gods, controlling lightning and thunder. Hera was his wife, and she looked after marriage and families. Athena was known for her wisdom and helped with warfare, while Poseidon ruled over the oceans. Apollo brought light and music, and Artemis protected hunters and the moon.  These were just a few of the many powerful beings that the ancient Greeks worshipped – it’s like having a whole team of superheroes!

The most famous temple built to honour one of these gods was the Parthenon in Athens. This huge building, called an acropolis, was dedicated to Athena, the goddess of wisdom and warfare. The Greeks built temples like this all over Greece as a way to show their respect for their gods and goddesses. They would also hold special festivals and sacrifices – offering gifts to the gods to thank them for their blessings.
keyIdeas: Zeus was the king of the gods.
The Parthenon was a temple dedicated to Athena.
Greeks built temples to honour their gods.

questions.prompt: Which god ruled over the sea?
questions.explanation: Poseidon was the Greek god responsible for controlling the oceans and earthquakes.

questions.prompt: What was the Parthenon built to honour?
questions.explanation: The Parthenon was a grand temple dedicated to Athena, the goddess of wisdom and warfare.

questions.prompt: Why did the Greeks build temples?
choices: A. To have a place to play games.
B. To honour their gods and goddesses.
questions.explanation: The Greeks built temples as places of worship and to show respect for their gods.

explanation: Compose and rehearse sentences orally before writing, progressively building varied and rich vocabulary and an increasing range of sentence structures including dialogue

explanation: Distinguish body fossils (preserved bones, teeth, shells) from trace fossils (footprints, trackways, eggs, burrows, coprolites) and explain what each type can tell scientists

Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

mt_1dXhJp6qLJ.title: Amazing Dinosaur Poo! (Coprolites)
explanation: Have you ever wondered what dinosaurs ate? Well, scientists have found a really amazing clue – fossilized dinosaur dung! These are called coprolites, which is just a fancy word for ‘poo rocks.’ They’re like ancient leftovers that give us a peek into the past and show us exactly what these giant creatures were eating.

Sometimes, paleontologists, who are scientists who study dinosaurs, carefully cut open these coprolites with special tools. When they open them up, they might find tiny things inside, like seeds from plants the dinosaur ate, small bone fragments, or even fish scales! It’s like a detective puzzle – each piece of evidence helps us understand more about the dinosaur.

Imagine you found a pile of your favorite cookies and discovered peanut butter in them. That would tell you someone loves peanut butter! Similarly, coprolites can reveal what dinosaurs ate by showing us the bits and pieces they swallowed. For example, some coprolites have plant seeds inside, proving that certain dinosaurs munched on plants – just like a modern-day herbivore!
keyIdeas: Coprolites are fossilized dinosaur poo.
Scientists examine coprolites to find clues about diet.
Coprolite contents reveal what dinosaurs ate by analyzing their remains.

questions.prompt: What is a coprolite?
choices: B. Fossilized animal droppings preserved as rock
C. A type of dinosaur footprint
questions.explanation: Coprolites are exactly that – the remains of animal poop turned into stone!

questions.prompt: How do paleontologists study coprolites?
choices: A. They dig them up and look at them directly
B. They carefully cut them open to find inside contents
C. They use lasers to scan for dinosaur footprints
questions.explanation: Cutting open the coprolites allows scientists to examine what was in the dinosaur's stomach.

questions.prompt: If a coprolite contains seeds, what does this tell us about the dinosaur?
questions.explanation: Seeds are found in plant remains, so the dinosaur likely consumed plants.

mt_yNGrY9xJ8Y.title: Exploring Ancient Egypt Through Treasures
explanation: Imagine you're an archaeologist – someone who digs up and studies things from the past! Ancient Egyptians were a really old civilization, and we can learn so much about their lives by looking at what they left behind. Tomb paintings and artefacts, like tools and jewelry, are like clues that help us understand how people lived thousands of years ago. These objects aren't just pretty; they’re real pieces of history waiting to be discovered!

When archaeologists find things in tombs – like the beautiful paintings inside Nebamun’s tomb – it’s called ‘evidence’. Evidence is anything we can use to figure out what happened in the past.  These paintings show Egyptians hunting animals and enjoying big feasts, which tells us they loved to eat good food and spend time together with family and friends. Just like a detective uses clues to solve a mystery, archaeologists use evidence to understand ancient civilizations.

Let’s say you find a small pot in an Egyptian tomb. That pot wasn't just for holding water; it shows that Egyptians used pottery to store grains like wheat or barley – the food they ate!  Similarly, jewellery and furniture show how skilled Egyptian craftsmen were, and everyday objects like tools reveal what ordinary people did for work. These items are all evidence that helps us build a picture of ancient Egypt.
keyIdeas: Tomb paintings and artefacts are sources of evidence.
Archaeologists use these objects to understand the past.
These finds tell us about daily life in Ancient Egypt.

questions.prompt: What does ‘evidence’ mean when archaeologists study ancient Egyptian tombs?
choices: B. Things that help us learn about the past
questions.explanation: Evidence is anything we can use to understand what happened in the past, like a tomb painting or a tool.

questions.prompt: Nebamun’s tomb paintings showed people doing which of these activities?
questions.explanation: The paintings depicted scenes of hunting, fishing, and enjoying large meals – a glimpse into their daily lives.

questions.prompt: If you found a small clay pot in an Egyptian tomb, what could it tell us about the Egyptians?
choices: A. They loved to play games
B. They used pottery to store food
questions.explanation: The pot shows that they stored grains like wheat or barley – a key part of their diet.

explanation: Humans are great at spotting patterns; computers can learn to spot patterns too, but they need lots of examples; sorting and classification activities as the basis of machine learning

mt_Wpvuz3mvBq.title: Dinosaur Trackways: Following Footprints!
explanation: Imagine you're a paleontologist, that’s a scientist who studies ancient things! Dinosaur trackways are like frozen footprints left behind by dinosaurs long ago. These tracks tell us amazing stories about how these giant creatures lived and moved – it’s like reading their history through their feet! Let’s look closely at some dinosaur tracks to see what we can learn.

Sometimes, a dinosaur's footprint is really big, like the size of your bedroom! That usually means that the dinosaur itself was very large. If you see a trackway with lots of huge footprints, it probably belonged to a giant dinosaur like a Brachiosaurus who ate leaves high up in trees. The bigger the footprint, the bigger the dinosaur we can guess!

Now, let’s look at how far apart the tracks are. If the prints are spread out really wide, that means the dinosaur was running super fast! But if the footprints are close together, it probably meant the dinosaur was walking slowly.  Think about a race – runners take big steps, but walkers take smaller ones!
keyIdeas: Large footprints often indicate large dinosaurs.
Spacing between tracks helps us tell if a dinosaur was running or walking.
Trackways can show dinosaurs traveling together.

questions.prompt: A dinosaur trackway with wide, separated prints most likely shows the dinosaur…
questions.explanation: Wide tracks suggest a high speed.

questions.prompt: If you see a line of tiny, closely spaced dinosaur footprints, what can you infer?
choices: A. The dinosaur was eating berries
B. The dinosaur was walking slowly
C. The dinosaur was fighting another dinosaur
questions.explanation: Close spacing indicates a slower pace.

questions.prompt: Imagine you find a set of parallel trackways leading to a watering hole. What might this tell us about the dinosaurs?
choices: B. They were traveling in a group
C. They were hiding from predators
questions.explanation: Parallel tracks suggest coordinated movement.

mt_tHtjfjjFrl.title: Suffix Superpowers!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about something super cool called suffixes! A suffix is like adding a little piece to the end of a word to change its meaning. Think of it like building with LEGOs – you add new bricks (suffixes) to make something bigger and different. For example, if you take the word ‘act’ and add '-ion', you get ‘action’! This changes the word from what an action *is* to what we *do* an action.

Let's look at some examples. We can use suffixes like -ation (as in 'information') or -ly (as in 'happily'). These additions tell us more about how something works or how it’s done.  Imagine you are building a tower with blocks – you might add a special piece to make it stronger, just like a suffix makes a word stronger and clearer. We can also use suffixes like -ous, which as in ‘famous’, means full of something.

Now, let's talk about some tricky sounds! Sometimes letters make different sounds when they’re part of a suffix. For instance, the letter 'y' can make an /ɪ/ sound – like in the word ‘myth’. Or the letter 'ou' can make an /ʌ/ sound, as in ‘young’.  Learning these patterns helps us spell words correctly and understand how they are put together.
keyIdeas: Suffixes add to the end of words.
They change a word’s meaning.
Some suffixes have tricky sounds!

questions.prompt: Which suffix means ‘full of’?
questions.explanation: The suffix '-ous' makes a word like 'famous' which means full of fame.

questions.prompt: What does the suffix '-ly' make a word describe?
questions.explanation: The suffix '-ly' often describes how an action or process happens, like ‘happily’.

questions.prompt: If you see the letter 'y' at the end of a word, what sound might it make?
questions.explanation: The letter ‘y’ can often make an /ɪ/ sound, as in the word ‘myth’.

mt_26OJ9MetR9.title: The Brave Anglo-Saxons!
explanation: Long, long ago, before England was called England, there were people called the Anglo-Saxons. They came from faraway lands like Germany and Denmark – they were Germanic peoples! After the Romans left Britain a very long time ago, these brave new settlers decided to make their home here, building a brand new life.

The Anglo-Saxons didn’t just settle down; they created lots of kingdoms! Some of the most important ones were Wessex, where King Alfred the Great ruled, Mercia, which was really powerful for a while, and Northumbria. Each kingdom had its own king and people, and they often fought with each other to be in charge – it was like having different teams playing games!

You can still see their influence today! Look at place names all over England; many end in words like -ton (like Nottingham), -ham (like Burnham), or -bury (like Canterbury). These Anglo-Saxon words tell us where these places were first settled by the Anglo-Saxons, and they’re a reminder of this amazing history.
keyIdeas: The Anglo-Saxons came to Britain after the Romans left.
They formed powerful kingdoms like Wessex, Mercia, and Northumbria.
Anglo-Saxon words are still used in English place names today.

questions.prompt: Which group of people first settled in Britain after the Romans left?
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Saxons were Germanic peoples who came to Britain after the Roman Empire disappeared.

questions.prompt: Which of these was a famous Anglo-Saxon kingdom?
questions.explanation: Wessex was ruled by King Alfred the Great and was one of the most important Anglo-Saxon kingdoms.

questions.prompt: Many English place names end in -ton or -ham. What do these words tell us?
choices: They show where people first settled
They mean the places are very cold
questions.answer: They show where people first settled
questions.explanation: These suffixes come from Anglo-Saxon words and indicate the original settlement of a place.

explanation: Understand that Earth moves in two ways: it rotates (spins) on its axis once every 24 hours causing day and night, and it orbits (travels around) the Sun once every 365 days, which is one year
