mt_CqzsM0BDFP.title: Castle Battles! Learning About Siege Warfare
explanation: Imagine you're building a really cool LEGO castle, and someone wants to take it from you! That’s kind of like what happened during something called siege warfare. A siege is when one army tries to capture a castle or city by surrounding it completely – cutting off all supplies and escape routes. These attacks could last for weeks, even months, because castles were incredibly strong defenses!

Medieval knights and soldiers used some amazing tools to attack castles. They built huge machines like trebuchets, which were giant catapults that flung rocks really far! Battering rams were also used – big wooden poles they’d smash against castle walls to break them down. And siege towers allowed attackers to climb up the walls and fight inside. It was a very clever way to try and get into a fortress!

Castles weren't easy targets; defenders had many tricks up their sleeves too! They built arrow slits in the walls so archers could shoot at enemies without exposing themselves. Also, they created murder holes – openings in the ceiling where they could pour boiling water or drop rocks on anyone trying to climb over the walls. These defenses made it incredibly difficult for attackers to win.
keyIdeas: Sieges involved surrounding and attacking castles.
Attackers used weapons like trebuchets and battering rams.
Castles had clever defenses, such as arrow slits and murder holes.

questions.prompt: Which of these was a giant catapult used to throw rocks at castles?
questions.explanation: A trebuchet is a machine designed specifically for launching heavy objects over long distances.

questions.prompt: What were arrow slits in a castle used for?
choices: A) To let people enter the castle quickly.
B) To allow archers to shoot at enemies safely.
C) To pour boiling water onto attackers.
questions.explanation: Arrow slits provided archers with a protected position from which to fire arrows.

questions.prompt: Why might a siege last for so long?
choices: A) Because the attackers were very brave.
B) Because castles were difficult to attack and defend.
C) Because the weather was always sunny.
questions.explanation: Castles had strong defenses, making it challenging for attackers to break through.

mt_pOstrrS763.title: Teamwork Makes the Dream Work!
explanation: Imagine you and your friends are running a lemonade stand at the school fair! A really good lemonade stand needs lots of different jobs to be successful, just like a real business. One person could be in charge of squeezing all the lemons – that’s a messy job but someone needs to do it! Another person might make the sugar syrup and mix it with water, while another designs a cool sign to attract customers.

Businesses need teamwork because no one person can do *everything* well. Think about your favorite toy – maybe you love building with LEGOs, but your friend is really good at drawing pictures. If you both work together, you can build an amazing LEGO castle *and* have a beautiful picture to go next to it! Using everyone's special skills makes the business much stronger and better.

Now, what if someone didn’t want to help or didn’t do their job properly? That could cause problems like running out of lemons before you sell all your lemonade, or forgetting to put up the sign. When a team doesn’t work together, it can make things much harder and less successful for everyone involved.
keyIdeas: Divide tasks based on strengths
Use different skills for success
Poor teamwork causes problems

questions.prompt: If you were running a toy shop with your friends, who would be best at designing the displays?
choices: A) Someone good at building things
C) Someone good at counting money
questions.explanation: Creative people are great at making things look nice and attractive to customers.

questions.prompt: Why is it important for a team to use everyone’s strengths?
choices: A) So one person can do all the work
B) So they can make the business bigger and better
C) So they can argue about who's in charge
questions.explanation: Different skills help a team achieve more than if just one person did everything.

questions.prompt: What might happen if someone on a team doesn’t want to help with cleaning up after selling lemonade?
choices: A) They would make lots of money
B) The stand would stay very clean and organized
C) There wouldn't be enough tables or chairs
questions.explanation: Cleaning is important for keeping the business running smoothly.

mt_8oAzr0WxRb.title: The Black Death: A Time of Sickness and Change
explanation: Imagine a time long ago, around the year 1348. That's when something terrible happened – it was called the Black Death! This wasn’t just any sickness; it was a deadly plague that swept across Europe and Asia, causing widespread fear and sadness. The name ‘Black Death’ came from the dark patches on people’s skin as they got sick, and many people died very quickly.

The Black Death caused some really awful symptoms. People would often get painful swellings called buboes, which were filled with pus, under their arms or in their legs. They also had a high fever, chills, and felt incredibly weak and tired. Sadly, most people who got the plague didn’t survive, and it spread very fast because doctors didn't understand how to stop it.

The Black Death traveled through fleas that lived on rats. Rats scurried around in towns and cities, carrying these fleas, and when a flea bit a person, it would inject germs into their body. Then, if someone touched an infected area, the disease could spread to others, or even from person to person through coughing and sneezing. It was a frightening time with no way to stop the sickness!
keyIdeas: The Black Death was a deadly plague.
It spread because of rats and fleas.
The plague changed how people worked and earned money.

questions.prompt: What were two symptoms of the Black Death?
choices:
A) A runny nose and cough
B) Swelling under the arms and a high fever
C) Feeling happy and energetic
questions.explanation: The Black Death caused painful swellings and a very high temperature.

questions.prompt: How did the plague spread?
choices:
A) Through the air people breathed.
B) By rats carrying fleas.
C) By drinking dirty water.
questions.explanation: Fleas on rats were the main way the Black Death spread from person to person.

questions.prompt: What happened because fewer workers died during the Black Death?
choices:
A) Workers became poorer and had less power.
B) Workers earned higher wages because there weren't enough of them.
C) Workers stopped working altogether.
questions.explanation: With so many people dying, those who remained were needed more, which meant they got paid better.

mt_6XnezHOcM3.title: Vikings vs Anglo-Saxons
explanation: The conflict between Vikings and Anglo-Saxons for control of England: Viking raids, Alfred the Great's resistance, the Danelaw, Athelstan as first king of all England, Edward the Confessor
You know this idea when you can: Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact; Explain who Alfred the Great was and why he was important.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact
Explain who Alfred the Great was and why he was important
Describe what the Danelaw was (the area of England under Viking control)

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact
questions.explanation: Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain who Alfred the Great was and why he was important
questions.explanation: Explain who Alfred the Great was and why he was important

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe what the Danelaw was (the area of England under Viking control)
questions.explanation: Describe what the Danelaw was (the area of England under Viking control)

mt_OiDHqtLoln.title: The Battle of Hastings: A King's Fight!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs, and lots of people want to play with your set! That’s kind of what happened in England back in 1066. King Edward the Confessor had died without a clear son to take over his throne – he didn't leave instructions for who should be king. This caused a big problem because many different people thought they should rule, creating a huge disagreement and uncertainty about who was really in charge.

Three men all believed they were entitled to the crown: Harold Godwinson, William of Normandy, and Edgar Atheling. Harold was chosen by the English army, while William claimed Edward had promised him the throne years before. They argued and fought over this right, leading to a very important battle that would change England forever. It’s like a really intense game of ‘who gets the prize’ but with kingdoms instead of toys!

The Battle of Hastings took place on October 14th, 1066. William's army, from France, fought against Harold and his English soldiers. After a long and tough fight, William won! This victory allowed him to become King of England, marking the beginning of a new era in British history – an era shaped by Norman rule.
keyIdeas: Edward the Confessor died without naming a successor.
William of Normandy defeated Harold Godwinson at the Battle of Hastings.
The Bayeux Tapestry provides a visual record of this important event.

questions.prompt: Why was there a problem in England in 1066?
choices: A. There was a really good harvest.
B. King Edward the Confessor died without naming a king.
questions.explanation: King Edward’s death created confusion because he didn't say who should be in charge.

questions.prompt: Who won the Battle of Hastings?
choices: A. Harold Godwinson and his army.
B. William of Normandy and his army.
questions.explanation: William’s army defeated Harold’s, leading to him becoming King.

questions.prompt: What is the Bayeux Tapestry?
choices: B. A giant picture showing the Battle of Hastings and events leading up to it.
questions.explanation: The Bayeux Tapestry is a long piece of cloth with pictures that tell the story of the Norman invasion.

mt_SsLWS_APM7.title: Fraction Friends!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about fractions, which are parts of a whole! Imagine you have a yummy chocolate bar divided into ten equal pieces. Each piece is called one-tenth, or 1/10, of the whole bar. Fractions help us describe how much of something we have – it’s like sharing things fairly with our friends!

Let's say you and your friend want to share a pizza cut into eight slices. If you eat three slices, you’ve eaten three-eighths (3/8) of the pizza! We write this as 3 out of 8 pieces. The bottom number, called the denominator, tells us how many total parts there are, and the top number, called the numerator, tells us how many parts we have.

Now, let’s compare! If you eat five slices of that same pizza, you've eaten five-eighths (5/8) of it. We can see that 3/8 is less than 5/8 because 5 is bigger than 3.  Comparing fractions helps us understand which amount is larger or smaller – just like deciding who gets the biggest piece of cake!
keyIdeas: Fractions represent parts of a whole.
The numerator tells you how many parts you have, and the denominator tells you how many total parts there are.
Comparing fractions helps us understand which amount is larger or smaller.

questions.prompt: If a cake is cut into 6 pieces and you eat 2, what fraction of the cake did you eat?
questions.explanation: The denominator (6) represents the total number of pieces. You ate 2 out of those 6 pieces, so it’s 2/6.

questions.prompt: Which fraction is bigger: 1/4 or 3/8?
questions.explanation: To compare, we can draw pictures of the fractions. 3/8 represents more shaded area than 1/4.

questions.prompt: Sarah has a bag of 12 crayons. 1/3 of the crayons are blue. How many crayons are blue?
questions.explanation: (1/3) * 12 = 4. There are 4 blue crayons.

mt_S7UTAhptLi.title: Different Lives, Different Stories
explanation: Imagine you have a favorite toy – maybe it’s a race car or a stuffed animal. Now think about a friend who doesn't have that same toy because their family might not be able to afford it, or perhaps they live in a place where toys aren't as common. Everyone has different things in their lives, like the toys they play with, the food they eat, and even where they live. These differences can make life feel very different for each person.

Sometimes, children face challenges that others don’t understand. For example, a classmate might have a disability that makes it hard to run and play like you do, or their family might be struggling financially and not always have enough food. It's important to remember that these situations are often beyond someone’s control, and they can feel very difficult for the person experiencing them. Recognizing these differences is the first step towards understanding.

It's really important *not* to judge people based on their circumstances – meaning not to make negative assumptions or treat them unfairly because of something about their life. Judging someone doesn’t help anyone, and it can actually hurt their feelings. Instead, we should try to be kind, patient, and understanding, remembering that everyone deserves respect.
keyIdeas: People's lives are different.
It's important not to judge others.
Kindness can make a difference.

questions.prompt: Mia has a wheelchair because she has cerebral palsy. What does this mean?
choices: A) Mia is very strong and athletic.
B) Mia needs help moving around sometimes.
C) Mia always makes bad decisions.
questions.explanation: This means Mia’s body doesn't work the same way as yours, and she might need extra support to do things.

questions.prompt: Why shouldn't you judge someone based on how they look or where they come from?
choices: A) Because it's always okay to make fun of people.
B) Because everyone deserves respect, no matter their situation.
C) Because judging people is a good way to be popular.
questions.explanation: Judging someone means making assumptions about them based on things they can’t change. Everyone deserves our kindness and respect.

questions.prompt: If you saw a new student who didn't speak your language, what could you do?
choices: A) Ignore them because it would be too hard to talk to them.
B) Try to find someone who can help translate or offer a friendly smile.
C) Tell everyone that they don’t belong here.
questions.explanation: Offering a friendly gesture shows kindness and willingness to connect, even when there's a language barrier.

mt_3tz3Otap5j.title: Sharing is Caring: Halves and Quarters!
explanation: Let’s talk about sharing money! Imagine you have a pizza with eight slices. If you want to share it equally with your friend, you would cut each slice in half – that means you'd have four halves. Each half of the pizza is worth one-fourth of the whole pizza, and we call that ‘quarters’.  Understanding halves and quarters helps us make sure everyone gets a fair amount when we’re splitting things up.

Now, let’s look at some coins! You might have two quarters (which are worth 50 cents each), one dime (worth 10 cents), and three pennies (worth 1 cent each).  To find the total value, we need to add them all together: 50 + 10 + 1 = 61 cents. We can write this as $0.61 or 61 cents – it’s important to use the dollar sign ($) for larger amounts and cents (¢) for smaller ones.

When we divide something into halves, we're splitting it in two equal parts. When we divide it into quarters, we're splitting it into four equal parts.  This is really useful when you’re buying things at the store! If you want to know how much change you will get back after paying with a quarter, you need to understand halves and quarters.
keyIdeas: Halves = 1/2
Quarters = 1/4
Adding coins helps us find the total value

questions.prompt: You have two quarters and one dime. How many cents do you have in total?
questions.explanation: Two quarters are worth 2 * 50 = 100 cents, and one dime is worth 10 cents.  100 + 10 = 110 cents.

questions.prompt: Sarah has a collection of coins: 3 quarters, 2 dimes, and 4 pennies. What’s the total value of her coins?
questions.explanation: Three quarters are worth 3 * 25 = 75 cents, two dimes are worth 2 * 10 = 20 cents, and four pennies are worth 4 * 1 = 4 cents.  75 + 20 + 4 = 99 cents or $0.99.

questions.prompt: If you have a toy that costs $1.00 and you pay with one quarter, how much change will you receive?
questions.explanation: One quarter is worth 25 cents.  $1.00 - $0.25 = $0.75 or 75 cents.

mt_K6qtan847r.title: Machine Learning Basics
explanation: How machine learning works at a conceptual level: show the computer many examples, it finds patterns, then it makes predictions about new things; hands-on experience with Teachable Machine or similar tool

You know this idea when you can: Describe the three steps of machine learning in simple terms (give examples, find patterns, make predictions); Train a simple model using a tool like Teachable Machine and describe what happened.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe the three steps of machine learning in simple terms (give examples, find patterns, make predictions)
Train a simple model using a tool like Teachable Machine and describe what happened
Explain why showing more and better examples makes the model more accurate

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the three steps of machine learning in simple terms (give examples, find patterns, make predictions)
questions.explanation: Describe the three steps of machine learning in simple terms (give examples, find patterns, make predictions)

questions.prompt: True or false: Train a simple model using a tool like Teachable Machine and describe what happened
questions.explanation: Train a simple model using a tool like Teachable Machine and describe what happened

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain why showing more and better examples makes the model more accurate
questions.explanation: Explain why showing more and better examples makes the model more accurate

mt_aWOK1npO5s.title: Making Change - Like a Shopkeeper!
explanation: Let's talk about making change! When you go to the shop and buy something, like a yummy chocolate bar costing £1.50, the person giving you your money back is helping you get your correct change. Making change means figuring out how much money you need to pay back after you’ve already paid for what you wanted. It's like being a little shopkeeper yourself!

Imagine you buy a toy car that costs £3.25 and you pay with a £5 note. To find the change, we need to subtract the cost of the car from the amount you gave them. Subtracting means taking away – it’s just like when you take away toys from your collection! We can use numbers and calculations to do this accurately.

Making change is all about adding up costs and then figuring out how much you owe back. Let's say you buy a sandwich (£2.00) and a drink (£1.75). First, we add those prices together: £2.00 + £1.75 = £3.75. Then, if someone gives you £5 to pay for that, they should give you back £1.25 in change!
keyIdeas: Subtracting is key to making change.
Adding costs helps determine the total amount.
Understanding money calculations makes shopping easier.

questions.prompt: You buy a sticker sheet for £1.25 and pay with a £5 note. How much change should you receive?
questions.explanation: To find the change, subtract the cost of the sticker sheet (£1.25) from the amount paid (£5).  £5 - £1.25 = £3.75 is incorrect.

questions.prompt: You buy a book for £4.50 and a pencil case for £2.00. What is the total cost of your purchase?
questions.explanation: First, add the price of the book (£4.50) and the pencil case (£2.00).  £4.50 + £2.00 = £6.50.

questions.prompt: Sarah buys a balloon for £1.75 and pays with a £5 note. She receives some change. How much is her change?
questions.explanation: Calculate the change by subtracting the cost of the balloon from the amount paid: £5 - £1.75 = ?

mt_4vHa3I5bNj.title: Minibeasts – Not Just Bugs!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about minibeasts! A minibeast is a tiny creature that lives all around us, like the beetles and worms you might find in your garden. But not *all* minibeasts are insects – insects have six legs, just like ants and bees. We can sort them into groups based on what they look like and how they move.

Let’s think about a toy car! Most cars have four wheels to help them roll around, right? Well, some minibeasts have different ways of moving. Spiders, for example, have eight legs instead of six, so they aren't insects. Woodlice also have lots of legs – fourteen in fact – and snails use their shells for protection.

It’s important to look closely at the details when we’re sorting minibeasts. Worms don’t have any legs at all; they wiggle through the soil! This is a big difference between worms and insects, which always have six legs. By noticing these differences, we can become expert minibeast detectives!
keyIdeas: Insects have six legs.
Not all minibeasts are insects – they have different numbers of legs or body parts.
Looking closely at a minibeast helps us figure out what group it belongs to.

questions.prompt: A spider has ______ legs.
questions.explanation: Spiders are arachnids, and arachnids have eight legs.

questions.prompt: Woodlice have ______ legs.
questions.explanation: Woodlice are crustaceans, and crustaceans have many legs – fourteen to be exact!

questions.prompt: Which of these minibeasts has NO legs?
questions.explanation: Worms move by wiggling without using any legs.

mt_izien3ZX51.title: More or Less with Numbers!
explanation: Today, we’re going to learn how to add or take away ten or even one hundred from a number! Imagine you have a box of LEGOs – let's say you have 25 LEGO bricks. If someone gives you 10 more, you now have 35 bricks because you added 10.  Adding and taking away ‘more’ or ‘less’ means changing the amount we have.

Now, let’s try with a bigger number like 487. If we want to know what is 100 more than 487, we add 100 to it, which gives us 587.  Similarly, if we wanted to find 10 less than 487, we subtract 10 from it, resulting in 477. Notice that only the bigger numbers changed – the smaller ones stayed the same!

Understanding place value is super important! When we add or take away ten, we’re really just changing the tens column.  If we are adding or subtracting one hundred, we're changing the hundreds column. This helps us see exactly how much our number changes when we make these additions or subtractions.
keyIdeas: Adding and subtracting 10 changes only the tens place.
Adding and subtracting 100 changes only the hundreds place.
Place value helps us understand how numbers change.

questions.prompt: What is 10 more than 634?
questions.explanation: To find 10 more, we add 10 to 634.  634 + 10 = 644.

questions.prompt: What is 100 less than 952?
questions.explanation: To find 100 less, we subtract 100 from 952. 952 - 100 = 852.

questions.prompt: You have 275 marbles. Your friend gives you 20 more. How many do you have now?

mt_bPFToj0OhZ.title: AI's Big Oops! Why Machines Make Mistakes
explanation: Imagine you have a really helpful voice assistant like Siri or Alexa. Sometimes, it doesn’t understand what you say perfectly – it might hear ‘apple’ when you say ‘elephant’! That's because AI, which stands for Artificial Intelligence, is learning to recognize things by looking at lots and lots of examples. These assistants are trained on huge amounts of spoken words, but they can still get confused if the way someone speaks is different from what they’ve learned.

AI mistakes happen when the information it learns from isn't complete or accurate. Think about auto-correct on your phone – sometimes it changes a word into something completely wrong! This happens because the computer has been shown examples of words and their corrections, but if there are similar-looking words that aren’t in its training data, it might make an error. It's like learning a new language; you need lots of practice to get it right!

It’s important to remember that AI isn’t magic! It’s actually just really complicated math being used on information – called data. The computer looks for patterns in the data and uses those patterns to make decisions or predictions, like recommending a movie you might enjoy based on what you've watched before. But if the data is messy or incomplete, the AI will also make mistakes.
keyIdeas: AI learns from data.
AI can make mistakes if the data is bad.
AI follows mathematical rules.

questions.prompt: If an AI has only been shown pictures of cats, what might it do when shown a picture of a dog?
choices: A. Recognize it as a cat.
B. Recognize it as a dog.
C. Not recognize it at all.
questions.explanation: AI learns from the data it's given. If it hasn't seen dogs before, it won’t know what they are.

questions.prompt: Why might auto-correct change ‘there’ to ‘their’?
choices: A. Because ‘there’ is a very common word.
B. Because the computer has not been shown enough examples of both words.
C. Because auto-correct always wants to be correct.
questions.explanation: Auto-correct relies on patterns in data. If it hasn't seen enough examples of 'there' and 'their', it might make a mistake.

questions.prompt: AI is like a really good student. What does that mean?
choices: A. It always knows the answer.
B. It learns from what it’s taught.
C. It doesn’t need to study.
questions.explanation: AI learns by looking at data – just like students learn from books and teachers!

mt_B1ATUEVNPz.title: Meet the Gods of Ancient Egypt!
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to ancient Egypt, a land filled with amazing stories and powerful gods! The Egyptians believed that many things happened because of these gods and goddesses. They were like superheroes who controlled the sun, the stars, and even where people went after they died. These gods weren't just names; they had important jobs and were worshipped by everyone in Egypt.

You might notice that some Egyptian statues have animal heads – a jackal, an ibis, or a cat! This wasn’t just for fun; it was because the Egyptians believed these animals represented qualities of the god. For example, a jackal like Anubis had a strong nose and keen senses, perfect for guiding souls to the afterlife. The animals helped people understand what each god stood for and how they were connected to nature.

The Egyptians built huge temples where they could honor their gods. These temples weren't just buildings; they were homes! People would bring offerings like food and jewelry to the gods, and they performed special rituals – songs, dances, and prayers – every single day. It was a way of showing respect and asking for good luck and protection.
keyIdeas: Ra ruled the sun.
Animals represented god's qualities.
Temples were homes for the gods.

questions.prompt: Which Egyptian god was known as the ruler of the afterlife?
questions.explanation: Osiris was believed to be in charge of where people went after they passed away.

questions.prompt: Why did some Egyptian gods have animal heads?
choices: B. The animals represented the god’s powers
C. It was just for decoration
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed that the animal's characteristics reflected the god's abilities and purpose.

questions.prompt: What did people do in Egyptian temples?
choices: B. They worshipped the gods with rituals and offerings
questions.explanation: The Egyptians performed daily rituals and offered gifts to show their respect for the gods.

mt_RTwmvr9R7V.title: Having a Business Idea
explanation: Where business ideas come from; spotting everyday problems and thinking of solutions; the difference between inventing something new and improving something that exists

You know this idea when you can: Identify a simple everyday problem and suggest a product or service that could solve it; Explain the difference between an invention (something new) and an innovation (making something better).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Identify a simple everyday problem and suggest a product or service that could solve it
Explain the difference between an invention (something new) and an innovation (making something better)
Describe where one real entrepreneur got their business idea from

questions.prompt: True or false: Identify a simple everyday problem and suggest a product or service that could solve it
questions.explanation: Identify a simple everyday problem and suggest a product or service that could solve it

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain the difference between an invention (something new) and an innovation (making something better)
questions.explanation: Explain the difference between an invention (something new) and an innovation (making something better)

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe where one real entrepreneur got their business idea from
questions.explanation: Describe where one real entrepreneur got their business idea from

mt_phpn6KhCAv.title: Making a Simple Plan for Your Business!
explanation: Let’s imagine you want to sell lemonade at the school fair! Before you start squeezing lemons and mixing sugar, it's super important to make a plan – just like a little business owner. A plan helps you know exactly what you need to do so your lemonade stand is successful. It’s like having a map that guides you from starting an idea all the way to selling delicious drinks.

Your plan needs to answer some important questions. First, what are you going to sell? Maybe it's lemonade, cookies, or even friendship bracelets! Then, who will buy your product? Think about your friends, family, and maybe other people at the fair. Next, what materials do you need – lemons, sugar, cups, a table, and maybe some signs?

Finally, you need to figure out how much things cost and how much you’ll charge for each cup of lemonade. This is called pricing! Planning ahead helps you avoid surprises like running out of ingredients or accidentally charging too little. It's better than just jumping in without a plan because then you might make mistakes.
keyIdeas: Plan your product.
Know your customers.
Consider costs and prices.

questions.prompt: You want to sell cookies at the school fair. What’s one thing you need to write down in your plan?
choices: B) Write down what kind of cookies you will bake.
C) Ask your mom to do it for you.
questions.explanation: Writing down the type of cookies helps you focus on a specific product.

questions.prompt: Let's say you need $10 to buy lemons and sugar. You decide to sell each cup of lemonade for $1. How many cups do you need to sell to make $10?
questions.explanation: To earn $10 at $1 per cup, you need to sell 10 cups.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to make a plan before starting your business?
choices: A) It's always fun to just try things out.
B) It helps you avoid mistakes and makes sure you have everything you need.
C) It’s only for older people who run big businesses.
questions.explanation: Planning ahead ensures you have the right materials and don't make costly errors.

mt_9gpUHWVKMR.title: Money Matters: Costs, Revenue & Profit!
explanation: Let's talk about money! When we make or buy something, it costs us money – these are called ‘costs’ or ‘expenses’. Think about building with LEGOs; the plastic bricks themselves cost money to make and ship to the store. These costs include things like the materials used and the people who helped build them.

Now, what if you sell those LEGO creations? That's where ‘revenue’ comes in! Revenue is the money you earn when you sell something. If you sold your LEGO castle for $10, that was your revenue – the money coming *into* your pocket. It's important to understand that costs and revenue are different things.

Finally, let’s see what happens if you make a profit! Profit is what’s left over after you subtract your costs from your revenue. In our LEGO example, if you spent $5 on the bricks (your cost) and sold the castle for $10 (your revenue), your profit would be $5! If you spent more than you earned, that's a loss.
keyIdeas: Costs are expenses.
Revenue is money earned from sales.
Profit is revenue minus costs.

questions.prompt: Imagine you buy a toy car for $6. You sell it for $12. How much profit did you make?
questions.explanation: To find the profit, subtract the cost ($6) from the revenue ($12).  $12 - $6 = $6. But wait! The question asks how much *profit* you made, which is the money left over after paying for the car.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes ‘revenue’?
choices: A) Money spent on making something
B) Money earned from selling something
C) Money lost when something costs more to make than sell
questions.explanation: Revenue is the money you receive when you sell a product or service. It's the income coming in.

questions.prompt: Sarah spent £8 on making cookies and sold them for £15. Did she make a profit or a loss? What was it?
questions.explanation: This question requires students to calculate the profit (or loss) themselves. Encourage them to subtract the costs (£8) from the revenue (£15). The answer is a profit of £7.

mt_dknMcCqvoY.title: Learning from When Things Don’t Go As Planned
explanation: Sometimes, when we try something new, it doesn't work out the way we hoped. Think about building with LEGOs – you might build a really cool spaceship, but then it falls apart! That’s okay; even grown-up entrepreneurs, people who start their own businesses, sometimes have ideas that don’t work at first. It’s like when you try to bake cookies and they burn – it's disappointing, but it doesn't mean you can't learn from the mistake.

One famous example is Thomas Edison, who invented the light bulb! He tried *hundreds* of different materials before he finally found one that worked.  He didn’t give up after each failed attempt; instead, he used what he learned to keep trying new things. This is a really important part of being an entrepreneur – it's about figuring out what works and what doesn’t.

When a business idea doesn’t work the first time, a smart entrepreneur would look back at why it didn’t work. Maybe they realized their toy car wasn’t fun to drive, or that people weren’t interested in buying lemonade if it was too sour.  It's much better than just giving up; failing gives you information and helps you make things even better next time! That’s called iteration – trying something again with changes.
keyIdeas: Failure is a learning opportunity.
Entrepreneurs keep trying.
Iteration improves ideas.

questions.prompt: Thomas Edison tried many different things before inventing the light bulb. What does this show us about being an entrepreneur?
choices: A. It's okay to give up when something is hard.
B. It’s important to try new ideas and learn from mistakes.
C. Light bulbs are always easy to invent.
questions.explanation: Edison didn’t stop trying even when things were difficult, showing that entrepreneurs need to be persistent.

questions.prompt: Imagine you sell lemonade at a school fair. Your lemonade is too sour. What should you do?
choices: B. Add more sugar to make it sweeter.
C. Ask someone else to help you make it.
questions.explanation: Adjusting the recipe (adding sugar) shows how entrepreneurs change their ideas based on feedback.

questions.prompt: Why is failing and trying again better than just giving up?
choices: A. It’s faster to succeed if you don't fail.
B. Failing gives you information to make things better next time.
C. Everyone succeeds the first time they try something.
questions.explanation: Failing provides valuable data that helps entrepreneurs improve their ideas.

mt_-mw3JeIjhU.title: Talking Characters!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn how to write down exactly what characters say in a story! When someone talks in a book or movie, it’s called direct speech. It means we hear their words directly, just like when you talk to your friends – it’s important to show us *exactly* what they said, not just describe them talking.

To do this, we use special marks called quotation marks (also known as speech marks!). These marks tell us that the words inside are something a character is saying. For example, if you write ‘Hello,’ it shows us exactly what the person said.  We always put punctuation – like commas or question marks – at the end of the quoted words too!

Let's try an example: Imagine your favorite toy, maybe a teddy bear named Barnaby. You could write, 'I love you!' he squeaked happily. See how the quotation marks show us Barnaby’s words?  This makes the story more exciting and helps us understand what each character is feeling and thinking.
keyIdeas: Direct speech uses quotation marks.
Punctuation goes at the end of quoted words.
Quotation marks show exactly what a character says.

questions.prompt: What punctuation mark do we use to show someone is speaking in a story?
questions.explanation: Quotation marks are used to enclose the exact words spoken by a character.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses direct speech correctly?
questions.explanation: The quotation marks show the exact words spoken by the characters.

questions.prompt: If a character says, "I am hungry," what does the quotation mark do?
choices: It tells us they are sad.
It shows us their exact words.
It means we should give them food.
questions.explanation: The quotation marks enclose and represent the precise words of the character's statement.

mt_ZJu8s-Q1xa.title: Earth's Big Changes!
explanation: Our planet Earth is always changing! Sometimes these changes happen super fast, like when the ground shakes during an earthquake. Imagine building with LEGOs – you can quickly knock them over and rearrange them in a new way. These quick changes are called rapid events because they happen very, very quickly, creating big surprises for us.

Other times, Earth’s changes take millions of years to happen. Think about the Grand Canyon; it wasn't made by one giant crash! Instead, a river slowly, slowly carved away at the rock over many, many years, bit by bit. These slow changes are called processes because they happen gradually and steadily.

Scientists study these different kinds of changes to understand how our planet works. They look for clues like cracks in rocks after an earthquake or layers of sand that show how a river has changed its path over time. By noticing both the fast and slow ways Earth can change, we learn about its amazing history!
keyIdeas: Earth changes happen at different speeds.
Rapid events like earthquakes are sudden.
Slow processes like erosion take a very long time.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a rapid Earth change?
choices: A) A river flowing slowly down a mountain.
B) An earthquake shaking the ground.
C) A volcano erupting with lava.
questions.explanation: Earthquakes happen suddenly and quickly, while rivers flow and volcanoes erupt over longer periods.

questions.prompt: What happens during erosion?
choices: A) Rocks build up to form mountains.
B) Wind and water slowly wear away rocks and soil.
C) Volcanoes explode with hot ash.
questions.explanation: Erosion is the process of rocks and soil being worn down by wind, rain, or other natural forces.

questions.prompt: True or False: Mountain building happens quickly like a flash flood.
questions.explanation: Mountain building takes millions of years, not just a short amount of time.

mt_V_kAitNbLN.title: Scribes and the Rosetta Stone
explanation: Know that scribes were specially trained people who could read and write hieroglyphs, that the Rosetta Stone — a slab with the same text in three scripts — was the key to cracking the hieroglyphic code, and that Jean-François Champollion used it to decipher hieroglyphs in 1822 after centuries of mystery
You know this idea when you can: Explain the importance of scribes as the only people who could read and write; Describe the Rosetta Stone as having the same message in three different scripts.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Explain the importance of scribes as the only people who could read and write
Describe the Rosetta Stone as having the same message in three different scripts
Name Champollion as the person who decoded hieroglyphs using the Rosetta Stone

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain the importance of scribes as the only people who could read and write
questions.explanation: Explain the importance of scribes as the only people who could read and write

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the Rosetta Stone as having the same message in three different scripts
questions.explanation: Describe the Rosetta Stone as having the same message in three different scripts

questions.prompt: True or false: Name Champollion as the person who decoded hieroglyphs using the Rosetta Stone
questions.explanation: Name Champollion as the person who decoded hieroglyphs using the Rosetta Stone

mt_O2dS6gvClw.title: Spelling Superstars!
explanation: Let’s talk about spelling! Sometimes, words look easy but can be tricky to write down correctly. Spelling is like a puzzle – we need to pay close attention to each letter and how they fit together. When you're writing your name or even telling a story, making sure you spell the words right helps everyone understand what you mean.

Many words in English don’t follow simple rules about adding letters or changing sounds. Think about the word ‘believe’. It has two 'e's at the end, but it doesn’t sound like you’re saying ‘ee’ twice! Sometimes, we can use tricks to remember these tricky words, like making a little picture in our heads – like ‘a rat’ for ‘separate’!

Practicing spelling helps us become better writers and readers. When we spell words correctly, it's easier to understand the meaning of stories and write our own ideas clearly. Let's try to learn some words from a list that schools use for Year 3 and 4 students – these are important words to know!
keyIdeas: Spelling is about remembering letter combinations.
Tricky words don’t always follow spelling rules.
Practice helps us spell correctly.

questions.prompt: Which word has a tricky part that you can remember as ‘a rat’?
questions.explanation: The word 'separate' has the letters 'a rat' in the middle, which is a helpful trick to remember.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to spell a word correctly?
choices: To write the word down accurately
To draw a picture of the word
questions.answer: To write the word down accurately
questions.explanation: Spelling means writing the letters in the right order so everyone can read and understand your words.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are writing about your favourite toy. Which word would be important to use?
questions.explanation: 'Believe' is a common word used when talking about things you like – like believing your toy is special!

mt_v6CBCMuvz1.title: Rainforest Weather Wonders!
explanation: Imagine a place where it's always warm, like when you’re playing outside on a sunny day! Tropical rainforests are incredibly hot – usually between 25 and 30 degrees Celsius (that’s about 77 to 86 degrees Fahrenheit) – almost all the time. Plus, they get a *lot* of rain, more than 2000 millimeters (or over 79 inches!) every single year.

This constant heat and rain are super important for plants. Think about your favorite toy car; it needs to be kept at a certain temperature to work properly. Similarly, the warm temperatures help plants grow quickly, and the lots of water helps them stay healthy and strong. This makes rainforests amazing places for life to thrive!

Let’s compare this to where you live! The UK, for example, has cooler summers and often doesn't get nearly as much rain as a tropical rainforest. That’s why you might see fewer big trees and colorful flowers – the rainforest climate is perfect for plants that love heat and moisture.
keyIdeas: Rainforests are hot (25-30°C).
They receive over 2000 mm of rain each year.
Heat and moisture help plants grow quickly.

questions.prompt: What is the average temperature in a tropical rainforest?
questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests are known for their consistently warm temperatures.

questions.prompt: How much rain does a tropical rainforest get each year?
questions.explanation: Rainforests receive an enormous amount of rainfall annually.

questions.prompt: Which climate is similar to a tropical rainforest?
questions.explanation: The UK has cooler temperatures and less rainfall than a tropical rainforest.

mt_ZWTk6eP1qF.title: Rainforest Water Cycle Adventure!
explanation: Imagine a rainforest – it's super amazing and full of tall trees, colorful birds, and lots of plants! The water cycle is like a big adventure for the water in this forest. It’s how rain keeps coming back to the rainforest, making sure all the plants and animals have what they need. This cycle never stops; it’s always moving water around!

First, the trees drink water through their roots, just like you drink juice with a straw! But instead of drinking it all up, they release some of that water back into the air as tiny droplets – this is called transpiration. These droplets rise up, up, up into the sky and join together to form clouds. It’s like magic!

Then, those clouds get full of water and eventually, they become heavy with rain! This rain falls back down onto the rainforest floor, watering the plants and filling the rivers. The whole process starts again, creating a beautiful cycle that keeps the rainforest alive and thriving – it's truly amazing!
keyIdeas: Trees release water through transpiration.
The water cycle creates its own rainfall.
Rainforests are self-sustaining thanks to this cycle.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the process where trees release water into the air?
questions.explanation: Transpiration is when plants release water vapor from their leaves.

questions.prompt: Where do rainforests get most of their rain from?
choices: C. Rivers flowing into the forest
questions.explanation: The rainforest’s water cycle creates much of its own rainfall.

questions.prompt: Describe the order of events in the rainforest water cycle.
questions.answer: Trees absorb water, it is released through transpiration, moisture rises to form clouds, and rain falls back down.

mt_C7FNeIDGc6.title: Cells, Tissues & Organs
explanation: Understand that the body is organised in a hierarchy: tiny cells are the building blocks, groups of similar cells form tissues, tissues combine into organs (like the heart or stomach), and organs work together in organ systems (like the circulatory system)

You know this idea when you can: State that cells are the smallest building blocks of the body, too small to see without a microscope; Describe the hierarchy: cells → tissues → organs → organ systems.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: State that cells are the smallest building blocks of the body, too small to see without a microscope
Describe the hierarchy: cells → tissues → organs → organ systems
Give an example: muscle cells form muscle tissue, which forms the heart organ, which is part of the circulatory system

questions.prompt: True or false: State that cells are the smallest building blocks of the body, too small to see without a microscope
questions.explanation: State that cells are the smallest building blocks of the body, too small to see without a microscope

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the hierarchy: cells → tissues → organs → organ systems
questions.explanation: Describe the hierarchy: cells → tissues → organs → organ systems

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example: muscle cells form muscle tissue, which forms the heart organ, which is part of the circulatory system
questions.explanation: Give an example: muscle cells form muscle tissue, which forms the heart organ, which is part of the circulatory system

mt_ahSqW_kK1b.title: Amazing Changes & Separations!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn about some really cool changes that happen with matter – that’s anything around us! Sometimes things change their form, like when ice turns into water or when steam disappears. These changes are caused by heat and energy moving through the material. We can use special words to describe these changes accurately, which will help us understand how the world works.

Let's talk about dissolving. When you stir sugar into tea, it *dissolves* – that means tiny pieces of sugar spread out evenly throughout the water. This is different from melting; when ice melts, it turns completely into liquid water without mixing in. We use words like ‘soluble’ to describe things that dissolve easily, like salt or sugar, and ‘insoluble’ for things that don't, like sand.

Now let’s look at evaporation and condensation. Evaporation is when a liquid, like water from a puddle, turns into a gas – steam! Condensation happens when the opposite occurs: the gas (steam) turns back into a liquid as it cools down. Think about how dew forms on grass in the morning; that's condensation happening because warm air meets cooler surfaces.
keyIdeas: Dissolve means to spread out evenly.
Soluble things dissolve easily, while insoluble things don’t.
Evaporation is a liquid turning into a gas, and condensation is a gas turning back into a liquid.

questions.prompt: When you stir sugar into tea, what happens to the sugar?
questions.explanation: Dissolving means the sugar spreads out in the water.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes a ‘soluble’ material?
choices: A. Something that doesn't mix with water.
B. Something that easily mixes with water.
C. Something that turns into steam.
questions.explanation: Soluble means the substance can be mixed in, like sugar dissolving in water.

questions.prompt: What is evaporation?
choices: B. A liquid turning into a gas.
questions.explanation: Evaporation happens when a liquid changes to a gaseous state, like steam rising from hot water.

mt_j8Pv3s7TZR.title: Choosing the Right Coping Strategy
explanation: Understand that different situations require different coping strategies — what works for anger might not work for sadness, and what helps at school might be different from what helps at home
You know this idea when you can: Name at least two different coping strategies and explain when each one is most useful; Choose an appropriate strategy based on the specific situation and emotion.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name at least two different coping strategies and explain when each one is most useful
Choose an appropriate strategy based on the specific situation and emotion
Reflect on a time a strategy didn't work and explain what they might try instead

questions.prompt: True or false: Name at least two different coping strategies and explain when each one is most useful
questions.explanation: Name at least two different coping strategies and explain when each one is most useful

questions.prompt: True or false: Choose an appropriate strategy based on the specific situation and emotion
questions.explanation: Choose an appropriate strategy based on the specific situation and emotion

questions.prompt: True or false: Reflect on a time a strategy didn't work and explain what they might try instead
questions.explanation: Reflect on a time a strategy didn't work and explain what they might try instead

mt_xWg0lI_gG4.title: Measuring Lengths – How Much Longer?
explanation: Today, we're going to learn how to compare the lengths of different things! Measuring is all about figuring out how long or short something is. We use tools like rulers and measuring tapes to help us do this accurately, just like when you measure ingredients for baking a cake – knowing exactly how much flour you need makes sure your cake turns out perfectly!

Let's say we have a toy car that’s 10 centimeters long and a building block that’s 6 centimeters long. To find out how much longer the car is, we subtract the length of the block from the length of the car: 10 cm - 6 cm = 4 cm! This means the car is 4 centimeters longer than the building block – that's a big difference!

When we measure, it’s important to use the same unit. We always want to say things like ‘7 centimeters longer’ or ‘2 meters shorter’. Using the correct units helps us understand exactly how much bigger or smaller something is compared to another thing. Now you can compare anything!
keyIdeas: Measuring uses tools like rulers and tape measures.
Subtracting lengths tells us how much longer one object is than another.
Using the same units (like centimeters or meters) makes our measurements clear.

questions.prompt: A pencil is 12 cm long and a crayon is 7 cm long. How much longer is the pencil?
questions.explanation: To find the difference, subtract the crayon's length from the pencil’s length: 12cm - 7cm = 5cm.

questions.prompt: You measure a table and it is 150 centimeters long. You measure a bookshelf and it is 90 centimeters long. How much longer is the table?
questions.explanation: Subtract the bookshelf’s length from the table's length: 150cm - 90cm = 60cm.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a good way to measure something?
choices: A) Just guess how long it is.
B) Use a ruler or tape measure.
C) Ask your friend what they think.
questions.explanation: Using a ruler or tape measure gives you an accurate measurement.

mt_DLcEzmmj2r.title: Adding and Subtracting Lengths!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn how to solve problems where we add or take away lengths! Imagine you have a really cool toy car that’s 30 centimeters long. Then your friend gives it to you, and it’s now 42 centimeters long – that means someone added extra length to it! We can use addition to figure out how much longer the car is.

Let's look at another example: You have a ruler that’s 60 centimeters long. You measure out 25 centimeters of a block of wood, and then you need to subtract that amount from your ruler. This means we'll be taking away some length! We can use subtraction to find out how much of the ruler is left.

When we solve these problems, it’s important to draw pictures or diagrams to help us understand what’s happening. For example, if you have a rope that’s 45 cm long and cut off 18 cm, you can draw a line showing the rope and then cross out the part you cut off. Then we write an equation like 45 - 18 = ? to show how much rope is left.
keyIdeas: Addition helps us find lengths when things get longer.
Subtraction helps us find lengths when things get shorter.
Draw pictures to help you understand the problem!

questions.prompt: The rope is 45 cm long. I cut off 18 cm. How long is it now?
questions.explanation: To solve this, we subtract: 45 - 18 = 27

questions.prompt: You have a ruler that’s 60 centimeters long. You measure out 25 centimeters of a block of wood, and then you need to subtract that amount from your ruler. How much is left?
questions.explanation: To solve this, we subtract: 60 - 25 = 35

questions.prompt: Sarah has a pencil that is 12 centimeters long. Her brother gives her a crayon that is 7 centimeters long. How long are the pencil and crayon together?
questions.explanation: This is an addition problem! 12 + 7 = 19

mt_bEvMBUv4eG.title: The Pyramid of Power: Ancient Egyptian Jobs
explanation: Imagine building a giant pyramid – that’s exactly how ancient Egyptians organized their society! They built a pyramid shape, like a staircase, to show who was most important and who did what. At the very top of the pyramid lived the Pharaoh, who was like the king and had all the power. Below him were priests and nobles, wealthy people who helped the Pharaoh make decisions and led religious ceremonies.

The next level down included scribes – people who could write! They worked for the Pharaoh or temples, keeping records and writing important messages. Then there were soldiers, who protected Egypt from enemies, and craftworkers like potters and jewelers, making beautiful things. At the very bottom of the pyramid were farmers and laborers; they grew the food that everyone needed to survive – think about how many loaves of bread you eat each day!

It’s important to know that most people in ancient Egypt weren't Pharaohs or scribes. Farmers worked really hard, often for many hours a day, just to feed their families and provide food for the whole country. Because of where they were on the pyramid, farmers didn’t have much say in how things were run, unlike someone like a scribe who could write down their ideas.
keyIdeas: The Pharaoh was at the top of Egyptian society.
Most Egyptians were farmers and laborers.
Social position determined a person's job and life.

questions.prompt: Who was at the very top of the pyramid in ancient Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Pharaoh, as king, held all the power and ruled over everyone.

questions.prompt: What job did most people have in ancient Egypt?
questions.explanation: Farmers grew the food that fed the entire population of Egypt.

questions.prompt: Why couldn't farmers make important decisions about how Egypt was run?
questions.answer: Because they were at the bottom of the social pyramid.
questions.explanation: The people higher up in the pyramid, like the Pharaoh and scribes, had more power and influence.

mt_X7Tu94-a2m.title: Friendships Change – It’s Okay!
explanation: Sometimes, our friends change. Just like you grow taller and learn new things, your friendships can also change over time. It's perfectly normal for people to drift apart because they start liking different toys, playing different games, or going to different places. Think about your favorite LEGO set – maybe you used to build it with a best friend, but now you prefer building alone, and that’s okay!

When friendships change, it doesn't mean there’s anything wrong with anyone. It just means that people grow in different ways and have different interests. If you want to make new friends, try joining a club like the scouts or the art club – that way you'll meet lots of kids who enjoy similar things! You could also invite someone you think is nice to play your favorite game with you.

Let’s talk about Sarah and Tom. They were best friends in third grade and always played together at recess. But when they started fifth grade, Tom joined the soccer team and Sarah started taking ballet lessons. Soon, they weren't spending as much time together, but they still remembered each other fondly and sometimes saw them at school. It’s important to remember that friendships can change without anyone being upset – it just means their lives are going in different directions.
keyIdeas: Friendships naturally evolve over time.
Making new friends is possible through shared activities.
Changes in friendship don't reflect a problem with you or your friend.

questions.prompt: If your best friend starts spending more time playing video games, what should you do?
choices: A) Get angry and tell them to stop.
B) Try joining them for a game or activity.
C) Stop talking to them altogether.
questions.explanation: It’s good to be understanding and try to spend time with your friend doing something you both enjoy!

questions.prompt: Which of these is the BEST way to make new friends?
choices: B) Joining a club or activity that interests you.
questions.explanation: Joining a group with shared interests gives you opportunities to connect with others who enjoy similar things.

questions.prompt: Why is it okay if your friendships change?
choices: A) Because you’re not a good friend.
B) Because people grow and have different interests.
C) Because you should always stay friends with the same people.
questions.explanation: People change, and that's normal! It doesn’t mean you aren’t a good friend.

mt_FDKd7I79JZ.title: Meet the Gods! (Greek & Roman)
explanation: Let’s talk about ancient Greece, a very old place where people told amazing stories about gods and goddesses. These weren't just any characters; they were powerful beings who controlled things like the weather, love, and war! The Greeks believed these gods lived on Earth and had big impacts on their lives – it was like having superheroes in the sky.  Many of these gods are very familiar to us through stories and artwork.

The Romans also loved these stories, but they were a little different. They borrowed many of the Greek gods, which means they took them over and used them too! However, they changed the names to fit their own language – Latin. For example, the biggest god in Greece, Zeus, became Jupiter in Rome, and the goddess of marriage, Hera, became Juno. This shows how much the Romans admired and learned from the Greeks.

This swapping of names is a really cool way to see how cultures influence each other. It means that Rome was strongly influenced by Greek culture because they took ideas and stories from Greece and made them their own.  Think about it – if you played with your LEGOs and built something similar to a toy someone else had, that’s kind of like what happened here!
keyIdeas: Romans borrowed gods from Greeks.
Greek gods had different names in Rome.
This shows cultural influence.

questions.prompt: Which Roman god is the same as the Greek god Zeus?
questions.explanation: Jupiter was the Roman name for the powerful Greek god, Zeus.

questions.prompt: The goddess Hera became which Roman name?
questions.explanation: Hera in Greece became Juno in Rome – she ruled over marriage and family.

questions.prompt: Why did the Romans change the names of the Greek gods?
choices: A. They didn’t like the Greek names
B. To make them sound more powerful
C. Because they were using a different language (Latin)
questions.explanation: The Romans spoke Latin, so they changed the god's names to fit their own language.

mt_37QCuGOxFe.title: Homophones
explanation: Distinguish and correctly spell common homophones and near-homophones encountered at Y3-4 level (e.g., accept/except, affect/effect, brake/break, grate/great), including using the /eɪ/ sound spelt ei, eigh, or ey

You know this idea when you can: Spell pairs of homophones correctly in context (e.g., 'there/their/they're', 'brake/break', 'grate/great'); Choose the correct homophone to complete a sentence (e.g., 'The dog wagged its/it's tail').
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

mt_g4YSiOCS8g.title: Skeletons & Muscles
explanation: Identify that humans and some other animals have skeletons and muscles for support, protection, and movement

Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Identify that muscles are attached to bones and pull to create movement
Give examples of bones protecting organs (skull protects brain, ribs protect heart and lungs)

mt_AVk2EmSULC.title: Journey to the Planets!
explanation: Let's start our amazing space adventure by learning about the planets that orbit around our Sun! There are eight incredible planets, and they travel in a line – just like you might line up your toys before playing. The closest planet to the sun is Mercury, followed by Venus, then our very own Earth, and next comes Mars. It’s important to remember this order because it helps us understand how things move in space.

As we move further away from the Sun, we find Jupiter – a giant with swirling clouds! Then there's Saturn, famous for its beautiful rings made of ice and rock. After that is Uranus, which spins on its side, and finally Neptune, the farthest planet and known for its strong winds. Think of it like a really long line of friends all going around a central star – that’s our solar system!

Now, you might have heard about Pluto! Once, scientists thought Pluto was a planet too, but in 2006, they decided it wasn't big enough or round enough to be considered a full planet. Instead, Pluto became a ‘dwarf planet,’ which is still pretty cool and special.  It’s important to remember that even though some things change our understanding of space, there are still many amazing discoveries waiting for us!
keyIdeas: There are eight planets orbiting the Sun.
Pluto is now classified as a dwarf planet.
Inner planets are rocky and outer planets are gas giants.

questions.prompt: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the first planet from the sun.

questions.prompt: What happened to Pluto in 2006?
choices: A. It became a new star
B. It was reclassified as a dwarf planet
C. It moved closer to Earth
questions.explanation: Scientists determined Pluto didn’t meet all the criteria to be considered a full planet.

questions.prompt: Which type of planets are generally smaller and made of rock?
choices: C. Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars
questions.explanation: The inner planets – Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars – are small and rocky.

mt_QxsoqVUt6u.title: Teamwork Makes the Dream Work!
explanation: Imagine you're building a LEGO castle with your friends! When working together, it’s super important to share ideas and listen to what everyone else is thinking. If one person wants to build a tall tower, another might suggest adding a moat around the base – that’s teamwork in action!

To be a good team player, you need to really hear what your friends are saying. When someone has an idea, try to understand why they think it's good and maybe even add something similar or build on their thought. It’s like adding blocks to a bigger picture – each piece is important!

Everyone needs to do their part too! If you’re assigned to put the windows on the castle, make sure you finish that job quickly and neatly so the whole team can keep building. When everyone pulls their weight, the group achieves its goal faster and has more fun!
keyIdeas: Share your ideas.
Listen to others' ideas.
Do your part.

questions.prompt: Sarah wants to build a spaceship, but her friend Michael wants to build a race car. What should Sarah do?
choices: A) Tell Michael his idea is bad and build the spaceship anyway.
B) Listen to Michael’s ideas and see if she can combine them with hers.
C) Ignore Michael and just build the spaceship.
questions.explanation: Listening to others' ideas helps the group come up with a better plan.

questions.prompt: You are building a tower with your friends. Your friend suggests adding glitter to the top. What does this show?
choices: A) You should always do what your friend says.
B) You are listening to and building on another group member’s idea.
C) Glitter is messy and shouldn't be used in building projects.
questions.explanation: Building on someone else’s idea means you understand and agree with it, adding something new.

questions.prompt: What happens when everyone in a group does their part?
choices: A) The project takes forever to finish.
B) The group has more fun and finishes the project quickly.
C) Only one person does all the work.
questions.explanation: When everyone contributes, teamwork is successful!

mt_My0OL6fhGL.title: Advertising & Spending
explanation: How advertising tries to influence what we buy; being a critical consumer; understanding 'value for money'; the difference between emotional and rational spending

You know this idea when you can: Spot at least two persuasion techniques in a real advert (bright colours, celebrity, special offer); Explain what 'value for money' means and compare two similar products at different prices.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Explain what 'value for money' means and compare two similar products at different prices
Describe a time when advertising made them want something they didn't really need
