mt_Oru08pKlxd.title: Shadows: Where Did They Go?
explanation: Have you ever played with building blocks and noticed a dark shape on the wall behind them? That's a shadow! Shadows are made when light shines on something, but an object gets in the way. Think about your favorite toy car – when the sun is shining brightly, it makes a shadow of the car on the floor. The light from the sun can’t pass through the car because it’s solid.

Light travels in straight lines, like a beam shooting out from a flashlight. When that light hits an object, like a tree or even your hand, and the object is opaque (meaning it blocks light), some of the light stops moving forward. This blocked light then falls onto another surface, creating a dark area – that’s your shadow! It's like a secret hiding place for the light.

The size of your shadow changes depending on how close you are to the light source. If you move closer to the lamp, your shadow gets bigger because more of the light is blocked by your body.  If you step back, your shadow becomes smaller because less light is being blocked. Shadows always change size as things move!
keyIdeas: Shadows are formed when light is blocked.
The closer an object is to a light source, the bigger its shadow will be.
Opaque objects create shadows.

questions.prompt: What happens when light shines on an opaque object?
choices: B. Light is blocked and creates a shadow
questions.explanation: Shadows are created because light is stopped by the object.

questions.prompt: If you move your toy closer to a flashlight, what happens to its shadow?
choices: B. It stays the same size
questions.explanation: The closer an object is, the larger the shadow it creates.

questions.prompt: Which of these objects would most likely create a large shadow?
choices: C. A thin piece of paper
questions.explanation: The bigger the object blocking light, the larger the shadow will be.

mt_T6nrrf2K43.title: Prefixes (age 7+)
explanation: Spell words with a range of prefixes (dis-, mis-, un-, re-, pre-, anti-, auto-, super-) understanding how each prefix modifies the root word's meaning without changing its spelling

You know this idea when you can: Spell words with prefixes dis-, mis-, re- correctly without altering the root (e.g., disappoint, misspell, return); Add prefixes un-, pre-, anti-, auto-, super- to root words and use the new word in a sentence (e.g., unhappy, preview, autograph).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Spell words with prefixes dis-, mis-, re- correctly without altering the root (e.g., disappoint, misspell, return)
Add prefixes un-, pre-, anti-, auto-, super- to root words and use the new word in a sentence (e.g., unhappy, preview, autograph)
Explain how a prefix changes the meaning of a root word (e.g., 'dis-' means 'not' or 'opposite of')

questions.prompt: True or false: Spell words with prefixes dis-, mis-, re- correctly without altering the root (e.g., disappoint, misspell, return)
questions.explanation: Spell words with prefixes dis-, mis-, re- correctly without altering the root (e.g., disappoint, misspell, return)

questions.prompt: True or false: Add prefixes un-, pre-, anti-, auto-, super- to root words and use the new word in a sentence (e.g., unhappy, preview, autograph)
questions.explanation: Add prefixes un-, pre-, anti-, auto-, super- to root words and use the new word in a sentence (e.g., unhappy, preview, autograph)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain how a prefix changes the meaning of a root word (e.g., 'dis-' means 'not' or 'opposite of')
questions.explanation: Explain how a prefix changes the meaning of a root word (e.g., 'dis-' means 'not' or 'opposite of')

mt_e3-_toGuWf.title: Testing Materials for Uses
explanation: Today, we're going to be scientists and test different materials! We’ll learn why things are made of certain materials like metal, wood, or plastic. Scientists do this by carefully comparing how materials behave when they’re put to work – it’s called a fair test.  A fair test means that you change only one thing at a time so you can see exactly what's happening.

Let’s think about your building blocks! You might use wood to build a tower, but if you tried to make a strong table out of wood it wouldn’t work very well.  Metal is much stronger and doesn’t bend easily, which is why metal is used for things like saucepans because they need to be sturdy and withstand heat. We can test materials by seeing how hard they are to break or bend – that's evidence!

When we do a test, we write down what happens. This written record is our ‘evidence.’  For example, if we tested metal and wood for bending, the metal wouldn’t bend at all while the wood would easily snap. That evidence shows us why metal is better for making things that need to be strong, like pots and pans! Understanding this helps us choose the right material for the job.
keyIdeas: Fair tests are important for comparing materials.
Different materials have different properties (like strength).
Evidence from testing shows us why materials are used in specific ways.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the BEST way to do a fair test when comparing two blocks?
choices: A. Use both blocks at the same time.
B. Change only one thing, like how hard you push on each block.
questions.explanation: Changing only one thing (pushing harder) lets you see if that’s why one block broke.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're testing wood and metal for bending. Which material would likely bend the easiest?
choices: C. Both are equally easy to bend.
questions.explanation: Wood is softer and more flexible than metal, so it bends more easily.

questions.prompt: Why do you think saucepans are made of metal instead of plastic?
questions.explanation: Metal can withstand high temperatures without melting or deforming. Plastic would melt if put on a stove.

mt_38d-k-dJPa.title: Exploring the Amazing Amazon Rainforest
explanation: Imagine a place so big and green it covers nine countries! That's the Amazon rainforest, and it’s the biggest one on Earth. It’s like a giant, leafy playground for animals and plants – a truly amazing place to learn about nature. The Amazon is found in South America, mostly in Brazil, but also parts of Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, Guyana, Suriname, and French Guiana.

The Amazon River is super special because it’s the largest river in the world when you measure how much water flows through it. Think about your bathtub – a little bit of water goes down, but the Amazon has *so* much! Because of all this water and plants, the rainforest holds almost 10% of all the animals and plants on our planet; that’s a huge number!

Scientists have discovered incredible things in the Amazon. They've found over 40,000 different types of plants growing there, like giant trees and colorful flowers! There are also around 1,300 kinds of birds soaring through the air, and more than 3,000 different fish swimming in its rivers – it’s a real treasure trove for nature lovers.
keyIdeas: The Amazon is the largest rainforest.
It's found across nine South American countries.
It has an incredible amount of plant and animal life.

questions.prompt: Which continent does the Amazon Rainforest mostly belong to?
questions.explanation: The Amazon rainforest is located in South America, primarily within Brazil and other neighboring countries.

questions.prompt: Approximately what percentage of all Earth's species lives in the Amazon Rainforest?
questions.explanation: The Amazon rainforest is home to about 10% of all known plant and animal species on our planet.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many different types of plants can be found in the Amazon Rainforest?
questions.explanation: Researchers have identified over 40,000 different plant species within the vast Amazon rainforest.

mt_p1imGSFgJT.title: Word Families and Root Words
explanation: Explore word families based on common root words, understanding how words are related in form and meaning through shared roots, prefixes and suffixes (e.g., solve → solution, solver, dissolve, insoluble)

You know this idea when you can: Generate at least 4 words in a word family from a given root (e.g., from 'play': player, playful, replay, playground); Explain how members of a word family are connected in meaning (e.g., 'solve, solution, dissolve all relate to finding answers or breaking apart').
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Generate at least 4 words in a word family from a given root (e.g., from 'play': player, playful, replay, playground)
Explain how members of a word family are connected in meaning (e.g., 'solve, solution, dissolve all relate to finding answers or breaking apart')
Use knowledge of a word family to predict the meaning of an unfamiliar member (e.g., knowing 'act' helps understand 'actor', 'action', 'react')

questions.prompt: True or false: Generate at least 4 words in a word family from a given root (e.g., from 'play': player, playful, replay, playground)
questions.explanation: Generate at least 4 words in a word family from a given root (e.g., from 'play': player, playful, replay, playground)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain how members of a word family are connected in meaning (e.g., 'solve, solution, dissolve all relate to finding answers or breaking apart')
questions.explanation: Explain how members of a word family are connected in meaning (e.g., 'solve, solution, dissolve all relate to finding answers or breaking apart')

questions.prompt: True or false: Use knowledge of a word family to predict the meaning of an unfamiliar member (e.g., knowing 'act' helps understand 'actor', 'action', 'react')
questions.explanation: Use knowledge of a word family to predict the meaning of an unfamiliar member (e.g., knowing 'act' helps understand 'actor', 'action', 'react')

mt_QhLAeAlHI0.title: Fueling Your Body: The Food Groups!
explanation: Let's talk about how we get the energy to play and learn! Our bodies are like amazing machines, and just like a car needs fuel, we need food. There are many different groups of foods that help us grow strong and healthy. Some important ones include carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, fiber, and water – let’s explore what each one does.

Think about your favorite lunchbox sandwich! The bread is full of carbohydrates, which give you energy to run around at recess. Chicken or beans are packed with protein, helping your muscles grow strong for playing sports or building new tissues when you get a scrape. And maybe there’s some avocado – that's fat, providing warmth and keeping your body healthy.

Vitamins and minerals are like tiny superheroes inside us! They help our bodies fight off germs and stay healthy. Fiber keeps our tummies happy and working well, and water is super important for everything from sweating to carrying nutrients throughout our bodies. Eating a mix of these food groups is what makes a balanced diet!
keyIdeas: Food groups provide energy.
Proteins help build and repair our bodies.
Vitamins and minerals protect us from getting sick.

questions.prompt: Which food group gives you the most energy for playing?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are broken down into sugar, which our bodies use as quick energy.

questions.prompt: What do proteins help your body do?
choices: B. Help muscles grow and repair themselves
questions.explanation: Proteins are made of building blocks that our bodies use to fix damaged tissues.

questions.prompt: Why are vitamins and minerals important?
choices: A. They make food taste better
B. They protect your body from getting sick
questions.explanation: Vitamins and minerals work with our bodies' systems to keep us healthy.

mt_uSUqTjOl8m.title: Banks & Saving: Keeping Your Money Safe!
explanation: Have you ever wondered where banks are, or what they do with all the money people put in them? Banks are like giant safe boxes for your money! They keep it really secure and help people save up for things they want or need. Think about a toy store – when you buy a new LEGO set, you’re saving up your allowance to pay for it!

Now, let's talk about something called ‘interest.’ It’s like a little thank-you gift from the bank! When you put money in a savings account, the bank gives you just a tiny bit extra – interest – for letting them hold onto your money. This means that over time, your savings can actually grow bigger because of this small reward.

Keeping all your money at home isn't always the safest idea. Banks have security guards and special systems to protect your money from being stolen or lost. Plus, if there’s a fire or flood, the bank will keep your money safe for you – it’s much better than keeping everything in your bedroom!
keyIdeas: Banks safely store money.
Interest is extra money earned on savings.
Bank accounts are safer than home savings.

questions.prompt: What does a bank mostly do with people’s money?
choices: B. Keeps it safe and helps you save
C. Gives it away to charities
questions.explanation: Banks are designed to hold your money securely and help you grow it through savings.

questions.prompt: What is ‘interest’?
choices: B. Extra money the bank pays you for keeping your money there
C. A game played at the bank
questions.explanation: Interest is like a small reward from the bank for letting them hold your savings.

questions.prompt: Why might it be better to keep your money in a bank than at home?
choices: B. Banks are safer and protect your money
C. Banks always give you free candy
questions.explanation: Banks offer security measures and protection against loss or theft that aren't available at home.

mt_zIzJGkaj0Q.title: Basic digital citizenship
explanation: Understand basic digital citizenship — being kind online, protecting personal information, recognising that people behind screens are real people with real feelings, and knowing what to do if something online makes them uncomfortable
You know this idea when you can: State at least three rules for staying safe and being kind online; Explain why hurtful messages online are just as harmful as saying them in person.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: State at least three rules for staying safe and being kind online
Explain why hurtful messages online are just as harmful as saying them in person
Describe what they should do if someone online makes them feel uncomfortable or asks for personal information

questions.prompt: True or false: State at least three rules for staying safe and being kind online
questions.explanation: State at least three rules for staying safe and being kind online

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain why hurtful messages online are just as harmful as saying them in person
questions.explanation: Explain why hurtful messages online are just as harmful as saying them in person

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe what they should do if someone online makes them feel uncomfortable or asks for personal information
questions.explanation: Describe what they should do if someone online makes them feel uncomfortable or asks for personal information

mt_z9jn9HogfE.title: Arctic vs. Antarctic: Two Cold Worlds!
explanation: Let’s explore two amazing places – the Arctic and Antarctica! They're both super cold, but they are actually very different. The Arctic is a giant frozen ocean that covers part of the North Pole, like a big icy playground for animals. It’s surrounded by land like Canada, Russia, and Greenland, where people live and build their homes.

Antarctica is a completely different story – it's a huge, solid continent buried under miles and miles of ice! This ice sheet is so thick, it's like a giant blanket covering the whole land. Because it’s covered in ice, there aren’t any people living there permanently; instead, scientists build research stations to study this amazing place.

Think about your favorite toy – maybe you have a polar bear or penguin figure! Polar bears live only in the Arctic, while penguins call Antarctica home.  Both places are incredibly cold and unique, with special animals that have adapted to survive there.
keyIdeas: The Arctic is an ocean surrounded by land.
Antarctica is a frozen continent covered in ice.
Different animals live in each region because of the climate.

questions.prompt: Which of these places is a giant frozen ocean?
questions.explanation: The Arctic is described as a ‘frozen ocean’.

questions.prompt: What kind of animals live in the Antarctic?
questions.explanation: The lesson states that penguins, leopard seals, and albatrosses are found only in Antarctica.

questions.prompt: Which of these is TRUE about Antarctica?
choices: A. It has many people living there permanently.
B. It's covered by a huge ice sheet.
questions.explanation: The lesson describes Antarctica as a continent ‘covered by ice sheet up to…’

mt_6_O6THdEDK.title: Giving Change!
explanation: Let's talk about giving change! When you buy something, like a toy at the shop or some sweets, the person selling it might give you more money back than what you paid. This extra money is called ‘change’ and it's how they let you know exactly how much you owe them. Giving change is all about adding up the prices of things and then figuring out how much to return.

Imagine you go to the playground and want to buy a hot chocolate for £2.50. You give the person selling them £5.00. They need to figure out how much money you *don't* need back, which is £2.50 - £5.00.  We can use what we know about adding and subtracting numbers to solve this problem – it’s like a puzzle! We are trying to find the difference between the amount given and the cost.

When you give change, it's important to write it down correctly using pounds (£) and pence (p). For example, if someone gives you £2.30 in change after you buy something, that’s written as £2.30.  We can also write it as 230p because one pound is equal to 100 pence. Practicing this will help you become a super-smart shopper!
keyIdeas: Change is money returned after buying something.
Adding and subtracting helps us calculate change.
Correct notation (£ and p) is important for recording calculations.

questions.prompt: You buy a sticker for 50p and a pencil for £1.25. You pay with a £5 note. How much change do you get?
questions.explanation: First, add the cost of the sticker and pencil: 50p + £1.25 = £1.75. Then subtract that from the £5 note: £5.00 - £1.75 = £3.25.  The correct answer is £3.25.

questions.prompt: Sarah buys a book for £3.47 and a pen for 65p. She gives the shopkeeper £10.00. How much change does she receive?
questions.explanation: First, add the cost of the book and pen: £3.47 + 65p = £4.12. Then subtract that from the £10 note: £10.00 - £4.12 = £5.88. The correct answer is £5.88.

questions.prompt: What does ‘change’ mean in simple words?
questions.explanation: Change means the extra money you receive back after paying for something.

mt_Ytd8XC3eQr.title: My Body Says Hello (and Goodbye!) – Feeling Emotions
explanation: Let’s talk about how our feelings make our bodies feel! Sometimes, when we're happy or excited, we might have a bouncy feeling in our tummy like when you get ready to open presents on your birthday. Or maybe you feel warm and fuzzy inside when you cuddle with your favorite teddy bear – that’s a good feeling! Our bodies are amazing at showing us how we’re really feeling because they react to the emotions we have.

But what happens when we don't *like* how we feel? Sometimes, if we're angry, our fists might clench up tight. That’s our body trying to show us that we need to take a deep breath and calm down. Similarly, if you are scared, your heart might beat really fast like a drum – this is because your body is preparing for something important or potentially dangerous.

It's important to remember that these physical feelings aren’t bad; they’re just signals! Our bodies react physically when we have strong emotions because our brains and bodies are connected. When you feel nervous before a school play, your heart races – it’s like your body is saying, ‘Let’s get ready!’ Understanding these signs helps us know what we're feeling and how to take care of ourselves.
keyIdeas: Emotions have physical sensations.
Our bodies give us clues about how we feel.
Recognizing these signals can help us manage our emotions.

questions.prompt: If you clench your fists and feel angry, what might be happening in your body?
choices: A. Your heart is beating fast.
questions.explanation: Clenching your fists is a physical sign of anger.

questions.prompt: You're about to give a presentation at school and you feel butterflies in your stomach. What emotion might this be?
questions.explanation: Butterflies in the stomach are often a sign of excitement or nervousness before something important.

questions.prompt: Why does your heart beat faster when you're scared?
choices: A. It’s trying to hide from danger.
C. It doesn’t have anything to do with feelings.
questions.explanation: A fast heartbeat is a natural response to fear, preparing your body for ‘fight or flight’.

mt_oajUvqAiBJ.title: Humans vs. Machines: Who’s Really Smart?
explanation: Let’s talk about something really cool – computers and machines! They are getting smarter all the time, but they're still very different from us humans. Think about your favorite toy robot or video game character; it follows instructions perfectly, but it doesn’t truly *understand* what it’s doing like you do when you build with LEGOs or draw a picture.  Machines are amazing at doing things quickly and accurately, especially if they're programmed to repeat the same action over and over again.

Now, let’s look at what humans are really good at. We have something called ‘creativity,’ which means we can come up with new ideas – like inventing a story or designing a cool invention!  We also have ‘empathy,’ that's our ability to understand how other people feel and share their emotions; you know when your friend is sad and you want to comfort them. And, we use something called ‘common sense’ which means we can figure out things without being told exactly what to do – like knowing not to touch a hot stove!

Scientists even came up with a test called the Turing Test! It's like this: a computer tries to trick a person into thinking it’s actually a human. The computer might type messages and answer questions, just like you would. If a person can’t tell the difference between the computer and a real person, then the computer has passed the test – but it doesn’t mean the computer is *really* smart; it just means it's good at pretending!
keyIdeas: Humans excel at creativity and empathy.
Computers are faster and better at repetitive tasks.
The Turing Test tries to trick us into thinking computers are human.

questions.prompt: Which of these is something humans are usually better at than a computer?
choices: C) Following the same instructions exactly
questions.explanation: Computers are great at math and following rules, but humans can create art and express feelings.

questions.prompt: What does ‘common sense’ mean?
choices: A) Knowing how to build a computer
B) Being able to figure things out without being told
C) Playing video games really well
questions.explanation: Common sense is our ability to use what we know about the world to make good decisions.

questions.prompt: The Turing Test tries to do what?
choices: A) Measure how fast a computer can run
B) Trick people into thinking a computer is human
C) Teach computers how to draw pictures
questions.explanation: The Turing Test tests if a computer can fool a person into believing it's another person.

mt_NYA50DFcOO.title: Rock Detectives!
explanation: Have you ever built with LEGOs or blocks? Rocks are like giant building blocks, but they’re made by the Earth itself! There are three main kinds of rocks: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. Each type is formed in a different way, and scientists study them to learn about our planet's history.

Igneous rocks start as hot, melted rock called magma or lava. When this molten rock cools down quickly, like when you pour cold water on hot asphalt, it turns into solid igneous rock! Think of granite countertops in your kitchen – that’s a type of igneous rock formed from cooled magma deep underground.

Sedimentary rocks are made by tiny pieces of other rocks and plants getting squished together over millions of years. Imagine layers of sand and shells building up on the beach; when they're pressed tightly together, they form sedimentary rock. And guess what? Fossils – like dinosaur bones – are often found inside these layered rocks!
keyIdeas: Igneous rocks form from cooled lava/magma.
Sedimentary rocks form from compressed layers.
Fossils are found in sedimentary rock.

questions.prompt: Which type of rock is formed when lava cools?
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks are created when molten rock, like lava, solidifies.

questions.prompt: Where do you typically find fossils?
questions.explanation: Sedimentary rock forms in layers where sediments accumulate and can trap ancient remains.

questions.prompt: What happens to tiny pieces of rocks and plants when they are squeezed together to make sedimentary rock?
choices: C. They get compressed and layered
questions.explanation: The pressure from the layers causes the particles to pack tightly together.

mt_lutxvMlkwS.title: What Plants Need to Thrive
explanation: Plants are amazing living things, and just like us, they need certain things to grow big and strong! They can’t make their own food like we do; instead, they get everything they need from the world around them. Think about your favorite toy – a race car or a stuffed animal – plants also need specific things to ‘live’ and ‘grow’. These include air, light, water, nutrients from the soil, and enough space.

Let's look at how each of these things helps. Plants take in air through tiny holes in their leaves, just like we breathe! Sunlight gives them energy to make food through a process called photosynthesis – it’s like they are using sunshine as fuel. Water is super important because it travels up the plant’s stem and into its roots, helping it stay hydrated.

Now, not all plants need exactly the same amount of everything. Cacti, for example, live in dry places and don't need as much water as ferns which like damp environments. If you take away something a plant needs, like if you didn’t give a plant enough sunlight, it won't grow well! This shows us that plants have specific requirements to thrive.
keyIdeas: Plants need air, light, water, nutrients, and space.
Different plants have different needs based on where they live.
Removing one of these needs can stop a plant from growing properly.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT something a plant needs to grow?
questions.explanation: Plants need sunlight and water, but they don't eat chocolate!

questions.prompt: Why do plants need space around them?
choices: B. To grow bigger roots and leaves
questions.explanation: Plants need room to grow their roots and leaves, just like you need space to move around!

questions.prompt: Imagine a plant that doesn't get any water. What do you think will happen?
questions.explanation: The plant will likely wilt and eventually die because it needs water to survive.

mt_Ag9NSWJu-X.title: Talking About Feelings: Building Better Communication
explanation: Let’s talk about how we communicate with each other! Sometimes, when we don't get what we want or feel strongly about something, it can lead to disagreements. Learning different ways to talk is super important for making friends and solving problems nicely. It’s like learning a new game – you need the rules to play it well!

There are lots of words that describe how we communicate. For example, ‘assertive’ means standing up for yourself in a calm and respectful way, like saying 'I don't like it when you take my toys without asking.'  ‘Aggressive’ is different – it means being loud or demanding, which isn’t always fair or helpful. Think about your favorite building blocks; if someone keeps taking them without asking, that would be aggressive!

When there's a problem, we can try to ‘resolve’ the conflict by working together to find a solution. One way to do this is using ‘I feel…’ statements – like saying 'I feel sad when you don't share.'  This helps everyone understand how they are feeling and what needs to be done to make things better.
keyIdeas: Assertive communication means expressing your needs calmly.
Conflict resolution is finding a solution to disagreements together.
Respecting boundaries and feelings is key to healthy friendships.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes assertive communication?
choices: A) Yelling at someone to get your way.
B) Saying what you need calmly and respectfully.
C) Ignoring the problem and hoping it goes away.
questions.explanation: Assertive means being confident but polite – not angry or demanding.

questions.prompt: What does ‘conflict resolution’ mean?
choices: B) Trying to find a solution to a problem with others.
C) Arguing until someone gives in.
questions.explanation: Conflict resolution is about working together to fix things when you disagree.

questions.prompt: You and your friend are arguing over a game. What’s one thing you could say to show you're being assertive?
questions.explanation: Example: ‘I feel frustrated that I didn’t get a turn.’

mt_iWGnyUyN2j.title: Friends Fight? Let's Solve It!
explanation: Sometimes, you and your friends don’t agree about things – like what game to play or who gets the last cookie! When disagreements happen, it’s important to remember that everyone has a different opinion, and that's okay. The first step is to calmly explain how *you* feel; use words like ‘I feel…’ or ‘I think…’ so your friend understands where you’re coming from.  It’s like when you build with LEGOs – if someone takes your block without asking, you can say, 'I was using that one!'

Next, it's super important to listen to what your friend is saying. Don’t interrupt them or start arguing before they finish! Try to understand *why* they feel the way they do; maybe they really wanted to play that game too.  Think about it like this: if a friend tells you their favorite color is blue, you listen and ask why they like blue – you don't just say ‘No, blue isn’t the best!’

Finally, let’s work together to find a solution! A compromise means that both of you get something you want. You could suggest taking turns playing different games or finding a new game that everyone enjoys.  It’s like sharing a toy – if you both agree on how to play with it, you'll have more fun together!
keyIdeas: Express your feelings calmly.
Listen carefully to understand others.
Work together to find solutions.

questions.prompt: You and a friend disagree about which movie to watch. You want a funny one, but your friend wants a superhero movie. What should you do first?
choices: A) Tell your friend they are wrong and pick the funny movie.
B) Explain why you like the funny movie and ask your friend why they prefer the superhero movie.
C) Just choose the superhero movie because it’s cooler.
questions.explanation: It's important to explain *your* feelings and listen to *their* perspective.

questions.prompt: Your friend is upset because you took their favorite toy without asking. What should you do?
choices: A) Tell your friend they are being silly for getting so upset.
B) Apologize for taking the toy and ask if you can play with it together.
C) Ignore them and continue playing with the toy.
questions.explanation: Apologizing and offering a solution shows you understand their feelings.

questions.prompt: You and your friend want to build a sandcastle, but you have different ideas. How can you solve this disagreement?
choices: A) Build your own sandcastle separately.
B) Compromise by building one big castle together with parts from both of your ideas.
C) Argue about whose idea is better until someone gives up.
questions.explanation: Finding a compromise means both people get something they want.

mt_HPf-dVtA3p.title: Fair Trade Heroes!
explanation: Have you ever looked at a toy or a snack, like your favorite chocolate bar? Everything we buy – from crayons to clothes – is made by someone! Often, those people live far away in other countries and work hard to make the things we enjoy. It’s important to remember that when you choose something to buy, you're helping someone earn a living.

Now, let's talk about 'fair trade.' Imagine if someone didn't get paid enough for their work – it wouldn't be fair, right? Fair trade means that the people who grow coffee beans or make clothes are paid a good price for what they do. This helps them and their families have enough money to buy food and other important things.

You can be a 'Fair Trade Hero' too! Look for special labels like ‘fair trade’ on products, especially chocolate or fruit. Choosing these items means you're supporting workers who get paid fairly and helping the environment by reducing waste – it’s a super kind choice!
keyIdeas: People make our things.
Fair trade helps workers earn money.
Choosing fair trade is a good decision.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when something is ‘fair trade’?
choices: A. It's the cheapest thing to buy.
B. Workers who make the product get paid fairly.
C. It always comes from a big company.
questions.explanation: Fair trade means that people making products are treated with respect and paid what they deserve.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re buying fruit. Which choice would be a good ‘fair trade hero’ action?
choices: A. Buying the most colorful fruit, even if it costs more.
B. Choosing fruit with less packaging to help the planet.
C. Not caring where the fruit comes from.
questions.explanation: Reducing packaging is one way to support sustainable practices and reduce waste.

questions.prompt: Why is it important for people who make things to get paid fairly?
choices: A. So they can buy more toys.
B. So they can have enough money for food and a good life.
C. It doesn't matter what they earn.
questions.explanation: Fair pay allows workers to meet their basic needs and live comfortably.

mt_xPqczp7zPX.title: Adding and Subtracting Hundreds!
explanation: Let’s talk about adding and subtracting numbers, especially when they get big! Sometimes we need to add or take away hundreds at a time. Think about your LEGO bricks – you might have 345 bricks in one box and then someone gives you another box with 200 bricks. To find out how many you have *total*, you need to add them together, but it’s easier if we think about the hundreds first.

When we add or subtract hundreds, it's like moving a group of things to a new place. For example, let’s say you have 762 crayons and your friend gives you 400 more! To figure out how many you have in total, we don't need to count every single crayon; instead, we can focus on the hundreds. The 700 from 762 will combine with the 400 to make 1100 crayons – that’s a big jump!

It’s important to remember that only the *hundreds* digit changes when you add or subtract hundreds. If you're adding 200 to 345, you don’t change the tens or ones; just add those 200 onto the 400 to make 600. This makes it much faster than counting every single number!
keyIdeas: Adding/subtracting hundreds involves only changing the hundreds digit.
We can use our fingers or draw pictures to help us add and subtract hundreds.
Practice mentally adding and subtracting hundreds will make you a speedy math whiz!

questions.prompt: What is 345 + 200?
questions.explanation: We add the hundreds (300 + 200 = 500) and then the tens and ones (45 + 0 = 45). So, 345 + 200 = 645.

questions.prompt: What is 762 - 400?
questions.explanation: We subtract the hundreds (700 - 400 = 300) and then the tens and ones (62 - 0 = 62). So, 762 - 400 = 362.

questions.prompt: If you have 500 blocks and someone gives you 300 more, how many do you have?
questions.explanation: You would have 800 blocks. This question tests your understanding of adding hundreds.

explanation: Mentally add and subtract a three-digit number and tens
You know this idea when you can: Calculate 345 + 40 mentally; Calculate 462 − 30 mentally.

questions.explanation: Explain that only the tens digit changes (unless bridging through a hundred)

mt_98c2qwEF7Q.title: Place Value Adventure to 1000!
explanation: Let's talk about numbers! When we write a number, like 342, it’s made up of different groups of ones, tens, and hundreds. Think about your LEGO bricks – you can build with individual bricks (ones), stacks of ten bricks (tens), or even bigger piles of one hundred bricks (hundreds). Each place value shows us how much each digit is worth.

The number 342 has three parts: the ‘3’ is in the hundreds place, meaning it represents 300. The ‘4’ is in the tens place, showing that there are 40. And finally, the ‘2’ is in the ones place, which means we have just 2 individual ones.  We can write this as 3 hundred + 4 ten + 2 one.

Place value helps us understand big numbers! Imagine you're counting your pocket money – let's say you have £2.87. The ‘2’ represents two pounds, the ‘87’ shows how many pennies (or cents) you have.  Understanding place value is like having a secret code to unlock all sorts of math problems and help us solve real-world situations.
keyIdeas: Numbers are made up of hundreds, tens, and ones.
Each digit has a specific value based on its place.
Place value helps us understand and work with large numbers.

questions.prompt: I have 2 hundreds, 5 tens, and 8 ones — what number is that?
questions.explanation: Let's break it down! 200 + 50 + 8 = 258.

questions.prompt: Which number is the same as 700 + 30?
questions.explanation: Adding 30 to 700 means you're adding 30 to the hundreds place, which equals 730.

questions.prompt: What does the ‘5’ represent in the number 156?
questions.explanation: The ‘5’ is in the tens place, so it represents fifty (50).

mt_w4nSIDhIgC.title: Teamwork Roles!
explanation: Imagine you’re building a LEGO castle with your friends – that’s like being in a group! People can play different roles to help the castle get built. Some people might be the ‘builder’, carefully putting bricks together, while others could be the ‘designer’, deciding what the castle should look like.  It's really cool how many ways we can work together and each person can contribute something special.

Let’s think about some other roles. A 'helper' could bring snacks to keep everyone energized during building time! Or maybe someone is a ‘questioner’, asking important questions like, "Should we make the tower taller?"  Even just listening carefully and offering encouragement is a role – being a supportive friend makes a big difference in any group. These roles aren’t about bossing people around; they're about working together.

Groups work best when everyone takes on different jobs. If only one person wanted to be the leader all the time, it wouldn't be much fun!  Different roles bring different ideas and skills to the table, making the group stronger and more creative. It’s like a team – each player has a part to play to win the game!
keyIdeas: Roles help groups work well.
Everyone can contribute in different ways.
Variety of roles makes a group stronger.

questions.prompt: Which role is someone who helps others?
questions.explanation: A helper supports and assists other members of the group.

questions.prompt: What’s a good reason why groups need different roles?
choices: A. To have someone in charge all the time
B. To bring different ideas and skills
C. To make building castles harder
questions.explanation: Different roles allow for a wider range of perspectives and abilities.

questions.prompt: If you like to come up with new ideas, what role might you play in a group?
questions.explanation: You could be the idea-generator!  Think about times you've had great suggestions – that’s your role shining through.

mt__bhJX2SuFJ.title: Sorting and Identifying Minibeasts
explanation: Using classification keys to identify minibeasts. Branching yes/no questions: 'Does it have legs?' → 'How many legs?' → 'Does it have wings?' Dichotomous keys as a systematic tool for sorting and identifying creatures.

You know this idea when you can: Follow a simple branching key to correctly identify at least four different minibeasts; Create a yes/no question that separates insects from spiders, such as 'Does it have six legs?'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Follow a simple branching key to correctly identify at least four different minibeasts
Create a yes/no question that separates insects from spiders, such as 'Does it have six legs?'
Explain why asking questions in a set order helps identify a creature you have never seen before

questions.explanation: Create a yes/no question that separates insects from spiders, such as 'Does it have six legs?'

explanation: Describe how robots and rovers have explored places humans cannot easily go — Mars rovers like Curiosity and Perseverance drive across Mars taking photos, collecting rock samples, and searching for signs of past water

You know this idea when you can: Name at least one Mars rover (Curiosity or Perseverance) and state it drives on Mars's surface; Explain that rovers take photos, analyse rocks, and look for evidence of water.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name at least one Mars rover (Curiosity or Perseverance) and state it drives on Mars's surface
Explain that rovers take photos, analyse rocks, and look for evidence of water
State why we send robots instead of people: Mars is very far away and has no breathable air

questions.explanation: State why we send robots instead of people: Mars is very far away and has no breathable air

mt_JpQUM1129q.title: The Amazing Journey of Your Food!
explanation: Imagine you just ate a yummy sandwich – that’s a big adventure for your food! When you take a bite, your mouth starts the process. Your teeth are like tiny grinders, breaking down the bread and filling with saliva which is a watery liquid that helps start to break down the food into smaller pieces.

Next, the sandwich slides down a long tube called the oesophagus – think of it like a slide! It pushes the food all the way to your stomach. Your stomach is like a mixing bowl; it uses strong juices to churn and mix up the food even more, preparing it for the next stage.

After the stomach, the food goes into the small intestine where most of the good stuff – vitamins and minerals – are pulled out and sent to your body. Finally, what’s left goes into the large intestine, which takes out extra water before everything leaves your body as waste.
keyIdeas: Food travels through a series of organs.
Each organ has a special job in breaking down food.
Nutrients are absorbed from the small intestine to help our bodies grow.

questions.prompt: What is the first place your sandwich goes after you bite it?
questions.explanation: Your mouth starts breaking down the food with teeth and saliva.

questions.prompt: Where do most of the nutrients from your sandwich get absorbed?
questions.explanation: The small intestine is designed to absorb vitamins and minerals from food.

questions.prompt: What happens in the large intestine after the nutrients are absorbed?
choices: B. Water is removed from the waste.
C. The stomach mixes the food.
questions.explanation: The large intestine takes out extra water, helping to form solid waste.

mt_yxL1v4LuqR.title: Exploring the Ocean Floor!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs – sometimes you build flat walls, but other times you make mountains and valleys! The ocean floor is just like that; it's not a smooth, flat surface. Scientists have discovered amazing things hidden beneath the waves, including huge mountains, long underwater ridges, and incredibly deep trenches.

The deepest part of our planet’s oceans is called the Mariana Trench. It’s located in the Pacific Ocean near Japan, and it’s absolutely astonishing! This trench is so deep that if you put Mount Everest – the tallest mountain on Earth – right at the bottom, it would still stick out way up above the water!

Scientists use special submarines to explore these incredible places. They've found all sorts of amazing creatures living in the darkness and pressure of the trenches. It’s a really exciting place for scientists to learn about how life can exist even in some of the most extreme environments on Earth.
keyIdeas: The ocean floor has varied terrain.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest point in the ocean.
Scientists explore the ocean floor using submarines.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the deepest part of the ocean?
questions.explanation: The Mariana Trench is known as the deepest point in the world’s oceans.

questions.prompt: Which mountain is deeper than Mount Everest?
questions.explanation: The Mariana Trench is deeper than Mount Everest, making it the Earth's deepest point.

questions.prompt: What kind of features does the ocean floor have besides mountains?
questions.explanation: The ocean floor has underwater ridges and deep trenches, making it a complex landscape.

mt_wHN14Unk7h.title: Animal Communication
explanation: Understand that animals communicate in many different ways — birds sing to attract mates and defend territory, whales call across vast ocean distances, bees dance to show other bees where food is, wolves howl to keep the pack together, and fireflies flash light signals — and that communication is essential for survival
You know this idea when you can: Describes at least 4 different methods of animal communication (sound, dance, light, scent, body language); Gives a specific animal example for each method.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describes at least 4 different methods of animal communication (sound, dance, light, scent, body language)
Gives a specific animal example for each method
Explains that animals communicate to find mates, warn of danger, or share food locations

questions.prompt: True or false: Describes at least 4 different methods of animal communication (sound, dance, light, scent, body language)
questions.explanation: Describes at least 4 different methods of animal communication (sound, dance, light, scent, body language)

questions.explanation: Gives a specific animal example for each method

questions.explanation: Explains that animals communicate to find mates, warn of danger, or share food locations

mt_AxGPeVRm__.title: Amazing Coral Reefs!
explanation: Have you ever seen a colorful toy castle? Coral reefs look like amazing castles underwater, but they aren't made of rock! They’re actually built by tiny little animals called coral polyps. These polyps are like busy builders, slowly adding layer after layer to create the beautiful shapes and structures we see on a reef.

Coral reefs are incredibly important because they’re home to more different kinds of plants and animals than almost any other place in the ocean. Think about all the fish you might see at an aquarium – coral reefs have even *more*! This means they're one of the most biodiverse habitats, which is a fancy way of saying there are lots and lots of different living things.

That’s why scientists call coral reefs the ‘rainforests of the sea.’ Just like rainforests on land are full of amazing plants and animals, coral reefs are packed with an incredible variety of life. Many creatures rely on the reef for food, shelter, and a place to raise their young – it's a really busy and important place!
keyIdeas: Coral reefs are built by tiny animals called polyps.
Reefs have more different types of life than almost any other ocean habitat.
Reefs are often compared to rainforests because of their incredible biodiversity.

questions.prompt: Coral reefs are made of:
choices: B. Tiny living animals called polyps
questions.explanation: The coral itself is built by these tiny, living creatures!

questions.prompt: How many different kinds of life can you find on a coral reef?
choices: B. More than any other ocean habitat
questions.explanation: Coral reefs are incredibly diverse and home to a huge range of creatures.

questions.prompt: Why are coral reefs sometimes called ‘rainforests of the sea’?
choices: B. Because they have lots of plants
C. Because they have many different kinds of life
questions.explanation: Just like rainforests, coral reefs are full of a huge variety of living things!

mt_f4O__f3OU4.title: Amazing Ancient Greek Theatre!
explanation: Long ago, in ancient Greece, people loved to put on shows! They invented theatre – that’s a fancy word for plays – and they were really good at it. These plays weren't like the ones you see today; they were part of important religious celebrations and told exciting stories about gods, heroes, and everyday life. The Greeks thought these performances were a way to connect with their gods and learn valuable lessons.

The Greek theatres were huge! They were built outdoors in big hills called amphitheaters – think of them like giant outdoor stadiums. Actors wore special masks that covered their faces and helped make their voices loud enough for everyone to hear, even though the theaters could hold thousands of people. These masks also showed different emotions, like happiness or sadness, without the actors having to change their expressions constantly.

These plays weren't just fun; they were important! They were performed as part of festivals dedicated to Dionysus, a god who represented wine and theatre. The Greeks believed these performances honored him and helped them understand the world around them. It was like a big, exciting religious show with lots of music and dancing!
keyIdeas: The Greeks invented theatre.
Actors wore masks for louder voices and to show emotions.
Plays were part of religious festivals.

questions.prompt: What was the main purpose of Greek theatre?
choices: A. To entertain people with funny stories
B. To honor the god Dionysus and teach lessons
C. To build big stadiums for sports
questions.explanation: Greek plays were part of religious festivals, so their primary goal was to honor the gods and share important ideas.

questions.prompt: Why did Greek actors wear masks?
choices: A. Because they wanted to look pretty
B. Because it was hot outside and they needed shade
C. To make their voices louder
questions.explanation: The masks helped the actors project their voices across the large, open-air amphitheaters.

questions.prompt: Where were Greek plays performed?
choices: B. In giant, outdoor hills called amphitheaters
questions.explanation: Greek theatres were built into hillsides, creating large open-air spaces for performances.
