questions.explanation: Explain the difference between the topic of a story and its central message or lesson

mt_SH7QgFl8-v.title: Shape Transformations!
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes moving around! When we change how a shape looks without changing its size or shape, that's called a transformation. There are three main types of transformations: reflections, translations, and rotations. Think about your LEGO bricks – you can build something amazing and then rearrange the pieces in different ways to create new designs; these changes are similar to transformations.

A reflection is like looking at a picture in a mirror! It's when you flip the shape across a line, just like how your image appears in a mirror. For example, if we reflect a square across the x-axis (the horizontal line), it will appear as an identical copy but on the other side of that line. We can use coordinates to find exactly where each point goes during this reflection.

Translations are like sliding shapes! You move them without flipping them. Imagine you’re moving a toy car across your playroom floor – you're translating it. When we translate a shape, every point moves the same distance in the same direction, and we can describe that movement with a 'vector', which is just a fancy way of saying how much to move left, right, up or down.
keyIdeas: Reflections involve flipping shapes across a line.
Translations involve sliding shapes without changing their orientation.
Rotations change the angle of a shape around a central point.

questions.prompt: If we reflect a triangle across the y-axis, what happens to its coordinates?
choices: A. They stay exactly the same.
questions.explanation: When you reflect a shape across the y-axis, every x-coordinate changes sign (positive becomes negative and vice versa).

questions.prompt: A translation moves a shape 3 squares to the left and 2 squares up. How would you describe this movement?
choices: A. 3 squares right and 2 squares up.
B. 3 squares left and 2 squares up.
C. 3 squares right and 2 squares down.
D. 3 squares left and 2 squares down.
questions.explanation: The phrase '3 squares to the left' means moving in the negative x-direction, and '2 squares up' means moving in the positive y-direction.

questions.prompt: What does it mean for a shape’s image after a rotation to be congruent to the original shape?
choices: A. It is bigger than the original.
B. It has a different color.
C. The two shapes are identical in size and shape.
questions.explanation: Congruent means exactly the same – they have the same size and shape, even if they look rotated.

mt_i4bDqjyglj.title: Animal Groups & Survival
explanation: Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive, such as herds, packs, or colonies

You know this idea when you can: Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools); Explain how group living provides survival advantages (protection, finding food, raising young).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools)
Explain how group living provides survival advantages (protection, finding food, raising young)
Construct a simple argument with evidence for why a specific animal benefits from group behaviour

questions.prompt: True or false: Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools)
questions.explanation: Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain how group living provides survival advantages (protection, finding food, raising young)
questions.explanation: Explain how group living provides survival advantages (protection, finding food, raising young)

questions.prompt: True or false: Construct a simple argument with evidence for why a specific animal benefits from group behaviour
questions.explanation: Construct a simple argument with evidence for why a specific animal benefits from group behaviour

mt_-af65bxfdp.title: Measuring Liquids and Masses!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about measuring things like liquids and solids! Measuring means figuring out exactly how much of something there is. We use special tools, like scales for weight (mass) and measuring cups or jugs for liquids, to get accurate numbers. Think about when you’re baking cookies – you need to measure the flour and sugar carefully so your cookies turn out just right!

There are two main things we can measure: mass and volume. Mass is how much ‘stuff’ something has, like a toy car or a box of crayons. We measure mass in grams (g) for smaller items and kilograms (kg) for bigger ones – one kilogram is equal to 1000 grams! Volume is the amount of space something takes up, like the water in your juice bottle.

We use litres (L) to measure liquids, and grams or kilograms to measure solids. Let’s try an example: If you have three bags of marbles that each weigh 250 grams, we can add those numbers together to find the total weight! This is a simple way to solve problems about mass – just remember to keep track of all the individual amounts.
keyIdeas: Measuring involves using tools like scales and measuring cups.
Mass is how much ‘stuff’ something has, measured in grams or kilograms.
Volume is how much space something takes up, measured in litres.

questions.prompt: A scale measures what?
questions.explanation: A scale is used to measure the amount of matter, or mass, in an object.

questions.prompt: Which unit do we use to measure a large box of toys?
questions.explanation: Kilograms are used for heavier objects like boxes of toys.

questions.prompt: Three bags each weigh 150 grams. What is the total weight in grams?

mt_hbe_kdE_7C.title: Spotting Rules & Patterns!
explanation: Have you ever noticed something happening over and over again? That's a pattern! A rule is just a way to describe that repeating pattern, like saying ‘every time I clap my hands, someone else will clap back.’ Patterns can be found in all sorts of things – think about the blocks you build with; you might notice you always stack them up high before making a tower. Recognizing these patterns helps us understand how things work and make predictions.

Let’s try an example: Imagine you're building with LEGOs. You might start by putting down one brick, then another, then another – that’s a pattern! Now, let’s call this rule ‘Add One Brick.’ To test if the rule works, we can build a tower with five bricks and then add another brick to see if it follows our rule. If it does, great! We've found a rule that works.

It’s important to understand that just because something happens once or twice doesn't mean it *always* will.  We need to test the rule with lots of different examples to be sure it really is a rule and not just a lucky coincidence.  So, noticing a pattern is like seeing a clue, but proving it’s a rule means we have solid evidence that it always works the same way.
keyIdeas: Patterns repeat.
Rules describe patterns.
Testing rules helps us prove they work.

questions.prompt: You see a pattern: Red, Blue, Red, Blue… What is the rule?
choices: A. Add one color each time
B. Repeat colors in a sequence
questions.explanation: The pattern repeats red and blue in that order.

questions.prompt: You build a tower with 10 blocks, following your rule ‘Add One Brick.’ You add another brick. Does your rule still work?
choices: B. No, the tower will fall down
C. Maybe, depending on how you added the brick
questions.explanation: If the rule is ‘Add One Brick,’ adding one more block continues the pattern.

questions.prompt: Sarah says, 'Every time I eat an apple, I feel happy!' Is this a rule or just a guess?
choices: A. It’s definitely a rule because she always feels happy after eating an apple
B. It might be a rule, but we need to test it with more examples
questions.explanation: Just feeling happy once doesn't prove that every time she eats an apple, she will feel happy.

mt_pjfmCMMPjO.title: Mental Math Adventures: Multiplication and Division!
explanation: Today, we're going to become mental math superheroes! We’ll learn how to solve multiplication and division problems in our heads without using a calculator. It might seem tricky at first, but with practice, you’ll be amazed at how quickly you can do it. This is called ‘mental multiplication’ and ‘mental division’, and it's like having a secret superpower for math!

Let’s look at an example: Imagine you have 4 boxes of crayons, and each box has 6 crayons inside. To find out the total number of crayons, we can think about it this way – if we had 4 groups of 6 crayons each, that would be like saying 4 multiplied by 6. We know that 4 x 6 = 24, so you have 24 crayons in total! This is a great example of how to use what you already know about multiplication.

Now let’s talk about dividing. If we had 24 crayons and wanted to share them equally between 6 friends, we would divide by 6. We can think of it as asking ‘how many times does 6 go into 24?’ The answer is 4!  Any number divided by 1 always equals itself – that’s why dividing by 1 is super easy. And remember, multiplying by zero always gives you zero!
keyIdeas: Place value helps us with multiplication.
Multiplying by 0 or 1 is always easy.
Choosing a useful pair of numbers can make multiplication faster.

questions.prompt: What is 40 x 6?
questions.explanation: We can think of this as 4 groups of 6, which equals 24.

questions.prompt: What is 1 x 35?
questions.explanation: Any number multiplied by one remains the same.

questions.prompt: If you have 100 cookies and want to share them equally with 100 people, how many cookies does each person get?
questions.explanation: Dividing by 100 gives you 0.

mt_wB-GBDkoNr.title: Decimals and Fractions: Measuring & Money!
explanation: Today, we’re going to explore decimals and fractions – they both help us talk about parts of things! Think about your favorite toy; maybe you have a LEGO set with lots of different pieces. Fractions like 1/2 or 1/4 show us how many pieces are in a group, while decimals like 0.5 or 0.25 show us smaller amounts – like half a piece if the LEGO was really tiny!

Let’s look at money! When you buy something, you often pay with dollars and cents. Decimals are perfect for representing these amounts; for example, $1.75 is one dollar and seventy-five cents. We can use fractions too – 3/4 of a dollar is the same as $0.75 because three out of four parts make up a whole dollar.

We’ll practice some problems using both decimals and fractions to measure things like rope lengths or weights, and even calculate how much money we have. It's all about understanding that both ways can show us exactly the same amount – just in different ways!
keyIdeas: Decimals represent parts of a whole smaller than one.
Fractions also represent parts of a whole.
Both decimals and fractions can be used to solve measurement and money problems.

questions.prompt: What is 1/4 of £3.20?
A rope is 2.5 m long; how much is left after cutting 0.75 m?
questions.explanation: First, subtract: 2.5 m - 0.75 m = 1.75 m

questions.prompt: Find 3/10 of 1 kg and express the answer in grams and as a decimal of a kg.
questions.explanation: First, calculate 3/10 of 1kg: (3/10) * 1kg = 0.3 kg.  Then convert to grams: 0.3 kg * 1000g/kg = 300g

mt_vnJEztczji.title: Shape Nets: Unfolding Fun!
explanation: Have you ever taken apart a cardboard box to build a fort? That’s kind of like working with shape nets! A shape net is a flat pattern made up of shapes that, when folded together, create a three-dimensional object. Think about a cube – it has six square faces. When you unfold a cube, you'll see all those squares arranged in a specific way to make the net.

Let’s look at some examples: A cuboid is like a box - it has six rectangular faces. You can create a net for a cuboid by arranging these rectangles into a flat shape that shows how they connect when you fold it up.  A triangular prism has five faces, including two triangles and three rectangles; the same principle applies – you’ll arrange them to form a net.

The key is understanding that each face of the 3D shape appears on the net. If you can see all the faces and how they fit together, you can fold it back up!  It's like solving a puzzle where you have to figure out which flat pattern will create a solid shape when put together.
keyIdeas: Nets are 2D patterns that make 3D shapes.
The faces of the 3D shape appear on the net.
You can fold a net to build a 3D shape.

questions.prompt: Which of these nets could be folded into a cube? (Image of a cube net)
choices: A. A square with a smaller square cut out
B. A rectangle with two squares attached to each side
C. A shape made of six squares arranged in a specific pattern
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C because it shows the six faces needed to form a cube when folded.

questions.prompt: You have a net with three long rectangles and one triangle. What 3D shape could this make? (Image of the net)
questions.explanation: This net represents the faces of a triangular prism, which has three rectangular sides and two triangular ends.

questions.prompt: Sarah builds a 3D shape from its net. She counts 5 faces, 6 edges, and 4 vertices. What 3D shape did she build? (Image of a square-based pyramid net)
questions.explanation: She built a square-based pyramid because it has a square base and four triangular sides – that’s 5 faces, 8 edges (4 from the base and 4 connecting to the apex), and 5 vertices (4 at the corners of the base and one at the apex).

mt_LMX-nZETLM.title: Vivid Word Choices
explanation: Choose precise and vivid words and phrases to create specific effects in writing

You know this idea when you can: Replace vague words in 'The nice man went to the big house' with more precise alternatives and explain the effect; Write two versions of the same scene — one with plain language and one with carefully chosen words for suspense or humour.

questions.explanation: Replace vague words in 'The nice man went to the big house' with more precise alternatives and explain the effect

questions.prompt: True or false: Write two versions of the same scene — one with plain language and one with carefully chosen words for suspense or humour
questions.explanation: Write two versions of the same scene — one with plain language and one with carefully chosen words for suspense or humour

questions.prompt: True or false: Select from a word bank the most effective verb and adjective for a given sentence, justifying each choice
questions.explanation: Select from a word bank the most effective verb and adjective for a given sentence, justifying each choice

mt_w4wKFP3jud.title: Unlocking Connections in Stories!
explanation: Today we’re going to be detectives – but instead of finding clues, we'll be looking for connections between ideas in stories and information! When you read something, it's not just about what happens one after the other; authors often want us to see how things are related. Think about building with LEGOs - each brick connects to another to make a bigger picture, right?  We can do the same thing when we read by noticing how ideas link together.

There are different ways ideas connect. One way is *cause and effect*, which means one thing makes something else happen. For example, if you spill juice (the cause), it makes a mess (the effect). Another important connection is *sequence*, where things happen in a specific order – like following a recipe to bake cookies!  Authors use words like ‘first,’ ‘next,’ and ‘finally’ to show us this sequence.

Let's look at how paragraphs connect. Sometimes, one paragraph explains something that the next paragraph talks about further. Maybe a paragraph describes a problem (the cause) and then another paragraph tells you what happened as a result (the effect).  Or perhaps two paragraphs compare different things – like comparing how a bird builds its nest versus how a beaver builds its dam. Recognizing these connections helps us understand the whole story better!
keyIdeas: Cause and Effect: One thing leads to another.
Sequence: Events happen in a specific order.
Connections: Authors link ideas together for clarity.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are building a tower of blocks. What might be an example of cause and effect?
choices: A) You put the first block on the table.
B) Because you added more blocks, the tower fell over.
questions.explanation: The falling tower is the *effect* because it happened *because* you added more blocks – that's cause and effect!

questions.prompt: You are reading instructions for making a sandwich. What kind of connection is being shown when the instructions say ‘First, spread the peanut butter on the bread.’?
choices: A) A comparison between two different sandwiches.
B) The sequence of steps to make the sandwich.
C) An explanation of why peanut butter is good.
questions.explanation: The words 'first' show us that this step comes *before* any other steps in the process – it’s a connection based on order.

questions.prompt: A paragraph describes a volcano erupting. The next paragraph talks about how animals fled to higher ground. What type of connection is most likely being shown?
questions.explanation: The eruption *caused* the animals to flee, making this a cause-and-effect relationship.

mt_M7XhBBzYof.title: Ecology Explorers: Who Eats Whom?
explanation: Let’s talk about how living things live together in nature! Everything in an ecosystem, like a forest or even your backyard, is connected through what we call feeding relationships. These relationships show us who eats whom and how energy moves around – it's super important for understanding how our planet works. Think about building blocks; each piece has a job, and when they fit together correctly, you get something amazing!

There are different roles animals play in this system. Producers like plants make their own food using sunlight, just like a tiny chef! Consumers then eat those plants or other consumers, getting energy from them. Predators hunt and eat other animals (prey), while herbivores only eat plants – imagine a cow munching on grass! These categories help us understand the flow of energy through an ecosystem.

Decomposers are nature’s cleanup crew; they break down dead plants and animals, returning nutrients back to the soil. This is important because those nutrients help producers grow. For example, when leaves fall from a tree, decomposers like fungi and bacteria work hard to turn them back into something that can be used by new plants – it's a continuous cycle of life!
keyIdeas: Producers make their own food.
Consumers eat other organisms for energy.
Decomposers recycle nutrients.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a producer?
questions.explanation: Producers, like grass, use sunlight to make their own food.

questions.prompt: A carnivore eats what kind of animal?
questions.explanation: Carnivores are meat-eaters; they hunt and consume other animals.

questions.prompt: What does a decomposer do in an ecosystem?
choices: B) Breaks down dead plants and animals
questions.explanation: Decomposers recycle nutrients by breaking down organic matter.

mt_oB-L8EVdIP.title: Food Chains: Who Eats Whom?
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs – everything starts with a basic block, right? In nature, food chains are like LEGO chains too! They show how living things get their energy by eating other living things. The most important part of any food chain is the producer; these are plants that make their own food using sunlight, like grass in your backyard or leaves on a tree.

Now let’s talk about who eats what. A predator is an animal that hunts and eats other animals – think of a lion hunting a zebra! Prey is the animal being hunted, and it's trying to stay safe from predators. These roles are super important because they keep populations balanced within an ecosystem.

Let’s put it all together with an example: A rabbit might eat grass (the producer), a fox might then eat the rabbit (the predator), and a hawk could eat the fox (another predator). This chain shows how energy moves from the sun to the plants, then to the animals that eat them. If we remove the rabbit, it would affect the fox and even the hawk!
keyIdeas: Producers make their own food.
Predators hunt and eat other animals.
Energy moves through a food chain.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a producer?
questions.explanation: Producers like grass use sunlight to make their own food.

questions.prompt: What does a predator do?
choices: B) Hunts and eats other animals
questions.explanation: Predators are hunters that eat other living things for energy.

questions.prompt: If all the rabbits in a food chain disappeared, what might happen to the fox?
choices: A) The fox would thrive and have more babies
B) The fox would become hungry and struggle to find food
C) The fox would eat more plants
questions.explanation: The fox relies on rabbits for food; without them, it wouldn't have a source of energy.

mt_SUOhjmRqv9.title: Literal vs Figurative Language
explanation: Distinguish literal from nonliteral (figurative) language in context and interpret common idioms and phrases

You know this idea when you can: Identify whether 'It's raining cats and dogs' is literal or nonliteral and explain what it means; Read a passage and circle three phrases used nonliterally, restating each in literal terms.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Identify whether 'It's raining cats and dogs' is literal or nonliteral and explain what it means
Read a passage and circle three phrases used nonliterally, restating each in literal terms
Explain the difference between 'She was on fire' (figurative — performing well) and 'The log was on fire' (literal)

questions.prompt: True or false: Identify whether 'It's raining cats and dogs' is literal or nonliteral and explain what it means
questions.explanation: Identify whether 'It's raining cats and dogs' is literal or nonliteral and explain what it means

questions.prompt: True or false: Read a passage and circle three phrases used nonliterally, restating each in literal terms
questions.explanation: Read a passage and circle three phrases used nonliterally, restating each in literal terms

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain the difference between 'She was on fire' (figurative — performing well) and 'The log was on fire' (literal)
questions.explanation: Explain the difference between 'She was on fire' (figurative — performing well) and 'The log was on fire' (literal)

mt_OltpfaX7l6.title: Detective Readers: Uncovering Author’s Purpose
explanation: Let's become detective readers! Sometimes, when we read something, we want to know *why* the person who wrote it was writing. Authors have a reason for sharing their ideas – this is called ‘purpose.’ Think about your favorite building blocks; maybe you built a tall tower because you wanted to see how high you could go, or perhaps you were just having fun creating!  Understanding an author’s purpose helps us understand what they want us to think about the topic.

There are three main reasons authors write: to inform, to persuade, and to entertain. ‘Informing’ means giving us facts and details – like a science textbook explaining how plants grow. ‘Persuading’ means trying to convince us of something, like an advertisement telling us why we should buy a new toy.  ‘Entertaining’ is when the author wants to make us laugh or feel excited, like a funny story.

Let's look at word choices! The words an author uses can show us how they feel about what they are writing. If an author keeps using words like ‘amazing,’ ‘fantastic,’ and ‘wonderful,’ we know they probably want to entertain us.  If they use words like ‘important’ and ‘serious,’ it might be because they're trying to inform us, and if they use strong verbs like 'attack' or 'defend', then they are likely trying to persuade.
keyIdeas: Authors have a purpose for writing.
There are three main author purposes: inform, persuade, and entertain.
Word choices can reveal an author’s attitude.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes the purpose of a news report?
choices: B. To convince us to buy something
questions.explanation: News reports are meant to inform us about current events.

questions.prompt: If an author uses words like ‘best,’ ‘greatest,’ and ‘most amazing’ repeatedly, what is their most likely purpose?
questions.explanation: Using enthusiastic language often indicates the author wants to entertain.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a book about protecting animals. If the author uses words like ‘threatened,’ ‘endangered,’ and ‘destruction,’ what is their purpose?
questions.explanation: This suggests the author is trying to persuade us to care about animal conservation.

mt_3WMADSy0mA.title: Forces All Around Us!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how your toys don't just move around randomly? That’s because of forces – they are pushes and pulls that make things start moving, stop moving, or change direction. Forces are all around us, from the wind blowing leaves to a car speeding down the road. Understanding forces helps us understand why things do what they do!

When forces are balanced, it means two equal forces are acting on an object at the same time. Think about a book sitting perfectly still on your desk – gravity is pulling it down, but the table is pushing up with an equal amount of force. Because these forces are equal and opposite, the book doesn't move; that’s balanced! Unbalanced forces happen when one force is stronger than another.

If you push a toy car across the floor, you're applying an unbalanced force because you're pushing it in one direction while friction (a force from the floor) is trying to slow it down. The car will move until either your push stops or the friction becomes strong enough to stop it. Observing these changes helps us see how forces can cause motion!
keyIdeas: Balanced forces have equal and opposite forces.
Unbalanced forces cause a change in motion.
Forces are pushes and pulls that affect objects.

questions.prompt: A toy car rolling across the floor is an example of:
questions.explanation: When a force (the push from you) and another force (friction) are equal, the car moves in a straight line – that’s balanced!

questions.prompt: What happens when you push a heavy box across the floor?
choices: A: It stays still because of balanced forces.
B: It slides quickly because of unbalanced forces.
questions.explanation: The force you apply is stronger than the friction, causing the box to move – that’s an unbalanced force.

questions.prompt: Imagine a seesaw with someone on each side. What must happen for it to stay balanced?
questions.explanation: Both people need to push down with equal amounts of force to keep the seesaw level; this demonstrates balanced forces.

mt_AlLYwCm92a.title: Rolling Ramp Races: Predicting Motion!
explanation: Today, we're going to explore how things move and learn to predict what will happen next! When you push a toy car down a ramp, it rolls pretty far, right? That’s because of something called force – a push or pull that makes things move. Scientists use observations like this to understand how forces affect motion, and we can even make predictions based on what we see.

Let's try an experiment! We’ll roll balls down ramps made of different heights. We need to measure how far the ball rolls each time – we could use a ruler or even just count steps.  We’ll also pay attention to whether the ramp is steeper or flatter, because that changes how quickly the ball rolls down.  Collecting this information will help us find a pattern.

Once we've rolled the balls many times and recorded our measurements, we can look for a pattern. Maybe we notice that the steeper the ramp, the further the ball rolls! That’s evidence – it’s proof based on what we observed. We can then use this pattern to make a prediction: If we build an even steeper ramp, will the ball roll even farther?
keyIdeas: Motion is caused by forces.
Patterns in motion can be predicted.
Evidence helps us test our predictions.

questions.prompt: If a ball rolls down a steeper ramp, what will likely happen?
questions.explanation: Steeper ramps create more force, causing the ball to accelerate and roll faster.

questions.prompt: What is evidence in science?
questions.explanation: Evidence comes from what we see and measure when we investigate something – it's the proof that supports our ideas.

questions.prompt: You rolled a ball down three ramps: flat, medium, and steep. The ball rolled furthest on the ____ ramp.
questions.explanation: This question is open-ended to encourage students to apply their understanding based on their experiment.

mt_bn5ggh84qD.title: Plural vs. Possessive: Who Owns What?
explanation: Let's talk about words that show ownership! Sometimes we use the letter 's' at the end of a word to show something belongs to someone or something, like when we say ‘dog’s bone’. This is called a possessive noun – it means ‘belonging to.’  Think about your favorite toy; you might say ‘my teddy bear,’ showing that the bear belongs to *you*. The 's' tells us whose thing it is!

Now, let’s look at plurals. A plural word shows more than one of something. For example, we have ‘dogs’, because there are many dogs!  The '-s' at the end just means there are multiple things. It’s important to remember that a plural doesn't show ownership; it simply tells us how many there are – like ‘three cats’ or ‘five pencils’.

Sometimes, we use both! We can have a plural *and* a possessive at the same time.  For instance, 'the girls' coats' means that many girls own coats. However, 'the cat's bowl' shows that one specific cat owns its own bowl. It’s all about figuring out who or what is being described!
keyIdeas: Plural nouns show multiple items.
Possessive nouns show ownership.
The suffix '-s' can indicate both plural and possession.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a possessive noun correctly?
choices: A) The dog’s toy is red.
B) The dogs toys are red.
C) The dog's toys are red.
questions.explanation: The correct answer shows that the toy belongs to the dog.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a plural noun correctly?
choices: A) The children’s books are on the shelf.
B) The child is reading a book.
C) The children's books are on the shelf.
questions.explanation: This sentence indicates there are multiple books.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct way to write this sentence: ‘The students’ pencils.’ Is it plural or possessive?
choices: B) Possessive – showing pencils belonging to the students.
questions.explanation: The 's' shows that the pencils belong to the students.

mt_Ruk2-lyGPZ.title: The Digestive System
explanation: Describe the simple functions of the basic parts of the human digestive system: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine

You know this idea when you can: Name the main organs in order: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine; Describe the function of each (mouth chews, stomach breaks down, small intestine absorbs nutrients).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name the main organs in order: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine
Describe the function of each (mouth chews, stomach breaks down, small intestine absorbs nutrients)
Draw or label a simple diagram of the digestive system

questions.explanation: Name the main organs in order: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the function of each (mouth chews, stomach breaks down, small intestine absorbs nutrients)
questions.explanation: Describe the function of each (mouth chews, stomach breaks down, small intestine absorbs nutrients)

mt_EbiGRVK8uR.title: Building Better Descriptions!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how sometimes a simple sentence doesn’t quite paint a picture in your mind? It's like looking at a photo with just one color – it’s missing something! Expanding noun phrases is all about adding extra details to make our writing and speaking much more interesting and descriptive. We do this by adding words that describe the nouns, like adjectives (describing words) or even whole other sentences!

Let's say we have the simple sentence: ‘The teacher.’ That’s a perfectly fine sentence, but it doesn’t tell us anything about the teacher! We can make it much better by adding details. For example, we could change it to ‘the strict maths teacher with curly hair.’ See how that adds so much more information? This is what expanding noun phrases does – it builds up a richer picture.

Think about your favorite toy. Maybe you have a 'bright red race car.' The words 'bright' and 'red' are adjectives describing the noun ‘race car’. Or perhaps you’re building a sandcastle on the beach - you might describe it as ‘the magnificent, towering sandcastle with glistening shells.’ Adding these extra details makes our writing much more vivid and engaging for anyone reading or listening!
keyIdeas: Noun phrases can be expanded.
Adding adjectives improves descriptions.
Prepositional phrases add location information.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the most expanded noun phrase?
choices: The fluffy brown puppy chased the ball.
A small, happy dog ran across the park.
questions.answer: The fluffy brown puppy chased the ball.
questions.explanation: This sentence uses multiple adjectives and a prepositional phrase to create a more detailed description.

questions.prompt: What does ‘the tall man with the grey coat’ mean?
choices: It describes a man using details like height and clothing.
questions.answer: It describes a man using details like height and clothing.
questions.explanation: The words 'tall' and 'grey coat' are adjectives that add information about the noun ‘man.’

questions.prompt: In the sentence, ‘the tiny grey kitten under the table,’ what is the head noun?
questions.explanation: The head noun is the main word being described – in this case, it’s 'kitten.' The other words are modifiers that give us more information about the kitten.

mt_y8sicbhMci.title: Spellings from Greek, French and Latin
explanation: Spell words with etymological letter patterns from Greek (ch = /k/), French (ch = /ʃ/, -gue, -que), and Latin (sc = /s/) origins

You know this idea when you can: Spell words with Greek ch such as 'scheme', 'chorus', 'chemist', 'echo' and explain the /k/ sound pattern; Spell words with French ch such as 'chef', 'chalet', 'machine' and -gue/-que endings like 'league', 'unique'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Spell words with Greek ch such as 'scheme', 'chorus', 'chemist', 'echo' and explain the /k/ sound pattern
Spell words with French ch such as 'chef', 'chalet', 'machine' and -gue/-que endings like 'league', 'unique'
Spell words with Latin sc such as 'science', 'scene', 'discipline' and identify the silent letter pattern

mt_ujwtRoYJ34.title: Exploring Stories: Chapters, Scenes, and Stanzas!
explanation: Let's talk about stories! When you read a book like 'Harry Potter,' it’s broken down into different parts to help tell the whole story. These parts have special names – chapters, scenes, and stanzas – and they all work together to make the story exciting. Think of a chapter as one big section of the book, maybe about a specific adventure Harry has with his friends.

A scene is like a smaller part *within* a chapter. It’s often focused on a particular event or conversation. For example, in a scene where Harry and Ron are trying to get past Snape in potions class, that whole scene would be a ‘scene’ within the larger chapter about their time at Hogwarts. Stanzas are most commonly used when reading poetry; they're groups of lines that make up a verse.

Understanding these parts helps us see how the story is building! If a later chapter talks about something Harry learned in an earlier chapter, that shows how the plot keeps moving forward. By using words like ‘chapter,’ ‘scene,’ and ‘stanza,’ we can talk about stories with more detail and understand exactly how each part contributes to the overall adventure.
keyIdeas: Chapters are large sections of a book.
Scenes focus on specific events within chapters.
Stanzas form verses in poetry.

questions.prompt: In 'Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone,' what is a chapter?
choices: A small part of a scene
B A large section of the book
C A verse in a poem
D A group of lines in a stanza
questions.explanation: Chapters are the biggest parts of a story, like a big chunk of the adventure.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're reading a poem. What is a stanza?
choices: B A single line of text
C A group of lines that make up a verse
D An event in a chapter
questions.explanation: Stanzas are groups of lines together, often forming a verse.

questions.prompt: Why do authors use chapters and scenes?
questions.explanation: Authors use these parts to organize their stories and help readers follow the plot more easily. They create a flow that makes it easier for us to understand what's happening.

mt_eMtV6tBSJm.title: Area Adventures: Building Shapes!
explanation: Today we're going on an adventure with shapes, and we’ll be learning about area! Area is the amount of space inside a shape – think of it like how much paint you need to cover a wall. We can find the area of bigger shapes by breaking them down into smaller, simpler shapes that we *do* know how to measure, like squares and rectangles. Let's start with an example: imagine your LEGO set has lots of different blocks, and you want to build a cool castle – you’d need to figure out how much space all those blocks take up!

Now, let’s look at something we might see in our house - a rug! A rug can be made up of several rectangles. If you have an L-shaped rug, it's like a puzzle where you need to split it into smaller pieces. We can do this by drawing lines to divide the shape into two separate rectangles; each rectangle will have its own area that we can calculate. Remember, area is always measured in square units – like square centimeters or square meters!

Let’s say your bedroom floor is shaped like an ‘L’. You could split it up into two rectangles: one 3 meters long and 5 meters wide, and the other 2 meters long and 4 meters wide. We find the area of each rectangle (length times width) and then add those areas together to get the total area of the L-shaped room! This is how we can solve bigger problems with shapes.
keyIdeas: Area is the space inside a shape.
You can find the area of complex shapes by breaking them into rectangles.
Add up the areas of the individual rectangles to get the total area.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have a floor plan shaped like a T. You split it into two rectangles. What do you need to do first?
choices: A. Measure the length and width of each rectangle
B. Draw lines to divide the shape into two rectangles
C. Calculate the area of the entire T-shape
questions.explanation: To find the area of a complex shape like a ‘T’, we first need to split it up into smaller, manageable shapes – in this case, two rectangles.

questions.prompt: A room is L-shaped (3m×5m plus 2m×4m) — what is the total area?
questions.explanation: Remember to calculate the area of each rectangle separately and then add them together: (3m * 5m) + (2m * 4m) = 15 sq m + 8 sq m = 23 sq m.

questions.prompt: You are building a model town. You need to find the area of a building that is made up of two rectangles. What information do you need?
questions.explanation: You need the length and width of each rectangle to calculate their areas.

mt_Zdv-b-iW5K.title: Multiplication & Division Adventures!
explanation: Today, we're going on an adventure with numbers – specifically multiplication and division! These two operations are like secret codes that help us solve problems about equal groups and sharing. Think of it like this: if you have three plates and put six cookies on each plate, you’ve used multiplication to figure out how many cookies you have in total. Multiplication is just a faster way to add the same number over and over again.

Let's look at an example – imagine you are building with LEGO bricks. You want to make 5 towers, and each tower has 4 blocks. To find out how many blocks you need altogether, you would multiply 5 x 4 = 20! That means you’d use 20 LEGO bricks to build all your towers. Division is the opposite – it's like splitting something into equal groups; if you have 20 LEGO bricks and want to make 4 towers, you divide them by 4.

Now, let’s try a measurement problem! Suppose you have a ribbon that is 28 centimeters long, and you want to cut it into pieces that are each 4 centimeters. To figure out how many pieces you can get, you would do division: 28 / 4 = 7. This means you can make 7 pieces of ribbon that are 4 cm long! These problems help us understand how things relate to each other.
keyIdeas: Multiplication is repeated addition.
Division is splitting into equal groups.
Both operations can solve real-world problems.

questions.prompt: Sarah has 6 bags of candy. Each bag contains 5 pieces of candy. How many pieces of candy does Sarah have in total?
questions.explanation: This is a multiplication problem! You need to multiply the number of bags (6) by the number of candies per bag (5).  6 x 5 = 30. So, we made an error and chose B.

questions.prompt: A baker makes 12 cookies in each batch. If he makes 4 batches, how many cookies does he have?
questions.explanation: This is a multiplication problem! We need to multiply the number of cookies per batch (12) by the number of batches (4). 12 x 4 = 48. So, we made an error and chose C.

questions.prompt: You have 24 pencils and want to put them into boxes with 6 pencils in each box. How many boxes do you need?
questions.explanation: This is a division problem! We need to divide the total number of pencils (24) by the number of pencils per box (6). 24 / 6 = 4. So, we made an error and chose A.

mt_9XVFje6Tyr.title: 1000 More or Less!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn how to add or subtract 1,000 from a number. Think of it like this: adding 1,000 is like moving the whole group of thousands over – it's like having an extra thousand toys! Subtracting 1,000 is like taking away that same group of thousands; you’re left with less. We can do this easily by just looking at the biggest number in the number and changing it.

Let’s look at an example: Suppose we have the number 4,562. If we want to find out what number is 1,000 *more* than 4,562, we simply add 1,000 to it. That gives us 5,562!  If we wanted to know what number is 1,000 *less* than 4,562, we subtract 1,000 from it, and we get 3,562. Notice how only the thousands place changed – the hundreds, tens, and ones stayed the same.

This works because numbers are made up of different ‘place’ values: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. When we add or subtract 1,000, we're only changing the value in the thousands place.  Imagine you have a big box of LEGOs – if you added 1,000 new bricks to it, you’d have more than 4,562 bricks. If you took away 1,000 bricks, you would have less.
keyIdeas: Adding or subtracting 1,000 changes only the thousands digit.
Place value is important for understanding how numbers change.
We can quickly find ‘1,000 more’ or ‘1,000 less’ by focusing on the thousands place.

questions.prompt: What number is 1,000 more than 2,895?
What number is 1,000 less than 6,742?
questions.explanation: To find 1,000 less than 6,742, subtract 1,000 from it. 6,742 - 1,000 = 5,742.

questions.prompt: If you have the number 9,123, and you add 1,000 to it, what will the new number be?
questions.explanation: The new number will be 10,123.  You added 1,000 to 9,123.

mt_WfrE_4r-kY.title: Time Travelers: 12-Hour and 24-Hour Clocks
explanation: Let’s talk about telling time, but we're going to learn two different ways! Most of us are used to seeing clocks that show the hours with numbers like 1, 2, and 12. These are called 12-hour clocks, and they use ‘am’ (ante meridiem) for morning and ‘pm’ (post meridiem) for afternoon and evening. Think about your favorite toy – maybe a race car or a stuffed animal; the clock on its base probably uses this 12-hour system.

Now, some clocks, especially those used in aviation and around the world, use a different way to track time called 24-hour time. This system counts from midnight (00:00) all the way until midnight again.  It’s like counting hours on a really long road – you just keep going! When it's 12:00 pm, that's the same as noon.

To convert between these two systems, we need to understand how many hours have passed since midnight. For example, if it’s 3:45 pm, we can add 15 hours to get 15:45 in 24-hour time.  It's like figuring out how much longer until dinner – you’re calculating the remaining hours!
keyIdeas: 12-hour and 24-hour clocks represent different ways of measuring time.
‘am’ and ‘pm’ indicate morning and afternoon/evening.
Converting between clock systems involves adding or subtracting multiples of 12.

questions.prompt: What is 3:45 pm in 24-hour time?
questions.explanation: To convert, you add 15 hours to 3:45 pm. This gives you 15:45.

questions.prompt: What is the 12-hour equivalent of 19:30?
questions.explanation: 19:30 is 7 hours and 30 minutes after midnight, which corresponds to 7:30 pm.

questions.prompt: A train leaves at 14:35. What time is this in 12-hour format?
questions.explanation: 14:35 is 2 o'clock and 35 minutes past 2pm.

mt_uVaS12lN1i.title: Food Web Adventures!
explanation: Imagine you're building a LEGO castle – each piece needs to connect to others to make it strong and complete, right? Food webs are similar! They show how living things in an ecosystem eat each other and share energy. A food web isn’t just one line (like a food chain), but many connected lines showing all the different ways animals get their food. Let's look at a diagram of a pond – it might have plants, insects, frogs, and even fish!

Within this pond food web, we can identify three main types of eaters: producers, herbivores, and carnivores. Producers like algae or water lilies make their own food using sunlight - they’re the base of the whole system! Herbivores, such as tadpoles eating plants, eat only producers. Carnivores, like frogs eating insects, eat other animals – they are at a higher level in the food web.

Now, let's trace some connections. If we follow an arrow from algae to a frog, it shows that the frog eats the algae. We can also find two different food chains: one going from grass to a rabbit to a fox, and another going from insects to a frog to a snake. Removing a key part like the rabbits would cause ripples throughout the whole web – this is why ecosystems are so delicate!
keyIdeas: Producers make their own food.
Animals eat other animals or plants.
Removing one animal can affect many others.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a producer in a food web?
questions.explanation: Producers like grass use sunlight to make their own food.

questions.prompt: If a snake eats a frog, what type of relationship does this show?
questions.explanation: This shows a carnivore (snake) eating another animal (frog).

questions.prompt: What might happen to the number of frogs if all the insects disappeared?
choices: A. The frog population would increase
B. The frog population would decrease
C. The frog population wouldn't change
questions.explanation: Frogs eat insects, so without insects, there would be less food for them.

mt_Uq5vYqboCR.title: Moving Shapes: Translations!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about something called ‘translations’. Imagine you have a toy car, like a race car or even a building block! When you move the car from one spot to another without turning it around or changing its shape, that's a translation. Translations are all about moving shapes and points on a grid – think of sliding them across the paper.

Let’s say we have a point at (2, 3) on a piece of graph paper. A ‘translation’ means we move it to a new spot. If we tell you to move that point three units to the right, that means we add three to the first number (the x-coordinate). So, the new position would be (5, 3), because we moved it three spaces to the right of its original location.

Translations are really useful for describing how things change their position. We can use numbers to show exactly how far and in what direction something moves. This is important when we’re working with shapes on a grid, like drawing or playing games where you have to move pieces around – it's all about knowing the translation!
keyIdeas: Translations are movements without rotation.
We use numbers to describe how far and in what direction we move.
Translating a point changes only its coordinates.

questions.prompt: If a shape is at (1, 2) and you translate it 5 units to the right, where will it be?
questions.explanation: Adding 5 to the x-coordinate (the first number) gives you the new coordinates.

questions.prompt: A point moves from (4, 1) to (4, 6). How many units did it move up?
questions.explanation: The y-coordinate (the second number) didn’t change, so the movement was only up.

questions.prompt: You translate a shape 2 units left and 1 unit up from its original position of (7, 5). What are the new coordinates?
questions.explanation: To find the new coordinates, subtract 2 from the x-coordinate and add 1 to the y-coordinate.

mt_xZvDCYA5Ae.title: Unlocking Multiplication and Division Secrets!
explanation: Today we’re going to explore multiplication and division, which are like secret codes for numbers! They help us figure out how many things there are when we group them together or how many groups we can make. Think about your LEGOs – if you have 5 boxes of LEGOs with 8 LEGOs in each box, multiplication helps us find the total number of LEGOs. We can write this as 5 x 8 = 40, meaning there are 40 LEGOs altogether!

Division is like sharing equally. It’s the opposite of multiplication! Let's say you have 24 cookies and want to share them with 6 friends – division helps us figure out how many cookies each friend gets. We write this as 24 ÷ 6 = 4, showing that each friend receives 4 cookies.  Both multiplication and division are linked; they’re like two sides of the same number-solving coin.

To find a missing number in a multiplication or division problem, you can use what you already know! If we see ‘8 x ? = 48’, we could think about what number multiplied by 8 makes 48. We know that 6 times 8 is 48, so the missing number is 6.  Similarly, if we have '? ÷ 5 = 9', we can ask ourselves 'What number divided by 5 gives us 9?' The answer is 45.
keyIdeas: Multiplication and division are related operations.
You can use known facts to solve missing numbers.
Understanding the problem helps you choose the right strategy.

questions.prompt: What number does ? x 7 = 28?
questions.explanation: We need to find a number that, when multiplied by 7, equals 28.  Let’s try 5; 7 x 5 = 35 (too high!), so 5 is incorrect. We can also think of how many times 7 goes into 28 - it's four times.

questions.prompt: If 10 ÷ ? = 2, what number is missing?
questions.explanation: We need to find the number that, when divided by 2, equals 10.  The answer is 5 because 10 ÷ 5 = 2.

questions.prompt: A toy store has 36 action figures and wants to put them into boxes with 4 action figures in each box. How many boxes will they need?
questions.explanation: This is a multiplication problem! We can write it as 36 ÷ 4 = 9.  Nine boxes are needed to hold all the action figures.

mt_idbKDrf9qZ.title: Fractions as Parts of Shapes
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about fractions and how they can describe parts of shapes! A fraction is just a way to show a part of something, like a piece of pizza or a slice of cake. We write it with two numbers – the top number (the numerator) tells us how many pieces we have, and the bottom number (the denominator) tells us how many total pieces make up the whole.

Let’s look at some examples. Imagine you have a LEGO brick shaped like a rectangle. You can divide it into six equal parts – that means each part is one-sixth of the whole brick! We write this as 1/6.  Or, think about a chocolate bar; if you break it into four equal pieces, each piece represents one-fourth (1/4) of the entire bar.

Shapes are really helpful for understanding fractions because we can easily divide them into equal parts. You might see a square divided into four parts – that means each part is one-quarter (1/4) of the whole square!  It’s important that all the pieces are exactly the same size; otherwise, it wouldn't be a fraction.
keyIdeas: Fractions represent parts of a whole.
The denominator tells us the total number of equal parts.
Unit fractions show one part of the whole (e.g., 1/2, 1/3).

questions.prompt: If you cut a pizza into 8 slices and eat 3 slices, what fraction of the pizza did you eat?
questions.explanation: The denominator (8) is the total number of slices. The numerator (3) is the number of slices you ate.

questions.prompt: A square is divided into 4 equal parts. What fraction represents one of those parts?
questions.explanation: The denominator (4) shows that the whole square is split into four equal pieces.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has been divided into three equal parts? (Imagine a picture of a shape with three equal sections)
questions.explanation: This question requires visual assessment – encourage children to look for shapes where all three sections are the same size.

mt_MewIRdzpzz.title: Roman numerals to 100
explanation: Read Roman numerals to 100 (I to C) and understand that the numeral system changed over time to include zero and place value
You know this idea when you can: Read and write Roman numerals I, V, X, L, C and combinations up to 100; Convert between Roman numerals and Hindu-Arabic numerals (e.g. XLIV = 44).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Read and write Roman numerals I, V, X, L, C and combinations up to 100
Convert between Roman numerals and Hindu-Arabic numerals (e.g. XLIV = 44)
Explain that Roman numerals have no zero and no place-value system

questions.prompt: True or false: Read and write Roman numerals I, V, X, L, C and combinations up to 100
questions.explanation: Read and write Roman numerals I, V, X, L, C and combinations up to 100

questions.prompt: True or false: Convert between Roman numerals and Hindu-Arabic numerals (e.g. XLIV = 44)
questions.explanation: Convert between Roman numerals and Hindu-Arabic numerals (e.g. XLIV = 44)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that Roman numerals have no zero and no place-value system
questions.explanation: Explain that Roman numerals have no zero and no place-value system

mt_DbXWivdJFB.title: Amazing Teeth!
explanation: Our bodies are amazing, and so are our teeth! We have different kinds of teeth that help us eat all sorts of yummy foods. Just like a superhero has special tools for their job, each type of tooth has a specific role to play in getting the food we need to grow strong and healthy. Let’s learn about the three main types: incisors, canines, and molars – they're really important for our eating adventures!

Incisors are like tiny little scissors at the very front of your mouth. They’re flat and sharp, and their job is to cut food into smaller pieces, like when you bite into an apple. Canines, which are a bit bigger and pointier, are designed to tear food – think about ripping open a piece of meat or even tearing through a tough carrot! Finally, molars are the big, flat teeth in the back of your mouth; these teeth grind food with their bumpy surfaces.

Different animals eat different things, and that’s reflected in their teeth. Herbivores like cows and deer have wide, flat molars to help them grind up plants all day long. Carnivores, such as lions and tigers, have sharp canines for tearing meat – they're perfect for ripping chunks off of prey!  Even though we eat a mix of foods, our teeth are built to do the job best suited for what we’re eating.
keyIdeas: There are three main types of human teeth: incisors, canines, and molars.
Incisors cut food, canines tear food, and molars grind food.
Different tooth shapes help animals adapt to their diets.

questions.prompt: Which type of teeth are used for cutting food?
questions.explanation: Incisors have a flat, sharp edge perfect for slicing and chopping.

questions.prompt: What is the main job of molars?
questions.explanation: Molars are large, flat teeth that crush and grind food into smaller pieces.

questions.prompt: A lion uses its strong canines for what purpose?
questions.explanation: Canines are sharp and pointed, ideal for ripping and tearing tough food like meat.

mt_NNNbPccwB4.title: Will the Bulb Light Up?
explanation: Let’s talk about electricity and how it makes things work! When you turn on a lamp, you're using energy to make the light bulb glow. Electricity needs a path – like a road – to travel through in order to do this. This path is called a circuit, and it’s made up of wires connected to a battery.

Imagine your favorite toy car; it needs batteries to move, right? A lamp works in a similar way! But for the electricity to flow from the battery, all the way around and back to the battery, there must be a complete loop. If there’s even one tiny break or gap in that loop, like a loose wire, the electricity can't travel, and the bulb won’t light up.

Think about building with LEGOs – you need all the pieces connected to build something strong. A circuit is just like that; it needs every part connected properly for the energy to flow. If any connection is missing or loose, it’s like a broken piece in your LEGO creation and nothing will work!
keyIdeas: Electricity needs a complete path (circuit) to flow.
A break in the circuit stops electricity from flowing.
Loose wires or missing connections can prevent a bulb from lighting.

questions.prompt: If a lamp has a broken wire, will it light up?
choices: A. Yes, if the battery is strong.
B. No, because electricity needs a complete path.
C. Maybe, depending on the color of the bulb.
questions.explanation: Electricity can’t flow through a broken circuit.

questions.prompt: What does it mean for a circuit to be ‘complete’?
choices: A. It has too many wires.
B. It has a break in the path.
C. It allows electricity to flow from one part to another.
questions.explanation: A complete circuit means electricity can travel around and around continuously.

questions.prompt: You see a lamp that won’t light up. What's one thing you could check?
questions.explanation: Check for loose wires or missing connections in the circuit.

mt_Hah24nbToi.title: How switches work
explanation: Recognise that a switch opens and closes a circuit, controlling whether a lamp lights or a buzzer sounds

You know this idea when you can: Explain that a closed switch completes the circuit (current flows) and an open switch breaks it (current stops); Demonstrate adding a switch to a circuit and using it to control a bulb or buzzer.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Explain that a closed switch completes the circuit (current flows) and an open switch breaks it (current stops)
Demonstrate adding a switch to a circuit and using it to control a bulb or buzzer
Relate the switch concept to everyday switches in the home

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that a closed switch completes the circuit (current flows) and an open switch breaks it (current stops)
questions.explanation: Explain that a closed switch completes the circuit (current flows) and an open switch breaks it (current stops)

questions.prompt: True or false: Demonstrate adding a switch to a circuit and using it to control a bulb or buzzer
questions.explanation: Demonstrate adding a switch to a circuit and using it to control a bulb or buzzer

questions.prompt: True or false: Relate the switch concept to everyday switches in the home
questions.explanation: Relate the switch concept to everyday switches in the home

mt_hRZyKvz1KN.title: Exploring Lines of Symmetry
explanation: Today, we're going to explore something really cool about shapes – lines of symmetry! A line of symmetry is like a mirror that divides a shape exactly in half, so both sides are identical. Think about your LEGO bricks; sometimes you build a square and if you draw a line right down the middle, it perfectly splits the square into two matching halves. This line is its line of symmetry – it's where you could fold it to make everything look the same on both sides.

Let’s look at some shapes like rectangles, squares, and triangles. A rectangle has four lines of symmetry: one down the long side and one down the short side. A square is even cooler because it has *four* lines of symmetry – you can draw them in every direction!  Even an equilateral triangle, which looks pointy, has just one line of symmetry that runs right through its top point.

Now, imagine a star shape or a butterfly. These shapes often have more than one line of symmetry because you can fold them along different lines and still get matching halves. A regular hexagon, like the ones you might see on a honeycomb, has six lines of symmetry – that’s a lot!  Finding these lines helps us understand how symmetrical a shape is.
keyIdeas: A line of symmetry divides a shape into two identical parts.
Some shapes have one line of symmetry, while others can have multiple.
We can use lines of symmetry to draw and fold shapes perfectly in half.

questions.prompt: Which shape has exactly one line of symmetry?
questions.explanation: A triangle can be folded down the middle and both sides will match.

questions.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle have?
questions.explanation: You can draw two lines through the center of a rectangle to make it symmetrical.

questions.prompt: Can a star shape have more than one line of symmetry?
questions.explanation: Yes, a star shape can have multiple lines of symmetry because you can fold it along different lines.

mt_hR2Y7NhMSY.title: Lines of Symmetry - Mirror, Mirror!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about something called lines of symmetry! Imagine you have a toy car – many cars are perfectly symmetrical, right? That means if you draw a line down the middle, both sides look exactly the same. A line of symmetry is like an invisible line that divides a shape into two halves that are mirror images of each other; it’s like looking in a mirror!

Let's try making our own symmetrical shapes! We can use paper and pencil to draw shapes and then add lines of symmetry. Think about a pizza – when you cut it perfectly in half, the two pieces are identical. This is because the circle has an infinite number of lines of symmetry that go through its center; we’ll focus on one for now.

To make a symmetrical shape, you need to draw a line that goes right down the middle. If you fold your paper along this line, the two halves will match up perfectly! This is how we know we've drawn a shape with a line of symmetry – it can be folded in half and the sides will be identical.
keyIdeas: A line of symmetry divides a shape into equal parts.
Symmetrical shapes have mirror images when folded along their lines of symmetry.
You can draw a line to create a line of symmetry in a shape.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have a square. Which line could be its line of symmetry?
choices: A: A line going diagonally across the square
B: A line going straight down the middle
C: A line going straight across the middle
questions.explanation: A line running vertically through the center would create two identical halves.

questions.prompt: You draw a heart shape and want to add a line of symmetry. What should you do?
choices: A: Draw a wiggly line around the heart
B: Draw a straight line down the middle of the heart
C: Color in half of the heart
questions.explanation: Drawing a straight line down the center will create two identical halves, making it symmetrical.

questions.prompt: If you fold a shape in half and the two sides match perfectly, what does this tell you?
choices: A: The shape has no lines of symmetry
B: The shape has at least one line of symmetry
C: You should draw a different shape
questions.explanation: Matching halves indicate that a line of symmetry exists.

mt_ydtcIBwHB9.title: Sound Travels!
explanation: Have you ever wondered how you hear someone talking, even if they’re not right next to you? It all starts with sound – it's actually tiny vibrations! These vibrations are like little waves that travel through something. Just like ripples in a pond when you throw a stone in, sound travels through materials, and we can hear it because our ears pick up those vibrations.

Now, here’s the important part: sound needs something to travel *through*. It can't just float around in empty space – that’s called a vacuum. Think about your favorite toy car; when you push it across the floor, the floor is vibrating and sending those vibrations to your ears! Similarly, sound travels through air, water, or even solid things like wood or metal.

Let's look at an example: imagine a string telephone. When you speak into one cup, your voice makes the string vibrate. These vibrations travel along the string until they reach the other cup, where someone else can hear you! This shows us that sound travels through materials – it’s like a chain reaction of tiny shaking movements.
keyIdeas: Sound is made up of vibrations.
Vibrations need a material (medium) to travel.
Different materials affect how sound travels.

questions.prompt: What does sound travel through?
questions.explanation: Sound needs a material, like air or water, to travel. It can’t travel in a vacuum.

questions.prompt: Why can you hear someone talking through a wall?
choices: A. The wall is made of sound
B. Sound travels through the wall
questions.explanation: Sound vibrations travel through the wall to reach your ears.

questions.prompt: Which material would best allow sound to travel quickly?
questions.explanation: Generally, liquids like water transmit sound faster than air or solid materials.

mt_X1L9DoUwjF.title: Comparing and Ranking!
explanation: Today we're going to learn about comparing things! When we compare, we’re saying how one thing is similar or different from another. We use special words like 'more' and 'most' to make our sentences sound just right. Think about your favorite toy – maybe you have a race car and a stuffed animal; we can compare them to see which is better!

There are two main ways to compare: the comparative and the superlative. The comparative tells us how something is *different* from something else, like saying ‘This book is bigger than that one.’ The superlative tells us something is the *most* or *best* of all things – for example, ‘This is the best toy ever!’ We do this by adding '-er' to some adjectives (like 'bigger') and using 'more' and 'most' with others.

Let’s look at some examples. If you want to say something is bigger, you add '-er' – like ‘tall’, becomes ‘taller’. To say it’s the biggest, you use ‘most’ - so ‘biggest’! Similarly, if you are describing how well someone does something, we can say they did it 'more carefully' or that they did it 'the most carefully'. Now let's practice!
keyIdeas: Comparatives describe differences between two things.
Superlatives describe the best or most of a group of things.
'More' and 'most' are used with some adjectives to make comparisons.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct? ‘This car is _____ (faster/more fast) than my bike.’
choices: A. This car is faster than my bike.
B. This car is more fast than my bike.
questions.explanation: The correct way to compare speed is to use ‘faster’.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a superlative correctly?
choices: A. She sings better than her sister.
B. She is the best singer in our school.
C. This cake tastes more sweet than the other one.
questions.explanation: ‘Best’ describes the top of a group, so ‘the best singer’ is correct.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses both a comparative and a superlative correctly:
choices: A. The dog ran faster than the cat.
B. The lion is the strongest animal in the world.
C. She painted more beautifully than anyone else.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines ‘more beautifully’ (comparative adverb) with ‘the most beautifully’ (superlative adjective).

mt__00ZSLnB7p.title: Sound Waves & Loudness!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how a tiny bell makes a very quiet sound, but when you shake it really hard, it gets much louder? That’s because sound is made by vibrations – tiny movements that travel through the air! These vibrations are like ripples in a pond; they spread out from where the sound started.  The more energy we put into making the vibration, the bigger the ripple becomes, and the louder the sound we hear.

Think about your favorite toy – maybe it’s a rattle or a musical instrument. When you shake these things, you're creating vibrations! The stronger you shake them, the faster the vibrations move, and that makes the sound much louder.  Scientists call this relationship between vibration strength and loudness ‘volume’. It’s all about how quickly those tiny movements are happening.

Volume is simply a way of describing how loud or quiet a sound is. It's directly related to the size of the vibrations. If the vibrations are small, like when you whisper, the sound will be quiet. But if the vibrations are big, like when you shout, the sound will be very loud!  So, volume isn’t just about how much noise there is; it’s about the power behind those moving waves.
keyIdeas: Vibrations create sound.
Stronger vibrations make louder sounds.
Volume describes how loud or quiet a sound is.

questions.prompt: If you push a drum harder, what will happen to the sound?
questions.explanation: Pushing the drum harder creates stronger vibrations, which make the sound louder.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows a small vibration?
questions.explanation: A gentle whisper produces smaller vibrations than a loud shout or a crashing cymbal.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re making waves in a bathtub. What does volume have to do with the size of your waves?
questions.explanation: Volume is related to the size of the vibrations; bigger vibrations create larger waves (louder sounds).

mt_ds2TOtP8I1.title: Reporting & Recounting
explanation: Report on a topic or recount an experience with organised facts and descriptive details, speaking clearly at an understandable pace

You know this idea when you can: Deliver a one-minute oral report on a chosen topic, including at least three organised facts; Recount a personal experience using descriptive details and a clear beginning, middle, and end.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Deliver a one-minute oral report on a chosen topic, including at least three organised facts
Recount a personal experience using descriptive details and a clear beginning, middle, and end
Self-assess a recorded presentation for pace, clarity, and inclusion of relevant details
