questions.explanation: Deliver a one-minute oral report on a chosen topic, including at least three organised facts

questions.prompt: True or false: Recount a personal experience using descriptive details and a clear beginning, middle, and end
questions.explanation: Recount a personal experience using descriptive details and a clear beginning, middle, and end

questions.prompt: True or false: Self-assess a recorded presentation for pace, clarity, and inclusion of relevant details
questions.explanation: Self-assess a recorded presentation for pace, clarity, and inclusion of relevant details

mt_LQt4vnKeB4.title: Earth & Space Adventures!
explanation: Let’s explore some amazing words about our Earth and the space around it! Our planet is always moving, and these movements explain a lot about what we see – like why the sun rises and sets. When Earth spins on its axis, that's called *rotation*, and it takes about 24 hours for one complete spin. This rotation is what causes day and night; as one part of Earth turns towards the Sun, it experiences daylight.

Now, imagine Earth traveling around the Sun – that’s *revolution*! It takes roughly 365 days for Earth to make a full revolution, which is why we have a year. Think about your favorite toy car circling a track; Earth's revolution keeps us in orbit around the sun and creates our seasons. The further away from the sun you are, the less direct sunlight you receive.

The Moon also moves around Earth, creating beautiful changes called *lunar phases*. These phases happen because we see different amounts of the lit-up part of the Moon as it orbits us. When the moon is getting bigger, we say it’s *waxing*; when it's shrinking, we say it’s *waning*.  The solstice and equinox are special times when Earth’s tilt causes dramatic changes in daylight hours.
keyIdeas: Earth rotates on its axis, causing day and night.
Earth revolves around the Sun, creating our seasons.
The Moon's phases are caused by how much of its lit side we see.

questions.prompt: What does ‘rotation’ mean when talking about Earth?
choices: A. The movement of Earth around the Sun
B. Earth spinning on its axis
questions.explanation: Rotation is the spinning motion of Earth on its axis, which causes day and night.

questions.prompt: What causes the different shapes of the Moon we see?
choices: B. The Moon’s orbit around Earth
questions.explanation: The lunar phases are caused by how much of the illuminated side of the Moon is visible as it orbits our planet.

questions.prompt: Which event causes the longest and shortest days of the year?
questions.explanation: The solstice (summer) and equinox (spring/fall) are times when Earth's tilt creates significant differences in daylight hours.

mt_99G6Msdzw-.title: Super Skill Sharing!
explanation: Have you ever built an amazing LEGO castle, and then used the same way of organizing blocks to build a really neat tower out of cardboard boxes? That’s what ‘transferring skills’ is all about – it means using something you've learned in one place or situation to help you do something new. Scientists have actually studied this a lot, and they’ve found that our brains are super clever at noticing patterns and ways of doing things. It’s like your brain is saying, ‘Hey, I saw how to build something strong before – let's use that here!’

Sometimes, we think learning one thing doesn't matter when we do something different, but it actually does! Researchers like Perkins and Salomon have created a way of thinking about this called a 'transfer taxonomy'. This basically means they’ve broken down how our brains can use what we learn in different ways. For example, if you learn to solve math problems with shapes, that skill could help you when you're drawing or building with blocks – it’s all about seeing the connections.

Let's say you learn how to follow instructions carefully while baking cookies with your mom (that’s a skill!). Then, at school, you need to read and understand a complicated science experiment. You can use the same focus and attention to detail you used when baking to help you understand the experiment! Transferring skills helps us become better learners because we're not starting from scratch every time.
keyIdeas: Skills can be transferred between different areas.
Your brain looks for patterns to help you learn new things.
Using one skill in a new way makes learning easier.

questions.prompt: You learned how to sort toys by color. Now you need to organize your school supplies by color. Which of these is the best reason this skill helps you?
choices: A. Sorting toys is much more fun than organizing supplies.
B. You’ve used a similar strategy (sorting) in both situations.
C. Toys are always easier to sort than supplies.
questions.explanation: Sorting involves grouping things based on shared characteristics – color! This skill directly applies.

questions.prompt: Imagine you learned how to write neatly in your handwriting class. Now, you need to write a detailed report for your science project. What does this show about transferring skills?
choices: A. Handwriting is only useful for writing letters.
B. Your neat handwriting can help you make your report look organized and professional.
C. You should always take handwriting classes before doing any writing.
questions.explanation: Neatness and organization are valuable in all types of writing.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes ‘transferring skills’?
choices: A. Only learning things that are exactly the same.
B. Using what you’ve learned in one place to help you in another.
C. Never needing to learn anything new again.
questions.explanation: Transferring skills is about using knowledge and strategies from one situation to solve problems or learn something new in a different context.

mt_2NfIKEYdbm.title: Agreement Time! Subjects and Pronouns Need to Match!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how sentences sound a little strange if the words don't quite fit together? That’s because of something called ‘agreement.’ In simple terms, it means that parts of your sentence – like the subject (who or what is doing the action) and the verb (the action itself), or pronouns (words that replace nouns) and their related words – need to match. Think about building with LEGOs; each piece needs to connect properly for the whole thing to stand strong, just like sentences!

Let’s look at an example: Imagine you're playing with your toy cars. If you say ‘The group of children were laughing,’ it sounds a little odd because ‘group’ is one thing – it’s singular! The verb ‘were’ means ‘plural’ (more than one). We need to change it to ‘was’ which agrees with the single subject, ‘group.’ This makes the sentence sound much better and more natural: ‘The group of children was laughing’!

Now let's talk about pronouns. Pronouns replace nouns – like saying 'he,' 'she,' or 'it' instead of repeating a name. If you say ‘Each student must bring his or her book,’ we need to make sure ‘his or her’ matches the person being talked about - the ‘student.’ The antecedent is the word that the pronoun refers back to, and in this case, it’s ‘each student’. So, the correct way to write it is ‘Each student must bring their book’ because 'student' is singular.
keyIdeas: Subjects and verbs need to agree in number (singular or plural).
Pronouns must match the gender of their antecedent.
Fixing agreement errors makes your writing clear and easy to understand.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct? a) The dogs is barking loudly. b) The dog is barking loudly. c) The dogs are barking loudly.
questions.explanation: The subject ‘dog’ is singular, so the verb ‘is’ is correct.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct pronoun: a) Each student should bring his or her pencil. b) Each students should bring their pencils. c) Every student should bring his pencils.
questions.explanation: ‘Each student’ is singular, so ‘his or her’ correctly replaces the singular antecedent.

questions.prompt: Find and fix the agreement error: 'The team of players were excited about the game.' What is the correct sentence?
choices: The team of players was excited about the game.
The teams of players were excited about the game.
The team of players is excited about the game.
questions.answer: The team of players was excited about the game.
questions.explanation: 'Team' is singular, so 'was' is the correct verb form.

mt_Pau0aqLNgp.title: Sound Fading with Distance
explanation: Have you ever been at a concert or outside playing with friends and noticed that the music or voices seem to fade away as they get further from where it started? That’s because sound travels in waves, just like ripples when you throw a stone into a pond. These waves spread out in all directions from the source – whether it's a singer, an instrument, or even your voice – and that spreading is what makes the sound quieter as it moves away.

Let’s do a simple experiment to see this happen! Imagine someone clapping their hands right next to you. You’ll hear a loud clap, right? Now, have them clap while you take a few steps back. Notice how the clap sounds quieter and quieter as you move further away – that's because the sound waves are spreading out over a larger area.

Think about it like this: when someone shouts, the vibrations from their voice travel through the air. As those vibrations travel further and further, they spread out like ripples in water. The more space they cover, the weaker each individual vibration becomes, which is why the sound fades away with distance.
keyIdeas: Sound waves spread outwards.
The farther a sound travels, the quieter it gets.
Vibrations weaken as they travel further from their source.

questions.prompt: If you are standing close to a speaker playing music, the sound will be:
questions.explanation: The sound waves are concentrated when you're close to the source.

questions.prompt: As someone walks away from you while talking, their voice gets quieter because:
choices: B. The sound waves spread out over a larger area
C. The air is getting thicker
questions.explanation: Sound waves lose energy as they travel further.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are listening to someone talking from across the playground. What happens to the sound?
questions.explanation: The sound gets quieter because the vibrations spread out over a larger distance.

mt_KTSoXcO7OL.title: Sound Waves and How They Change!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how different things make different sounds? Sounds like music, voices, or even the clatter of toys all have something in common: they’re made by vibrations! These vibrations travel through the air as waves – imagine throwing a pebble into a pond; it creates ripples that spread out.  The faster something vibrates, the higher its pitch will be, and the louder the sound.

Think about your favorite toy car. When you push it across the floor, it makes a buzzing sound, right? That buzz is because the wheels are vibrating as they hit the ground. Smaller objects, like a tiny drum or a small bell, vibrate much faster than bigger ones, which means they create higher-pitched sounds.  It’s all about how quickly something moves back and forth!

Scientists call this ‘pitch,’ which simply means how high or low a sound is. When an object vibrates really fast, like a tiny guitar string, it makes a high-pitched sound. Conversely, when an object vibrates slowly, such as a large bass drum, it produces a lower-pitched sound.  So, the speed of vibration directly determines the pitch we hear!
keyIdeas: Faster vibrations = higher pitch
Smaller objects vibrate faster
Pitch describes how high or low a sound is

questions.prompt: A small bell makes a higher-pitched sound than a large bell because…
choices: A. The small bell vibrates slower
B. The small bell vibrates faster
C. Both bells vibrate at the same speed
questions.explanation: The smaller the object, the quicker it can vibrate, creating a higher pitch.

questions.prompt: Which of these objects would make the highest-pitched sound?
questions.explanation: The metal triangle is smaller and lighter, so it will vibrate faster than the other options.

questions.prompt: What happens when an object vibrates quickly?
choices: A. It makes a low sound
B. It makes a high sound
C. It doesn’t make any sound
questions.explanation: Quick vibrations create a higher pitch, which is what we hear as a high-pitched sound.

mt_d-EKO-pKkP.title: Electricity Explorers: Conductors & Insulators
explanation: Imagine you’re building a really cool LEGO castle, and you want to make it light up with flashing lights! Electricity is like the power that makes things work, but not all materials let electricity flow through them easily. Some materials are called conductors – they're like super highways for electricity, letting it zoom right through. Others are insulators; they’re more like walls, blocking electricity from passing through.

Let’s test some things to see which ones are conductors and which ones are insulators! A conductor is anything that allows electricity to flow easily, while an insulator stops the flow. We can do this by building a simple circuit – a loop of wires with a battery and a light bulb. If the light bulb lights up, it means the material is conducting electricity!

Metals like copper and aluminum are fantastic conductors because they have lots of tiny particles that move around easily when electricity passes through them. That's why electrical wires are made from metal – it allows electricity to travel quickly and safely to power our homes and devices. The plastic coating protects us from getting shocked!
keyIdeas: Conductors let electricity flow.
Insulators block electricity.
Metals are generally good conductors.

questions.prompt: Which of these materials is MOST likely to be a conductor?
questions.explanation: Copper wire is made of metal and conducts electricity well.

questions.prompt: What does an insulator do?
choices: A) Allows electricity to flow easily
C) Makes things light up brightly
questions.explanation: Insulators prevent electricity from moving through them.

questions.prompt: Why are electrical wires often covered in plastic?
choices: A) To make them look pretty
B) To protect people from electric shocks
C) To stop the electricity from flowing
questions.explanation: The plastic coating acts as an insulator, preventing us from getting hurt if we touch the wire.

mt_Tls5qJ4p0L.title: Traits: What Makes You, You?
explanation: Let’s talk about what makes you special! Just like your parents and grandparents, you have traits – things like the color of your eyes or how tall you might grow. These traits are mostly passed down from your family through something called ‘inheritance,’ which is like a secret recipe that gets handed down through generations. Think about your favorite toy car; it’s probably shaped like a car because that's what cars usually look like, but you chose the color and maybe even added stickers – those choices are part of *you*!

However, not everything comes just from family! Your environment – things around you like food, sunlight, and how much you exercise – can also change your traits. For example, plants need sunshine to grow big and strong; if one plant gets lots of sun and another doesn’t, they will grow differently. Similarly, animals that eat a lot of healthy food tend to be bigger than those who don't!

It’s important to understand the difference between traits you inherit and ones influenced by your environment. Eye color is usually something you get from your parents – it’s an inherited trait. But if someone starts lifting weights regularly, their muscles will grow stronger; that’s because of the exercise they do – an environmentally influenced trait. So, traits are a mix of what you're born with and how you live!
keyIdeas: Traits come from both inheritance (family) and environment.
The environment can change some traits, like plant size or muscle strength.
Inherited traits are passed down through families; environmentally influenced traits change due to experiences.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a trait influenced by the environment?
questions.explanation: Muscle strength changes based on how much you work out.

questions.prompt: Why might two identical seeds grow into different-sized plants?
choices: A) One seed is better than the other
B) The plant with more sunlight grows bigger
C) They both need the same amount of water
questions.explanation: Plants need sunlight to grow; less sunlight means slower growth.

questions.prompt: What does ‘inheritance’ mean in this lesson?
choices: A) Something you learn from your friends
B) Something passed down from family
C) A choice you make about a toy
questions.explanation: Inheritance refers to traits that are passed on through generations.

mt_8bIXVKTdtK.title: Abstract nouns
explanation: Understand and use abstract nouns to name ideas, qualities, and states that cannot be perceived by the senses

You know this idea when you can: Sort words like 'freedom', 'table', 'courage', 'pencil', 'childhood' into abstract and concrete noun categories; Complete sentences using appropriate abstract nouns: 'Her _____ (brave → bravery) inspired the whole team'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Sort words like 'freedom', 'table', 'courage', 'pencil', 'childhood' into abstract and concrete noun categories
Complete sentences using appropriate abstract nouns: 'Her _____ (brave → bravery) inspired the whole team'
Write three sentences each containing a different abstract noun (e.g. 'honesty', 'friendship', 'happiness')

questions.explanation: Sort words like 'freedom', 'table', 'courage', 'pencil', 'childhood' into abstract and concrete noun categories

questions.explanation: Complete sentences using appropriate abstract nouns: 'Her _____ (brave → bravery) inspired the whole team'

questions.explanation: Write three sentences each containing a different abstract noun (e.g. 'honesty', 'friendship', 'happiness')

mt_0T0Zf0YG6k.title: Dinosaur Discoveries: How Scientists Learn!
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – you might start with a spaceship, but then you find a new brick and decide to build a castle instead! Scientists do something similar when they study dinosaurs. For many years, scientists thought that dinosaurs were all huge and powerful predators, like the T-Rex, because of the fossils they found. However, as more fossils were discovered, especially smaller ones, our understanding began to change.

Scientists use different tools and techniques now too! They can analyze fossil bones to figure out how fast a dinosaur ran or what it ate. Sometimes, new discoveries like footprints or even preserved dung (called coprolites!) provide clues that weren't available before. This means that the way we understand dinosaurs isn’t set in stone; it’s constantly evolving as scientists learn more.

It's important to remember that sometimes different scientists might look at the same fossil and come up with slightly different ideas about what it meant. That doesn’t mean one scientist is wrong, but it shows how complex science can be! The best scientific explanation isn't just based on one piece of evidence, but on lots of evidence gathered from many sources – like many scientists working together.
keyIdeas: Scientific knowledge evolves.
Fossil evidence shapes our understanding.
Multiple sources of evidence are important.

questions.prompt: Scientists originally thought most dinosaurs were giant predators. What kind of fossil evidence led to this idea?
choices: B) Large, powerful dinosaur skeletons like the T-Rex
C) Fossilized footprints of tiny creatures
questions.explanation: Early paleontologists primarily focused on large, imposing dinosaur fossils, leading them to believe most dinosaurs were apex predators.

questions.prompt: Two scientists are studying the same fossil. They interpret it differently because…
choices: A) One scientist is smarter than the other
B) They have different ideas about what the fossil represents based on their training and perspective
C) The fossil is actually two different dinosaurs
questions.explanation: Scientists can interpret evidence differently due to varying backgrounds, expertise, and interpretations of available data.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes how scientists determine the ‘best’ explanation for dinosaur extinction?
choices: A) The explanation that sounds the most exciting
B) The explanation supported by the greatest amount of evidence from many different sources
C) The explanation that only one scientist agrees with
questions.explanation: The strongest scientific explanations are those backed by a large body of supporting evidence gathered through multiple lines of inquiry.

mt_ebPelt-qAl.title: Equivalent fractions (age 9+)
explanation: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to (n×a)/(n×b) using visual models; use this principle to recognise and generate equivalent fractions, including tenths and hundredths

You know this idea when you can: Use a fraction strip to show 2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9; Explain that multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number gives an equivalent fraction because the size of the whole is unchanged.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

explanation: Make sense of complex multi-step problems involving large numbers, fractions, decimals, and percentages by analysing what is known and unknown, planning multi-step strategies, and evaluating reasonableness through estimation and inverse operations

You know this idea when you can: Break a three-step problem involving unit conversion and multiplication into sub-problems and solve systematically; Estimate 4,832 × 7 as roughly 5,000 × 7 = 35,000 to check a calculated answer of 33,824.

mt_q9EaJc2FP8.title: Long Multiplication Adventures!
explanation: Let’s talk about multiplying bigger numbers! Sometimes, we can't just multiply two numbers in a line like we do with smaller numbers. Long multiplication is a way to make sure we get the right answer when multiplying a larger number by a smaller one – it’s like building up our calculations step-by-step. We use columns and rows to keep everything organized, which helps us avoid mistakes.

Imagine you're buying 23 boxes of crayons that each cost $7. You need to figure out the total cost. Instead of adding $7 twenty-three times, we can use long multiplication! We’ll multiply 7 by 20 and 7 by 3 separately, then add those results together – it's a much faster way to do it. This method works for any number you want to multiply, as long as you carefully set out your columns.

Let's look at an area model! An area model is like a rectangle where we break down the multiplication into smaller parts. For example, if we wanted to multiply 45 by 23, we could draw a rectangle and split it into four rows and twenty-three columns. Then, we calculate the area of each small rectangle (45 x 20 and 45 x 3) and add them up – this shows us exactly how long multiplication works!
keyIdeas: Long multiplication helps with bigger numbers.
It involves setting out columns to organize calculations.
Area models can visually show the steps of long multiplication.

questions.prompt: What is 2,347 x 6?
questions.explanation: Remember to multiply each digit of 2,347 by 6 and then add the results.

questions.prompt: Calculate 34 x 27 using long multiplication.
questions.explanation: Set out the problem carefully, multiplying 34 by 20 and then 34 by 7.

questions.prompt: For 45 x 23, draw an area model. What is the total area of the rectangle?
questions.explanation: The total area represents the product of 45 and 23.

mt_eosO26KE-Z.title: Medieval Worlds Beyond Europe
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs – you can create all sorts of amazing things, right? Well, the medieval world wasn't just about knights and castles in Europe! There were incredibly clever people and powerful empires happening far away, like in the Middle East and Asia. These places developed their own unique cultures and made incredible advancements that we still see today, shaping our world in surprising ways.

Let’s talk about the Islamic Golden Age. During this time, which lasted for many centuries, brilliant Muslim scholars made huge breakthroughs in math, medicine, and architecture. They improved algebra – a system of equations – developed hospitals with advanced treatments, and built stunning mosques and palaces that are still beautiful today. Think of it like a golden age of learning and creativity fueled by curiosity and innovation.

Another important empire was the Mali Empire in West Africa, ruled by Mansa Musa. He was an incredibly wealthy ruler who traveled to Mecca – the holiest city for Muslims – and brought back huge amounts of gold! Because of his wealth and generosity, he’s remembered as one of the most famous rulers of that time.  The Silk Road, a network of trade routes, connected places like China, Europe, and Africa, allowing goods and ideas to travel across vast distances.
keyIdeas: The medieval world extended beyond Europe.
The Islamic Golden Age was a time of great learning and innovation.
The Silk Road connected distant civilizations through trade.

questions.prompt: Which of these was an achievement of the Islamic Golden Age?
questions.explanation: Algebra is a branch of mathematics that developed during this time.

questions.prompt: Mansa Musa is famous for what?
choices: B. Traveling to Mecca with gold
questions.explanation: He was a wealthy ruler who made a pilgrimage to Mecca and brought back enormous amounts of gold.

questions.prompt: What did the Silk Road do?
choices: B. It connected distant parts of the world through trade
C. It was used for building castles
questions.explanation: The Silk Road facilitated the exchange of goods and ideas between different civilizations.

mt_ArVBEpMAPk.title: Super Sleuth Scientists: Controlling Variables!
explanation: Have you ever wondered why some things work differently than others? Scientists do too, and they use something called ‘controlling variables’ to make sure their experiments are fair and accurate. Basically, a variable is anything that can change in an experiment – like the amount of water you give a plant or the temperature of a cooking recipe. When we control variables, we're trying to keep everything the same so we can see exactly what’s causing a change.

Let’s say you want to find out if different amounts of sunlight affect how quickly grass grows. You could set up two patches of grass – one in full sun and one in shade. The amount of sunlight is your *independent variable* – that's the thing you're changing. The height of the grass after a week would be your *dependent variable* – that’s what you’re measuring to see if it changes because of the sunlight. You wouldn’t want to change things like the type of soil or how much water each patch gets, those are *controlled variables*!

Why is controlling variables so important? Well, if you didn't control them, your results would be mixed up and confusing! Imagine trying to figure out if a new fertilizer makes plants grow taller if you also changed the amount of sunlight they received. It would be impossible to know which factor was actually responsible for the growth. Controlling variables helps us make sure our experiments are reliable and we can draw accurate conclusions.
keyIdeas: Variables change in an experiment.
Independent variables are what you change.
Controlled variables keep everything else the same.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of an independent variable?
choices: A. The amount of water a plant receives
B. The color of a toy car
C. The number of students in a class
questions.explanation: The independent variable is what the scientist changes.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to ‘control’ a variable in an experiment?
choices: A. To make it change quickly
B. To keep it the same throughout the experiment
questions.explanation: Controlling variables means keeping everything else constant.

questions.prompt: Why is controlling variables important for a fair test?
choices: A. It makes the results more complicated
B. It helps scientists draw accurate conclusions
C. It’s not important at all
questions.explanation: If you don't control variables, your results won't be reliable.

mt_6xj94tmpi-.title: When Secrets Get Tricky
explanation: Imagine your best friend, Alex, tells you a secret: they’ve been sneaking into the school playground after dark. On one hand, you really value loyalty – keeping a promise to be there for a friend and trusting them. It feels wrong to immediately tell someone else about something they confided in you, especially if it seems like a harmless activity.

However, another important value is safety! If Alex is sneaking around the playground after dark, there could be real dangers involved – maybe they’re getting hurt, or worse, someone else could get hurt. This creates a tension between loyalty and responsibility; you're wrestling with whether to protect your friend's feelings or ensure their well-being.

Deciding what to do in this situation isn’t about finding a ‘right’ answer that everyone agrees on. Instead, it’s about carefully considering both sides. You might talk to Alex privately and gently express your concerns about safety, offering support if they're struggling with something. Ultimately, you would make the decision based on what you believe is most important at that time.
keyIdeas: Ethical dilemmas have competing values.
Sometimes there isn’t a single ‘right’ answer.
Weighing values helps us make responsible decisions.

questions.prompt: Your friend tells you they're skipping homework to play video games. You value honesty, but also want your friend to do well in school. What should you do?
choices: A) Immediately tell their parents because skipping homework is wrong.
B) Talk to your friend about the importance of completing assignments and offer help if needed.
C) Ignore it and hope they change their mind.
questions.explanation: Talking to your friend allows you to address the values in tension – honesty and responsibility.  Choosing B shows you're considering both sides.

questions.prompt: Someone tells you a rumor about another student that might be unkind. You value kindness, but also want to respect your friend’s confidence. What is the best approach?
choices: A) Spread the rumor to see if anyone else believes it.
B) Tell your friend that spreading rumors can hurt others and encourage them to think before they speak.
questions.explanation: This choice demonstrates understanding of valuing kindness while respecting boundaries.  It’s about encouraging responsible behavior.

questions.prompt: Imagine you find a lost toy that belongs to someone else. You value honesty and returning things, but also don't want to be seen as bragging about finding it. How do you handle the situation?
questions.explanation: This question is open-ended to encourage critical thinking and justification of their approach.

mt_RNeEF1JU4J.title: Thinking About What’s Right and Wrong
explanation: Let's talk about ethics – that means thinking about what is right and wrong, and how we should treat each other and the world around us. Sometimes, it’s not always easy to know what the ‘right’ thing to do is, especially when there are different ideas about what’s fair or good.  Think about a time you saw someone being treated unfairly – maybe at school, or even just watching on TV; that's an ethical situation.

Imagine your friends are playing a game of tag and one person keeps pushing other kids to the ground, even though it hurts them. Some people might say this is okay because they’re winning, and it’s just part of the game.  Others would strongly disagree, believing that everyone should be treated with respect, no matter what happens during the game – these are two different perspectives on the same situation.

It's important to understand that even if you don’t agree with one perspective, it doesn’t mean they’re wrong. The person who thinks pushing is okay might believe in winning at all costs and not caring about the other players' feelings.  Acknowledging this shows you are a thoughtful thinker and can consider different viewpoints, which is a key part of being ethical.
keyIdeas: Ethics involves considering right and wrong actions.
Different people can have different perspectives on the same issue.
It’s important to understand why others hold their views, even if you disagree.

questions.prompt: Someone is cheating in a school race. One person says it's okay because they want to win, while another says it’s unfair to everyone else. Which perspective do you agree with most?
choices: A) The person who wants to win
B) The person who thinks it’s unfair
questions.explanation: Choosing B shows you understand that fairness is important and cheating undermines the spirit of competition.

questions.prompt: Your friend says it's okay to take toys from younger kids because they are ‘just playing.’ What should you do?
choices: B) Tell your friend it’s not right
C) Ignore what your friend said
questions.explanation: Choosing B demonstrates an understanding of fairness and respecting others' belongings.

questions.prompt: Imagine a group is deciding who gets to use a special playground set. One person wants to choose based on how loud they are, while another wants to choose based on how nicely they play.  What’s one thing you could say to understand the other person's viewpoint?
questions.explanation: This question assesses your ability to consider different criteria and acknowledge validity in opposing views.

mt_5PgQB0QkWi.title: Super Sleuths: Fair Testing!
explanation: Have you ever built with LEGOs and wanted to know which design was the strongest? Fair testing is all about figuring things out in a way that’s honest and reliable, so we can be sure of our answers. It's like being a super sleuth – we need to gather clues carefully and use them to solve a problem! This means making sure everything is the same except for one thing we are changing.

Let’s say you test how long it takes different types of blocks to fall down a ramp. You could try using the same ramp, the same blocks, and the same height each time. If you change things like the color of the block or the angle of the ramp, your results won't be fair because those changes would affect how fast they fall!  A fair test keeps everything constant to see what’s *really* causing a difference.

Once you have some results from your test, you can use them to make a prediction about something new. For example, if you found that red blocks fell faster than blue blocks, you could predict that a red block will fall fastest on a longer ramp. Then, you design another test to see if your prediction is correct – this helps us learn more and answer bigger questions!
keyIdeas: Predictions are based on evidence.
Fair tests control variables for accurate results.
Follow-up tests confirm or change predictions.

questions.prompt: You’re testing how far a toy car goes when pushed. You push it with the same amount of force each time, but sometimes you push on carpet and other times on wood. What is the MOST important thing to do to make your test fair?
choices: B. Push the car with different amounts of force
questions.explanation: It’s important to keep everything the same except for the one thing you are testing – in this case, the surface!

questions.prompt: Maria tested how quickly different brands of cereal dissolve in water. She used the same amount of cereal and water each time. What should Maria do next to make her test more fair?
choices: A. Use a different brand of milk
B. Measure the temperature of the water
C. Use different amounts of water
questions.explanation: Changing the temperature would affect how quickly the cereal dissolves, so using the same temperature is key for a fair comparison.

questions.prompt: You tested if plants grow taller with more sunlight. Your results showed that plants in direct sunlight grew taller than plants in shade. What’s one question you could now investigate based on this evidence?
questions.explanation: This opens up the possibility of testing different types of light, like artificial lights or different intensities of natural sunlight.

mt_NaqEP8xDhZ.title: Tenths and Hundredths: Making Fractions Bigger!
explanation: Let's talk about fractions that have a denominator of 10 – those are called tenths. Think about a pizza cut into ten slices; you’d get one tenth of the pizza if you ate just one slice! To make it easier to work with, we can turn these fractions into hundredths.  A hundredth is simply splitting each slice in half again, so now we have 100 tiny pieces instead of 10.

To do this, we multiply both the top and bottom number of the fraction by the same amount. For example, if we have 7/10, we can multiply both the 7 and the 10 by 10 to get 70/100. This means that 7/10 is the same as 70 out of 100 pieces – it's just a different way of writing the same amount!  This process is called finding an equivalent fraction.

Now, let’s add some fractions with tenths and hundredths. Remember, we need to make sure both denominators are the same before we can add them together. If we have 3/10 + 4/100, we first rewrite 3/10 as 30/100 because 3 x 10 = 30 and 10 x 10 = 100.  Then we can add the fractions: 30/100 + 4/100 = 34/100 – that’s our answer!
keyIdeas: Equivalent Fractions
Multiplying to Convert Denominators
Adding Fractions with Same Denominators

questions.prompt: What does it mean to rewrite 7/10 as an equivalent fraction?
choices: A. To change the number of slices in a pizza
B. To find another way to represent the same amount
C. To make the denominator smaller
questions.explanation: Equivalent fractions are different ways to show the same value.  7/10 and 70/100 both represent the same portion of the pizza.

questions.prompt: If you have 3/10 of a chocolate bar and your friend gives you 4/100 of a chocolate bar, how much of the whole chocolate bar do you have?
Why is it important to have the same denominator when adding fractions?
choices: A. It makes the numbers bigger
B. It allows you to add the numerators directly
C. It ensures you are adding the same amount
questions.explanation: When denominators are different, you can't directly add the numerators because you’re not comparing quantities of the same size.

mt_Vi4Vo5xs_g.title: Decimals for Tenths & Hundredths
explanation: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100; read and write decimal numbers as fractions (e.g. 0.62 = 62/100, 0.71 = 71/100)

Write 3/10 as 0.3 and locate on a number line

questions.explanation: Rewrite 0.62 as 62/100

questions.explanation: Read 0.07 and express as 7/100

mt_xfwv0M83mJ.title: Math Vocabulary Superpowers!
explanation: Let's talk about how important it is to use the *right* words when we’re talking about math! Sometimes, just saying 'a number' isn’t enough. When we’re working with shapes and numbers, using specific vocabulary makes sure everyone understands exactly what you mean – it’s like having superpowers for math!

Think about building with LEGOs. If you have a box of bricks, the ‘factors’ are the different sizes of bricks that make up the whole set. A ‘multiple’ is just a number of that brick size; for example, if you have three 2x4 bricks, then three is a multiple of 2x4. It's crucial to understand this difference because it helps us solve problems and describe what we see.

Let's say you're calculating the area of a square with sides that are 6cm long. You wouldn’t just write ‘48’; you would write ‘48 cm²’. This tells us exactly how many square centimeters make up the whole surface! Similarly, when finding volume, we can estimate using units like 'cm³' – this is important for accuracy.
keyIdeas: ‘Factor’ and ‘multiple’ are different terms with distinct meanings.
Always include units (like cm², m³, °) in your answers to show the size of things.
Using notation like 5² helps us be precise when talking about squares.

questions.prompt: What does ‘factor’ mean in math?
choices: A. A number that is multiplied by another number
B. A number that divides evenly into another number
C. A shape with many sides
questions.explanation: A factor is a number that divides completely into another number, leaving no remainder.

questions.prompt: You calculate the area of a rectangle and get 48 cm². How would you write this answer correctly?
questions.explanation: The ‘cm²’ tells us that the area is measured in square centimeters.

questions.prompt: What does 5² mean, and how would you read it?
questions.explanation: 5² means 5 multiplied by 5, which is equal to 25.  It's read as 'five squared'.

mt_2ESZh70NyS.title: Fraction Fun: Comparing and Understanding Parts of a Whole
explanation: Let's talk about fractions! A fraction is simply a part of something. It shows how many pieces you have out of the total number of pieces that make up a whole. For example, if you have a pizza cut into eight slices and you eat three slices, you’ve eaten 3/8 (three-eighths) of the pizza – that means three parts out of the eight total parts.

Fractions are all about comparing sizes. To do this, we often need to find a ‘common denominator,’ which is a number both fractions can be written with. If you want to compare 3/4 and 2/3, we first turn 3/4 into 6/12 (because 3 x 3 = 9 and 4 x 3 = 12) and 2/3 into 8/12 (because 2 x 4 = 8 and 3 x 4 = 12). Now, you can easily see that 6/12 is bigger than 8/12, so 3/4 is greater than 2/3!

This helps us understand fractions better because we’re looking at the same amount.  We use visual models like drawings or shapes to help us picture what a fraction represents.  Remember, fractions are just ways of describing parts of something – they're super useful in lots of situations, from baking cookies to measuring ingredients!
keyIdeas: Fractions represent parts of a whole.
Common denominators make comparing fractions easier.
Visual models help us understand what fractions mean.

questions.prompt: Which is greater: 1/2 or 2/5?
questions.explanation: To compare, we can convert both fractions to have a common denominator (10). 1/2 becomes 5/10 and 2/5 remains 4/10. Since 5/10 is greater than 4/10, 1/2 is greater.

questions.prompt: What does the bottom number of a fraction (the denominator) tell you?
choices: A. The size of the part
B. The total number of parts
questions.explanation: The denominator represents the total number of equal parts that make up the whole.

questions.prompt: You have a rectangle divided into 6 equal parts and you shade 4 of them. What fraction represents the shaded area?
questions.explanation: The denominator is 6 (the total number of parts) and the numerator is 4 (the number of shaded parts), so the fraction is 4/6.

mt_8xVHooT4aI.title: Maths Models: Real Life Adventures!
explanation: Have you ever made a cake with your family? Recipes are full of numbers, but they're not just about how much flour to use – they’re actually using maths! When a recipe says ‘double it,’ that means we need to multiply everything by two. This is called modelling because we’re making a math version of something real, like a delicious cake. We can use multiplication and fractions to figure out exactly how much of each ingredient we need when we want to make more or less than the original recipe.

Let's say your favorite chocolate chip cookie recipe calls for 1/2 cup of sugar. If you wanted to bake twice as many cookies, you’d double that amount – so you’d need 1 cup of sugar! This is a simple scaling problem where we are using multiplication to change the amounts. It’s important to remember that when we scale up or down, we're keeping everything proportional; the ratios between ingredients stay the same.

Now imagine you want to buy a rug for your bedroom. The room is 10 feet long and 8 feet wide. We can use this information to draw a scaled diagram – a smaller version of the room that uses easy-to-measure numbers, like inches or centimeters. Then we can apply the area formula (length times width) to find the actual area of your bedroom rug in square feet, which is super helpful for figuring out how much it will cost!
keyIdeas: Maths models use real-world problems as a starting point.
Scaling involves multiplying or dividing quantities proportionally.
Different representations (diagrams, fractions) can help us solve problems.

questions.prompt: You are doubling a recipe that calls for 2/3 cup of milk. How much milk do you need?
questions.explanation: Doubling means multiplying the fraction by 2, so (2/3) * 2 = 4/6

questions.prompt: A room is 12 meters long and 9 meters wide. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Area = length * width, so 12m * 9m = 108 square meters

questions.prompt: A toy is on sale for 25% off. If the original price was £30, what is the sale price?
questions.explanation: Calculate 25% of £30 (which is £7.50) and subtract it from the original price (£30 - £7.50 = £22.50)

mt_MCu_SNg_OW.title: Arrays and Multiplication: Sharing Equally!
explanation: Today we're going to explore how arrays help us understand multiplication and division. An array is simply a way of arranging things in rows and columns – like soldiers standing at attention or toys neatly organized on a shelf! When you have an array, you can easily see how many groups of something you have, which is exactly what we need when we're dividing numbers. Think about building with LEGOs; you could arrange them into a square array to help you figure out how many bricks are in the whole thing.

Let’s look at an example: imagine you have 4,932 candies and want to share them equally among 6 friends. We can use an array to show this! First, we would arrange the candies into rows and columns – maybe 6 rows with a certain number of candies in each row.  The number of columns (or rows) tells us how many groups we're making, and the total number of candies represents the size of each group. This will help us use short division to find out how many candies each friend gets.

When we divide, sometimes we don’t get a perfect whole number for every group.  This is where remainders come in! If 4,932 divided by 6 gives us a remainder of 0, it means everyone gets exactly the same amount. However, if there was a remainder like 5, that would mean you have 5 candies left over – maybe you could give them to someone extra or keep them for later!  The key is understanding what the remainder represents: how many items are left after we've made as many complete groups as possible.
keyIdeas: Arrays help us visualize multiplication and division.
Remainders show leftover items when dividing.
We can use arrays to check if our division answers are correct.

questions.prompt: You have 125 cookies and want to share them equally among 8 friends. What is the quotient (the whole number of cookies each friend gets)?
questions.explanation: 125 ÷ 8 = 15 with a remainder of 5. This means each friend gets 15 cookies and there are 5 left over.

questions.prompt: You are dividing 2,457 by 6. Your short division shows a quotient of 409 and a remainder of 3. What does the remainder (3) represent?
choices: A: The number of rows in your array
B: The number of cookies left over after sharing equally
C: The total number of cookies you started with
questions.explanation: The remainder represents the items that couldn't be divided evenly into groups.

questions.prompt: You calculate 3,684 ÷ 9 and get a quotient of 409.  To check your answer, what multiplication problem could you use to verify it?

mt_p-nbe0w_lf.title: Division with Remainders: Sharing Fairly!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn about division and something called remainders. Division is when you split a bigger number into equal groups. Think about sharing cookies – if you have 13 cookies and want to share them equally between 4 friends, each friend gets 3 cookies with one leftover! The leftover is the remainder.

Remainders are important because they tell us what's left over after we’ve divided as much as possible. Let's look at an example: imagine a school buys 7 boxes of 24 pens and wants to share them equally between 8 classes. We can divide 24 by 8, which gives us 3 pens per class. But since 7 boxes * 24 = 168 pens, and we have 168 / 8 = 21 pens, there are no remainders. This means the problem is actually about dividing 168 pens into 8 groups.

We can represent this with an equation! Let's say ‘n’ is the number of pens in each box. We could write it as 7 * n / 8 = some whole number. To solve for ‘n’, we need to find a number that, when multiplied by 7 and then divided by 8, gives us a whole number.  This helps us understand how many pens are in each box and how the division works.
keyIdeas: Division involves splitting a number into equal groups.
Remainders show what's left over when you can’t divide evenly.
Equations with letters help us solve multi-step problems.

questions.prompt: 52 children go on a trip in minibuses holding 9 each. How many minibuses are needed? (There might be one left over!)
questions.explanation: We need to find out how many groups of 9 fit into 52. 5 * 9 = 45, and 52 - 45 = 7. So we need 5 minibuses with 7 children left over.

questions.prompt: A shop sells packs of 6 pencils for £1.50 each. How much do 5 packs cost?
questions.explanation: Each pack costs £1.50, and we're buying 5 packs.  So the total cost is 5 * £1.50 = £7.50. This question has a mistake - it should be 5 x 6 = 30. The cost of 5 packs is 5 x 1.50 = £7.50.

questions.prompt: Sarah buys 12 apples and wants to share them equally with her 3 friends. How many apples does each friend get, and how many are left over?
questions.explanation: Each friend gets 4 apples (12 / 3 = 4), and there are 0 apples leftover.

mt_EaWjCyn8W2.title: Amazing Animal Adaptations!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how different animals look and act? Animals live in all sorts of places, from hot deserts to freezing oceans, and they’ve developed special features – we call them adaptations – to help them survive. An adaptation is basically a part of an animal's body or a way it behaves that helps it get food, stay safe, and thrive where it lives. Think about your favorite toy car; its wheels are shaped just right for rolling smoothly across the floor – that’s a simple example of an adaptation!

There are three main types of adaptations: structural, behavioral, and physiological. Structural adaptations are physical features like a giraffe's long neck or an eagle's sharp talons. Behavioral adaptations are things animals *do*, such as migrating south for the winter to find food or hibernating during cold months to save energy. Physiological adaptations involve how an animal’s body works, like the antifreeze in some fish blood that keeps them from freezing in icy waters.

It's important to know that these amazing adaptations don't just appear overnight! They develop over many generations through a process called natural selection. Natural selection means that animals with traits – or adaptations – that help them survive and reproduce are more likely to pass those traits on to their babies. Over time, this leads to populations of animals becoming better suited to their environments.
keyIdeas: Adaptations help animals survive.
There are three types of adaptations: structural, behavioral, and physiological.
Adaptations develop over many generations through natural selection.

questions.prompt: A giraffe has a long neck. What type of adaptation is this?
questions.explanation: A long neck helps giraffes reach high leaves for food, which is a physical feature that aids in survival.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a behavioral adaptation?
choices: A) A polar bear’s thick fur
B) Birds migrating south for the winter
C) The camouflage coloring of a chameleon
questions.explanation: Migration is a pattern of movement, and it's a behavior that helps animals find food and suitable climates.

questions.prompt: Why do adaptations develop over many generations?
questions.explanation: Adaptations develop through natural selection. Animals with helpful traits are more likely to survive and have babies who also have those traits.

mt_NDZYiLvApW.title: Biodiversity: Why Lots of Animals Matter!
explanation: Imagine your favorite LEGO set – it’s awesome because you have so many different pieces, right? Biodiversity is kind of like that, but instead of LEGOs, we're talking about all the different kinds of animals and plants living together in a place. Biodiversity means there’s a huge variety of species, or types, of living things in an ecosystem – like a forest, a pond, or even your backyard!  Having lots of different creatures makes an environment much more interesting and complex.

Now, why is this variety so important? A healthy ecosystem with high biodiversity is much stronger and more stable. If one plant or animal gets sick, the others can still survive because there are many different ways to get food and shelter. This also means that ecosystems provide us with ‘ecosystem services’ like clean air and water – things we need to live!  A diverse environment is better able to bounce back from problems like storms or disease.

Some animals, called 'keystone species,' have a really big impact on their ecosystem, even though they might not be the biggest or strongest. Think about wolves in Yellowstone National Park; when wolves were removed, other animals thrived, leading to overgrowth of plants and eventually impacting other animal populations.  Sea otters are another example – they eat sea urchins which would otherwise destroy kelp forests, so the otters keep the forest healthy!
keyIdeas: Biodiversity means a wide range of different species.
Healthy ecosystems need lots of variety to be strong and stable.
Keystone species have a major impact on their environment.

questions.prompt: What does biodiversity mean?
choices: A. Only having one type of animal
B. Having many different types of plants and animals
C. Just having big, strong animals
questions.explanation: Biodiversity means there's a lot of variety – lots of different kinds of living things!

questions.prompt: Why is biodiversity important for an ecosystem?
choices: A. It makes the ecosystem easier to control
B. It helps the ecosystem recover from problems
C. It only benefits the biggest animals
questions.explanation: A diverse environment can bounce back from challenges and provide essential services.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a keystone species?
questions.explanation: Sea otters control the kelp forests, showing their significant impact on the ecosystem.

mt_5S4byWDX6n.title: Endangered & Extinct Species
explanation: Understand why some animal species become endangered or go extinct — habitat destruction, hunting/poaching, pollution, climate change, and invasive species — and know examples like the giant panda, mountain gorilla, Amur leopard, and the now-extinct dodo and thylacine, using the IUCN Red List as the system scientists use to track threatened species
You know this idea when you can: Defines endangered as a species at risk of extinction; Names at least 3 causes of species becoming endangered.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Defines endangered as a species at risk of extinction
Names at least 3 causes of species becoming endangered
Gives at least 3 examples of endangered or extinct animals
Knows the IUCN Red List exists as a tracking system

questions.prompt: True or false: Defines endangered as a species at risk of extinction
questions.explanation: Defines endangered as a species at risk of extinction

questions.prompt: True or false: Names at least 3 causes of species becoming endangered
questions.explanation: Names at least 3 causes of species becoming endangered

questions.prompt: True or false: Gives at least 3 examples of endangered or extinct animals
questions.explanation: Gives at least 3 examples of endangered or extinct animals

mt_pWwV_8OgXD.title: Saving Our Animal Friends
explanation: Many animals around the world are becoming endangered, meaning there aren’t very many of them left. This happens because their homes are being destroyed, or people might hunt them for their fur or horns. Scientists and governments are working hard to stop this from happening, using clever ideas to protect these amazing creatures before they disappear forever. It's like a big puzzle – we need to figure out how to help the animals survive!

There are lots of ways people try to save endangered animals. One way is through national parks and marine reserves, which are special areas where animals can live safely without being bothered by humans or developers building new things. Another important thing is captive breeding programs – these are like animal nurseries where scientists carefully breed animals in captivity to increase their numbers. A great example of this was the California condor and Arabian oryx, which were almost extinct but have been brought back thanks to dedicated conservationists.

Finally, it’s not just about protecting habitats; we also need to help individual animals! Anti-poaching patrols stop people from illegally hunting endangered species, while wildlife corridors allow animals to travel safely between different areas where they can find food and mates. Even small things you do at home, like choosing toys made from recycled materials or planting trees, can make a difference – every little bit helps!
keyIdeas: Conservation efforts protect habitats.
Captive breeding programs help increase animal populations.
Everyone can contribute to saving endangered animals.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a way people protect animals’ homes?
choices: A) Building new roads and buildings
B) Creating national parks and marine reserves
questions.explanation: National parks and marine reserves set aside areas where animals can live without being disturbed.

questions.prompt: The California condor was saved from extinction through which of these methods?
questions.explanation: Captive breeding programs allowed scientists to increase the number of California condors.

questions.prompt: What can you do at home to help endangered animals?
choices: B) Plant trees and choose recycled products
questions.explanation: Choosing eco-friendly options helps protect the habitats that animals need.

mt_CBxOcjh69x.title: Invasive Species: When Animals Move Too Far
explanation: Have you ever seen a grey squirrel in your neighborhood? They're super cute, right? But did you know that grey squirrels weren’t always here in the UK? They were brought over from America many years ago. These animals are called ‘invasive species,’ which means they’ve been introduced to a place where they don’t naturally live and now they’re causing problems for other animals.

Invasive species are basically animals or plants that humans have moved to a new area. Think about it like this: if you bring a pet fish home, and it starts eating all the native fish in your aquarium, it's behaving like an invasive species! Similarly, when grey squirrels were introduced to the UK, they started competing with red squirrels for food and space, and sadly, many red squirrels had to leave or couldn’t survive.

There are lots of reasons why invasive species can be harmful. Often, they have no natural enemies in their new home – so there's nothing hunting them! They also often outcompete native animals for food and resources, meaning they take everything the native animals need. Sometimes, they even spread diseases that native animals aren’t prepared to fight off.
keyIdeas: Invasive species are non-native animals introduced to new areas.
Human actions can unintentionally create invasive species problems.
Invasive species negatively impact native wildlife through competition and disease.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes an invasive species?
choices: A. A plant that grows naturally in a region.
B. An animal or plant introduced to a new environment where it causes harm.
C. A native animal perfectly adapted to its habitat.
questions.explanation: An invasive species is defined as a non-native organism that negatively impacts the ecosystem it's now in.

questions.prompt: Why are cane toads considered an invasive species in Australia?
choices: A. They help control insect populations.
B. They were introduced by humans and poison native predators.
C. They’re a delicious food source for Australian birds.
questions.explanation: Cane toads were brought to Australia, and their poisonous skin harms the animals that naturally eat them.

questions.prompt: Imagine you bring a new type of grass seed to your yard. What could happen?
questions.explanation: This illustrates how introducing non-native plants can disrupt local ecosystems and outcompete native grasses for resources.

explanation: Select and use tools and representations strategically: choose between mental methods, formal written methods, protractors, fraction strips, and diagrams based on the demands of the problem

questions.explanation: Choose a common-denominator approach vs. benchmark comparison for ordering fractions, and explain why

mt_H8OgKZbgGe.title: Symbiosis: Friends and Foes in Nature
explanation: Have you ever noticed how some animals seem to help each other out? Symbiosis is a fancy word for when two different kinds of living things – like plants, animals, or even tiny bugs – live closely together and have a special connection. It’s not just about being near each other; it's about interacting in a way that benefits at least one of them. Scientists study these relationships because they show us how amazing and interconnected life on Earth really is.

There are three main types of symbiosis: mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism. Mutualism means both animals help each other out – like clownfish living in sea anemones! The clownfish get protection from predators hiding among the anemone's stinging tentacles, and the anemone gets cleaned by the clownfish. Commensalism is when one animal benefits without hurting the other; a great example is remora fish that attach themselves to sharks and eat leftover bits of food – the shark isn’t affected.

Finally, there’s parasitism, where one animal gains something at the expense of another. Think about ticks on deer: the tick feeds on the deer's blood, harming the deer but getting a meal for itself. Recognizing these different types of relationships helps us understand how animals and plants work together in nature, creating complex ecosystems.
keyIdeas: Symbiosis is a close relationship between two different species.
Mutualism benefits both species involved.
Parasitism benefits one species while harming another.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes symbiosis?
choices: A. Two animals fighting each other for food.
B. A close relationship between two different living things.
questions.explanation: Symbiosis refers to any interaction where two species live closely together and have a connection.

questions.prompt: A barnacle attaching itself to a whale's skin is an example of which type of symbiosis?
questions.explanation: The barnacle benefits from the whale’s movement and access to sunlight, but it doesn’t harm the whale.

questions.prompt: Imagine a bird building its nest in a tree. Does this represent symbiosis? Explain your answer briefly.
questions.explanation: Yes! The bird benefits by having a safe place to build its nest, and the tree is not harmed. This shows a mutualistic relationship.

mt_kdWoAel3Zl.title: Tenths - Pieces of a Whole!
explanation: Today, we're going to learn about tenths – that’s one part out of ten! Think about a pizza cut into ten equal slices; each slice represents one tenth. When we talk about fractions like 1/10 or 2/10, we mean exactly two pieces out of those ten total slices. Fractions are just a way to describe how much of something we have, and tenths are a really important part of understanding bigger fractions.

Let’s look at an example: imagine you're sharing a chocolate bar with your friends. If the chocolate bar is divided into 10 equal pieces, and you eat 3 of those pieces, you've eaten 3/10 (three tenths) of the whole chocolate bar! We can write this as a fraction – 3/10 – to show exactly how much you ate.  It’s important to remember that all the pieces have to be equal in size for fractions like these to work correctly.

Now, let's talk about relating tenths to other numbers. You might see something like 0.025 written down. This is the same as 25/1000 – twenty-five out of one thousand pieces.  Also, remember that 1 tenth is equal to 100 thousandths! If you have a small piece of cheese cut into 100 tiny slices and take just one slice, you’ve got one tenth.
keyIdeas: Tenths represent one part out of ten.
1/10 is the same as 100/1000.
Fractions help us describe parts of a whole.

questions.prompt: What fraction represents 3 slices out of 10?
questions.explanation: Three out of ten is represented by the fraction 3/10.

questions.prompt: Which fraction is equal to 1/10?
questions.explanation: One divided by ten is the same as one hundred divided by one hundred, which equals 1/10.

questions.prompt: If you eat 5 tenths of a cake, what fraction of the cake did you eat?
questions.explanation: This question requires students to apply their understanding. The answer is 5/10.

mt_CUmjcE7W6c.title: Fractions on a Number Line
explanation: Let's learn about fractions using number lines! A number line is like a road that stretches out forever, with numbers marked along it. We can use this road to show how big or small a fraction is by dividing the space between each number into equal parts. For example, if we want to represent 1/4 on a number line, we would divide the distance from zero to one into four equal sections.

Think about sharing a pizza with your friends! If you cut the pizza into eight slices and you eat three slices, you've eaten 3/8 of the whole pizza. We can place 3/8 on a number line by marking off three out of those eight equal parts starting from zero. The point where we mark it represents the amount of pizza you ate, showing us that 3/8 is bigger than 1/8 but smaller than 2/8.

Number lines are super helpful because they let us compare fractions easily. If one fraction is further to the right on the number line than another, it’s a larger value. We can also use them to add and subtract fractions if they share the same denominator – just like adding three slices of pizza from our earlier example!
keyIdeas: Number lines visually represent fractions.
Fractions on a number line show relative size.
We can use number lines to compare and add/subtract fractions.

questions.prompt: If 1/2 is marked on a number line from 0 to 1, what fraction would be halfway between 1/2 and 3/4?
questions.explanation: To find the fraction halfway between two fractions, we add them together and divide by 2. (1/2 + 3/4)/2 = (2/4 + 3/4)/2 = 5/4 / 2 = 5/8.

questions.prompt: Which fraction is represented by the point on a number line that is 2/5 of the way from 0 to 1?
questions.explanation: To find 2/5 of 1, we multiply: (2/5) * 1 = 2/5.

questions.prompt: Sarah walked 2/6 of the way around a track. John walked 1/3 of the way around the same track. Who walked further?
choices: C) They walked the same distance
questions.explanation: To compare, we need to see how far they went as fractions of the whole track.  2/6 = 1/3, so Sarah walked the same distance as John.

Give at least two examples of AI being used to help the environment
Describe the trade-off between AI's environmental cost and its environmental benefits

questions.explanation: Explain that training AI models uses a lot of electricity and water

questions.explanation: Give at least two examples of AI being used to help the environment

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the trade-off between AI's environmental cost and its environmental benefits
questions.explanation: Describe the trade-off between AI's environmental cost and its environmental benefits

mt_3S10OOGPqu.title: What Is an Angle?
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes! In math, we look at lots of different kinds of shapes, and some are special because they have corners. An angle is a corner formed when two straight lines, called rays, come together. Think about it like this: imagine you're building with LEGO bricks – sometimes you need to make a sharp turn, creating an angle where the bricks meet.

Every angle has three important parts: a vertex and two arms. The vertex is the very point where the two rays meet; it’s like the center of the angle. The arms are the straight lines that extend out from the vertex. You can find angles all around you, even in things like your favorite toy car – the wheels make an angle with the body of the car.

When two rays share a common endpoint, they create an angle. It's important to remember that both rays need to be straight lines. If you draw two lines from the same point on paper, you’ll see there is a space between them – and that space is an angle! This meeting point is called the vertex.
keyIdeas: An angle is formed by two rays.
The vertex is the point where two rays meet.
Angles are found in many shapes and everyday objects.

questions.prompt: What is a vertex of an angle?
choices: B the space between two lines
C the point where two rays meet
questions.explanation: The vertex is the exact spot where the two rays forming the angle are joined.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows an example of an angle?
choices: C two rays meeting at a point
questions.explanation: An angle needs two rays coming together to form the shape.

questions.prompt: If you draw two lines from the same point, what do you create?

mt_qUGMyMYn9m.title: Angles and Turns: Measuring the World!
explanation: Let's talk about shapes and how we measure them! Everything around us is made of angles, which are just corners where lines meet. A really important thing to know is that a full circle makes exactly 360 degrees – think about a spinning wheel or the way a pizza is cut into slices; each slice represents a part of that whole turn.

Now, imagine you're drawing a circle on paper and stopping halfway. That’s a right angle, which measures 90 degrees. One degree is like taking just one tiny piece out of that 360-degree circle – it’s equal to 1/360th of the whole turn.  It’s a really small amount, but it's important for measuring angles accurately!

Let’s look at an example: If you spin around four times and stop, you’ve turned through 45 degrees because each spin is one degree, and you have four spins. So, if we have an angle that has turned through 45 one-degree angles, it must be 45 degrees!  Understanding this helps us measure all sorts of things like the angle in a door or the shape of your favorite toy car.
keyIdeas: Angles are measured in degrees.
A full turn is 360 degrees.
One degree represents one-thirty sixth of a circle.

questions.prompt: What does a full turn equal in degrees?
questions.explanation: A full circle is made up of 360 degrees.

questions.prompt: If an angle turns through 10 one-degree angles, how many degrees does it measure?
questions.explanation: Each one-degree angle adds up to 10 degrees.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is closest in size to a right angle?
questions.explanation: A right angle measures exactly 90 degrees.
