questions.explanation: Calculate 3.45 × 100 = 345

questions.prompt: True or false: Calculate 72 ÷ 1000 = 0.072
questions.explanation: Calculate 72 ÷ 1000 = 0.072

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that multiplying by 10 shifts each digit one place to the left
questions.explanation: Explain that multiplying by 10 shifts each digit one place to the left

mt_-YYnLLIZh5.title: Sun-Driven Weather Systems
explanation: Have you ever noticed how a hot sidewalk feels much warmer than a grassy lawn on a sunny day? That’s because the Sun's energy doesn’t spread out evenly – it hits things differently! Land, like roads and buildings, heats up quickly when it gets sunlight. Water, such as oceans and lakes, takes much longer to warm up because it needs to absorb more heat.

This uneven heating creates differences in air pressure. Warm air is lighter than cool air, so the warmer air near the land rises up, creating an area of low pressure. As cooler air rushes in to replace it, this movement of air is what we call wind! Think about a hot air balloon – it rises because warm air is less dense.

These wind patterns are connected to huge weather systems like hurricanes and jet streams. The Sun’s heat causes the Earth to rotate, and these rotating winds drive ocean currents too. Because the equator gets more direct sunlight than areas closer to the poles, it's naturally warmer – a perfect example of how the Sun’s energy shapes our world.
keyIdeas: The Sun unevenly heats Earth.
Temperature differences create air pressure differences.
Sun-driven heating influences large weather patterns and ocean currents.

questions.prompt: Why does a sidewalk feel warmer than grass on a sunny day?
choices: A. The sidewalk is made of metal.
B. The Sun’s energy hits the sidewalk and grass differently.
C. The sidewalk reflects more sunlight.
questions.explanation: The Sun heats land (like the sidewalk) much faster than water, creating a temperature difference.

questions.prompt: What happens to warm air compared to cool air?
choices: A. Warm air sinks, and cool air rises.
B. Warm air rises, and cool air sinks.
C. They stay at the same level.
questions.explanation: Warm air is less dense (lighter) than cool air, so it naturally rises.

questions.prompt: Which area of Earth receives the most direct sunlight?
questions.explanation: The equator is located directly facing the Sun and therefore receives the most direct sunlight.

mt_TMOzMCE17H.title: Climate Zones
explanation: Understand that Earth has distinct climate zones — tropical (hot and wet near the equator), temperate (moderate, with four seasons), polar (freezing cold), arid/desert (very dry), and mountain (cold at high altitude) — and that each zone supports different ecosystems and ways of life
You know this idea when you can: Name and describe at least four climate zones; Explain what determines which zone a place belongs to (mainly latitude and geography).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name and describe at least four climate zones
Explain what determines which zone a place belongs to (mainly latitude and geography)
Give an example of how a climate zone affects the plants, animals, or people living there

questions.prompt: True or false: Name and describe at least four climate zones
questions.explanation: Name and describe at least four climate zones

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain what determines which zone a place belongs to (mainly latitude and geography)
questions.explanation: Explain what determines which zone a place belongs to (mainly latitude and geography)

questions.prompt: True or false: Give an example of how a climate zone affects the plants, animals, or people living there
questions.explanation: Give an example of how a climate zone affects the plants, animals, or people living there

mt_g1qgxmlJQ2.title: Polar Oceans and Our World's Climate
explanation: Imagine a giant bathtub filled with water – that’s kind of like our Earth’s oceans! The water at the North and South Poles is incredibly cold, much colder than anywhere else on the planet. Because it's so cold, this polar water becomes very dense, meaning it gets heavier than warmer water. This heavy, cold water sinks down deep into the ocean, starting a powerful movement called thermohaline circulation – think of it like a giant conveyor belt!

This ‘conveyor belt’ is super important because it moves huge amounts of water around the world. As the cold, dense water sinks, it gets replaced by warmer water that flows towards the poles. This process helps distribute heat all over the globe, which is why places near the oceans often have milder temperatures than places far away from them. It's like a giant system for sharing warmth!

Now, let’s talk about ice! Sea ice – like the kind you see in pictures of Antarctica – is white and reflects sunlight back into space. Dark ocean water absorbs sunlight, warming up. This reflection helps keep our planet cooler because less heat gets trapped here; this is called the albedo effect. The Southern Ocean around Antarctica plays a key role too - it’s incredibly rich with nutrients!
keyIdeas: Cold, dense water sinks to drive ocean currents.
Sea ice reflects sunlight, reducing Earth's temperature.
The Southern Ocean is highly productive due to nutrient upwelling.

questions.prompt: What causes the thermohaline circulation?
choices: A. Warm water rising and cold water sinking
questions.explanation: Cold, dense water is heavier than warm water and sinks, initiating the global conveyor belt.

questions.prompt: Why does sea ice help regulate Earth’s temperature?
choices: A. It absorbs sunlight and warms the ocean
B. It reflects sunlight back into space
C. It creates strong currents that cool the air
questions.explanation: White sea ice reflects sunlight, preventing heat from being absorbed by the ocean.

questions.prompt: The Southern Ocean is special because it ____.
choices: B. Brings nutrients to the surface for plants and animals
C. Is always covered in snow
questions.explanation: Upwelling brings nutrient-rich water from the deep ocean to the surface, supporting a thriving marine ecosystem.

mt_oqziWKry-L.title: Angle Adventures: Adding Up Angles!
explanation: Today, we're going to explore some amazing rules about angles! Imagine you’re building a really cool LEGO spaceship – you need to make sure all the corners are just right. In geometry, we look at shapes and how their angles fit together, and there are some special ways they can add up. These rules help us figure out exactly what size each angle needs to be.

One important rule is that if two angles make a straight line, like the edge of a ruler, they always add up to 180 degrees. Think about when you’re drawing a straight line on paper – it's made up of two angles that together measure exactly 180 degrees! This means if one angle is 60 degrees, the other must be 120 degrees because 60 + 120 = 180.

Another rule is super helpful when you have four right angles – like a square or rectangle. All those right angles (90 degrees each) add up to 360 degrees, which makes one whole turn!  If you see three right angles at a point, you know the fourth one must be 270 degrees because 3 * 90 = 270. Let’s use this knowledge to solve some angle puzzles!
keyIdeas: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°.
Four right angles at a point make 360°.
Vertically opposite angles are always equal.

questions.prompt: If two angles on a straight line measure 90° and 70°, what is the size of the third angle?
questions.explanation: Remember, angles on a straight line add up to 180°. So, 90° + 70° = 160°, and the third angle must be 180° - 160° = 20°.

questions.prompt: A square has four right angles. What is the total number of degrees in all four angles?
questions.explanation: Four right angles, each measuring 90°, add up to 4 * 90 = 360 degrees.

questions.prompt: Two pairs of vertically opposite angles are given. One pair measures 60° and the other measures 120°. What is the measure of the third angle?
questions.explanation: This question requires understanding that all three angles at a point add to 360 degrees. The two known angles are 60° and 120°, so the third must be 360 - (60 + 120) = 360 - 180 = 180 degrees.

mt_33zncDHC3N.title: Giving and Receiving Feedback Like a Friend
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs with your friend, Liam. You both create amazing castles, but sometimes one of you might accidentally knock over the other's tower! Giving feedback is like gently telling Liam what he did well – ‘Wow, Liam, that turret looks really strong!’ – and then offering a suggestion to help him build even better - ‘Maybe if you used a wider base, it would be even more stable.’ The goal isn’t to make him feel bad; it's about helping him improve his building skills.

Receiving feedback can sometimes feel tricky. Let’s say your teacher reads your story and points out that some sentences are a little confusing. It’s okay to feel a bit surprised or even a little disappointed, but try not to get upset! Instead, listen carefully to what your teacher says and ask for clarification if you don't understand – ‘Can you tell me more about which parts were confusing?’ This shows you’re open to learning.

Why is giving and receiving feedback so important? When we give each other helpful suggestions, we help people learn and grow. And when we listen to feedback without getting defensive, we show respect for others' ideas and build stronger friendships. Constructive feedback helps us become better at everything we do, from building LEGOs to writing stories.
keyIdeas: Feedback should be specific – point out exactly what someone did well or could improve.
Listen carefully when receiving feedback without getting defensive.
Constructive feedback helps people learn and grow together.

questions.prompt: You’re helping a friend build a model airplane. You say, ‘The wings look really neat!’ and then suggest, ‘Maybe you could add some extra struts for more support.’ This is an example of…
questions.explanation: Constructive feedback includes both a positive comment and a suggestion for improvement.

questions.prompt: Your classmate shows you their drawing, and you notice some of the colors are muddy. Instead of saying ‘That looks terrible!’ what could you say?
choices: A) Nothing at all – it’s their art.
B) ‘I like your use of color, but maybe try using lighter shades.’
questions.explanation: This response offers a specific suggestion for improvement in a polite way.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to listen carefully when someone gives you feedback?
choices: A) Because everyone always says things the right way.
B) Because it’s important to understand what they are trying to tell you.
C) Because it's okay to just disagree with them.
questions.explanation: Understanding the feedback allows you to learn and make changes.

mt_Cqm8iy48UI.title: Reading Timetables
explanation: Timetables are like secret maps that tell us when things happen! They’re super helpful for planning our days, whether we're waiting for a bus or train. These tables show us the exact times of departures and arrivals, so we know exactly when to be somewhere. Timetables use numbers and words to give us precise information – it’s all about being organized and knowing what to expect.

Let's look at an example: Imagine you’re waiting for the school bus. The timetable will show you exactly when the bus leaves from your stop, like 8:15 AM. It also tells you when it arrives at school, which is important so you don’t miss the start of class! Reading timetables carefully helps us avoid being late or missing out on anything.

When reading a timetable, pay close attention to the headings and columns. These usually tell you things like the day of the week, the time, and the destination.  Make sure you understand what each column means before you start looking for specific information – that’s how we can accurately find out when something will happen.
keyIdeas: Timetables show us scheduled times.
They help us plan our activities.
Pay attention to headings and columns in a timetable.

questions.prompt: What does a timetable mainly tell you?
questions.explanation: Timetables show us the scheduled times for events and journeys.

questions.prompt: If a bus timetable says ‘10:30 AM’ it means…
choices: B. The bus leaves at 10:30 AM
C. The bus arrives at 10:30 AM
questions.explanation: The time listed on the timetable indicates when the bus departs from its stop.

questions.prompt: You are looking at a train timetable and see ‘London to Manchester – 2 hours 45 minutes’. What does this tell you?
questions.explanation: This tells you the duration of the journey between London and Manchester.

mt_v5DyOEpbbr.title: Words Matter: Picking the Right Voice
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – your friends might want to see a super cool spaceship, while your little brother just wants to build a simple tower. When we write or talk, it’s the same idea: we need to think about *who* we’re talking to and *why*.  Knowing your audience helps you choose the best words and ideas so everyone understands what you're trying to say. It’s like picking the right tools for a job – you wouldn’t use a hammer to screw in a nail, would you?

Let's say you’re writing instructions for how to build a sandcastle on the beach. If you were writing for your friends, you might use fun words and tell jokes! But if you were writing for a younger sibling who doesn’t know much about building, you’d need to be very clear and step-by-step with simple language.  The *tone* is how you sound or write – are you friendly, serious, excited, or calm? Choosing the right tone makes your message easier to understand.

Think about a school report. You wouldn't use silly words like 'awesome' or 'super cool,' would you? Instead, you’d use more formal language that shows you’re serious and knowledgeable.  Your audience is your teacher, so you need to write in a way that shows respect and understanding of the topic.  Adjusting your tone and vocabulary makes sure everyone gets what you mean – it's all about being considerate of who you are talking to!
keyIdeas: Audience matters: Who are you writing for?
Purpose shapes your voice: Why are you writing?
Tone adjusts the message: How do you sound or write?

questions.prompt: If you were writing a story about dinosaurs for younger children, what kind of words would you use?
questions.explanation: Using simple language is best when your audience is young.  It makes the story easier to understand.

questions.prompt: You are writing a letter to your principal asking for more recess time. What tone should you use?
questions.explanation: A polite and respectful tone is most appropriate when communicating with an authority figure like the principal.

questions.prompt: Which of these writing forms would be best for giving instructions on how to ride a bike?
questions.explanation: Step-by-step directions are perfect for providing clear and concise instructions.

mt_eiB3-6pu6a.title: Shape Detectives: Area & Perimeter
explanation: Today, we're going to become shape detectives and learn about area and perimeter! These are two really important things you can measure about rectangles – shapes with four straight sides where opposite sides are equal. Remember, perimeter is the total distance *around* a shape, like walking around the edge of a playground. We’ll use formulas to help us calculate these measurements quickly and accurately.

Let's talk about area first. Area tells us how much space something covers – think about it like covering a table with tiles! The formula for calculating the area of a rectangle is length multiplied by width (l × w).  For example, if you have a room that’s 8 meters long and 6 meters wide, its area would be 48 square meters. We use ‘square meters’ (m²) because we're measuring space – it’s like counting how many squares fit inside the shape.

Now let’s look at perimeter. The formula for calculating the perimeter of a rectangle is 2 times the length plus 2 times the width (2l + 2w). This means you add up all four sides!  Let's say your favorite toy car is rectangular – you could use this to figure out how much fencing you’d need to build around it.  We can also estimate areas of irregular shapes by breaking them down into smaller rectangles.
keyIdeas: Area measures the space inside a shape.
Perimeter measures the distance around a shape.
The formulas l × w and 2l + 2w help us calculate area and perimeter of rectangles.

questions.prompt: A rectangle has a length of 5 cm and a width of 3 cm. What is its perimeter?
questions.explanation: To find the perimeter, add all four sides: (5 + 3) * 2 = 16cm

questions.prompt: A square has a side length of 7.5 cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: The area of a square is side * side: 7.5cm * 7.5cm = 56.25 cm²

questions.prompt: You want to cover a rectangular pond with tiles that are 10 cm by 10 cm. The pond is 3 meters long and 2 meters wide. How many tiles will you need?
questions.explanation: First, convert meters to centimeters: 3m = 300cm and 2m = 200cm. Then calculate the area of the pond in square centimeters: 300cm * 200cm = 60000 cm². Finally, divide the total area by the area of one tile: 60000 cm² / (10cm * 10cm) = 600 tiles.

mt_ZxdfRbwkKM.title: Evidence-Based Writing
explanation: Draw evidence from informational texts to support analysis, reflection, and research in writing, applying grade-level reading standards to non-fiction

You know this idea when you can: Write a research-based paragraph or short report that cites specific facts, details, and evidence from informational texts to support key points; Synthesise information from two informational sources into a written summary that accurately represents both, noting areas of agreement and difference.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Synthesise information from two informational sources into a written summary that accurately represents both, noting areas of agreement and difference

questions.explanation: Synthesise information from two informational sources into a written summary that accurately represents both, noting areas of agreement and difference

questions.prompt: True or false: Paraphrase information from informational texts accurately and cite sources appropriately, avoiding plagiarism
questions.explanation: Paraphrase information from informational texts accurately and cite sources appropriately, avoiding plagiarism

mt_X_aDUBh-HF.title: Energy Travelers!
explanation: Have you ever noticed how music sounds different depending on where you are listening to it? That's because sound energy travels! When you play a guitar, the vibrations of the strings create sound waves. These waves move through the air, and when they reach your ears, we hear them – that’s energy moving from the guitar to your brain!

Light is another amazing way energy travels. Think about a flashlight: it shines light because energy has been transformed into electromagnetic waves. These waves travel in straight lines until they hit something, like a wall or your eyes!  This shows us how light energy moves from the bulb to our vision.

Heat also travels through energy transfer. When you stand near a warm radiator, you feel its warmth because heat energy is moving from the radiator to your body. This happens because hot objects have more energy than cold ones, and that energy spreads out until it balances.
keyIdeas: Energy can move from one place to another.
Sound, light, heat, and electricity all transfer energy.
Energy travels through waves or currents.

questions.prompt: Which of these shows sound energy traveling?
questions.explanation: Sound travels as waves, and listening to music is how we hear those waves.

questions.prompt: Light from a lamp reaches your eyes because…
choices: B. Light energy is traveling through the air
questions.explanation: Light travels in straight lines as electromagnetic waves, reaching your eyes.

questions.prompt: What happens when you stand near a hot radiator?
questions.explanation: Heat energy is transferred from the radiator to your body, making you feel warm.

mt_VI5kdtf28e.title: Natural Resources: Where Does Our Energy Come From?
explanation: Let’s talk about where all the energy we use comes from! Everything around us – like the lights in our house, the gasoline that powers cars, and even the electricity that charges our tablets – relies on natural resources. These resources are things found naturally on Earth, like coal, oil, gas, wind, and sunshine. It’s amazing to think about how these materials provide the power for so many of our daily activities.

When we take these natural resources from the earth, it can have a big impact on our environment. For example, digging up coal or oil often causes pollution, which is when harmful substances get into the air and water. Also, using fossil fuels like gas and oil releases gases that trap heat in the atmosphere, leading to climate change – meaning the Earth gets warmer than it should.

Some natural resources are renewable, meaning we can keep using them without running out, like wind and solar energy. These sources constantly replenish themselves. However, other resources, called fossil fuels, are non-renewable because once we use them up, they’re gone – like coal and oil. Understanding the difference between these types of resources is key to protecting our planet.
keyIdeas: Natural resources provide energy for our lives.
Using natural resources can harm the environment.
Renewable resources are sustainable, while fossil fuels aren’t.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a non-renewable energy source?
questions.explanation: Coal is a fossil fuel, meaning it takes millions of years to form and cannot be replaced quickly.

questions.prompt: What happens when we burn oil for energy?
choices: B. It releases gases that contribute to climate change
C. It doesn’t affect the environment
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels like oil releases greenhouse gasses, trapping heat and warming the planet.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a renewable energy source?
questions.explanation: Sunlight is constantly available and can be harnessed to create electricity without depleting a resource.

mt_Eehl12cSnN.title: Unlocking Stories: Character & Setting Secrets
explanation: Let's talk about how stories really come to life! When we read, it’s not just about the words on a page; it’s about understanding *who* is in the story and *where* they are. Think about your favorite LEGO set – you have all these pieces (the characters) and a box (the setting), but it's only when you put them together that you can build something amazing!  Authors do the same thing with their stories, carefully crafting details to make everything feel real.

Now, let’s look at how authors show us what characters are like. They don’t just tell us someone is brave; they *show* us by describing what the character does in a dangerous situation. For example, if a character runs into a burning building to save a friend, that shows bravery!  Authors use a character's words – what they say – and their actions – what they do – along with their thoughts – what they’re feeling – to build a complete picture of who they are.

Similarly, setting isn’t just about the location; it’s about creating a mood.  Think about a dark, rainy forest versus a sunny beach. The author uses descriptions like ‘dripping leaves’ and ‘grey skies’ in the forest to make you feel scared or uneasy, while they might use ‘warm sand’ and ‘blue waves’ on the beach to create a feeling of happiness and relaxation. These details – what we see, hear, smell, taste, and even *feel* – all contribute to the setting's impact.
keyIdeas: Characters are revealed through their actions, words, thoughts, and how others react to them.
Setting creates a mood and atmosphere through sensory details and figurative language.
Analyzing these elements helps us understand how events drive the story forward.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're reading about a character who always avoids eye contact and fidgets with their hands. What does this suggest about their personality?
choices: A) They are confident and outgoing.
B) They might be nervous or uncomfortable.
C) They are trying to trick the reader.
questions.explanation: The character's actions – avoiding eye contact and fidgeting – provide evidence that they are likely feeling uneasy.

questions.prompt: An author describes a room as ‘cold, damp, and filled with shadows.’ What does this most likely tell you about the setting?
choices: A) It’s a cheerful and welcoming space.
B) It's a gloomy and unsettling place.
C) It’s a well-lit and comfortable room.
questions.explanation: The words ‘cold, damp, and shadows’ create a negative mood and suggest an unpleasant setting.

questions.prompt: A character makes a difficult decision that leads to a fight with their best friend. What kind of evidence would you look for in the text to understand this event fully?
questions.explanation: You'd be looking for details about what each character said and did before, during, and after the fight – showing how their actions caused the conflict.

mt_sdmm_m60qX.title: Unlocking Stories: Using Evidence in Your Writing
explanation: Have you ever told a story about something that happened to you? When you do, you probably give examples – like ‘I was playing with my LEGOs and suddenly the tower fell down!’ That’s because good stories need proof. In writing, we call this 'evidence' and it means using details from what you read or heard to back up your ideas.

Think about a favorite book, maybe *Harry Potter* or *Percy Jackson*. When you write about why Harry is brave, you wouldn’t just say ‘He’s brave.’ Instead, you would find a quote from the book where he does something courageous, like facing Voldemort. That quote becomes your evidence – it shows that you're not just guessing; you’re using what the author wrote to support your point.

Using evidence makes your writing much stronger! It helps your reader understand *why* you believe what you believe and shows them you've really thought about the story. When we use quotes and details, our arguments become more convincing and interesting for everyone to read.
keyIdeas: Evidence supports claims.
Quotes from texts are powerful evidence.
Strong writing uses specific details.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes evidence in writing?
choices: A. An opinion about a story
B. Details or quotes from a text that support an idea
C. A summary of the entire plot
questions.explanation: Evidence is information used to prove something is true, like a quote from a book.

questions.prompt: Let's say you’re writing about a character who is sad. What would be the best evidence to use?
choices: B. Finding a passage where the character says something sad or describes feeling sadness,”C. Telling everyone how you feel when you are sad
questions.explanation: Specific details from the text, like dialogue or descriptions, provide direct proof of the character's emotions.

questions.prompt: Imagine you’re comparing two stories about friendship. What would be a good way to use evidence?
choices: A. Saying one story is better than the other
B. Finding quotes that show how each friend acts and describes their feelings
C. Just telling people which story you liked best
questions.explanation: You need to find specific details from both stories to compare and contrast them effectively.

mt_E_OryWIYkn.title: Narrator's Point of View
explanation: Compare and contrast the point of view from which different stories are narrated, including distinguishing between first-person and third-person narration and explaining how the narrator's perspective shapes the reader's understanding

You know this idea when you can: Identify whether a story is told in first person (I/we) or third person (he/she/they) and explain how this affects what the reader knows about characters' thoughts and feelings; Compare two versions of a story or event told from different points of view and explain how each narrator's perspective changes what is emphasised, omitted, or revealed.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Compare two versions of a story or event told from different points of view and explain how each narrator's perspective changes what is emphasised, omitted, or revealed

mt_0MfpLj0Uhb.title: Book Buddies: Sharing Your Reading Picks!
explanation: Today we're going to learn how to be a great book buddy – someone who loves reading and wants to share their favorite books with others! When you recommend a book, you’re helping your friend discover something new and exciting. It’s like telling them about the coolest toy they could ever have; you want them to know why it's so awesome!

To make a good recommendation, you need to talk about some important things about the book. First, tell your friend what *kind* of book it is – is it fantasy with dragons and magic? Is it a mystery where someone has to solve a crime? Or maybe it’s a story about friendship and growing up like when you play on the playground with your friends?

Then, explain why you think they'll enjoy it! You can compare it to books they already love. For example, if they really liked ‘Harry Potter,’ you could say this new book is also magical but has a different kind of adventure.  It’s important to give specific reasons – like the characters are brave or the story keeps you guessing!
keyIdeas: Genre Matters: Knowing what type of book it is helps your friend decide if they'll like it.
Compare & Contrast: Linking a new book to something they already enjoy makes the recommendation stronger.
Give Reasons, Not Just Opinions: Explain *why* you think it’s good – focus on characters, plot, or themes.

questions.prompt: Your friend loves books about animals. You recommend ‘Charlotte's Web.’ Why did you choose this book?
choices: A) Because it’s a funny story about pirates.
B) Because it’s a heartwarming story about friendship between a pig and a spider.
C) Because it’s a scary ghost story.
questions.explanation: Charlotte's Web is known for its beautiful story of friendship, which aligns with your friend's preference for animal stories.

questions.prompt: You want to recommend ‘The Lightning Thief.’ What information would you share about the book?
choices: A) It’s a long and complicated history book.
B) It’s a fantasy adventure story about a boy who is a demigod.
C) It's a book about learning how to bake.
questions.explanation: You should explain that ‘The Lightning Thief’ is a fantasy adventure, which fits with the genre your friend enjoys.

questions.prompt: When recommending a book, what’s one important thing you need to do?
choices: A) Just tell them you like it.
B) Explain why you think they would enjoy it.
C) Ask them if they've read any other books.
questions.explanation: Giving reasons is key – explaining the genre, characters, or themes helps your friend understand why you’re recommending it.

mt_A1Xfu5p5KT.title: Animal Life Cycles: A Wild Journey!
explanation: Let’s explore the amazing ways animals grow up! Every animal has a life cycle, which is like a set of steps it goes through from birth to adulthood. Mammals, like puppies and kittens, go through direct development – they grow slowly and steadily over time, changing gradually as they get bigger. Think about your pet; you see them start small and then watch them develop their adult features little by little.

Amphibians, such as frogs, have a really cool life cycle called metamorphosis. They begin as tadpoles in the water with gills and a tail, but then slowly change into frogs with legs and lungs! Insects, like butterflies, also go through metamorphosis, transforming completely from their early stages to their adult forms. This is a huge difference compared to animals that grow directly, like a snake.

Birds have a life cycle too, though it’s often similar to mammals in that they hatch as babies and grow steadily. However, some birds, like swallows, undergo a short period of metamorphosis where they develop feathers before becoming fully grown. Understanding these different cycles helps us see how diverse and wonderful the animal kingdom really is!
keyIdeas: Life cycles have distinct stages.
Metamorphosis involves a complete change in form.
Different animals follow different developmental paths.

questions.prompt: Which of these animals undergoes metamorphosis?
questions.explanation: Metamorphosis means a complete change in form, like a caterpillar turning into a butterfly.

questions.prompt: What is direct development?
choices: A) A process where an animal changes completely
B) A process where an animal grows gradually without major changes
C) A process where an animal lives underwater
questions.explanation: Direct development means the animal slowly develops its adult features over time, like a baby growing into a toddler.

questions.prompt: A frog starts as a tadpole. What is this part of its life cycle called?
questions.explanation: The change from a tadpole to a frog is an example of metamorphosis.

mt_yNWt3GQBNp.title: Plant & Animal Reproduction
explanation: Describe the life process of reproduction in some plants and animals, including sexual and asexual reproduction in plants

You know this idea when you can: Describe sexual reproduction in plants: pollination, fertilisation, seed production; Give examples of asexual reproduction in plants: runners (strawberries), bulbs, cuttings.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Give examples of asexual reproduction in plants: runners (strawberries), bulbs, cuttings
Compare reproduction in egg-laying animals (birds, frogs) vs live-bearing mammals

questions.explanation: Describe sexual reproduction in plants: pollination, fertilisation, seed production

questions.prompt: True or false: Give examples of asexual reproduction in plants: runners (strawberries), bulbs, cuttings
questions.explanation: Give examples of asexual reproduction in plants: runners (strawberries), bulbs, cuttings

questions.prompt: True or false: Compare reproduction in egg-laying animals (birds, frogs) vs live-bearing mammals
questions.explanation: Compare reproduction in egg-laying animals (birds, frogs) vs live-bearing mammals

mt_SXbZ3bC9z7.title: Fraction Face-Off: Comparing Fractions!
explanation: Today, we're going to become fraction detectives! Fractions are parts of a whole – like if you have a pizza cut into 8 slices and eat 3, you’ve eaten 3/8 of the pizza. When fractions look different, it can be tricky to tell which is bigger or smaller; that’s where comparing comes in. We'll learn how to make sure we're really comparing apples to apples by finding a common number for both parts – this is called a ‘common denominator’.

Finding a common denominator helps us compare fractions more easily. Imagine you have 3/8 of a chocolate bar and your friend has 5/12 of the same chocolate bar. To compare them, we need to change them so they both have the same number of slices (the denominator). We can find a common multiple of 8 and 12 – in this case, 24 – and then convert each fraction to have 24 as its denominator.

Let's do another example: ordering fractions like 2/3, 3/5, and 7/10. We can turn all the fractions into equivalent fractions with a common denominator (like 30). This makes it much easier to see which fraction is largest because they all represent the same amount of the whole! Remember, comparing fractions is about finding out which part represents the bigger piece.
keyIdeas: Common denominators are essential for fair comparisons.
Equivalent fractions represent the same amount, even if they look different.
Benchmarks like 1/2 can help us quickly estimate and compare fractions.

questions.prompt: Compare 1/3 and 1/4. Which is larger?
questions.explanation: To compare, we can find a common denominator (12).  1/3 becomes 4/12 and 1/4 becomes 3/12. Since 4 is greater than 3, 1/3 is larger.

questions.prompt: Order the following fractions from smallest to largest: 2/5, 3/7, 1/2
questions.explanation: First, convert all fractions to have a common denominator (35).  Then compare the numerators: 2/5 = 14/35, 3/7 = 15/35, and 1/2 = 17.5/35. Therefore, 2/5 is smallest, 1/2 is in the middle, and 3/7 is largest.

questions.prompt: You eat 5/8 of a cookie, and your friend eats 3/4 of a cookie. Who ate more?
questions.explanation: To compare, we need common denominators (8).  5/8 = 10/16 and 3/4 = 12/16. Since 12 is greater than 10, your friend ate more cookie.

mt_42QD6nYjiZ.title: Measuring with Line Plots!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about line plots, which are a super cool way to show how things change over time! Imagine you're growing seeds and measuring how tall they get each day. A line plot helps us see all the measurements neatly organized on a graph – it’s like drawing a picture with numbers instead of just writing them down. We can use this to track changes in something, like plant growth or even how many times someone throws a ball.

A line plot uses a number line as its base and then marks points for each measurement you take. Each point shows one data value – in our seed example, it would show the height of a seed on a particular day. The more points we have, the better we can see patterns and trends in our measurements.  It’s important to make sure your points are spaced evenly along the number line so that you can accurately read them later.

Let's say we measured caterpillar lengths in 1/8-inch increments and plotted them on a line plot. We could then use this line plot to find out which caterpillar was the longest or shortest, and even add up all the measurements to find the total length of all the caterpillars! This is how we can use line plots to solve problems with fractions.
keyIdeas: Line plots help us see patterns in data.
We can use line plots to compare measurements.
Adding fractional measurements on a line plot helps us find totals.

questions.prompt: If our seed growth line plot shows the tallest seed grew 3/8 of an inch, what does that mean?
choices: B. The seed grew 3/8 of an inch.
C. The seed grew 8 times as tall as the shortest seed.
questions.explanation: This question tests understanding of what a point on a line plot represents – it's the measurement at that specific spot.

questions.prompt: You measured three caterpillars and plotted their lengths on a line plot. The longest caterpillar was 5/8 inch long, and the shortest was 1/8 inch long. How much longer was the longest caterpillar than the shortest?
questions.explanation: This question requires adding fractions: 5/8 - 1/8 = 4/8, which simplifies to 1/2.

questions.prompt: You have a line plot showing the lengths of five toy cars. The total length of all the cars is 7/8 inch. If one car is 1/8 inch long, how long are the other four cars combined?
questions.explanation: This question tests the ability to subtract from a total: 7/8 - 1/8 = 6/8

mt_p6MhZJYYPN.title: Measuring Up: Metric and Imperial Units
explanation: Let's talk about how we measure things! We use different systems, like the metric system (which uses numbers like 1, 2, 5) and the imperial system (which uses inches, feet, pounds, etc.). The metric system is super common around the world because it’s easy to work with – it's based on powers of ten.  We’ll focus today on understanding how some common imperial units relate to the metric system, especially inches and kilograms.

One important thing to know is that 1 inch is about equal to 2.5 centimeters. Think about your favorite LEGO bricks; they are often measured in inches! If you have a LEGO brick that’s 4 inches long, you can estimate how long it would be in centimeters by multiplying 4 by 2.5 – that's 10 centimeters! This is an *approximate* conversion because things aren't always perfectly precise.

Now let’s look at weight. We use kilograms (kg) to measure heavier things, and pounds (lbs) for lighter things.  We know that approximately 1 kilogram is about equal to 2.2 pounds. If a baby weighs 7 pounds, we can estimate that's roughly 3 kilograms – because 2.2 multiplied by 3 gets us pretty close to 7!
keyIdeas: Metric and imperial systems measure the same things differently.
1 inch is approximately equal to 2.5 centimeters.
1 kilogram is approximately equal to 2.2 pounds.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many centimeters are in 3 inches?
questions.explanation: Remember, 1 inch is about 2.5 cm. So, 3 inches would be 3 * 2.5 = 7.5 centimeters.

questions.prompt: If a bag of apples weighs 4 pounds, approximately how many kilograms does it weigh? (Use: 1 kg ≈ 2.2 lbs)
questions.explanation: Divide the weight in pounds by 2.2 to get the approximate weight in kilograms: 4 / 2.2 = approximately 2 kilograms.

questions.prompt: Which is heavier, a bag of potatoes weighing 5kg or a bag of potatoes weighing 10lbs?
questions.explanation: 5 kg (approximately 11 lbs) is heavier than 10 lbs.

mt_v5yDTWEiyQ.title: Thinking About Thinking: Reflecting on Your Learning
explanation: Have you ever built with LEGOs and made a really cool castle, or maybe finished a tricky puzzle? When we learn something new – like how to solve math problems or even learning about dinosaurs – it's super important to think about *how* we learned it. Reflecting means taking a moment to look back at the whole experience, not just if you got the answer right.

There are some clever ways grown-ups and teachers help us learn better by thinking about our learning too! One way is called the ‘Kolb reflective learning cycle,’ which basically says we go through steps like experiencing something, then thinking about it, talking about it with others, and finally, trying it again. Another idea comes from researchers Hattie and Timperley who found that feedback – both good and bad – helps us learn more effectively.

So, after you finish a project at school or even learning something new at home, like building with blocks, take a few minutes to think about what helped you the most. Did having someone explain it to you help? Or maybe working alone was better? Thinking about these things will make your next time learning even easier and more fun!
keyIdeas: Reflection helps you understand how you learn best.
Feedback is a powerful tool for improving your learning.
Thinking about what worked and didn't work makes future learning easier.

questions.prompt: You are building with LEGOs and you get stuck. What’s the first thing you should do?
choices: A) Keep trying to build it the same way.
B) Ask a friend or family member for help.
C) Give up and start something new.
questions.explanation: Asking for help is a great reflection – you're recognizing that you need more information to understand how to proceed.

questions.prompt: Your teacher gives you feedback on a math problem. What should you do with this feedback?
choices: A) Ignore it because you already got the answer right.
B) Pay attention to what your teacher said and try to figure out where you went wrong.
C) Ask someone else to solve the problem for you.
questions.explanation: Feedback helps you see if you understood the concept correctly, so you can learn from your mistakes.

questions.prompt: After learning about a new animal, what’s one thing you could do to reflect on what you learned?

mt_JwP9QFv6gQ.title: Numbers All Around Us!
explanation: Today we're going to explore big numbers – all the way up to a million! Numbers aren’t just symbols on paper; they represent amounts of things. Think about your LEGO collection, maybe you have over 500,000 bricks if you count them carefully! Understanding how to read and write these large numbers is super important in math and even in everyday life.

Let's look at writing a number like 403,072. First, we break it down into its place values: hundreds of thousands (4), tens of thousands (0), thousands (3), hundreds (0), tens (7), and ones (2). Then, we write it out in words: four hundred thirty thousand, seven hundred seventy-two.  Expanded form is another way to show this – 400,000 + 30,000 + 7,000 + 700 + 70 + 2.

Comparing numbers is just as important! When we compare 548,301 and 543,801, we look at the digits from left to right. Both have 500,000 in them, then 40,000. The number with the biggest digit in the thousands place (548,301 has a 8 while 543,801 has a 3) is bigger!  Using symbols like < and > helps us clearly show which number is larger or smaller.
keyIdeas: Place value is key to understanding large numbers.
We can write numbers in words, expanded form, and using numerals.
Comparing numbers involves looking at the digits from left to right.

questions.prompt: What does 403,072 written out in words is?
choices: A) Four hundred thirty thousand, seven hundred seventy-two
B) Three hundred forty thousand, seven hundred seventy-two
C) Five hundred thirty thousand, seven hundred seventy-two
questions.explanation: We broke down the number into its place values and wrote it out correctly.

questions.prompt: Which is greater: 548,301 or 543,801? Explain why.
questions.explanation: 548,301 has a larger digit in the thousands place (8 is greater than 3).

questions.prompt: Order these numbers from smallest to largest: 762,981; 761,523; 762,819; 761,823.
questions.explanation: This requires students to arrange the numbers based on their value.

mt_8H2kO4k2B9.title: Regular vs. Irregular Polygons
explanation: Let’s talk about shapes! A polygon is any shape made up of straight lines that connect to form a closed figure, like the sides of a building or even your favorite toy car. We can sort these polygons into two groups: regular and irregular. The main difference between them comes down to whether all their sides and angles are exactly the same – if they are, it’s regular; if not, it's irregular.

A ‘regular’ polygon means that *every* side is the same length, and *every* angle is the same size. Think about a pentagon, like the shape of some building blocks or a stop sign! A regular pentagon always has five equal sides and five equal angles – that's what makes it special.  If you have an irregular polygon, at least one side will be longer or shorter than another, or one angle will be bigger or smaller.

Let’s look at a rectangle. It’s a very common shape – we see them on our walls and in buildings! A rectangle has four equal angles (right angles), but its sides are not all the same length. That's why it’s irregular, unless it happens to be a square, which is a special type of rectangle where *all* sides are equal.  Understanding this difference helps us describe shapes more accurately.
keyIdeas: Regular polygons have equal sides and angles.
Irregular polygons have at least one side or angle that differs.
A square is a regular polygon with all sides equal.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes is a regular polygon?
choices: A) A shape with 6 sides and unequal angles.
B) A shape with 5 sides and all sides and angles are equal.
C) A shape with 3 sides and all sides are different lengths.
questions.explanation: A regular polygon must have all its sides and angles the same size.

questions.prompt: A rectangle has ____ angles.
questions.explanation: Rectangles always have four right (equal) angles.

questions.prompt: Which shape is an example of an irregular polygon?
choices: B) An octagon with all sides and angles equal
C) A shape with 8 sides but not all sides are the same length.
questions.explanation: An irregular polygon has at least one side or angle that differs from others.

mt_VgOePicFYK.title: Fraction Addition Concepts
explanation: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts; decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way
You know this idea when you can: Show that 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 and also 3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8; Write two different decompositions of 5/6.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.

Use a visual model to justify a decomposition of 2 1/8 into whole-number and fraction parts

True or false: Write two different decompositions of 5/6
questions.explanation: Write two different decompositions of 5/6

questions.prompt: True or false: Use a visual model to justify a decomposition of 2 1/8 into whole-number and fraction parts
questions.explanation: Use a visual model to justify a decomposition of 2 1/8 into whole-number and fraction parts

mt_-V7EnqU7gG.title: Adding Fractions with Different Denominators
explanation: Today, we're going to learn how to add fractions that don’t have the same bottom number – we call these ‘different denominators’. Think about it like this: you can’t directly put a piece of pizza with a slice from another pizza together without first making them the same size. To do this, we need to find a ‘common denominator’, which is a number that both fractions can be easily turned into.

Let's look at an example! Imagine you have 1/3 of a chocolate bar and your friend gives you 1/6 of the *same* chocolate bar. To add these, we need to change them so they both have sixes as their bottom number. We can turn 1/3 into 2/6 because 2 multiplied by 3 equals 6. Now we can add: 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6, and finally, we simplify that fraction by dividing the top and bottom by 3 to get 1/2!

Now let's try another one! Suppose you have 3/4 of a pie and someone gives you 1/8 of the *same* pie. To add these fractions, we need a common denominator. The smallest number that both 4 and 8 can divide into is 8, so we convert 3/4 to 6/8. Then we add: 6/8 + 1/8 = 7/8. See how finding the common denominator helped us solve the problem?
keyIdeas: Find a Common Denominator
Convert Fractions
Simplify Your Answer

questions.prompt: What is the first step when adding fractions with different denominators?
questions.explanation: To add fractions with different denominators, you must first find a common denominator – a number that both fractions can be easily converted to.

questions.prompt: Calculate 3/4 − 1/8 by converting to eighths.
questions.explanation: First, convert 3/4 to eighths: 6/8. Then subtract the fractions: 6/8 - 1/8 = 5/8.

questions.prompt: If you need to add 2/5 + 3/10, what is the common denominator?
questions.explanation: The common denominator is 10. You would convert 2/5 to 4/10 before adding.

mt_89riIKwGYp.title: Angle Adventures!
explanation: Let's talk about shapes and angles! An angle is a measure of how much something turns. Think about your LEGO bricks – when you build them, you often create corners, and each corner makes an angle. We can use special tools like protractors to measure these angles accurately, but today we’ll learn how to figure them out using math!

Sometimes, we don't have a protractor handy, so we can use what we know about angles. Remember that when two lines meet and make a corner, they form an angle. If you draw a straight line, it has 180 degrees – that’s like all the way around! We can add or take away from this total to find out the size of other angles.

Let's look at some examples. Imagine you are building with blocks and create a shape where two sides meet to form an angle. If we know one angle is 65 degrees, and the other angle is ‘x’ degrees, we can add them together to find out what ‘x’ is! This works because all angles on a straight line always add up to 180 degrees.
keyIdeas: Angles measure turns.
Angles on a straight line add up to 180 degrees.
We can use addition and subtraction to find unknown angles.

questions.prompt: Two angles on a straight line are 65° and x°. Find x = ?
questions.explanation: Angles on a straight line always add up to 180 degrees. So, 65° + x° = 180°.  To find x, we subtract 65° from both sides: 180° - 65° = x°, which gives us 115°.

questions.prompt: An angle is decomposed into 35° and 40°. State the whole angle is ?
questions.explanation: When an angle is broken down into smaller parts, we add those parts together to find the total angle. So, 35° + 40° = 75°.

questions.prompt: Angles at a point are 120°, 90°, and x°. Find x = ?
questions.explanation: All angles around a single point add up to 360 degrees. Therefore, 120° + 90° + x° = 360°.  Solving for x: x° = 360° - 120° - 90° = 150°.

mt_1KkvzwYxbR.title: Negative numbers in context
explanation: Interpret negative numbers in context (temperature, sea level, bank balance); count forwards and backwards with positive and negative whole numbers, including through zero
You know this idea when you can: Place –3, –1, 0, 2, 5 on a number line; The temperature is –4°C and rises by 7 degrees — what is the new temperature?.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Place –3, –1, 0, 2, 5 on a number line
The temperature is –4°C and rises by 7 degrees — what is the new temperature?
Count backwards from 3 in ones: 3, 2, 1, 0, –1, –2

questions.explanation: Place –3, –1, 0, 2, 5 on a number line

questions.prompt: True or false: The temperature is –4°C and rises by 7 degrees — what is the new temperature?
questions.explanation: The temperature is –4°C and rises by 7 degrees — what is the new temperature?

questions.prompt: True or false: Count backwards from 3 in ones: 3, 2, 1, 0, –1, –2
questions.explanation: Count backwards from 3 in ones: 3, 2, 1, 0, –1, –2

mt_4-vfMgmCVB.title: Building Blocks of Awesome Paragraphs!
explanation: Have you ever built with LEGOs? You start with one brick, then add another, and another, until you have a fantastic castle or spaceship! Writing a good paragraph is similar – it’s about connecting your ideas so they flow together smoothly. A cohesive paragraph means that each sentence relates to the sentences before and after, making sense for the reader.

To make your writing stickier like glue, we use special words and phrases called ‘cohesive devices.’ These help us link our thoughts. For example, instead of saying 'The dog ran fast,' you could say 'Then the dog ran fast.' Or, if you’re describing a game you played at school, you might say, 'Firstly, we formed teams; then, we took turns throwing the ball.'

Let's look at an example: Imagine writing about your favorite toy. Instead of saying ‘The teddy bear is soft and cuddly. It makes me happy.’ You could rewrite it as ‘The teddy bear is soft and cuddly, and therefore makes me happy.’ See how using 'therefore' connects the ideas? This helps readers understand exactly what you mean!’
keyIdeas: Use pronouns to avoid repeating words.
Adverbials show the order of events.
Linking devices create a smooth flow.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a pronoun correctly?
choices: A) The cat chased the mouse. B) It ran quickly across the yard. C) He jumped over the fence.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'it' replaces the noun 'cat.'

questions.prompt: What does ‘firstly’ do in a paragraph?
choices: A) Shows a contrast B) Indicates the order of events C) Expresses an opinion
questions.explanation: ‘Firstly’ tells you that something happened first.

questions.prompt: Which sentence could be improved with a cohesive device to show cause and effect?
choices: A) I ate my dinner. B) Because it was raining, we stayed inside. C) The sun is shining.
questions.explanation: 'Because' shows that the rain caused them to stay inside.

mt_uvILgZq9HN.title: Linking paragraphs with adverbials
explanation: Link ideas across paragraphs using adverbials of time (later, meanwhile, after a while), place (nearby, far away, beyond the wall), and number (secondly, finally, in addition) to guide the reader through a multi-paragraph text

You know this idea when you can: Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed'; Use adverbials of place to move the reader to a new location between paragraphs, e.g. 'Beyond the forest, the land was flat and dry'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed'
Use adverbials of place to move the reader to a new location between paragraphs, e.g. 'Beyond the forest, the land was flat and dry'
Use numbering or addition adverbials to structure non-fiction across paragraphs, e.g. 'The first reason... The second reason... In addition...'

questions.prompt: True or false: Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed'
questions.explanation: Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed'

questions.prompt: True or false: Use adverbials of place to move the reader to a new location between paragraphs, e.g. 'Beyond the forest, the land was flat and dry'
questions.explanation: Use adverbials of place to move the reader to a new location between paragraphs, e.g. 'Beyond the forest, the land was flat and dry'

questions.prompt: True or false: Use numbering or addition adverbials to structure non-fiction across paragraphs, e.g. 'The first reason... The second reason... In addition...'
questions.explanation: Use numbering or addition adverbials to structure non-fiction across paragraphs, e.g. 'The first reason... The second reason... In addition...'

mt_YUJ5pwalqL.title: Angle Explorers!
explanation: Today we're going to be angle explorers, learning about different types of angles and how to guess their sizes! An angle is formed when two lines or line segments meet. Think about the corners of your LEGO blocks – they make angles! We’ll learn to estimate these angles using our eyes and then try to figure out if they are big or small.

There are three main types of angles: acute, obtuse, and reflex. An *acute* angle is smaller than a right angle (90 degrees), like the corner of a sandwich. An *obtuse* angle is bigger than a right angle but less than 180 degrees – imagine the opening of a book! Finally, a *reflex* angle is bigger than 180 degrees and smaller than 360 degrees; it’s like turning around completely!

Let's try an example: Imagine you're building a sandcastle on the playground. The shadow your bucket makes in the sun creates an angle. If that angle looks pretty wide, we could estimate it as obtuse because it’s bigger than 90 degrees. We can use our estimations to compare angles and understand how they relate to each other – it’s like being a detective for shapes!
keyIdeas: Angles are formed by lines meeting.
Acute angles are less than 90 degrees, obtuse angles are more than 90 degrees but less than 180, and reflex angles are greater than 180 degrees.
We can estimate angles using our observations.

questions.prompt: Which of these angles is the smallest?
questions.explanation: An acute angle is smaller than a right angle, so a 45-degree angle is the smallest.

questions.prompt: You see a clock showing 3:30. What type of angle does this make?
questions.explanation: The hands point to 3 and 30, creating an angle greater than 90 degrees but less than 180 degrees – it’s obtuse.

questions.prompt: Order these angles from smallest to largest by estimation: 60°, 150°, 90°, 210°
questions.explanation: This question requires students to apply their estimation skills and ordering knowledge.

mt_XrvPx5kUfO.title: Tectonic Plates
explanation: Understand that Earth's crust is broken into large pieces called tectonic plates that float on hotter, softer rock beneath and move very slowly — a few centimetres per year
You know this idea when you can: Describe Earth's crust as broken into large plates; Explain that the plates float on hotter, partially melted rock underneath.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Describe Earth's crust as broken into large plates
Explain that the plates float on hotter, partially melted rock underneath
State that plates move very slowly, typically a few centimetres per year

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe Earth's crust as broken into large plates
questions.explanation: Describe Earth's crust as broken into large plates

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that the plates float on hotter, partially melted rock underneath
questions.explanation: Explain that the plates float on hotter, partially melted rock underneath

questions.prompt: True or false: State that plates move very slowly, typically a few centimetres per year
questions.explanation: State that plates move very slowly, typically a few centimetres per year

mt_-JnOhdei6F.title: Plate Boundaries: Earth's Shaky Spots!
explanation: Imagine you’re building with LEGOs, and you have a big baseplate. That baseplate is like the Earth – it's made up of huge pieces called tectonic plates! These plates aren't just sitting still; they are constantly moving, very slowly, over millions of years. This movement creates forces that can cause some really dramatic events like earthquakes and volcanoes.

There are three main ways these plates move: convergent boundaries where plates smash into each other, divergent boundaries where they pull apart, and transform boundaries where they slide past one another. When plates collide with force, it’s like pushing two cars together – the friction creates a lot of energy! This energy builds up until it suddenly gets released as an earthquake.

Volcanoes also form at plate boundaries because when plates move apart, magma (molten rock) from deep inside the Earth rises to fill the space. Similarly, when one plate slides under another (like in subduction zones), the melting rock can also push upwards and create a volcano. So, plate boundaries are where most of the world’s earthquakes and volcanoes happen!
keyIdeas: Plate tectonics drive earthquakes and volcanoes.
Different plate movements – convergent, divergent, transform – cause different events.
Volcanoes form when magma rises to the surface due to plate movement.

questions.prompt: Which type of plate boundary involves plates pushing together?
questions.explanation: Convergent boundaries are where plates collide, causing uplift and often earthquakes.

questions.prompt: Earthquakes happen because:
choices: A. Magma rises to the surface
B. Plates grind against each other
C. The Earth's core cools down
questions.explanation: The friction between moving plates generates seismic waves, which cause ground shaking – earthquakes.

questions.prompt: Where do you typically find volcanoes forming?
choices: A. In the middle of continents
B. At plate boundaries where one plate subducts
questions.explanation: Volcanoes often form at subduction zones, where one tectonic plate slides beneath another.

mt_rML9unnd9x.title: Volcanoes, Pangaea, and Earth-Shaking Events!
explanation: Let's talk about volcanoes! They are like giant pipes under the ground where hot, melted rock called magma is pushing upwards. A really famous eruption happened in 1980 with Mount St. Helens in Washington State – it was a huge surprise because scientists hadn’t expected such a powerful explosion. The mountain blew apart sending ash and debris high into the air, causing widespread damage to forests and towns nearby.

When volcanoes erupt, they release gases like sulfur dioxide into the atmosphere. These gases can act like a blanket, trapping heat from the sun and making the Earth cooler for a short time. Think about it – if you put a dark blanket over a light bulb, it would absorb more heat! Similarly, volcanic ash reflects sunlight back into space, leading to temporary cooling effects around the world.

Long ago, all of the continents were joined together in one giant landmass called Pangaea. Scientists believe that this supercontinent existed about 300 million years ago. Over millions of years, the Earth's plates – huge pieces of the crust that make up the continents – have slowly moved apart, like puzzle pieces drifting away from each other. This movement is what causes earthquakes and creates new landforms!
keyIdeas: Volcanoes release hot magma and gases.
Volcanic eruptions can temporarily cool global temperatures.
Continents were once joined in Pangaea and have drifted apart.

questions.prompt: Which of these volcanoes famously erupted in 1980?
choices:
A) Mount Vesuvius
B) Mount St. Helens
C) Krakatoa
questions.explanation: Mount St. Helens is famous for its powerful eruption in 1980.

questions.prompt: Volcanic ash can cool the Earth by:
choices:
A) Adding heat to the atmosphere.
B) Reflecting sunlight back into space.
C) Creating new landforms.
questions.explanation: The ash reflects sunlight, reducing the amount of energy reaching our planet.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the supercontinent that existed millions of years ago?
choices:
A) Australia
B) Pangaea
C) Antarctica
questions.explanation: Pangaea means 'all land' and describes the ancient supercontinent.

mt_J6uccv2Bo4.title: Ocean Pollution: A Problem We Can Fix
explanation: Our oceans are amazing places, full of incredible creatures like dolphins, whales, and colorful fish! But sadly, humans are causing a lot of problems for the ocean. Things like plastic bottles, fishing nets, and even chemicals from factories can make their way into the water, harming these beautiful animals and disrupting the balance of life in the sea. It's important to understand how we’re impacting this vital part of our planet.

Most of the pollution that ends up in the ocean doesn't come directly from ships; it actually starts on land! Think about all the things we use – toys, packaging, and even some food comes wrapped in plastic. When this plastic isn’t properly disposed of, it can easily wash into rivers and streams, which eventually flow out to the ocean. This shows us that our actions on land have a huge impact on the health of the sea.

Specifically, plastic pollution is incredibly harmful because animals often mistake it for food. Sea turtles might eat plastic bags thinking they’re jellyfish, or seabirds can feed plastic pieces to their chicks. Overfishing also causes problems – when we take too many fish out of the ocean, it disrupts the entire food chain and threatens the survival of many species.  Ocean acidification, caused by carbon dioxide in the air, makes it harder for shellfish and coral to build their shells.
keyIdeas: Humans cause significant harm to the oceans through various activities.
Most ocean pollution originates from land-based sources.
Plastic pollution and overfishing are major threats to marine life.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a way humans harm the ocean?
choices: B. Plastic pollution from discarded bottles
questions.explanation: Plastic pollution directly harms marine animals when they ingest it.

questions.prompt: Where does most of the plastic that ends up in the ocean come from?
choices: B. Rivers and streams carrying waste from land
questions.explanation: Runoff from rivers carries pollutants, including plastic, into the ocean.

questions.prompt: Imagine a sea turtle eating a plastic bag. What is one way this harms the turtle?
choices: A. It makes the turtle stronger
B. It fills up the turtle's stomach and prevents it from eating real food
C. It helps the turtle swim faster
questions.explanation: The plastic blocks the turtle’s ability to digest nutrients, leading to starvation.

mt_W4j7T_PnGH.title: Volcanoes: Gentle Flows or Mighty Explosions?
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – sometimes you use smooth bricks to make a nice, even wall, and other times you smash them together really hard to create a bumpy castle. Volcanoes are similar! There are two main ways they erupt: effusive eruptions and explosive eruptions. Effusive eruptions are like the smooth LEGO wall; lava flows slowly and gently down the sides of the volcano because it’s runny and doesn't have much gas inside.

The type of eruption depends on what’s *inside* the volcano – specifically, the magma. Magma is molten rock deep underground. If the magma is thick and has lots of gases trapped in it, like a shaken soda bottle, it builds up pressure until it explodes with ash and rocks. This is an explosive eruption, just like smashing those LEGO bricks!

Volcanoes aren’t all shaped the same way because of how they erupt! Shield volcanoes are wide and gently sloping, formed by runny lava flows – think of a shield protecting you. Steep cone volcanoes are made from thick, explosive eruptions that pile up loose ash and rock, creating pointy shapes. So, the volcano's shape tells us a lot about how it’s going to erupt!
keyIdeas: Effusive eruptions involve slow lava flows.
Explosive eruptions depend on magma's gas content.
Volcano shape reflects eruption style.

questions.prompt: Which type of eruption involves flowing lava?
questions.explanation: Effusive eruptions are characterized by the slow movement of molten rock, or lava.

questions.prompt: What makes an explosive volcanic eruption more likely?
choices: B. Thick magma with lots of gas
questions.explanation: Magma with a high gas content creates pressure that can lead to violent explosions.

questions.prompt: Why do shield volcanoes have a gentle slope?
choices: A. They are made of explosive rock
B. They are formed by flowing lava
C. They are very tall and steep
questions.explanation: Shield volcanoes form from the slow, steady flow of runny lava, creating their wide, gently sloping shape.

mt_r8c43QB6wx.title: Shape Detectives: Finding Lines!
explanation: Today, we're going to become shape detectives! We’ll be looking closely at different shapes and figuring out how they are put together using lines. Remember, a line is something that goes on and on forever – like the edge of your favorite building block or the road you ride your bike down.  We can use these lines to sort shapes into groups based on what we see.

Let’s start with quadrilaterals, which are four-sided shapes like a square or rectangle. Some quadrilaterals have sides that run alongside each other – these are called parallel sides. Think about the table in your dining room; the top and bottom edges are parallel! Other quadrilaterals don't have any parallel sides, meaning they form angles where they meet.  It’s important to be able to spot these differences because it helps us understand how shapes are built.

Now let’s look at triangles – shapes with three sides. A special kind of triangle is a right triangle, which has one angle that's exactly 90 degrees (a right angle) – just like the corner of a book!  Right triangles are super important because they have a specific property: they always have one perpendicular line, meaning it forms a perfect ‘L’ shape with another side. Recognizing these angles helps us classify shapes accurately.
keyIdeas: Parallel lines run alongside each other.
Perpendicular lines form right angles (an 'L' shape).
Right triangles have one 90-degree angle.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes has parallel sides?
questions.explanation: A square is a quadrilateral with two pairs of parallel sides.

questions.prompt: What does 'perpendicular' mean?
choices: B) Forming a right angle (an ‘L’ shape)
questions.explanation: Perpendicular lines meet at a 90-degree angle.

questions.prompt: Which of these shapes is a right triangle?
choices: B) A triangle with angles that are not 90 degrees
C) A triangle with one 90-degree angle
questions.explanation: A right triangle always has one angle measuring exactly 90 degrees.

mt_4IxR66uGLc.title: Poetry, Drama & Prose: Three Ways to Tell a Story
explanation: Today we’re going to explore three different ways writers tell stories – poetry, drama, and prose. They all share the same basic idea: they want to communicate something to us! However, each form uses its own special tools and techniques to do it. Think of them like different languages for telling a story; they have their own rules about how things should look and sound.

Poetry is often full of beautiful words arranged in lines called verses. These verses might have a rhythm or beat that makes you want to tap your foot! Drama, on the other hand, is usually written as a play script with characters speaking their lines – we call this dialogue.  A play has a cast of actors and uses stage directions to tell us where they should stand and what they should do.

Prose is just regular writing like stories in books or articles you read. It’s organized into chapters and paragraphs, making it easy to follow along. Each form uses different structural elements – verse and rhythm for poetry, characters and dialogue for drama, and chapters and paragraphs for prose – all to create a unique experience for the reader.
keyIdeas: Poetry uses verses, rhythm, and meter.
Drama features characters, dialogue, and stage directions.
Prose is organized into chapters and paragraphs.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes a poem’s structure?
questions.explanation: Poems are built around verses, which have rhythm and meter – patterns that create the musicality of the poem.

questions.prompt: What is a stage direction in a play script?
choices: B. Instructions for actors on set
C. A line spoken by an actor
questions.explanation: Stage directions tell actors where to move and what actions to take, often indicated with italics or brackets.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a key element in prose writing?
questions.explanation: Prose uses chapters and paragraphs to organize the story and make it easy for readers to follow.

mt_1GVAmcwAiO.title: Science Can Be Revised
explanation: Have you ever built with LEGOs and thought your creation was perfect, only to realize it wouldn't stand up? That’s kind of like how science works! Scientists start with ideas – called theories – that explain how things work in the world. These theories are based on what they observe and what they think is true, but importantly, they aren’t always 100% correct right away. Just like a LEGO tower needs adjustments to be strong, scientific ideas need updates too.

Throughout history, people had some pretty amazing beliefs about the world that turned out to be wrong. For example, for a long time, many people thought the Sun went around the Earth! This idea was based on what they could see – it *looked* like the sun was moving across the sky. However, scientists like Copernicus and Galileo gathered evidence through careful observations and math showing that actually, the Earth goes around the Sun.

Scientists are always looking for new information to test their ideas. When experiments give results that don't match what they expected, it’s a really important moment! It means something needs to be changed or rethought. This isn’t a failure; it’s actually a sign that scientists are being thorough and open-minded – constantly checking if their understanding is accurate. Testing ideas keeps science moving forward.
keyIdeas: Scientific theories can change.
Unexpected results from experiments lead to revisions.
Testing ideas is crucial for scientific progress.

questions.prompt: People once believed that:
choices: A. The Earth goes around the Sun.
B. The Moon orbits the Earth.
C. Stars are made of diamonds.
questions.explanation: For many years, people thought the moon was orbiting our planet.

questions.prompt: What happens when experiments don’t match what scientists expected?
choices: A. Scientists ignore the results and stick with their original idea.
B. Scientists investigate further to understand why the results were unexpected.
C. Scientists declare that science is wrong.
questions.explanation: Unexpected results show that a theory needs adjustment.

questions.prompt: Why is it important for scientists to keep testing ideas?
choices: B. To make sure their ideas are based on the best available evidence.
C. Because they always want to be right.
questions.explanation: Testing ensures that scientific knowledge remains accurate and reliable.

mt_KFKUR_gBg_.title: Angles in Rectangles!
explanation: Let's talk about shapes, specifically rectangles! A rectangle is a shape with four sides where all the corners are perfectly square – we call these corners ‘right angles’. Think about your LEGO bricks; many of them are rectangular, and you can see those sharp 90-degree turns. Understanding angles helps us build and design things, from houses to toys!

All rectangles have special properties because of their right angles. This means that every single corner in a rectangle measures exactly 90 degrees.  Imagine a square – it's also a rectangle! We can use this knowledge to solve problems about rectangles; it’s like having a secret code for these shapes. Knowing this helps us understand how things fit together and why they look the way they do.

Now, let's put our knowledge to work with some examples. If you have a rectangular garden that is 7 meters long, you know that the opposite side will also be 7 meters long because rectangles are symmetrical.  The four corners of your garden will always be right angles – 90 degrees each! This makes measuring and calculating much easier.
keyIdeas: Rectangles have four right angles (90°).
The opposite sides of a rectangle are equal in length.
Right angles are fundamental to understanding shapes and their properties.

questions.prompt: What is the measure of each angle in a rectangle?
questions.explanation: Rectangles have four right angles, which means each angle measures 90 degrees.

questions.prompt: If one side of a rectangle is 6 cm and the perimeter is 22 cm, what is the length of the other side?
questions.explanation: Perimeter = 2 * (length + width).  So, 22cm = 2 * (6cm + width), which means 11cm = 6cm + width and therefore width = 5cm. The other side is 10cm.

questions.prompt: A rectangle has an area of 36 square inches and one side is 9 inches long. What is the length of the other side?
questions.explanation: Area = length * width. So, 36 sq in = 9in * width. Dividing both sides by 9in gives us width = 4 inches.
