mt_lzCcQzPJZi.title: Ancient Architects: Greeks & Romans!
explanation: Let’s travel back in time to Ancient Greece and Rome, where amazing buildings were built! These ancient civilizations were incredibly skilled builders, and they developed special ways of designing structures that we still see today. They used materials like stone and marble to create temples, theaters, and public spaces – places for people to gather and celebrate. The Greeks and Romans were obsessed with creating beautiful and impressive designs, focusing on symmetry and balance in their architecture.

The Greeks created three main styles of columns, each looking different and having a specific purpose. The Doric style is the simplest, with plain capitals (the top part) that look like little rectangles. Ionic columns are more decorative, featuring scrolls at the top, while Corinthian columns are the most elaborate, covered in leafy designs! These column styles were used to build important buildings like the Parthenon in Athens, a temple dedicated to the goddess Athena.

The Romans took things even further with their building techniques. They invented the arch – a curved shape that allowed them to build larger and stronger structures. They also developed concrete, a super-strong material made from volcanic ash, which helped them create massive buildings like the Colosseum in Rome and impressive domes. These innovations had a huge impact on architecture for centuries to come.
keyIdeas: Greek columns have distinct styles: Doric, Ionic, Corinthian.
Romans invented the arch, dome, and concrete – key building materials.
Ancient architectural designs influence buildings we see today.

questions.prompt: Which of these column styles has scrolls at its top?
questions.explanation: Ionic columns are known for their scroll-shaped capitals.

questions.prompt: What is concrete, invented by the Romans, primarily used for?
questions.explanation: Concrete was a key ingredient in Roman architecture, allowing them to build large structures like domes and arches.

questions.prompt: Imagine you're walking past a courthouse. Which ancient architectural element might you notice on the columns?
questions.explanation: You might see Doric, Ionic, or Corinthian columns – styles developed by the Greeks!

mt_H6LlpWgEYS.title: Reading and Comparing Bar Graphs
explanation: Today we're going to learn how to read and understand bar graphs! A bar graph is a way of showing information using bars. Each bar’s length represents a number, and the longer the bar, the bigger that number is. Think about your favorite toy – maybe you have a collection of LEGO bricks; a bar graph could show how many you have of each color!

When we look at a bar graph, we need to pay attention to what the axes (the lines) are showing us. The horizontal axis usually shows time or categories like colors or types of animals. The vertical axis shows the numbers that represent how much of something there is.  Let's say you’re looking at a bar graph showing the number of students in your class who like different fruits – one axis might show ‘Fruit’, and the other shows ‘Number of Students’.

Bar graphs are great for comparing things! We can easily see which category has the most or least. For example, if you look at a bar graph showing rainfall over two months, you could quickly find out which month had more rain by looking at the tallest bar.  Understanding this helps us solve problems like finding the difference in amounts shown on the graph.
keyIdeas: Bar graphs represent data visually.
Axes show categories and values.
We can compare information using bar lengths.

questions.prompt: A tall bar on a bar graph shows…
questions.explanation: The taller the bar, the bigger the number it represents.

questions.prompt: If one bar is 5 units long and another is 2 units long, what’s the difference?
questions.explanation: Subtract the smaller value (2) from the larger value (5).

questions.prompt: What does a bar that is going upwards on a time-series graph usually indicate?
choices: C. The amount stayed the same
questions.explanation: An upward trend means the value is growing over time.

mt_z7AJZapsJj.title: The Amazing Rock Cycle!
explanation: Have you ever looked at a really cool rock – maybe one you found on the playground or in your backyard? Rocks aren’t just sitting there forever; they're actually part of something incredibly amazing called the Rock Cycle! This cycle is like a giant, slow-moving journey where rocks change into different types over millions and even billions of years. It’s a continuous process with no real start or finish – it just keeps going around and around.

The rock cycle starts with igneous rocks, which are formed when hot melted rock (magma) cools down underground. Think about volcanoes! When lava erupts from a volcano and hardens, that's how igneous rock is created. Over time, weathering – like rain and wind breaking down the rock – turns this igneous rock into smaller pieces called sediment, like sand or dirt.

This sediment then gets pressed together by layers of new material and eventually becomes sedimentary rock, such as sandstone. If you heat and squeeze that sedimentary rock with lots of pressure and heat deep underground, it can transform into metamorphic rocks like marble! And if you melt any type of rock back into liquid magma, the cycle starts all over again – it's a never-ending adventure!
keyIdeas: Rocks change forms continuously.
Weathering, heat, and pressure drive the rock cycle.
The rock cycle takes millions of years to complete.

questions.prompt: Which process breaks down rocks into smaller pieces?
questions.explanation: Weathering is the breakdown of rocks into smaller bits, like sand or gravel. This is how igneous rock becomes sediment.

questions.prompt: If you heat and squeeze sedimentary rock, what type of rock might it become?
questions.explanation: Heat and pressure change the minerals in sedimentary rocks, creating metamorphic rocks like marble.

questions.prompt: The rock cycle is a(n) _______ process.
questions.explanation: The rock cycle keeps repeating itself without a clear beginning or end – it’s always changing!

mt_Psun-u_lPf.title: Measuring Like a Scientist!
explanation: Let's talk about how we find out exactly how much of something there is – that’s called measurement! Scientists use special tools to measure things, and it’s important to be accurate. Accuracy means getting the right answer, while precision means being consistent with your measurements; think about measuring the length of a table - you want to know if it's really 1.5 meters long, not just close to that number.

Imagine you're building a LEGO castle and need to measure out exactly 20 blocks for each tower. You could use a ruler or even a scale to weigh the blocks! Taking multiple measurements – like three or four – is super important because it helps us see if our measurement is reliable, meaning it’s likely correct. If you measured something five times and got different results, that means we need to check our work.

Sometimes, you might get a measurement that seems really strange compared to the others – this is called an anomalous result! It could be because of a mistake in how you read the tool, or maybe the thing you’re measuring isn't exactly what you thought it was. Always double-check your equipment and your reading before deciding if a measurement should be kept.
keyIdeas: Precision is about consistency; accuracy is about getting the right answer.
Repeat readings improve reliability in measurements.
Anomalous results need investigation to ensure accurate data.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes precision?
choices: A. Getting the exact right answer every time.
B. Taking multiple measurements to check for consistency.
C. Using a very sensitive measuring tool.
questions.explanation: Precision means taking several measurements that are close together, even if they aren't exactly the same.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to take at least three readings of something?
choices: A. It’s faster than just taking one reading.
B. It helps us see if our measurement is reliable.
C. It makes the measurement look more complicated.
questions.explanation: Multiple readings allow you to identify and reduce errors, making your results more trustworthy.

questions.prompt: You measure a block five times and get 10cm, 11cm, 10cm, 9cm, and 12cm. What should you do?
choices: A. Immediately use the longest measurement (12cm).
B. Ignore the measurement of 9cm and use the average.
C. Investigate why the measurements are so different and consider if there was an error.
questions.explanation: Anomalous results require careful examination to identify potential problems with the measurement process.

mt_yrSdVrXrsF.title: Dinosaur Hip Groups!
explanation: Imagine you’re sorting your toys – maybe you have cars and trucks, or dolls and stuffed animals. Scientists do something similar when they study dinosaurs! They noticed that some dinosaurs had a hip bone structure that looked like a lizard's, while others had one that resembled a bird's. This difference in their hips was a really important clue about how these amazing creatures lived and what they ate.

The two main groups of dinosaurs are called Saurischia and Ornithischia. ‘Saurischia’ comes from Greek words meaning ‘lizard-hipped,’ and this group includes dinosaurs like Tyrannosaurus Rex (T. rex) and long-necked sauropods. The ‘Ornithischia’ name means ‘bird-hipped,’ and this group contains dinosaurs with a more bird-like hip structure, such as Triceratops with its horns or Stegosaurus with its plates.

Scientists figured out these groups by carefully examining the bones of extinct dinosaurs. They noticed that the way the hip bones were shaped gave them clues about how the dinosaur moved and what kind of lifestyle it had.  This method of classifying things based on shared features is called taxonomy, and it’s used to organize all sorts of living things – even us!
keyIdeas: Dinosaurs are divided into two groups: Saurischia and Ornithischia.
Saurischia dinosaurs have lizard-like hips, while Ornithischia dinosaurs have bird-like hips.
Hip bone structure is a key piece of evidence scientists use to understand dinosaur evolution.

questions.prompt: Which group does the T. rex belong to?
questions.explanation: T. Rex has a lizard-like hip structure, which is characteristic of the Saurischia group.

questions.prompt: Which dinosaur belongs to the Ornithischia group?
questions.explanation: Triceratops has a bird-like hip structure, placing it in the Ornithischia group.

questions.prompt: What is the main reason scientists classify dinosaurs into groups?
choices: A) They all looked the same.
C) They examined differences in bone structure
questions.explanation: Scientists use bone structure to understand how dinosaurs evolved and lived.

questions.prompt: True or false: List at least two features birds share with theropods (e.g. hollow bones, wishbone, three-toed feet)
questions.explanation: List at least two features birds share with theropods (e.g. hollow bones, wishbone, three-toed feet)

mt_q15w--Fb5H.title: Earth’s Spin: Why We Have Day and Night
explanation: Imagine you’re spinning a globe! The Earth is like that globe, and it's constantly spinning around on something called an axis – think of it as a giant line running through the middle. This spinning takes about 24 hours to complete one full turn, which is why we have what we call a ‘day’. It’s really important to understand that this spin is what makes our world work!

As the Earth spins, different parts of it face the sun. When your part of the Earth is facing the sun, you experience daylight – it's bright and sunny! But as the Earth keeps spinning, your part turns away from the sun, and you enter nighttime when it’s dark.  It's like a giant light switch flipping back and forth!

Now, here’s a really cool trick: we don’t actually see the sun moving across the sky! It looks that way because *we* are spinning on Earth. The sun stays in roughly the same spot in space, but as our part of the planet rotates, it creates the illusion of movement for us.
keyIdeas: Earth rotates on its axis.
Rotation causes day and night.
We perceive the sun's movement due to Earth’s spin.

questions.prompt: What is the main reason we have day and night?
choices: A. The moon moves around the Earth.
B. The Earth rotates on its axis.
C. The sun gets bigger and smaller.
questions.explanation: The Earth’s rotation causes one side to face the sun (day) while the other side faces away (night).

questions.prompt: If you were standing on a part of the Earth facing the sun, what would you experience?
questions.explanation: When your location is facing the sun, it’s daytime – you'd be experiencing sunlight.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes why the sun appears to move across the sky?
choices: A. The sun orbits around the Earth.
B. The Earth rotates on its axis.
C. The stars are moving closer and further away.
questions.explanation: It’s actually the spinning Earth that creates this apparent movement of the sun, not the sun itself.

mt_x_Lg4RASVU.title: Unlocking Words with Roots!
explanation: Have you ever looked up a word in the dictionary and been totally confused? Many words we use every day come from ancient languages like Greek and Latin. These languages are still used today, but they’ve left behind lots of roots – these are the basic building blocks of words that have specific meanings. Knowing these roots can really help you understand new words and even learn faster!

Think about a LEGO set; each brick has a simple shape, but when you put them together in different ways, you can build amazing things!  Words work similarly – they’re made up of smaller parts called affixes (prefixes and suffixes) attached to roots. For example, the root ‘port’ means ‘carry,’ so words like ‘transport’ and ‘portable’ both relate to carrying something.

Let's break down a word: 'un-believe-able'.  ‘Un-‘ is a prefix that means ‘not.’ ‘Believe’ is the root – it means “to trust.” And ‘-able’ turns it into an adjective, meaning “capable of.” Putting it all together, ‘unable to be believed’ makes perfect sense! Recognizing these parts helps you understand the word's overall meaning.
keyIdeas: Roots are the core meanings of words.
Prefixes and suffixes modify root meanings.
Recognizing roots helps with vocabulary building.

questions.prompt: What does the root ‘dict’ mean?
questions.explanation: The root ‘dict’ comes from Latin and means ‘say.’

questions.prompt: Which of these words uses the prefix ‘un-’ correctly?
questions.explanation: ‘Un-‘ is a prefix that means ‘not,’ as in ‘unable to be believed.’

questions.prompt: What does the root ‘bio’ mean in the word ‘biology’?
questions.explanation: The root ‘bio’ comes from Greek and means ‘life.’

mt_ZOJ6EbdPOb.title: Fractions: Scaling Up!
explanation: Today we’re going to learn about fractions and how they can help us solve problems where things are being scaled up or down. Imagine you're baking cookies – a recipe often tells you how much of something to use when you’re making enough for a certain number of people. Fractions let us change those amounts proportionally, so we don’t end up with too little or too much! This is really useful in lots of situations, like cooking, building, and even measuring things.

Let's look at an example: A recipe calls for 200 grams of flour when you’re making cookies for 4 people. If you want to make enough for 6 people, you need to figure out how much flour to use. This is where fractions come in handy – we can multiply the amount by a fraction that represents the change in people! We'll see exactly how to do this with some calculations.

Scaling involves multiplying or dividing quantities by a fraction.  For instance, if you want to make a recipe for 6 people instead of 4, you’re essentially increasing the recipe by a factor of 6/4, which simplifies to 3/2. This means you need to double the amount of each ingredient – so, 200 grams * (3/2) = 300 grams of flour! Understanding this process will help you tackle many other fraction problems.
keyIdeas: Fractions represent parts of a whole.
Scaling involves multiplying or dividing by fractions.
Changing the number of people affects the amount of ingredients needed.

questions.prompt: A recipe for 4 pizzas needs 2 cups of cheese. If you want to make enough for 6 pizzas, how much cheese do you need?
questions.explanation: You are scaling the recipe up by a factor of 6/4 which simplifies to 3/2.  So, you multiply the amount of cheese (2 cups) by 3/2, resulting in 3 cups.

questions.prompt: If 3 kilograms of apples costs £12, how much will 5 kilograms cost?
questions.explanation: This is a scaling problem. You are increasing the amount of apples by a factor of 5/3.  To find the new price, you multiply the original price (£12) by 5/3, which equals £20.

questions.prompt: A shape has dimensions of 4 cm x 6 cm. If you scale it down by a factor of 1/2, what are the new dimensions?
questions.explanation: Scaling down by 1/2 means dividing each dimension by 2. So the new width is 4cm / 2 = 2cm and the new length is 6cm / 2 = 3cm. The new dimensions are 2 cm x 3 cm.

mt_hJW7hVflm3.title: Similes & Metaphors: Painting with Words
explanation: Today, we’re going to explore some cool ways writers make their stories and poems more interesting – using similes and metaphors! A simile is a comparison that uses the words ‘like’ or ‘as’. Think about your favorite toy car; you might say it's ‘as fast as a cheetah.’ This tells us something about the car – that it goes really, really quickly – by comparing it to a cheetah. These comparisons help us picture things in our minds more clearly.

A metaphor is a bit different. Instead of saying something *is like* something else, a metaphor says something *is* something else! For example, if someone says ‘Her laughter was sunshine,’ they don’t mean her laughter literally looked like the sun. They mean it felt warm, bright, and happy – just like sunshine does. Metaphors create strong images by directly linking two different things together.

Both similes and metaphors help writers make their writing more vivid and interesting. When an author uses these tools, they’re trying to create a specific image or feeling in the reader's mind.  Consider a description like 'The rain was a curtain of silver'; it doesn't just say it rained; it paints a picture of how heavy and shimmering the rain felt.
keyIdeas: Similes use ‘like’ or ‘as’ to compare things.
Metaphors directly state that one thing *is* another.
Both tools create strong images in our minds.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a simile?
choices: B) He ran as fast as the wind.
C) She is a beautiful flower.
questions.explanation: This sentence uses ‘as’ to compare his speed to the wind.

questions.prompt: What does the metaphor ‘Time is a thief’ mean?
choices: B) Time takes things away from us.
C) Time is always rushing quickly.
questions.explanation: This compares time to a thief because it secretly and gradually takes our moments.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: ‘The classroom was a zoo.’ What image does the author want you to imagine?
questions.explanation: The author wants you to picture a noisy, chaotic environment – like a zoo full of animals!

mt_sJyZW4qYUG.title: Mastering Spelling Words!
explanation: Let's talk about spelling words – they’re super important for reading and writing! Many words don’t follow the usual rules, so you need to learn them specifically. Think about your LEGO set; each brick has a specific shape and name, and you have to know how to put them together correctly. Just like with spelling, some words just *are* the way they are, and we need to memorize them.

There are lists of words that all students in Year 5 and 6 need to learn, often called ‘statutory word lists.’ These include words you’ll see a lot – like 'accommodate' or 'necessary.' One trick is to use the ‘look-cover-write-check’ method: first, look at the word, then cover it up, write it down yourself, and finally, check your work. This helps you catch mistakes before you even finish!

Learning tricky spelling patterns can be a lot of fun! For example, consider the word ‘separate.’ You could create a silly sentence like 'a rat in separate' to help you remember how all those letters go together.  When you’re proofreading your writing – maybe a story about building a fantastic castle – pay close attention and try to catch any spelling errors yourself.
keyIdeas: Memorize statutory word lists.
Use strategies like look-cover-write-check.
Proofread your work carefully.

questions.prompt: Which strategy helps you write a word correctly by first looking at it and then covering it up?
choices: C. Ask a friend to check your spelling
questions.explanation: This method involves carefully examining the word before writing it down, helping you avoid mistakes.

questions.prompt: What is a ‘statutory word list’ used for?
choices: A. A list of fun words to use in stories
B. A list of words all Year 5 and 6 students need to learn
C. A list of words that are always spelled incorrectly
questions.explanation: These lists contain words you must know as part of your English learning.

questions.prompt: You’ve just written a story about building a tower. What should you do before submitting it?
choices: B. Proofread for spelling and grammar errors
C. Ask someone else to build the tower with you
questions.explanation: Proofreading helps you catch any mistakes you made while writing.

explanation: Reflect on their own assumptions and biases — recognising that everyone carries unconscious assumptions about others, and that actively questioning these assumptions is an ongoing practice that leads to greater fairness and empathy
You know this idea when you can: Describe a time they made an assumption about someone that turned out to be wrong; Explain what unconscious bias means in simple terms.

mt_HFN1pGASpZ.title: Understanding Prejudice and Discrimination
explanation: Let’s talk about fairness! Sometimes, people treat others differently because of things they can't change – like their skin color, where they come from, or if they have a disability. This is called prejudice, which is having a negative opinion about someone *before* you even know them. Discrimination happens when those negative opinions lead to unfair actions, treating someone badly because of who they are.

Imagine a classmate named Leo who uses a wheelchair. If some kids tease him or won't let him play on the playground because they think it’s ‘too hard,’ that’s discrimination. It can make Leo feel sad, angry, and lonely – like he doesn't belong. Discrimination causes real pain and makes people feel bad about themselves, and it hurts communities when people aren't treated with respect.

But we all have the power to stand up against unfair treatment! One thing you can do is speak out if you see someone being treated unfairly. You could tell a teacher or an adult you trust, or even just offer a friendly word to the person who’s being left out. Remember, kindness and respect are always the best choices.
keyIdeas: Prejudice is a negative opinion.
Discrimination causes harm.
We can all take action against unfair treatment.

questions.prompt: What does prejudice mean?
choices: B. Having a negative opinion about someone before knowing them
questions.explanation: Prejudice is when you judge someone based on things they can't change, like their race or religion.

questions.prompt: Why does discrimination hurt people?
choices: B. It causes sadness and loneliness
questions.explanation: Discrimination can make someone feel bad about themselves and like they don't belong.

questions.prompt: What is one thing you can do if you see someone being treated unfairly?
questions.explanation: Speaking up or telling an adult helps stop unfair treatment.

mt_ae-cHHFR76.title: Why the Sun Looks Brightest
explanation: Have you ever looked at a flashlight or a torch and noticed how bright it seems, especially when you hold it close to your eye? The sun looks incredibly bright too, much brighter than all the other stars we see in the night sky. But here's a secret: the sun isn’t actually the biggest or brightest star in the entire universe! It just happens to be our closest neighbor.

Think about it like this – when you hold a flashlight close to your face, it seems super bright because the light is traveling a short distance directly to your eyes. The farther away something is, the more its light spreads out and gets weaker as it travels. This is why a distant floodlight might be much more powerful than the torch, but still looks dim compared to the sun.

The sun appears so bright because it's incredibly close to Earth – about 93 million miles away! Because of this distance, we see the sun’s light as if it were shining from a very small point. All other stars are much farther away, and their light spreads out over a larger area, making them appear smaller and dimmer.
keyIdeas: Distance is key to brightness.
Apparent brightness differs from actual brightness.
The Sun's proximity makes it seem brightest.

questions.prompt: Why does the sun look brighter than other stars?
choices: A. The sun is the biggest star in space.
B. The sun is much closer to Earth than other stars.
C. The sun gives off more light than any other star.
questions.explanation: The sun appears brightest because it’s our closest star.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between apparent brightness and actual brightness?
choices: A. Apparent brightness is how much light a star actually gives off.
B. Actual brightness is how bright something looks to us, depending on its distance.
C. They are exactly the same thing.
questions.explanation: Apparent brightness depends on how far away something is; actual brightness is the amount of light it produces.

questions.prompt: Imagine holding a flashlight close to your eye and then looking at a distant floodlight. Which one looks brighter?
questions.explanation: The flashlight will look brighter because its light travels a shorter distance directly to your eyes.

mt_m3-eXac3aP.title: Brackets and dashes for parenthesis
explanation: Use brackets, dashes, and commas to indicate parenthesis — additional information inserted into a sentence that could be removed without changing the sentence's core meaning

You know this idea when you can: Insert a parenthetical phrase using paired brackets, e.g. 'The oldest building (built in 1642) stands in the town square'; Use paired dashes to add an aside or extra detail within a sentence, e.g. 'My brother — who is older than me — lives in London'.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Insert a parenthetical phrase using paired brackets, e.g. 'The oldest building (built in 1642) stands in the town square'

mt_tAtMET4EIU.title: Counting Big: Powers of Ten!
explanation: Today we're going to explore how to count really, really big numbers! When we count, we usually start with one and go up. But sometimes, we need to jump in bigger steps like ten, a hundred, or even a thousand. Think about your LEGOs – you might build something by adding blocks together, but what if you wanted to add them in groups of ten? That’s what counting in powers of ten is all about!

Let's try an example: Imagine you have 462,000 dollars. You want to know how much more money you need to buy a really cool toy that costs 500,000 dollars. To figure this out, we can count *up* from 462,000 in steps of 10,000. We would add 10,000 (the first step), then another 10,000 (second step), and so on until we reach the toy’s price.

Counting backwards is just as important! Let's say you have 800,000. We can count *down* in steps of 100,000 to see how much lower we are from that number. It’s like taking steps back on a staircase – we carefully subtract the step size each time until we reach zero. This skill is super useful for things like figuring out how many people are left at a party after some have gone home.
keyIdeas: Counting in powers of ten involves jumps of 10, 100, 1000, etc.
We can count forwards or backwards using these steps.
Understanding place value is key to accurately counting large numbers.

questions.prompt: If you start at 375,000 and count up in steps of 10,000, what will be the next number?
What is the difference between 800,000 and 600,000?
questions.explanation: To find the difference, you subtract: 800,000 - 600,000 = 200,000.

questions.prompt: You start at 545,000 and count down in steps of 100,000. What is the last number you say before going below zero?

mt_Ii1hV4V5ql.title: Decimal Place Value – Rounding Like a Pro!
explanation: Let’s talk about decimals! Decimals are numbers that have parts after the dot, like fractions. They show us exactly how much something is in smaller units, such as hundredths of a dollar or tenths of a meter. Think about your favorite toy – maybe you have a Lego set that costs $9.99; the ‘9’ is a whole number and the ‘9’ after the dot is a tenth (0.9) of a dollar.

Rounding decimals means we make them simpler by chopping off the parts after the decimal point. We do this when we don't need to be super precise, like when talking about an estimate or a quick guess. To round to the nearest whole number, we look at the tenths digit (the first digit after the dot). If it’s 5 or more, we round up; otherwise, we keep it as is.

Let's try some examples! If you have 3.47, rounding to the nearest whole number gives us 3 because 0.47 is less than 0.5. Rounding to one decimal place (1 d.p.) means we look at the tenths digit – it’s 4, so we keep it as 3.5.  Rounding can be really useful when you're measuring things like a length or calculating an amount.
keyIdeas: Decimals represent parts of a whole.
Rounding involves chopping off decimal places for simplicity.
The ‘5 or more’ rule helps us decide whether to round up or down.

questions.prompt: What number is 3.47 rounded to the nearest whole number?
questions.explanation: Look at the tenths digit (0.4). It's less than 0.5, so we keep the whole number part as 3.

questions.prompt: What number is 12.95 rounded to one decimal place?
questions.explanation: The tenths digit is 9, which is 5 or more, so we round up from 12.9 to 13.0.

questions.prompt: You measure a table that is 4.67 meters long. Rounded to the nearest whole meter, how long is it?
questions.explanation: The tenths digit (0.67) is greater than or equal to 0.5, so we round up from 4.67 to 5. However, the question asks for rounding *to* the nearest whole meter, so 4 is correct.

mt_0Rx1ISxXFE.title: Global Trade: Why We Share Stuff!
explanation: Imagine you love eating pizza, but your town only makes pepperoni pizza. You might want to buy some tomatoes and cheese from another country where they grow amazing tomatoes and make delicious mozzarella! That’s because not every place can grow every single thing – it's much easier and cheaper to trade with other countries for things we need or want. Global trade is simply when countries exchange goods and services with each other, making sure everyone has access to a wider variety of products.

Think about your favorite toy car. Chances are, the plastic that makes it, the wheels, and maybe even the paint all came from different places around the world! Countries specialize in making certain things – some might be really good at growing coffee beans (like Brazil), while others are experts at building computers (like China). This is why we ‘import’ goods from other countries; they're better or cheaper to get than if we made them ourselves. We also ‘export’ our own products, like toys and technology, to sell to people in other countries.

But here’s a tricky part: different countries use different money! If you went on vacation to Japan, you wouldn't pay with dollars – you'd need Japanese yen. That's why we have something called currency exchange; it’s like changing one type of money into another so that people can buy things in different places. Global trade involves a lot of moving money and goods across the world, making our lives much more connected.
keyIdeas: Countries trade because they don't produce everything.
Imports are goods we get from other countries; exports are goods we sell to them.
Different currencies require exchange when traveling or trading internationally.

questions.prompt: Why do you think your sneakers probably didn't come from the same country as you?
choices: A. Sneakers are always made in one specific place.
B. Countries specialize in making different things.
C. Sneakers are too expensive to ship across countries.
questions.explanation: Countries focus on what they do best, so sneakers might be made where rubber is plentiful or where skilled workers build shoes.

questions.prompt: Let's say you want to buy a mango from Thailand. What does this represent?
choices: A. An import of Thai goods.
B. A local farmer selling fruit.
C. A toy being shipped from America.
questions.explanation: Buying something from another country is an example of importing – getting goods from elsewhere.

questions.prompt: If you were traveling to Europe and needed euros, what would you need to do?
choices: B. Exchange your dollars for euros.
C. Ask a friend to pay for everything.
questions.explanation: Different countries use different currencies, so exchanging money is necessary when visiting another country.

mt_8qQ2IosZZw.title: The Weighing of the Heart: An Egyptian Adventure
explanation: Imagine you’re a pharaoh, just finished your life here on Earth! The ancient Egyptians believed that after death, your journey wasn't over – it was just beginning. They thought your heart held the most important part of who you were: all your thoughts, feelings, and deeds during your lifetime. If your heart was good, full of kindness and honesty, you could go to a wonderful place called the Field of Reeds.

The Egyptians had a very special ceremony called the Weighing of the Heart. A god named Anubis, who looked like a jackal, would carefully remove your heart from your chest. Then, they’d put your heart on one side of a scale and a feather – representing truth and justice – owned by the goddess Ma'at on the other. If your heart was lighter than the feather, you were considered pure and worthy to enter paradise!

But if your heart was heavy with bad deeds, it would be devoured by a fearsome creature called Ammit, who was part crocodile, lion, and hippopotamus.  A scribe named Thoth recorded everything that happened during this important judgment, making sure the record was accurate for eternity. This whole process showed how seriously the Egyptians took living a good life.
keyIdeas: The heart was weighed against truth to determine worthiness.
Anubis and Ma'at played crucial roles in the judgement.
Preserving the body was vital for the soul’s journey.

questions.prompt: Which god oversaw the weighing of the heart?
questions.explanation: Anubis was the jackal-headed god responsible for guiding souls to the afterlife and overseeing the Weighing of the Heart ceremony.

questions.prompt: What did the feather represent in the weighing of the heart?
choices: A) The weight of the soul
questions.explanation: The feather of Ma'at was a symbol of truth, balance, and cosmic order – essential for a good judgment.

questions.prompt: Why did Egyptians mummify bodies?
choices: A) To make them look pretty
B) Because they loved the smell of spices
C) To preserve the ka and ba for the afterlife
questions.explanation: The Egyptians believed that your body needed to be preserved so your *ka* (life force) and *ba* (personality) could return to it after death, allowing you to continue your journey in the underworld.

mt_Ge4Wtg6QMM.title: Amazing Materials: Testing Their Secrets!
explanation: Today we're going to become material detectives! We’ll be investigating how different things – like toys, food, and even our homes – are made of materials with special properties. These properties describe how a material behaves; for example, some materials are hard, while others dissolve easily in water. Understanding these properties is super important because it helps us choose the right material for building things or figuring out what will work best.

Let’s look at five key properties: hardness (how well something resists being scratched), solubility (whether it dissolves in water), conductivity (how well it lets electricity flow through it), magnetism (if it sticks to a magnet), and transparency (if light can pass through it). We'll test these properties using simple experiments. Remember, we’re looking for *evidence* – things we observe during our tests that help us understand how each material behaves.

For example, think about your LEGO bricks! They are hard, so they don't easily scratch. Sugar dissolves in water, showing solubility. Copper wire conducts electricity really well, and a magnet will stick to iron.  By carefully observing what happens during our tests, we can group materials based on these properties and learn why engineers and scientists choose specific materials for different jobs – like using copper for electrical wires because it’s excellent at conducting electricity.
keyIdeas: Materials have unique properties.
We can test materials to find their properties.
Properties help us understand why materials are used in certain ways.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes hardness?
choices: A. How easily something dissolves in water
B. How well something resists being scratched
C. Whether it attracts a magnet
questions.explanation: Hardness refers to a material's resistance to scratching or denting.  Think about a rock – is it easy to scratch with your fingernail?

questions.prompt: If you put sugar in water and it disappears, which property does this show?
questions.explanation: Solubility describes how well a substance dissolves in another. Sugar dissolving in water is a clear example of solubility.

questions.prompt: Copper wire is used for electrical wiring because it…
choices: A. Is very strong and durable
questions.explanation: Electrical conductivity allows electricity to flow through the wire efficiently.

mt_Mf-T-fYRLX.title: Dissolving & Solutions
explanation: Have you ever made hot chocolate? When you add sugar to the hot milk, it seems to disappear! That’s because dissolving is happening – it means a solid material, like sugar, mixes completely into a liquid, like milk, to create something new called a solution. You can’t see the individual sugar crystals anymore; they're now spread out evenly throughout the warm drink.

Now, let’s talk about getting things back from solutions. If you evaporate the water from salty water, what happens? The salt will become visible again! Evaporation is when a liquid turns into a gas – in this case, water vapor – leaving behind the dissolved substance. This is how we can recover things like salt or sugar from a solution.

It’s important to know that not all mixtures are solutions. If you mix sand and water, you can still see the individual grains of sand. A solution looks clear because everything has completely dissolved, unlike a mixture where you can clearly see the different parts. This difference is key to understanding how things change when they dissolve!
keyIdeas: Dissolving means mixing a solid into a liquid.
Evaporation removes liquid from a solution, leaving behind the dissolved substance.
Solutions look clear because everything has fully dissolved; mixtures have visible parts.

questions.prompt: What happens when sugar dissolves in water?
choices: A. The sugar stays as separate grains
questions.explanation: When sugar mixes completely into water, it creates a solution where the sugar particles are evenly distributed.

questions.prompt: How can you get salt back from salty water?
questions.explanation: Evaporation removes the water, leaving behind the dissolved salt crystals.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a mixture, not a solution?
questions.explanation: Sand and water are a mixture because you can still see the individual grains of sand; saltwater and orange juice are solutions.

mt_UKmtuAsSLN.title: Reversible Changes: Things That Come Back!
explanation: Have you ever built a sandcastle on the beach and then the waves washed it away? That’s an example of a change, but not always a *reversible* one. A reversible change is when something changes its form, but we can still get back to what it originally was! It's like magic, but it's actually science – we just need to understand how things work.

Let’s look at some examples of reversible changes. Melting ice into water is one; you can freeze the water again and turn it back into solid ice. Similarly, dissolving sugar in water makes a sweet solution, but if you evaporate the water (heat it up until it disappears), you'll get the sugar and water back as they were before! These are all changes that can be undone.

Think about your favorite chocolate bar – when it melts on a warm day, it’s a change. But if you put it in the freezer, it will turn back into solid chocolate again! This is why we call these ‘reversible’ changes; they don't create new materials, just different forms of the same ones. It's important to remember that reversible changes happen all around us!
keyIdeas: Reversible changes return materials.
Melting, freezing, dissolving, and evaporating are examples.
New materials aren’t created during these changes.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a reversible change? A) Baking a cake B) Breaking a glass C) Melting ice
questions.explanation: Melting ice returns to its solid form (ice).

questions.prompt: If you dissolve sugar in water and then heat the water away, what do you get back? A) Salt B) Sugar C) Water
questions.explanation: Evaporating the water removes it, leaving behind the original sugar.

questions.prompt: True or False: When chocolate melts, a new material is created.  Answer:
questions.explanation: False – you can freeze the melted chocolate back to its original form.

mt_rbPioPELM1.title: Irreversible Changes – When Things Change Forever!
explanation: Have you ever burned toast in the morning? It’s a frustrating experience, but it shows us something really important about changes in science: some changes are irreversible. Irreversible changes mean that a substance has been transformed into something completely new and we can't easily get back to what it originally was. Think of it like mixing paint – once you blend red and blue, you don’t get them back as separate colors; you have purple!

Let’s look at some examples. When you burn wood, the wood disappears and becomes ash, smoke, and gases – these are all new materials that weren't there before. Similarly, when iron is left outside in the rain, it slowly turns into rust, a reddish-brown coating. This happens because the iron reacts with oxygen and water, creating something entirely different. Even mixing bicarbonate of soda (baking soda) with vinegar creates carbon dioxide gas and a new liquid – you can't easily turn that back into the original ingredients!

Irreversible changes always create new materials. The key thing to look for is that the substance has changed in a way that’s permanent. You might see bubbles forming (like when mixing baking soda and vinegar), a color change (like rust on metal), or heat being released – these are all signs that an irreversible reaction has taken place, resulting in something brand new.
keyIdeas: Irreversible changes create new materials.
These changes cannot be easily reversed.
Observing evidence like gas, color change, or heat helps identify them.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of an irreversible change?
choices: B) Water boiling and turning to steam
C) Burning wood and creating ash
questions.explanation: Burning wood creates new materials (ash, smoke) that cannot be turned back into wood.

questions.prompt: Rusting is an example of an irreversible change because it...
choices: A) Is a quick process that can be reversed quickly
B) Creates a completely new substance – iron oxide – that can’t easily be changed back
C) Only happens when the metal is polished
questions.explanation: The original iron has been transformed into a different material (iron oxide), which is why it's irreversible.

questions.prompt: You mix baking soda and vinegar. What evidence shows this is an irreversible change?
choices: B) Carbon dioxide gas bubbles form
questions.explanation: The formation of bubbles indicates a chemical reaction has occurred, creating new substances.

mt_2b6CB0w3Yx.title: Separating Mixtures: Like Sorting Your Toys!
explanation: Have you ever tried to sort your toys after a big playdate? Sometimes, you have a mix of different things – maybe LEGOs, action figures, and stuffed animals all jumbled together. Separating mixtures is just like sorting those toys; it means taking something mixed up and putting the different parts back where they belong. Scientists do this too when they’re studying materials to understand what makes them up!

Mixtures are made when you mix two or more things together, but each thing still keeps its own special properties. For example, if you dissolve sugar in water, the sugar is still sugar and the water is still water – they just mixed! We can separate mixtures using different methods that take advantage of these differences, such as sieving, filtering, or evaporating. Each method works because it focuses on a specific property of the materials.

Let’s talk about how we do this. Sieving uses holes to separate big bits from small bits – like using a sieve to separate sand from smaller pebbles. Filtering uses something like paper to catch tiny particles that even sieving won't get, and evaporation uses heat to turn liquid into a gas, leaving behind the solid.
keyIdeas: Mixtures are combinations of different substances.
Different separation methods target different particle sizes.
Understanding material properties is key to choosing the right method.

questions.prompt: You have a mixture of sand and small rocks. Which method would be best to separate them?
questions.explanation: Sieving uses holes to separate larger particles like sand and rocks from smaller ones.

questions.prompt: You want to separate salt from water. What method should you use?
questions.explanation: Evaporation removes the water (liquid) as a gas, leaving behind the salt (solid).

questions.prompt: Why does filtering work to separate tiny particles from a liquid?
questions.explanation: Filtering uses a material with small holes that trap the tiny particles while letting the liquid pass through.

mt_PCX1jZZnf9.title: The Pharaoh: More Than Just a King
explanation: Imagine you have a really important toy – maybe a race car or a building block set. You want to take care of it, right? Well, the pharaohs of ancient Egypt were like that, but on a HUGE scale! They weren't just kings and queens; they were believed to be living gods, meaning they had special powers and were connected directly to the powerful gods who lived in the sky. This meant everyone looked up to them for guidance and protection, making their job incredibly important.

The Egyptians believed that the pharaoh was the link between the world of humans and the world of the gods. They thought if the pharaoh acted justly and wisely, it would keep balance and harmony – a concept called Ma'at. Think about how you might try to be fair when sharing toys with your friends; the pharaoh had to do the same thing for all of Egypt! This idea of Ma’at was super important because it meant everything in their society had to be just and orderly.

To help the pharaoh, there were people called viziers and officials. These weren't just regular helpers; they were like advisors and managers who made sure the laws were followed and that things ran smoothly for everyone. They worked on behalf of the pharaoh, making decisions based on Ma’at to keep Egypt safe and prosperous – just like a good team works together!
keyIdeas: Pharaohs were seen as living gods.
Ma'at was the principle of truth and order in Egyptian society.
Viziers helped the pharaoh govern effectively.

questions.prompt: The pharaoh’s role in ancient Egypt was best described as:
choices: A. A regular king who ruled with armies.
B. A god on Earth, responsible for maintaining order and justice.
C. Just a figurehead with no real power.
questions.explanation: Pharaohs were believed to be divine rulers, connecting the human world with the gods.

questions.prompt: What is ‘Ma’at’?
choices: A. A type of Egyptian food.
B. The principle of truth, justice, and cosmic order.
C. A special building used by the pharaoh.
questions.explanation: Ma'at was a core belief in ancient Egypt – it meant everything had to be balanced and fair.

questions.prompt: What was the job of viziers and officials?
choices: A. To fight wars for the pharaoh.
B. To advise and manage the government on behalf of the pharaoh.
C. To build pyramids for the pharaoh.
questions.explanation: Viziers were important advisors who helped the pharaoh make decisions based on Ma'at.

mt_HZyUwycFvf.title: Cleopatra and the End of Egypt
explanation: Know that ancient Egypt eventually came to an end: the last pharaoh was Cleopatra VII, who allied with Rome but was defeated by Octavian (later Augustus) in 31 BCE, after which Egypt became a province of the Roman Empire — ending over 3,000 years of pharaonic rule and beginning a new chapter in Egypt's history
You know this idea when you can: Name Cleopatra VII as the last pharaoh of ancient Egypt; Explain that Egypt was conquered by Rome and became part of the Roman Empire.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Name Cleopatra VII as the last pharaoh of ancient Egypt
Explain that Egypt was conquered by Rome and became part of the Roman Empire
Understand that the end of pharaonic rule did not mean the end of Egyptian culture

questions.prompt: True or false: Name Cleopatra VII as the last pharaoh of ancient Egypt
questions.explanation: Name Cleopatra VII as the last pharaoh of ancient Egypt

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that Egypt was conquered by Rome and became part of the Roman Empire
questions.explanation: Explain that Egypt was conquered by Rome and became part of the Roman Empire

questions.prompt: True or false: Understand that the end of pharaonic rule did not mean the end of Egyptian culture
questions.explanation: Understand that the end of pharaonic rule did not mean the end of Egyptian culture

mt_JdAnBKIDnw.title: Ancient Egypt's Lasting Legacy
explanation: Imagine you’re building a really cool LEGO castle – you need to plan where the walls will go, measure how long they should be, and make sure everything is strong. Ancient Egyptians were amazing builders and thinkers! They created things that were so clever, they still influence us today, even thousands of years later. Their greatest achievements included advanced medicine, a way to understand math, and a system for tracking the stars – all of which helped them build incredible structures like the pyramids.

Let’s think about the pyramids themselves. The Egyptians used very precise mathematics to figure out how to build these giant triangles! They needed to calculate angles and volumes to make sure each block fit perfectly. This math wasn't just for building; it also helped them survey land after the Nile River flooded, making sure they could accurately measure their fields for farming – a really important skill for any civilization.  This shows how practical and advanced their knowledge was.

Ancient Egypt’s influence spread far beyond its borders. The Egyptians developed a calendar based on observing the stars, which helped them predict floods and plan planting seasons. This system of astronomy influenced later civilizations like Greece and Rome, who also used it for navigation and understanding the world around them. Studying ancient Egypt helps us understand how ideas travel and change over time, showing us how human civilization has grown and developed through shared knowledge.
keyIdeas: Egyptian advancements in math and astronomy impacted later cultures.
Ancient Egypt shared features like writing, agriculture, and organized religion with other early civilizations.
Studying ancient Egypt reveals the development of human civilization through the exchange of ideas.

questions.prompt: Which of these was a major achievement of Ancient Egypt that influenced later cultures?
choices: B. Developing a calendar based on star observations
questions.explanation: The Egyptian calendar, which tracked the stars, was adopted and used by other civilizations like Greece and Rome.

questions.prompt: What do we call Ancient Egypt’s system of writing?
questions.explanation: Hieroglyphics were a complex system of symbols used by the Egyptians to record information and communicate ideas.

questions.prompt: Ancient Egypt shared many similarities with which other early civilization?
questions.explanation: Both Ancient Egypt and the Indus Valley Civilization had advanced cities, organized religion, and developed systems for agriculture.

mt_bqL8DD1SbV.title: Rockin’ Rocks! Exploring Different Types
explanation: Let's talk about rocks – they're everywhere, from the mountains to your favorite toy car! There are three main types of rocks: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. Igneous rocks start as hot, melted rock called magma deep underground; when this magma cools down quickly, like when a volcano erupts, it forms solid igneous rocks with big crystals. Sedimentary rocks form from tiny pieces of other rocks or even plants and animals that get squeezed together over long periods – think about sand on a beach!

Sedimentary rocks are made through a process called deposition, where layers of sediment (like mud, sand, or shells) get pressed down by more and more material above them. Over time, these layers turn into solid rock. Metamorphic rocks happen when existing rocks are changed by heat and pressure deep inside the Earth. This can happen if a sedimentary rock gets buried really deep, causing it to transform into something new like marble or slate – imagine a sandstone cliff turning into smooth stone!

These changes aren’t just random; they're part of how our planet works! Erosion is when wind and water slowly wear away rocks and soil. Weathering breaks down rocks at the surface through things like rain and ice. Finally, deposition is when those broken-down pieces are dropped somewhere new – like a river depositing sediment in a valley. It’s a continuous cycle that shapes our world!
keyIdeas: There are three main rock types: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic.
Erosion, weathering, and deposition are key processes that change rocks and landscapes.
Fossils form within sedimentary rock when sediment covers and preserves remains.

questions.prompt: Which type of rock forms from cooled magma or lava?
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks are created when molten rock cools down.

questions.prompt: What happens during the process of deposition?
choices: A. Rocks break down due to wind and water.
B. Layers of sediment get pressed together to form a new rock.
C. Hot magma deep underground cools down.
questions.explanation: Deposition is when sediments are compacted and cemented together over time.

questions.prompt: A dinosaur bone gets buried under layers of sediment. Over millions of years, the bone turns into a fossil within a sedimentary rock. What process led to this transformation?
questions.explanation: This demonstrates how fossils form through sedimentation and the preservation of organic material within sedimentary rocks.

mt_-G6erQvLig.title: Finding patterns in data
explanation: Analyse and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth's features, recognising that many features result from processes that occur over long periods

You know this idea when you can: Use a map to identify patterns in the location of mountains, rivers, lakes, and other features; Describe patterns observed (e.g. mountains in chains, rivers flow from high to low ground).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Use a map to identify patterns in the location of mountains, rivers, lakes, and other features
Describe patterns observed (e.g. mountains in chains, rivers flow from high to low ground)
Explain that Earth's features result from processes like erosion, volcanic activity, and plate movement over long periods

questions.prompt: True or false: Use a map to identify patterns in the location of mountains, rivers, lakes, and other features
questions.explanation: Use a map to identify patterns in the location of mountains, rivers, lakes, and other features

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe patterns observed (e.g. mountains in chains, rivers flow from high to low ground)
questions.explanation: Describe patterns observed (e.g. mountains in chains, rivers flow from high to low ground)

questions.prompt: True or false: Explain that Earth's features result from processes like erosion, volcanic activity, and plate movement over long periods
questions.explanation: Explain that Earth's features result from processes like erosion, volcanic activity, and plate movement over long periods

mt_vAP_A986IQ.title: Erosion and weathering
explanation: Make observations and measurements to provide evidence of the effects of weathering or the rate of erosion by water, ice, wind, or vegetation

You know this idea when you can: Define weathering (breaking down of rock in place) and erosion (movement of broken rock/soil); Describe the effects of at least three agents of weathering/erosion: water, ice, wind, vegetation.
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Define weathering (breaking down of rock in place) and erosion (movement of broken rock/soil)
Describe the effects of at least three agents of weathering/erosion: water, ice, wind, vegetation
Provide evidence from observations showing how weathering or erosion has changed a landscape

questions.prompt: True or false: Define weathering (breaking down of rock in place) and erosion (movement of broken rock/soil)
questions.explanation: Define weathering (breaking down of rock in place) and erosion (movement of broken rock/soil)

questions.prompt: True or false: Describe the effects of at least three agents of weathering/erosion: water, ice, wind, vegetation
questions.explanation: Describe the effects of at least three agents of weathering/erosion: water, ice, wind, vegetation

questions.prompt: True or false: Provide evidence from observations showing how weathering or erosion has changed a landscape
questions.explanation: Provide evidence from observations showing how weathering or erosion has changed a landscape

mt_GheLsWvrJ4.title: Fossils: Clues from the Past
explanation: Imagine you're building with LEGOs – you start with a base and then stack blocks on top of each other, right? Well, rocks do something similar! Over millions of years, layers of rock, called strata, are formed. These layers build up one after another, just like stacking blocks, and the bottom layer is always the oldest because it was there first.

Now, let's say you find a dinosaur bone buried in a rock layer. That fossil – that’s the preserved remains of an animal or plant – tells us something amazing! The fact that a dinosaur bone is in a particular layer means that dinosaurs lived on Earth when that rock layer was being created. Different fossils found in different layers show us what kinds of plants and animals existed at different times.

Scientists look for patterns in these rock layers and the fossils they contain to understand how landscapes have changed over enormous amounts of time. If you see a cliff face with many layers, you can tell that the bottom layer is much older than the top layer because it was formed first. Fossils within those layers give us clues about what lived there long ago and how the land might have looked then.
keyIdeas: Rock layers form over time.
Fossils show us what lived in the past.
Patterns in rock formations reveal changes in landscapes.

questions.prompt: Which layer of rock is usually the oldest?
questions.explanation: Oldest rocks are formed first, just like building with blocks.

questions.prompt: What do fossils tell us about Earth's history?
choices: A. How many stars there are
B. What kinds of plants and animals lived in the past
questions.explanation: Fossils are preserved remains that provide evidence of life from different eras.

questions.prompt: If you found a fossil of a woolly mammoth in a rock layer, what can you conclude about that rock layer?
questions.explanation: You can conclude that the rock layer formed during the time when woolly mammoths lived on Earth.

mt_U_HQXCnAaG.title: Advanced Spelling Conventions
explanation: Spell words using assorted Y5-6 conventions: doubling after -fer when the stress remains (referring but reference), using hyphens to join prefixes to root words (co-ordinate, re-enter), the /iː/ sound spelt ei after c (receive, ceiling), and the letter string ough representing different sounds (though, through, thought, thorough, plough)

You know this idea when you can: Apply the -fer doubling rule: double the r when the syllable is stressed (referring, preferred, transferred) but not when stress shifts (reference, preference); Use hyphens correctly when a prefix ends with the same letter the root begins with (re-enter, co-own) or to avoid ambiguity (re-cover vs recover).
Try this together: use a real example at home or school, then ask the learner to show you in their own words.
keyIdeas: Apply the -fer doubling rule: double the r when the syllable is stressed (referring, preferred, transferred) but not when stress shifts (reference, preference)
Use hyphens correctly when a prefix ends with the same letter the root begins with (re-enter, co-own) or to avoid ambiguity (re-cover vs recover)
Read and spell words containing ough by recognising its multiple pronunciations: /oʊ/ (though), /uː/ (through), /ɔː/ (thought), /ʌf/ (rough), /aʊ/ (plough)

questions.prompt: True or false: Apply the -fer doubling rule: double the r when the syllable is stressed (referring, preferred, transferred) but not when stress shifts (reference, preference)
questions.explanation: Apply the -fer doubling rule: double the r when the syllable is stressed (referring, preferred, transferred) but not when stress shifts (reference, preference)

questions.prompt: True or false: Use hyphens correctly when a prefix ends with the same letter the root begins with (re-enter, co-own) or to avoid ambiguity (re-cover vs recover)
questions.explanation: Use hyphens correctly when a prefix ends with the same letter the root begins with (re-enter, co-own) or to avoid ambiguity (re-cover vs recover)

questions.explanation: Read and spell words containing ough by recognising its multiple pronunciations: /oʊ/ (though), /uː/ (through), /ɔː/ (thought), /ʌf/ (rough), /aʊ/ (plough)

explanation: How AI is changing the world of work: some jobs disappear, new ones are created, many change; jobs AI can't do (yet); what skills matter in an AI world

You know this idea when you can: Name at least two jobs that AI is changing or replacing and two new jobs AI has created; Explain why creativity, empathy, and problem-solving are skills that matter more in an AI world.

Explain why creativity, empathy, and problem-solving are skills that matter more in an AI world
